19.name: Joe Flacco
19.headline: Insurance Only: Flacco's Value Lives And Dies With Burrow
19.body:
Flacco's 2025 line tells the story of a stopgap, not a fantasy asset: 13 games, a QB24 finish on 146.7 points, 15 touchdowns against 10 picks, and a 60.3% completion rate. The underlying marks were uglier still, a minus-0.1 EPA per play, a 41.1% success rate, and a minus-2.8 CPOE that flagged accuracy slipping below replacement level. At 416 attempts he leaned heavily on a short, dink-and-dunk profile, with completed air yards of just 5.7 per catch and only a 10.8% deep-attempt rate.

Now entering his age-41 season back in Cincinnati, he sits firmly behind Joe Burrow with no standalone path to snaps. Leave him undrafted; an ADP near 299 confirms the market agrees. The lone case is contingency: when Flacco started six games here he was on a roughly 4,600-yard, 37-touchdown pace, so handcuff him only if Burrow's availability turns shaky.

96.name: Aaron Rodgers
96.headline: A careful, low-ceiling streamer in his farewell lap
96.body:
The 2025 tape frames the bet cleanly: Rodgers finished QB15 with 226.1 fantasy points across 16 games, throwing 24 touchdowns against just seven picks but generating only 6.67 yards per attempt and a 42.3% success rate with a flat 0.02 EPA per play. He pushed deep on a mere 9.1% of throws and released the ball in 2.59 seconds, the profile of a quarterback emptying short and dodging contact rather than stretching the field. That efficiency floor caps the fantasy ceiling even when the volume holds.

At 42 and reportedly playing his final season, he reunites with Mike McCarthy and inherits modest help in Michael Pittman and rookie Germie Bernard. The 2024 QB8 finish shows what a healthier supporting cast once unlocked, but expectations have shrunk. He drafts near QB28 and is best left on the waiver wire as a superflex depth piece or matchup streamer, not a weekly starter.

421.name: Matthew Stafford
421.headline: Reigning QB1 returns as elite, age-defying anchor
421.body:
Stafford authored a career year in 2025, finishing as our QB1 on 350.4 fantasy points. Across a full 17 games he threw for 4,707 yards and a monster 46 touchdowns against just 8 interceptions, posting a 0.168 EPA per play and a 50.8 percent success rate. The volume was elite (597 attempts, 113 red-zone attempts, 14.5 percent deep-attempt rate), and his 4 completions of 50-plus yards underscore a downfield game that hummed.

The leap from 2024 is stark: he jumped from 20 touchdowns and 214.6 points (QB13) to more than double the scoring, driven by efficiency (0.055 to 0.168 EPA) and touchdown variance breaking his way. The risk is age 37, a No. 1 overall pedigree now 17 years deep, and the regression that 46 scores invites. He offers no rushing floor (1 rush yard), so a TD dip would sting. Still, as a stable, high-volume pocket passer in a loaded offense, Stafford is a locked-in weekly starter and low-end QB1.

827.name: Tyrod Taylor
827.headline: Veteran backup in Green Bay, no fantasy role
827.body:
Taylors 2025 line tells the story of a 36-year-old emergency starter: six games, a 59.7 completion rate, 779 yards, and a 5-to-5 touchdown-to-interception split that ranked QB43 with just 59.5 fantasy points. The underlying metrics were poor, a -0.045 EPA per play, 43.4 percent success rate, and a -0.9 CPOE, well off the +0.449 EPA he posted in a two-game 2024 cameo and the 1,341-yard, QB35 stretch run he managed for the Giants in 2023. His legs are largely gone too, with 143 rushing yards on 27 carries.

Now in Green Bay behind Jordan Love, Taylor is a pure insurance arm with zero standalone value while Love is healthy. He retains some streaming intrigue only in the event of an injury, given a rushing floor that has historically lifted spot starts, but that is a deep-league contingency, not a draftable asset. For 2026 redraft purposes he is a waiver-wire name to remember, nothing more, and irrelevant in all standard formats.

1166.name: Kirk Cousins
1166.headline: Stopgap starter in Vegas on a rookie's clock
1166.body:
The trend lines are unkind. Cousins finished as the QB31 across just 10 games in 2025, posting a 61.7% completion rate, a 10-to-5 touchdown-to-interception split, and a 44.3% success rate that ranked among the league's worst. His minus-0.021 EPA per play and minus-2.8 CPOE confirm the arm strength has faded at 37, with a deep-attempt rate of only 8.9% and 4.4 air yards per completion. That marks a steep slide from his QB18 line of 2024 (3,508 yards, 18 scores).

He lands in Las Vegas penciled in atop the depth chart, taking first-team reps this spring, but the job is borrowed time until rookie Fernando Mendoza is ready. With an ADP outside the top 250 and QB36 billing, he is strictly a superflex dart throw. Draft him only in the deepest formats, and expect a short leash.

1373.name: Geno Smith
1373.headline: Veteran reunion in New York, but the arrow points down
1373.body:
The advanced profile is flashing warning signs. Smith's 2025 in Las Vegas was a sharp fall from his Seattle peak: a minus-0.137 EPA per play, a 41.9% success rate, and 17 interceptions against just 19 touchdowns across 15 games, finishing as the QB20 with 173.9 fantasy points. That follows a 2024 line that, while productive at 4,320 yards, already showed his EPA flatlining near zero. He took 55 sacks behind a leaky front, and his deep-attempt rate sat at a conservative 8.7%, capping the ceiling that fantasy QBs need.

He returns to the Jets as the Week 1 starter, reuniting with the franchise that drafted him in 2013. Garrett Wilson, rookie pass-catchers Kenyon Sadiq and Omar Cooper Jr., plus Breece Hall give him real support. But at 35 with negative efficiency trends, he's strictly a streaming or bye-week backup. Let someone else draft him in single-QB formats.

1466.name: Travis Kelce
1466.headline: Steady TE1 floor at 36, ceiling fading
1466.body:
At 35 Kelce still graded TE4 with 117.2 fantasy points, but the underlying trend is clear: his 19.7 percent target share and 0.197 targets per route run both slipped from 2024 marks of 23.3 and 0.237, and his red-zone looks were halved from 26 to 12. He stayed durable across 17 games on an 81.9 percent snap share, hauling in 76 of 108 targets for 851 yards and five scores, and his 0.364 EPA per play rebounded sharply from 0.162 in 2024, so the efficiency is intact even as the volume erodes.

The warning signs are the seven drops, a 6.5 percent drop rate, and four interceptions thrown his way, plus a 11.2 yard-per-catch figure that leans entirely on Mahomes funneling him short targets. With Rashee Rice and Xavier Worthy commanding deeper roles, Kelce projects as a touchdown-dependent back-end TE1 with a thinning ceiling. Draft him as a steady TE1 floor play in the middle rounds, not the positional cheat code he once was.

2133.name: Davante Adams
2133.headline: Touchdown-dependent vet whose volume and efficiency are quietly fading
2133.body:
Adams turned 14 scores into a WR27 finish over 14 games last year, but the underlying line screams touchdown dependence. His 52.6% catch rate and 1.95 yards after the catch per reception were career worst, and a 0.481 WOPR on a 19.6% target share marked a sharp drop from the 0.541 and 0.804 marks of his prior two seasons. The EPA per play (0.237) and 51.8% success rate look healthy, but they're propped up by 32 red-zone and 23 end-zone looks that funneled into elite scoring rather than steady yardage.

The 14-TD spike is the obvious regression flag; when he didn't find the end zone, his weekly output cratered. In Sean McVay's offense with Matthew Stafford, he still profiles as a clear starter, but Puka Nacua commands the alpha share, and Adams is now 33 with a lingering lower-body issue. Treat him as a touchdown-reliant WR3 in the fifth round, not a foundational piece.

2152.name: Teddy Bridgewater
2152.headline: Veteran clipboard insurance with zero standalone fantasy value
2152.body:
Bridgewater, the 2014 first-rounder (Pick 32, Louisville) out of retirement, surfaces as a 32-year-old emergency arm now listed in Detroit. His 2025 line in Tampa Bay is a non-factor: three games, 15 attempts, eight completions for 62 yards, no touchdowns and a brutal -0.626 EPA per play with a 10 percent success rate and -16.3 CPOE. Those are mop-up reps, and they confirm a player whose career has wound down to ceremonial depth duty.

The snapshot peak of 0.7 points per game in 2025 says it all: there is no usable fantasy role here barring a catastrophic injury cascade ahead of him on the depth chart. Bridgewater is a pocket passer with a stable demeanor but no rushing equity (three carries, four yards) and no volume. For all formats, including superflex, he is a waiver-wire name only, not worth a roster spot until and unless he is named a starter.

2216.name: Mike Evans
2216.headline: Aging star changes coasts, deep-ball flex in San Francisco
2216.body:
Evans is coming off a wrecked 2025: just 8 games, a 65.6 percent snap share and 35.3 percent route rate that gutted his volume to 62 targets for 30-368-3 and 54.8 fantasy points, ranked WR76. The peripherals decayed, an 11.5 percent target share and 0.30 WOPR down sharply from 2024's 19.5 percent and 0.518, with catch rate cratering to 48.4 percent on a thin 1.23 yards after the catch. This is a 32-year-old whose 2023 line of 136 targets, 1,255 yards and a WR9 finish now reads like a different player.

The move to San Francisco reshapes things. He tops the outside-receiver depth ahead of De'Zhaun Stribling but shares targets with Ricky Pearsall, Christian Kirk and George Kittle in a run-leaning scheme, so his Tampa-era target shares are gone. Still, nine red-zone targets in just eight games confirm he keeps goal-line equity and a touchdown-driven ceiling. Draft him as a low-end flex and bye-week dart, not the weekly WR1 he once was.

2306.name: Jameis Winston
2306.headline: Backup arm behind Dart with no standalone value
2306.body:
Winston enters 2026 locked into a reserve role behind Jaxson Dart in New York, and his recent profile underscores why the ceiling stays capped. His three-game 2025 sample was loud but unstable: a 0.259 EPA per play that outran a 43.4% success rate, a minus-3.1 CPOE, and a 25.8% bad-throw rate. The bigger 2024 body of work in Cleveland tells the truer story, eleven starts producing 13 touchdowns against 12 picks on a flat minus-0.044 EPA and a sub-44% success rate.

That boom-bust volatility, paired with backup snaps, leaves nothing to draft in standard formats. Projections peg him near 154 pass yards a game with one score and one interception, a profile worth only a last-round Superflex flier if you already own Dart. Everywhere else he sits on waivers, a name to monitor only if injury hands him the job and the easy schedule behind it.

2307.name: Marcus Mariota
2307.headline: Capable bridge starter, but only matters if Daniels sits
2307.body:
Pressed into 10 games in 2025, Mariota showed he can still hold an offense together: 227 attempts, 1,695 yards, 10 TDs against 7 picks, plus 297 rush yards and a score on 50 carries for 125.5 fantasy points (QB32). The efficiency was merely league-average, though, a flat 0.012 EPA per play, a 61.2 percent completion rate and a 16.7 percent bad-throw rate, well off his pristine 2024 relief line (77.3 percent, 0.359 EPA, 16.6 CPOE in 3 games). At 31, he is what he is.

The rushing remains the fantasy hook: his 50 carries and 30 red-zone attempts in 2025 give a startable floor whenever he plays. But he sits firmly behind Jayden Daniels on Washington's depth chart, so his standalone value is purely contingent. He is a high-priority handcuff and emergency streamer with QB1 upside in any extended absence given the mobile profile, but in normal redraft leagues he is a waiver-wire stash, not a draftable asset.

2359.name: Ameer Abdullah
2359.headline: Deep-roster veteran in Jacksonville with no startable path
2359.body:
At 32, Abdullah is hanging on as a passing-down specialist, and the trend line is brutal. His 2025 split with Indianapolis collapsed to a 10.5% snap rate, 14 carries for 60 yards, and 16 catches on 17 targets, totaling just 21.9 fantasy points across 13 games. That is a far cry from his 2024 Las Vegas season, when a 36.9% snap share produced 85.2 points, 19 red-zone touches, and five total scores. The bell-cow profile has fully eroded: a 3.2% target share and 1.62 yards per route run leave nothing to project around.

Now in Jacksonville, he profiles as a reserve fighting for a depth job rather than touches. The flickers of efficiency remain, his 4.29 yards per carry and 0.057 EPA per play in 2025 were respectable on tiny volume, but volume is the entire problem. He carries no draft value with an ADP outside the top 270, and there is no reason to roster him in standard formats until an injury reshapes the room ahead of him.

3048.name: Chris Manhertz
3048.headline: A blocking specialist with no path to receiving value
3048.body:
The advanced numbers tell the whole story: in 2025 Manhertz played just four games, logged a 17.8% snap share, ran 50 routes, and saw only two targets, turning them into a single seven-yard grab worth 0.7 fantasy points. His -0.19 EPA per play and No. 111 positional finish underscore a player whose job is sealing edges, not catching passes. Even his best recent year, 2024, produced only three receptions, 30 yards, and one touchdown across eight games, good for nine fantasy points.

Now 33 and back on a one-year deal, he returns to a depth role in a crowded Giants tight end room. Projections of roughly 1.8 catches, 16 yards, and a fraction of a touchdown confirm there's nothing to draft here, with a TE ranking buried in the 140s and essentially zero ADP. Until an injury ahead of him forces real snaps, he stays off every fantasy radar.

3163.name: Jared Goff
3163.headline: Elite passing volume, low ceiling: a steady QB2 value
3163.body:
Goff just stacked another full 17-game slate, throwing for 4,564 yards and 34 scores against only 8 picks while finishing as the No. 2 fantasy quarterback by our advanced metrics. The efficiency stayed real: a 0.145 EPA per play, 47.9% success rate, 68% completions and a tidy 1.8 CPOE behind a clean 2.3-second pocket. He pushed 185 throws past 10 yards and 66 past 20, but a 6.3% deep-attempt rate and just 45 rushing yards cap the weekly explosiveness that separates the position's true difference-makers.

That profile is why the market keeps him in the QB2 tier despite three straight years leading the league in raw passing output. He has not missed a start since 2021, the volume is bankable, and the schedule projects soft. With no rushing floor to chase, treat Goff as a high-floor streamer or an excellent superflex anchor rather than a set-and-forget QB1.

3198.name: Derrick Henry
3198.headline: Still A Workhorse, But The Tape Is Slipping
3198.body:
Henry banked 264.5 fantasy points and an RB7 finish in 2025 on 307 carries, 1,595 yards, a 5.2 average and 16 rushing scores across all 17 games. Volume and the goal line stay locked in: 65 red-zone opportunities and 27 goal-line carries kept the touchdown floor sky-high even at age 31. That's the bankable part of the profile heading into his age-32 campaign.

The efficiency trend, though, demands attention. EPA per play collapsed from 0.143 to 0.019 year over year, yards after contact dipped to 2.25, and broken tackles cratered from 42 to just 12. Explosive-run rate slid to 11.7 percent as the bursts that defined 2024 thinned out. With Baltimore's interior line in flux, draft him as a touchdown-dependent RB1 in the back half of round one, not the dominant force he was a season ago.

3202.name: Austin Hooper
3202.headline: Hooper Returns to Atlanta as a Touch-Limited Afterthought
3202.body:
Hooper's 2025 line tells the story of a fading complementary piece: 13 games, a 5.4% target share on just 26 looks, and 263 yards with two scores good for TE45. He ran routes on only 37.6% of dropbacks and posted a 0.126 target-per-route-run rate, both well off his 2024 marks of 47% and 0.217. The efficiency flashes are real but empty calories for fantasy, a 0.646 EPA per play, a 73.1% success rate, and an 80.8% catch rate on tiny volume.

Now back in Atlanta, he slides in behind Kyle Pitts as the clear second tight end on a one-year deal. His ranking sits around TE59 with an ADP outside pick 440, and the math backs the gloom: five red-zone targets and a 1.27 yards-per-route-run figure won't move the needle. Until injury opens a path to snaps, treat him as a deep-league name to monitor rather than draft.

3214.name: Hunter Henry
3214.headline: TE7 finish hides a target share about to shrink
3214.body:
Henry's 2025 was his strongest New England campaign: a TE7 finish on 118.8 fantasy points, 60 grabs for 768 yards and 7 scores across all 17 games. The efficiency was real, with 0.433 EPA per play, a 63.2% success rate and 1.65 yards per route run, all season bests. The volume that fueled it stands out most: an 18% target share, 22 red-zone looks and 10 end-zone targets, the kind of scoring opportunity that drives touchdown totals.

That usage is the catch. His seven touchdowns came on a near-decade-high 8.8 yards per target, a spike that rarely repeats, and the depth chart got crowded. New arrivals at receiver thin the pecking order around Drake Maye, threatening the red-zone share that made him valuable. Drafted as a back-end TE1 near pick 149, he's a steady floor with realistic regression baked in. Projections of roughly 57-665-6 reflect a likely step back from his TE7 ceiling.

3257.name: Jacoby Brissett
3257.headline: Brissett's 2025 Spike Faces A Steep Comedown
3257.body:
Pressed into duty after Kyler Murray's injury, Brissett delivered the QB14 finish on 227.4 fantasy points across 14 games, throwing 23 touchdowns against just 8 picks. Dig under the volume, though, and the efficiency screams regression: 0.004 EPA per play, a 45.8% success rate, and only a 1.6 CPOE. He took 43 sacks behind a 27.5% pressure rate and posted a 17.6% bad-throw mark, so the production leaned heavily on opportunity rather than elite play.

Arizona hands him the keys again entering 2026, but the runway is short with Carson Beck and an unresolved contract hovering. A loaded supporting cast in McBride and Harrison Jr. props up the ceiling, yet the modest 8.7% deep-attempt rate and pedestrian air-yards profile cap upside. He's a viable QB2 and superflex target around the QB30 range, not a single-league starter worth chasing on last year's spike.

3271.name: Tyler Higbee
3271.headline: Aging Rams tight end clings to red-zone value
3271.body:
At 32, Higbee is a part-time touchdown specialist. In 2025 he played 10 games on a 55.5 percent snap share, caught 25 of 36 targets for 281 yards and 3 scores, and posted a strong 0.261 EPA per play, ranking TE44. The volume is thin (6.2 percent target share, 32.4 percent route share) but the red-zone usage is real: 8 red-zone and 4 end-zone targets. That continued the trend from his injury-wrecked 2024 (3 games, 2 TDs) and is a steep drop from his 2023 workhorse line of 47 catches, 495 yards on an 86.5 percent snap share (TE20).

The efficiency holds up, but the age, the limited route volume, and a 3.28 air yards per target paint a short-area, touchdown-reliant profile with little weekly floor. In a Rams offense that funnels targets to Puka Nacua and Davante Adams, Higbee is a streaming option in deeper leagues when the matchup points to red-zone work. For most formats he is a waiver-wire name, not a draftable starter.

3294.name: Dak Prescott
3294.headline: Dak Prescott: bounce-back QB1 with top-five ceiling
3294.body:
Prescott reclaimed elite status in 2025, ranking third among quarterbacks with 313.8 fantasy points across a full 17 games after the 2024 ankle injury limited him to eight starts and a dismal QB31 finish. His 4,552 yards, 30 touchdowns and a 0.138 EPA per play confirmed the dip was health-driven, not decline, though his 47.4% success rate still trailed the 50% he posted at his 2023 peak. At 32, the efficiency floor is firmly established, and a 67.3% completion rate keeps the volume productive.

The surrounding talent only strengthens the case: a CeeDee Lamb, George Pickens and Jake Ferguson trio gives him a complete passing menu, and sharper red-zone conversion could push him toward the top five. He profiles as a draft-day bargain, with consensus rankings near QB11 and a price hovering around the eighth quarterback off the board. Modest passing-volume regression is plausible, but the discount makes him a near-lock value at QB1.

3634.name: Kalif Raymond
3634.headline: Efficient depth piece who needs a route to volume
3634.body:
Raymond's 2025 line tells the story of a high-floor role player rather than a fantasy asset. On just 36.6% of snaps he ran 214 routes, drew 30 targets and a 5.5% target share, and turned them into 24 grabs for 289 yards and a score. The underlying marks stay sharp: a 0.507 EPA per play, a 66.7% success rate, an 80% catch rate, and zero drops, with 1.35 yards per route run. That efficiency is real, but it never scales because the volume isn't there.

Now 31 and on a fresh deal in Chicago, he profiles as a depth receiver buried behind the Bears' top options, with a draft cost deep past WR100 reflecting that. His value remains rooted in special teams and spot snaps. Unless an injury opens meaningful route share, treat him as a name to monitor rather than draft in any standard format.

3976.name: Mitchell Trubisky
3976.headline: Veteran backup in Tennessee, strictly an emergency option
3976.body:
Trubisky's recent advanced data is built on tiny mop-up samples in Buffalo. In 2025 he threw just 35 passes over 4 games but completed 71.4 percent for 313 yards and 4 touchdowns with no picks, a sparkling 0.428 EPA per play and a 7.8 CPOE on a paltry 2.9 percent deep-attempt rate, all in low-leverage spots. That contrasts with his last extended run, a 2023 Pittsburgh stretch where 107 attempts produced a -0.156 EPA, 5 interceptions and a 19 percent bad-throw rate.

Now in Tennessee, he projects as the clear QB2 behind Cam Ward, ahead of Will Levis and Hendon Hooker. That backup role means no projectable fantasy volume unless Ward misses time, and even then Trubisky's conservative, short-area profile, just 7.1 completed air yards per completion in 2025, caps the upside. He carries streaming intrigue only as a contingency starter. For 2026 redraft he is a waiver-wire name, draftable in superflex solely as injury insurance.

4017.name: Deshaun Watson
4017.headline: The numbers say bench him until proven otherwise
4017.body:
The efficiency profile is alarming. Across seven 2024 games Watson posted a brutal minus-0.23 EPA per play and a 34.3% success rate, finishing as QB39 with just five touchdowns against three picks. His 78.7% on-target mark and minus-2.2 CPOE show the accuracy slipped, and a 31.9% pressure rate with 33 sacks meant he never operated cleanly. The 2023 sample was only marginally better: minus-0.09 EPA and a 41.6% success rate over six starts.

The rushing equity that once propped up his ceiling has evaporated, leaving 148 yards and one score on 31 carries in 2024. He enters camp in an open competition for the starting job, drafted around QB32 in single-QB formats where he carries no standalone value. An upgraded receiving corps offers a faint path back, but until the efficiency stabilizes, he is a Superflex dart at best and nothing more.

4033.name: David Njoku
4033.headline: Herbert upgrade can't mask a two-year usage slide
4033.body:
Njoku's profile has eroded fast. After a TE6 peak in 2023 (123 targets, 882 yards, 20.6% target share), his role thinned to 15.3% on 97 targets across 11 games in 2024, then cratered to a 9.2% share, 48 targets and just 293 yards in an 11-game, injury-shortened 2025 that left him at TE39. The efficiency softened too: 0.98 yards per route run and a 47.9% success rate are well off his 1.61 and 40.7% breakout marks, and his negative EPA per play has persisted all three seasons.

The move to Los Angeles pairs him with Justin Herbert, a clear quarterback upgrade, but he must beat out a productive young tight end for the starting job and fight a deeper target tree. The red-zone history (nine red-zone looks, four scores in 2025) keeps his touchdown floor alive, yet entering an age-30 season on a one-year deal, he's a wait-and-see streamer until the snaps confirm a real role.

4034.name: Christian McCaffrey
4034.headline: Bounce-back RB2 finish proves the engine still runs
4034.body:
Last year erased any doubt the 2024 layoff created: McCaffrey posted 314.6 fantasy points and graded out as our RB2 over 17 games, pairing a 23.5% target share with 102 catches for 924 yards. The receiving floor is the separator. He turned 531 routes into seven scores and stacked 100 red-zone opportunities with 20 goal-line carries, the kind of every-down, every-zone deployment that almost no back commands. An 0.041 EPA per play with 83.1% snaps confirms the volume was earned, not gifted.

The caution is age and tread, not talent. At 29 turning 30, his 3.86 yards per carry and 1.52 yards after contact have softened from the 5.36 and 2.01 he ran in 2023, and only 8.68% of carries went explosive. San Francisco is already managing his workload through camp. Draft him as a back-end first-rounder, lean on the pass-game usage as insurance, and stash Jordan James as the handcuff.

4035.name: Alvin Kamara
4035.headline: Father Time arrives: efficiency collapse drops Kamara to dart-throw
4035.body:
The 2025 tape is alarming. Kamara managed just 3.6 yards per carry and a single rushing score across 11 games, posting a brutal -0.238 EPA per play and only 67.7 fantasy points to finish RB42. Both the explosion and the contact balance evaporated: 1.44 yards after contact per attempt, a 7.6% explosive-run rate, and zero runs of 20-plus. The receiving safety net that defined his value cratered too, with target share falling to 6.9% (down from 17.2% in 2024) and just 0.70 yards per route run. At age 30, this looks like decline rather than noise.

The context is equally grim. He projects as a backup behind a younger lead back, with his snap share already sliding to 63.7%. Drafters are treating him accordingly, pushing him toward an RB56 price near pick 183. There is no health excuse here, only a fading skill set. Leave him on the board until the final rounds and only as desperation depth.

4037.name: Chris Godwin
4037.headline: Evans gone, but health and decline cloud the rebound
4037.body:
The numbers tell a worrying story. Godwin managed just nine games in 2025, his efficiency cratering to a 0.268 EPA per play and 9.4% target share after posting a robust 0.573 EPA and 11% share across seven games in 2024. His 7.08 air yards per target signals a shrinking, underneath-focused role, and his 1.49 yards per route run was less than half the 2.48 mark he hit the prior year. At age 29 entering 2026, two straight injury-shortened campaigns make the age-related slide a real concern rather than noise.

The bull case rests on opportunity. With Mike Evans no longer commanding targets, Godwin inherits the clearest path to volume he's had in years, and his 2023 line of 130 targets and 1,024 yards proves the ceiling exists when healthy. He converted seven red-zone looks into limited scoring last year, so touchdown upside lingers. He's a mid-round dart throw: real league-winning equity if the body holds, with genuine bust risk if it doesn't.

4039.name: Cooper Kupp
4039.headline: Faded slot veteran is a deep-league dart throw at best
4039.body:
Kupp's first Seattle season laid the decline bare: 47 grabs for 593 yards and just two scores across 16 games, a 69.3-point output that ranked WR47. His 15.4% target share and 0.174 targets per route both slid from 18.5% and 0.282 in 2024, and his WOPR cratered from 0.406 to 0.329. The encouraging notes are a 0.094 EPA per play and 57.1% success rate, plus 6.23 yards after the catch per reception, signaling he can still move the chains when he gets looks.

The problem is volume. Now 33 and slotted behind Jaxon Smith-Njigba, Rashid Shaheed and Tory Horton, Kupp profiles as a secondary option whose 12 red-zone targets didn't translate to touchdowns. Projections peg him near 47 catches, 584 yards and three or four scores, echoing 2025. With an ADP around 202, treat him as a late-round flier in deep PPR formats and a waiver watch otherwise.

4040.name: JuJu Smith-Schuster
4040.headline: Veteran depth in New York with no clear path to targets
4040.body:
The arrow has been pointing down for years, and the underlying numbers confirm it. In 2025 with Kansas City, Smith-Schuster ran routes on just 55.9% of dropbacks, drew a 0.128 target-per-route rate, and finished with an 8.2% target share that produced 33 catches for 345 yards and one score across 17 games, worth a paltry 40.5 fantasy points. His 0.98 yards per route run and 0.161 WOPR are replacement-level marks for a player who now profiles as a possession option without explosive juice (10.5 yards per reception, longest grab of 30).

The move to New York only narrows his runway. He lands behind Malik Nabers, Darius Slayton, Darnell Mooney and a deep camp battle that includes other veteran additions, leaving him competing simply to make the roster. Even his red-zone usage has thinned, and at 29 there is no breakout case here. Treat him as a non-factor in drafts and a deep-watch name only if injuries clear a sudden path.

4046.name: Patrick Mahomes
4046.headline: Mahomes returns from ACL with legs and value in question
4046.body:
Before the Week 15 knee injury, Mahomes was building his most efficient season in years: a career-best 0.16 EPA per play across 14 games, a 49% success rate, and 284.7 fantasy points despite a modest 22 passing touchdowns against 11 picks. The real engine was his rushing, where he posted career highs of 422 yards and five scores on 64 carries. That mobility, not the 62.7% completion rate or middling 7.15 yards per attempt, carried his QB12 finish and separated him from a passing line that has quietly cooled three straight years.

The problem for 2026 is that the legs are exactly what a torn ACL and LCL threaten. He is targeting Week 1 but still limited to 7-on-7 work in June, and Kansas City answered by importing Kenneth Walker III to lighten his load. With no new receiving help around Kelce and a healthy Rice, expect a more pocket-bound passer. He profiles as a low-end QB1 worth a mid-round flier, not an every-week anchor.

4054.name: Mo Alie-Cox
4054.headline: Blocking-first Colt buried behind rookie Tyler Warren
4054.body:
Alie-Cox remains a low-volume, blocking-first tight end, and the 2025 line proves it: 11 games, a career-low 39.1 percent snap share, 173 routes and just 20 targets for 13-117-1 and 17.7 fantasy points, ranked TE63. His 3.8 percent target share and 0.071 WOPR are irrelevant, and a -0.043 EPA per play fell sharply from 2024's 0.358. The lone bright spot is red-zone usage, six red-zone and two end-zone targets, the touchdown-dependent profile that yielded 3 scores and 36.1 points in 2023.

The 2026 outlook gets worse with rookie Tyler Warren installed as Indianapolis's clear TE1. Alie-Cox slides to the TE2 in-line role, where his value is purely as a blocker who occasionally vultures a goal-line target. With route participation already at 30.1 percent and trending down, there is no realistic receiving volume to mine. He is off the fantasy radar in all redraft formats, a deep-bench afterthought only in a rare touchdown-or-bust week.

4066.name: Evan Engram
4066.headline: Engram's Denver Debut Cratered, And 2026 Offers Little Relief
4066.body:
Engram's first Denver season ranked as a TE25 disappointment: 50 grabs, 461 yards and a single touchdown across 16 games, good for just 52.8 fantasy points. The underlying profile was worse than the box score. He ran routes on only 56.9% of dropbacks, drew a 13% target share, and posted a 0.247 WOPR while drawing only 4.36 air yards per target on shallow, low-leverage routes. A 39.5% success rate and 0.058 EPA per play barely registered, and eight drops on 58 catchable balls didn't help.

That collapse traces straight back to his elite 2023 (TE3, 0.454 WOPR, 53.8% success rate), making the regression undeniable. Denver's pass-catching room is now overloaded after adding Waddle alongside Sutton, Mims and Franklin, plus rookie tight ends behind him. At a TE35 ADP, he's a touchdown-dependent dart throw, not a starter worth rostering before October.

4137.name: James Conner
4137.headline: A torn 2025 leaves Conner fighting for relevance
4137.body:
Conner's 2025 evaporated after three games, and the tape was ugly: 2.97 yards per carry, a -0.013 EPA per play, and just a 3.12 percent explosive-run rate as the foot injury sapped his burst. That cratered him to RB87 with only 25.3 fantasy points. The contrast with his prior form is stark. In 2024 he finished RB9 across 16 games on 1,094 rushing yards, 4.64 per carry, eight scores, and a 14.41 percent explosive-run rate, while 2023 paired 5.0 per carry with a strong 0.075 EPA per play.

The efficiency is real when he's upright, but the path narrowed. Arizona spent a premium pick on a back and added depth, threatening to push the 31-year-old into a committee or worse. Treat him as a late-round dart with clear standalone upside only if the rookie stumbles or a trade reopens the lead role he commanded for four years.

4147.name: Samaje Perine
4147.headline: A Steady Cincinnati Backup Drafted Only as Insurance
4147.body:
Perine's 2025 line tells the story of a complementary back doing exactly what's asked of him: 84 carries for 382 yards at a tidy 4.55 per rush, plus 17 grabs on 21 targets across 15 games for 60.9 fantasy points. The efficiency held up, but the volume didn't, and a 35.1% snap share with a 3.5% target rate keeps the ceiling low. A minus-0.027 EPA per play and just 1.99 yards after contact per attempt confirm he's a rotational piece, not a feature runner, even with three rushing scores and two 20-plus chunk runs mixed in.

The role behind Chase Brown caps his standalone value, since most of Perine's appeal lives in third-down work and the chance Brown misses time. He's a late-round handcuff and waiver-wire stash rather than a draft-day priority, with an RB72 market price. Grab him only if you roster Brown or play in deep formats.

4177.name: Mack Hollins
4177.headline: Efficient field-stretcher in New England, but volume caps his fantasy ceiling
4177.body:
Hollins quietly turned in his most productive efficiency season yet, posting a 0.418 EPA per play and a 63.1% success rate across 14 games while finishing as the WR54. His 13.5% target share and 0.331 WOPR landed him 65 looks for 46 grabs, 550 yards and 2 scores, and the underlying profile screams deep threat: 13.1 air yards per target, 18.5 air yards per reception and a 54-yard long. He dropped zero of 46 catchable balls and won 70.8% of his targets, so the hands and downfield juice are real.

The problem is opportunity. At 32 and slotted as a complementary piece in New England's pecking order, he projects for roughly 11 catches and 127 yards by consensus, with an ADP outside the top 280. Last year's red-zone usage was thin (5 targets, 2 TDs), and his best-ever mark of 9.1 PPR points per game underscores a floor-over-ceiling reality. He's a deep-league dart, nothing more.

4189.name: Eric Saubert
4189.headline: Blocking specialist with no path to fantasy snaps
4189.body:
Saubert remains a back-end blocking tight end whose 2025 line tells the whole story: nine games, a 39.5% snap rate but just 86 routes, and only seven targets that produced four catches for 31 yards and zero scores. A 17.1% route rate and 1.5% target share leave him buried in the passing game, and a 0.36 yards-per-route-run mark confirms he carries no aerial value even when he is on the field. The lone bright spot was efficiency on a tiny sample, with a 0.214 EPA per play, but volume that thin never translates to fantasy production.

Now 31 and entering 2026 in Seattle, he sits behind A.J. Barner and Elijah Arroyo on the depth chart, leaving him third at best with no clear route to meaningful work. His 2024 stint in San Francisco (11 catches, 97 yards, one touchdown) marks his recent ceiling. Outside the deepest dynasty formats, there is nothing to chase here unless an injury opens the door.

4199.name: Aaron Jones
4199.headline: Aging dual-threat slips into committee, flex value only
4199.body:
The 2025 tape flags real decline: Jones managed just 12 games, a -0.057 EPA per play, and a 4.15 yards-per-carry mark that trailed his 4.46 in a 17-game 2024 when he finished RB7 by our rank. His explosive-run rate dipped to 9.09 percent, breakaway runs to 3.79, and he forced a broken tackle only once every 26.4 carries while gaining a thin 1.77 yards after contact. The 90.7 fantasy points and rank of 39 reflect a back past his ground-game prime.

The receiving role is what keeps him relevant. He still ran 198 routes on a 55.1 percent snap share and turned 41 targets into 28 catches, banking 227 yards after the catch and an 8.8 percent target share. Now 30 in Minnesota, he splits early downs with Jordan Mason while owning passing-down work, leaving him a PPR-leaning flex with a capped ceiling. Hamstring, hip, and shoulder issues make a full season unlikely, so treat him as a late-round dart, not a weekly starter.

4217.name: George Kittle
4217.headline: Elite TE on Achilles watch, discounted into draftable value
4217.body:
Even a shortened 2025 underscored why Kittle remains the position's efficiency king. Across 11 games he posted a 0.671 EPA per play with a 68.1% success rate, turned 69 targets into 57 catches at an 82.6% catch rate, and found the end zone seven times on just 13 red-zone looks. His 2.12 yards per route run and 2.27-clip career target value travel; in his full 2024 he ranked third at the position with 1,106 yards and 158.6 fantasy points. The talent isn't the question.

Health is. A January Achilles tear clouds his Week 1 availability against the Rams, and while he's reportedly on track, that timeline defies the norm and merits monitoring through camp. That risk has pushed his cost to roughly TE9 and the early triple digits overall, a steep discount for a player who could be peaking by the fantasy playoffs. Draft him as a mid-round upside swing and stash Jake Tonges as insurance.

4219.name: Jeremy McNichols
4219.headline: Efficient depth back buried in a crowded Washington room
4219.body:
McNichols quietly delivered on his limited 2025 touches, averaging 5.02 yards per carry on 44 attempts and breaking off a 60-yard run that doubled as his only rushing score. He shed contact at a 3.48 yards-after-contact clip and added 196 receiving yards on 25 grabs, posting a tidy 7.2% target share across a 31.9% snap rate. The problem isn't the production rate; it's the volume. Sixteen games yielded just 47.7 fantasy points, and that ceiling shrinks further in 2026.

Washington imported Rachaad White and Jerome Ford this offseason, stacking the depth chart and pushing McNichols firmly into a contingency role. Season-long projections in the neighborhood of 17 carries and 7 catches confirm he won't sniff standalone value barring an injury ahead of him. He's a deep-league handcuff and dynasty afterthought, useful only if the backfield thins out and his proven efficiency finally gets a runway.

4314.name: Johnny Mundt
4314.headline: Blocking TE3 in Philadelphia with no passing-game path
4314.body:
Mundt's profile is a blocker who occasionally leaks into a route, and the advanced numbers say exactly that. In 2025 with Jacksonville he ran 156 routes on a 35.8% snap share, drew 19 targets for a 3.5% target share, and turned that into just 9 catches, 111 yards and zero scores at a 47.4% catch rate with three drops. His 0.066 WOPR and 0.71 yards per route run are non-factors, even though his 0.209 EPA per play and the 8.44 yards after catch per reception show he can do damage on the rare touch.

The situation in Philadelphia only narrows the lane further. He landed a one-year deal stuck behind Dallas Goedert, with Grant Calcaterra ahead of him for any pass-catching reps, leaving Mundt to fill two-tight-end sets as an extra blocker. A nine-year veteran with 74 career grabs, he carries no standalone fantasy value. Leave him on the wire unless an injury reshuffles the room.

4351.name: Tim Patrick
4351.headline: Backup body in Jersey, deep-bench floor only
4351.body:
Patrick keeps fading as a peripheral piece. After a useful 2024 in Detroit (15 games, 59.2 percent snaps, 335 routes, 44 targets, 33-394-3 on an 8.4 percent target share and 0.425 EPA per play), his 2025 split between Detroit and Jacksonville cratered to a 31.8 percent snap share, 152 routes and just 24 targets for 15-187-3. The 4.4 percent target share, 0.103 WOPR and 36.7 fantasy points ranked WR122, and even the 0.237 EPA per play was buoyed by three red-zone scores on nine red-zone looks rather than real volume.

Now 32 and parked behind Adonai Mitchell on the Jets depth chart, Patrick projects as a touchdown-or-bust backup whose only fantasy hook is the nine red-zone and four end-zone targets he drew in a part-time role. With Garrett Wilson commanding the targets and a thin route count likely again, there is no startable path here. Leave him on waivers and only spot-stream in deep best-ball or a bye-week emergency.

4435.name: Anthony Firkser
4435.headline: Veteran depth flier with no fantasy footprint in Washington
4435.body:
Firkser's 2025 tape leaves nothing to project upward. Across five games he logged only 116 routes for an 18.7% route share, drew 10 targets, and finished with eight catches for 53 yards and zero touchdowns. A 1.8% target share and 0.034 WOPR confirm he was barely a part of any passing plan, and 6.62 yards per reception with 5.12 air yards per target marks him as a short-area outlet, not a producer. The lone bright spot, three red-zone looks, never converted.

Now 31 and signed in Washington roughly two weeks ago, he projects to battle for the back end of the tight end room rather than meaningful snaps. Drafters echo that, slotting him around TE177 with a single expert even bothering to rank him and zero ADP. With no 2026 projection and roughly 0% rostered, he's a camp body and emergency depth, not a fantasy asset on any standard board.

4454.name: Kendrick Bourne
4454.headline: Efficient on a Shoestring, Buried in a Crowded Room
4454.body:
Bourne's 2025 tape was quietly excellent on volume nobody handed him. Across 14 games he turned just 53 targets into 37 grabs for 551 yards at 14.9 per catch, and the efficiency markers pop: a 0.388 EPA per play, a 64.2% success rate, and 1.62 yards per route run. The catch is the workload. A 9.6% target share, 0.155 targets per route, and a 0.24 WOPR are backup-receiver inputs, and the zero touchdowns capped a WR52 finish despite the strong per-rep production.

Arizona doesn't fix the volume problem. Bourne slots third or fourth behind Marvin Harrison Jr., Michael Wilson, and target magnet Trey McBride, projecting as a slot complement rather than a featured piece. The efficiency is real and the hands are dependable, but until injuries clear a path, this is a deep-bench dart and a bye-week patch, not a weekly starter.

4464.name: Nick Mullens
4464.headline: Veteran Jaguars backup with zero standalone fantasy value
4464.body:
Mullens remains exactly what his career resume says: a journeyman backup whose snaps come only when the starter is hurt. The 2025 sample is essentially noise, four games and three pass attempts for 19 yards and 0.0 fantasy points behind Trevor Lawrence, and 2024 was the same story in Minnesota. You have to go back to his 2023 fill-in run to find real volume: 148 attempts, 1,306 yards, 7 touchdowns against 8 interceptions over five starts, good for just 66.7 fantasy points and a 36 positional rank.

That 2023 stretch is the cautionary tale, a 20 percent bad-throw rate and turnover-heavy gunslinging that produced points but never sustained efficiency. Now 31 and locked behind a healthy, ascending Lawrence, Mullens has no realistic path to meaningful snaps. He carries no redraft or dynasty value and should not be rostered in any standard format. He is a deep two-QB streaming dart only if Lawrence goes down, and even then a low-ceiling one.

4602.name: Robert Tonyan
4602.headline: Fading veteran tight end buried on Pittsburgh's chart
4602.body:
Tonyan's 2025 was effectively a non-season: two games in Kansas City, an 11.1 percent snap share, 38 routes, and a single target that produced one catch for one yard and 0.1 fantasy points, alongside a brutal -0.519 EPA per play. That continues a steep slide from his 2023 peak in Chicago, when he played 12 games on a 28.2 percent snap share, ran 157 routes, and turned 17 targets into 11 catches for 112 yards and 11.2 points at TE66. At 32, the trend line points only one direction.

Now in Pittsburgh, he sits third on the tight-end depth chart behind blocking specialist Darnell Washington and pass-catcher Pat Freiermuth, capping any target path. His value is purely TD-dependent and matchup-driven, and even that requires injuries ahead of him. There is no redraft case here: he is a waiver-wire name, not a streaming option, and should be ignored in all standard leagues for 2026.

4866.name: Saquon Barkley
4866.headline: Aging Barkley a steady RB1 with diminished ceiling
4866.body:
Our model ranked Barkley the RB11 in 2025 at 195.3 fantasy points across 16 games, a clear step back from his RB1, 322.3-point monster of 2024. The volume stayed elite, but the explosion vanished: yards per carry fell sharply, his rushing scores were nearly halved, and his breakaway-run rate dropped. His EPA per play slipped negative, signaling the 2024 outlier season has corrected toward a more ordinary profile.

Now entering his age-29 season, he profiles as a high-floor RB1 rather than a league-winner. The workhorse role is secure, but an aging line and a career carry count climbing toward 2,000 invite caution. Expert boards back that read, placing him around RB7-8 with a top-15 floor; weigh him against younger backs before reaching early.

4881.name: Lamar Jackson
4881.headline: Lamar Jackson eyes elite QB1 rebound after injuries
4881.body:
Our advanced metrics tell two stories. In 2024 Jackson ranked sixth among quarterbacks with a monstrous 430.4 fantasy points across 17 games, posting a 0.284 EPA per play, 51.3% success rate, 41 passing touchdowns to four picks, and 915 rushing yards. Last season was a different animal: limited to 13 games, he slid to QB23 with just 214.9 points, 21 touchdowns, and his efficiency collapsed to a 0.067 EPA per play and 45.9% success rate, while rushing output cratered.

The gap is almost entirely health and a shrinking ground role rather than lost talent. A cascade of injuries gutted his trademark mobility and clouded his usage down the stretch. Still, drafters are treating the down year as noise, slotting him as a top-two quarterback in the early rounds. If his legs return, the dual-threat ceiling that produced an elite 2024 makes him a league-winning value.

4892.name: Baker Mayfield
4892.headline: Crossroads QB after a steep 2025 slide from elite
4892.body:
Mayfield's 2025 spine reads like a giveback of everything he built in 2024. EPA per play collapsed from 0.171 to 0.053, success rate slipped from 51.3% to 44.5%, and his completion rate over expected flipped from +3.6 to -2.1. The touchdowns followed, falling from 41 to 26, and the fantasy total dropped from 365.8 points (QB3) to 271.9 (QB10). A 15.7% bad-throw rate and 83 poor throws across 17 games tell you the accuracy regression was real, not noise, even as he stayed on the field through a shoulder issue and a battered line.

The surrounding picture only muddies the bet. Evans is gone, Godwin's health is unresolved, and Egbuka must take a real step after a hamstring stalled his rookie surge. A new playcaller adds another coordinator to an annual carousel. He's a back-end QB2 in the QB14-21 band, with genuine QB1 upside only if the accuracy stabilizes and the receiver room holds together.

4943.name: Sam Darnold
4943.headline: Proven QB1 floor in Seattle, modest weekly ceiling
4943.body:
Darnold has quietly stacked two top-five fantasy quarterback seasons. In 2025 with Seattle he played all 17 games, completed 67.7 percent for 4,048 yards, 25 touchdowns and 14 picks, adding a 5.2 CPOE and 0.089 EPA per play en route to 235.4 points and a QB5 rank. That followed his 2024 MVP-adjacent breakout in Minnesota: 35 touchdowns, 4,319 yards, and 308 points, also QB5. The efficiency profile is legitimate, an on-target rate near 80 percent and 57 throws of 20-plus yards in 2025, though the touchdown total dipped from 35 to 25 year over year.

The knock is the non-existent rushing floor, just 95 yards and zero rushing scores in 2025, which caps his ceiling relative to mobile QB1s. He is a reliable two-QB and superflex starter and a strong streaming option in 1QB leagues, but the lack of designed legs and a touchdown regression risk make him a back-end QB1 rather than a set-and-forget weekly anchor for 2026.

4950.name: Christian Kirk
4950.headline: Buried WR3 in San Francisco after a lost Houston year
4950.body:
The advanced numbers tell a worrying story. Kirk's 2025 in Houston was a collapse: 28 catches for 239 yards across 11 games, a brutal minus-0.389 EPA per play, and a 34.6% success rate that ranked him 106th. His target share fell to 9.4% and his yards per route run cratered to 0.9, a far cry from the 2.22 he posted during his 2023 peak (57-787-3, 51.8% success, plus-0.275 EPA). The downhill slide across three seasons is unmistakable, and turning 28 only sharpens the concern.

Now he lands in San Francisco on a one-year deal, projected behind Mike Evans and Ricky Pearsall, with McCaffrey and Kittle commanding a huge slice of the targets. An ADP near pick 230 reflects that crowded depth chart. He's a stash-only flier whose value hinges almost entirely on an injury opening a path, with a tough playoff slate working against him even then.

4972.name: Mason Rudolph
4972.headline: Steelers veteran backup with no standalone value
4972.body:
Rudolph remains exactly what his decade in the league suggests: a competent emergency starter, not a fantasy asset. In 2025 he played just five games on 52 attempts, completing 73.1 percent for 310 yards, 2 touchdowns and 2 picks with a tiny 1.5 completed air yards per completion and a 9.3 percent deep-attempt rate. His 0.063 EPA per play and 5.8 CPOE flatter a checkdown-heavy sample; the 15 fantasy points and rank of 55 tell the real story.

The more telling baseline is 2024 in Tennessee, when eight starts produced 1,530 yards, 9 touchdowns against 9 interceptions, a -0.006 EPA per play and just 95.8 points (QB37). A 2018 third-rounder out of Oklahoma State, he profiles as a pocket passer with no rushing floor (7 carries, 6 yards in 2025) and a 5.7-game-per-year availability pattern. Behind Pittsburghs starter, Rudolph carries zero redraft value and belongs on the waiver wire in all formats unless an injury hands him a job.

4981.name: Calvin Ridley
4981.headline: Broken fibula and a shrinking role threaten Ridley's relevance
4981.body:
The 2025 tape is alarming. Across just seven games before a fractured fibula ended his year, Ridley ran routes on only 24.6% of dropbacks, drew a 6.8% target share, and caught 17 of 36 looks for a 47.2% catch rate with zero touchdowns. That is a stunning collapse from his 2024 line, when he commanded a 23.7% target share, a 0.663 WOPR, and 1,017 yards over a full 17 games. A 12.58 air-yards-per-target figure confirms he was still being used downfield, but the volume and efficiency cratered.

Now 31 and reshuffled in Tennessee's pecking order behind rookie Carnell Tate downfield and Wan'Dale Robinson underneath, his path to consistent targets is narrow. Modest projections near 47-761-3 reflect that, and the ADP outside the top 70 receivers is fair. Treat him as a final-round dart with one realistic upside scenario: an in-season trade to a contender after injuries hit elsewhere.

4983.name: DJ Moore
4983.headline: Allen's new toy buys back Moore's WR1 ceiling
4983.body:
Moore's 2025 was a study in collapse: a WR39 finish on just 682 yards, a 16% target share, and a career-low 1.30 yards per route as Chicago funneled work to Rome Odunze and Colston Loveland. His 0.162 targets per route and 45.9% success rate were both shadows of the 0.252 and 57.4% he posted in 2023, when he ranked sixth at the position with 1,364 yards and a sterling 0.487 EPA per play.

Buffalo flips the variable that mattered most. Pairing with Josh Allen reopens the explosive ceiling Moore showed at peak, even if a run-leaning attack and a crowded room cap his volume near the double-digit-target range he saw last year. The red-zone usage is thin and the floor is volatile, but the talent and quarterback upgrade make him a defensible Round 5 swing on rebound equity.

4984.name: Josh Allen
4984.headline: Josh Allen stays the QB1 floor with rushing ceiling
4984.body:
Our model put Allen 11th among quarterbacks in 2025, a slip in positional rank that undersells the season: he set a personal best with a 69.3% completion rate, threw for 3,668 yards and 25 scores against just 10 picks, and added 579 yards and 14 touchdowns on the ground. That dual-threat profile delivered 364.6 fantasy points, the third straight year north of 360 after 392.6 in 2023 and 379 in 2024. His 0.171 EPA per play dipped from 2024's elite 0.311, and a 48.9% success rate signals modest passing-efficiency regression worth monitoring.

The rushing equity is the floor that makes him matchup-proof, with 500-plus yards and a dozen-plus scores on the ground in each of the last three seasons. A deeper receiver room should lift his aerial volume after years of thin perimeter help. Treat him as a back-half second-round pick in single-quarterback formats and the unquestioned first selection in superflex builds.

4993.name: Mike Gesicki
4993.headline: Gesicki's 2025 collapse leaves no fantasy floor
4993.body:
The 2024 version of Gesicki ranked 10th at the position on a 0.292 WOPR, 13.2% target share, and 83 targets that turned into 665 yards. None of that carried over. In 2025 he slid to TE36, drawing just 42 targets across 12 games for a 6.9% target share and a 0.175 target-per-route-run rate, while his route share cratered to 35.7% on a 38.5% snap rate. The 307 yards and two scores read like a backup line because that is what the role became.

The efficiency markers still flicker, a 0.127 EPA per play and 57.1% success rate, but volume that thin caps the ceiling. Cincinnati funnels everything to Chase, Higgins, and Chase Brown, with Fant and Sample splitting the leftovers behind him. He profiles as a touchdown-dependent dart in TE-premium and deep formats only, and a waiver name everywhere else until the snaps return.

4995.name: Ian Thomas
4995.headline: Pure blocking tight end with no passing-game role
4995.body:
Thomas is a real-snaps, no-volume tight end, and the 2025 profile in Las Vegas spells it out: nine games, a 35.9% snap share, but just a 25% route participation and 13 targets all season. That worked out to a 2.6% target share and a microscopic 0.049 WOPR, ranking him TE64. He did catch all 13 looks for 114 yards (8.77 per grab) with a healthy 76.9% success rate and 0.39 EPA per play, but the volume is the story — zero red-zone targets, zero touchdowns, and only 0.79 yards per route run.

He re-upped on another one-year deal to stay in a blocking-first depth role behind the Raiders' pass-catching options, so the path to fantasy snaps simply isn't there. Modest projections of roughly four catches for 36 yards on the season confirm it. He's a streaming non-factor and waiver afterthought outside of the deepest two-TE formats.

5001.name: Dalton Schultz
5001.headline: Volume king for Houston, but the touchdowns stay stubbornly scarce
5001.body:
Schultz quietly turned in his best advanced profile yet in 2025, ranking sixth among tight ends with 0.355 EPA per play and a 58.5% success rate while soaking up a 19.2% target share. He ran 512 routes, drew 106 targets, and converted 82 of them into 777 yards on a tidy 77.4% catch rate with just two drops. That target volume and 0.207 targets per route run make him a dependable PPR floor and a genuine safety valve for CJ Stroud.

The ceiling is the problem. Despite 11 red-zone and six end-zone looks, he found the end zone only three times, extending a years-long touchdown drought that caps his scoring. With Nico Collins, Tank Dell, and the rookie pass-catchers competing for work, his efficiency stays modest. As a back-end TE2 going around the 11th round, he is a steady plug-in whose top-10 upside hinges entirely on touchdowns finally arriving.

5008.name: Durham Smythe
5008.headline: A Blocking Specialist Without a Fantasy Pulse
5008.body:
Smythe's 2025 line tells the whole story: 7 games, a 25.4% snap share, and just 6 targets on 101 routes for a 1.1% target share and 0.059 targets per route run. He turned that into 4 catches, 25 yards, no scores, and a microscopic 2.5 fantasy points, while his -0.042 EPA per play and 33.3% success rate confirmed there was no efficiency hiding underneath the volume. That is a steep slide from the 28th-ranked TE finish he posted back in 2023.

Now in Baltimore, he profiles purely as an in-line blocker behind a featured starter, which only narrows an already empty passing-game footprint. With a TE78 valuation and effectively zero roster relevance, there is no realistic path to weekly points. He stays off fantasy radars in every format and should be left on the wire outside of deep two-TE dynasty rosters needing snaps.

5012.name: Mark Andrews
5012.headline: Andrews' Efficiency Cratered, But The Volume Lever Is There
5012.body:
The 2025 tape was a problem. Andrews dropped to TE27 on 1.27 yards per route run, a fall from his TE9 mark a year earlier when he posted 1.96. His EPA per play collapsed from 0.726 to a flat 0.022, and his 50% success rate told the same story as the 8.79 yards per reception. He still drew 70 targets and a 17.2% share across all 17 games, but the production thinned to 48 grabs, 422 yards, and five scores.

The path back is real. He held 15 red-zone looks and nine end-zone targets, so the touchdown equity that fueled his 11-score 2024 hasn't vanished. With Isaiah Likely gone to New York, Andrews is the only proven seam threat in Baltimore, and a healthier Lamar Jackson lifts the floor. He's a late-round dart with mid-range TE1 ceiling if the offense normalizes.

5022.name: Dallas Goedert
5022.headline: Career-high scores mask touchdown regression coming for Goedert
5022.body:
Goedert's 2025 looked like a breakout, but the engine tells a quieter story. He turned 82 targets into 60 grabs and 591 yards while finishing TE14 with 125.1 points, and his efficiency stayed elite: a 0.441 EPA per play and a 58.5% success rate that few tight ends touch. The catch is the touchdowns. Eleven scores, many on shovel passes inside the five, drove that fantasy line despite a pedestrian 1.44 yards per route run and a 17.7% target share that screams secondary option.

That scoring spike is the regression flag. He cleared 15 red-zone looks and nine end-zone targets, but more than double his previous single-season high in touchdowns rarely sticks, especially with a new play-caller and no guarantee the goal-line role survives. Back for a ninth year as Philadelphia's starter, he profiles as a steady late-round TE12-type bet — useful, but priced for the volume, not the touchdown luck.

5045.name: Courtland Sutton
5045.headline: Sutton's Touchdowns Hide Thinning Volume in Crowded Denver
5045.body:
Sutton's WR12 finish in 2025 was built on opportunity more than dominance: a 21.2% target share, 124 targets across 17 games, and a 34.4% air-yards share that kept him the engine of Denver's downfield game. The efficiency, though, slipped from the prior year, with EPA per play climbing to 0.359 but yards per route run dipping to 1.74 and a 0.212 target-per-route rate that trails his 2024 mark. Seven scores on 74 grabs and 19 red-zone looks carried the fantasy line.

That touchdown dependence is the worry now. Bo Nix has leaned on him two straight seasons, but the arrival of Jaylen Waddle splinters a passing tree with little fresh volume to absorb, nudging Sutton toward a 1B role. His 1,017 yards and contested deep work keep a floor intact, yet the ceiling narrows. Treat him as a WR3 worth grabbing around the mid-rounds rather than reaching early.

5086.name: Marquez Valdes-Scantling
5086.headline: A deep-shot specialist running out of runway
5086.body:
The arrow points down for Valdes-Scantling, and the numbers explain why. His 2025 stint in Pittsburgh cratered to a 5.3% target share across nine games, with a minus-0.105 EPA per play, a 41.4% success rate, and just 0.53 yards per route run on 226 routes. He caught 48.3% of his looks for 120 yards and a single score, landing 139th at the position. That is a far cry from his 2024 ceiling in New Orleans, where 411 yards and four touchdowns rode a 1.51 YPRR and a 17.9% air-yards share.

Now 31 and in Dallas, his fantasy case lives entirely on volume he hasn't earned in a year. He remains a true vertical threat, but route counts and target rate have eroded each season. Unless the depth chart hands him meaningful snaps, treat him as a late-round dart at best and a waiver-wire afterthought in most formats.

5113.name: Cedrick Wilson
5113.headline: Deep-roster Lions flier with a fading target profile
5113.body:
Wilson's recent trail of numbers tells the story plainly. His 2025 cup of coffee in Miami covered just six games on 75 routes, a 14.6% route share and nine targets, producing five grabs for 44 yards, a -0.246 EPA per play and a 33.3% success rate. That extends a multi-year slide: his snap share has dropped from 57.4% in 2023 to 39.6% to 35.5%, and his WOPR cratered from 0.174 to 0.046. The lone bright spot is a three-target, two-end-zone-look sample in the red zone, but on this volume that is noise.

Now 30 and signed in Detroit, he projects as a back-of-the-room body fighting for snaps behind an established receiving corps. ADP near WR167 and a sub-1% roster rate reflect the reality. An easy projected schedule means little without a path to targets. He is a camp watch only, not a draftable name in standard formats.

5127.name: Kyle Allen
5127.headline: Allen joins Buffalo's QB room strictly as insurance
5127.body:
The advanced profile underscores how marginal Allen's recent footprint has been. He logged two games in 2025 and went 0-for-2 with a -0.271 EPA per play and a -63.7 CPOE, posting -0.3 fantasy points on the season. The 2024 sample was equally tiny: one attempt, one completion for 19 yards. There simply isn't enough volume to project anything meaningful, and his career arc points squarely toward a clipboard role.

Now in Buffalo on a modest two-year deal, Allen sits buried on the depth chart behind an entrenched starter. Expert consensus pegs him outside the top 70 quarterbacks with no ADP and roughly zero rostership, and that ranking is generous given his path to snaps. Treat him as pure emergency insurance: unless an injury thrusts him into starts, there is no scenario where he belongs on a fantasy roster in 2026.

5133.name: Tyler Conklin
5133.headline: Veteran tight end fades into a Detroit backup role
5133.body:
Conklins 2025 was a sharp fall from relevance: signed to the Chargers, he played 6 games on a 19.2 percent snap share, ran just 79 routes, and caught 7 of 10 targets for 101 yards with no touchdowns and a -0.368 EPA per play, ranking TE70. That collapse followed two years as a dependable Jets volume target: 51 catches, 449 yards, 4 scores, and 70.9 points (TE27) in 2024, and 61 catches, 621 yards, and an 80-plus percent route share in 2023 (TE13).

Now in Detroit behind Sam LaPorta, the 30-year-old is a clear backup with no path to the target volume that powered his peak (15.5 percent share in 2023 versus 1.8 percent in 2025). His value depends entirely on a LaPorta injury, and even then the Lions pass-game pecking order limits the ceiling. There is no standalone redraft case here. He is a waiver-wire name to file away as an injury contingency, nothing more.

5374.name: Justin Watson
5374.headline: Field-stretching specialist who needs snaps he no longer commands
5374.body:
Watson's value has always lived on the vertical plane, and the numbers confirm a shrinking role. His best campaign came in 2023, when he turned 53 targets into 460 yards and three scores on a 17.5 air-yards-per-target diet and a healthy 1.35 yards per route. That market share quietly eroded the next year in Kansas City, where his target share slipped to 5.6 percent on 32 looks for 289 yards, even as his 0.596 EPA per play showed he remained efficient when used.

The 2025 stretch tells the real story: just three games, a 38.7 percent snap rate, four targets and 31 routes before things stalled. Now 29 and buried on Houston's depth chart, he profiles as a special-teams contributor and emergency deep option rather than a fantasy asset. Projections in the low single digits and near-zero roster rates are appropriate. Leave him on waivers until a clear path to volume materializes.

5844.name: T.J. Hockenson
5844.headline: Volume returns but ceiling capped at low-end TE1
5844.body:
Hockenson rebuilt his floor in 2025, playing 15 games on a 77.9 percent snap share with 422 routes, 66 targets, and a 14.1 percent target share that produced 51-438-3 and 61.8 points, good for TE26 by our rank. The catch rate climbed to 77.3 percent, but the air yards collapsed: just 4.98 per target and an 8.59 yards-per-reception that is a shadow of his 2023 peak (95-960-5, 124 points, 1.9 yards per route, TE4). His 1.04 yards per route in 2025 was among the weakest of his career.

The profile is now a high-volume, low-aDOT checkdown option rather than a difference-maker. Eleven red-zone targets keep modest touchdown upside alive, and 422 routes guarantee a target floor in an offense feeding him underneath. Fully recovered from the 2024 ACL recovery that limited him to 10 games, he is a reliable but unspectacular streaming TE1 in 12-team leagues, best valued as a back-end starter you can plug in weekly without expecting top-five weeks.

5846.name: DK Metcalf
5846.headline: Steelers volume cap keeps Metcalf a boom-bust flex
5846.body:
Metcalf's first Pittsburgh year settled him into mid-pack WR2 output: 59 catches, 850 yards and six scores on 99 targets over 15 games, good for 128.2 points and a WR23 finish. The efficiency held up where it matters, with a 0.333 EPA per play and a 50.5% success rate, plus three grabs of 40-plus yards that show the field-stretching gear remains. The drag is volume. A 19% target share and 0.219 targets per route run mirror the soft usage he's posted for three straight seasons, and his catch rate dipped to 59.6% on deep, contested looks.

Aaron Rodgers' quick-trigger style and the arrival of Michael Pittman threaten to thin those targets further rather than expand them. His 7.39 air yards before the catch confirm he's still the downfield specialist, which caps floor in a crowded room. Drafting around Round 7 with a WR31 consensus, he's a swing-for-upside flex, not an anchor.

5849.name: Kyler Murray
5849.headline: Vikings upgrade hands Murray his best supporting cast yet
5849.body:
The 2025 tape is a small, noisy sample: just five games before a foot sprain ended his year, but the efficiency held up at 0.048 EPA per play, a 43.7% success rate, 68.3% completions and a 6-to-3 TD-to-INT split. Lean on the fuller 2024 line instead, where he finished QB11 with 297.2 fantasy points, 21 passing scores, a 49.9% success rate and a positive CPOE of 2.1. The constant across every season is his legs, and he added 173 rushing yards and a score in five tries last fall.

Minnesota is a genuine upgrade. Justin Jefferson, Jordan Addison, Jauan Jennings and T.J. Hockenson give him the deepest weapon room of his career under a coordinator who develops quarterbacks. He profiles as a late-round QB1 or strong streamer, going around pick 110 as the QB17, and that rushing floor plus the projected 23 passing touchdowns make him a clear value with QB1 upside.

5850.name: Josh Jacobs
5850.headline: Elite goal-line volume props up a fading efficiency profile
5850.body:
Jacobs remains a touchdown machine even as the underlying numbers slip. He punched in 13 rushing scores across 15 games in 2025 on the back of 58 red-zone opportunities and 20 goal-line carries, but the efficiency cratered: 3.97 yards per carry, a -0.011 EPA per play, and just 1.82 yards after contact per attempt, all sharp declines from his 4.42-clip, 1,329-yard 2024. His broken-tackle rate collapsed too, from one every 9.1 carries to one every 19.5.

The fantasy case is volume, not explosiveness. He still owns Green Bay's backfield and red-zone work, and 36 catches give a modest PPR floor, but only 4.27% of his runs went for breakaways. He turns 28 with a knee and calf history, and off-field uncertainty clouds his availability. Bank on the touchdowns and treat him as a high-floor RB2 rather than the RB5 he was two years ago.

5854.name: Drew Lock
5854.headline: Seattle's QB2 stays a contingency bet behind Darnold
5854.body:
Lock's recent profile reinforces why he's a depth piece, not a fantasy asset. His 2024 sample in New York was the high-water mark: seven games, 1,071 yards, six touchdowns against five picks, but a 41.7% success rate, minus-0.145 EPA per play, and a minus-6.1 CPOE all flagged below-average efficiency. The 2025 cameo in Seattle was three attempts and a 0.1-point line, pure mop-up duty. Across three years his EPA per play has never cleared zero, and the turnover-prone tendencies (four fumbles in 2024 alone) cap any ceiling even in extended action.

The path forward is purely conditional. He's locked in behind Sam Darnold and now fending off Jalen Milroe for the No. 2 job, with an ADP near 389 and essentially no rosters. He matters only in Superflex builds, and only if Darnold misses time. Until then, treat Lock as a waiver-stash insurance policy rather than a draftable name.

5857.name: Noah Fant
5857.headline: Saints move tight end faces uphill volume battle
5857.body:
Fant lands in New Orleans after a diminished 2025 in Cincinnati, where his role shrank to a 37.9 percent snap share and 215 routes, turning 41 targets into 34 catches for just 288 yards and 3 touchdowns, 40.8 fantasy points and a TE41 finish. That short-area usage, a 3.34 air yards per target and 8.47 yards per catch, plus three fumbles and a negative 0.287 EPA per play, marked his least productive receiving season since becoming a regular. It was a steep drop from his 2024 Seattle line of 500 yards on 64 targets.

The move to New Orleans offers a chance at more route volume, but he still profiles as a complementary, TD-dependent streamer rather than a featured option, and the Saints` shaky quarterback situation caps the ceiling. His best season here looks like the high-40s fantasy points his recent profile suggests. For 2026 he is a low-end TE2 and matchup-based streaming candidate only, not a set-and-forget weekly starter. Leave him on the wire in 10- and 12-team leagues.

5859.name: A.J. Brown
5859.headline: Bona fide alpha lands a fresh featured role in New England
5859.body:
Even in a frustrating 2025, Brown still bent defenses toward him: a 26.1% target share, 0.614 WOPR and 31.8% of Philadelphia's air yards, with a positive 0.278 EPA per play across 121 looks. The dip to 1,003 yards and 142.3 fantasy points (WR18) was a volume and efficiency correction off his 0.637 EPA monster 2024, not a sign of decline at 28. His hands stayed elite, just one drop on 79 catchable balls, and he buried 14 catches of 20-plus yards.

Now he leaves a crowded Eagles pecking order to become the undisputed No. 1 for an ascending Drake Maye in a pass-leaning attack. That setup points squarely at a target share north of his recent marks and the bounce-back projection of roughly 90 grabs, 1,273 yards and eight scores. The schedule grades tough, but volume this concentrated is exactly how WR1 ceilings get rebuilt. Draft him as a back-end WR1.

5870.name: Daniel Jones
5870.headline: Efficient before the Achilles tear, but mobility is the question
5870.body:
Indianapolis bet on Jones and the early returns validated it: across 13 games in 2025 he posted a 0.169 EPA per play, a 51% success rate and a plus-2.3 CPOE, completing 68% for 3,101 yards with 19 touchdowns against just eight picks. That is a massive leap from his 2024 NYG line, where he graded out negative (-0.056 EPA, 44.9% success) over 10 games. He chipped in five rushing scores on 45 carries, and his 226.4 fantasy points landed him at QB18 despite the shortened year.

The catch is health. A December Achilles tear, on top of an earlier fibula fracture, clouds the legs that powered his ceiling, and OTA reports limited him to 7-on-7 work. The pass-catching room thinned with Pittman gone, leaving Pierce as the main holdover. Treat him as a back-end QB2 and a draft-day discount until Week 1 availability is confirmed.

5892.name: David Montgomery
5892.headline: Goal-line hammer lands in Houston as a value RB2
5892.body:
The film backs the volume case: across three Detroit seasons Montgomery converted 33 rushing touchdowns on heavy red-zone usage, including 36 red-zone opportunities and 16 goal-line carries in 2025 even while ceding the early-down lead role. He still ran efficiently at 4.53 yards per carry on 158 attempts with a 9.49% explosive-run rate, and his 2.11 yards after contact per attempt confirm he finishes runs through traffic. The slim 37.1% snap share and shrinking 5.3% target rate were a product of a committee, not decline.

A trade to Houston resets that ceiling. With only Woody Marks behind him and a rebuilt offensive line fronting a run-leaning attack, Montgomery profiles as the primary back and near-exclusive goal-line option. That touchdown equity, paired with usable receiving chops (24 catches in 2025), makes him a sturdy RB2 with weekly RB1 spike weeks. At an ADP outside the top 50, the value is obvious.

5906.name: Dawson Knox
5906.headline: Knox is touchdown-dependent insurance in a crowded Buffalo room
5906.body:
Knox quietly turned in his most efficient season in 2025, finishing as TE28 with 36 grabs on 49 looks for 417 yards and four scores across 16 games. The underlying work backs it up: a 0.335 EPA per play, a 63.3% success rate, and 1.45 yards per route run, all firmly up from his 2024 line. He cleared 12 red-zone targets and seven end-zone looks, which is where his fantasy juice still lives, and his 73.5% catch rate with only two drops showed reliable hands.

The ceiling stays capped because the numbers come on a thin diet. A 10.2% target share and 53.9% route rate confirm a true time-share with Dalton Kincaid, leaving Knox as a touchdown-or-bust streamer. Drafted near the back of the position, he is best treated as a Kincaid handcuff who only earns standalone weeks if injury opens the room. Hold him for the scores, not the volume.

5917.name: Mecole Hardman
5917.headline: Buried in Buffalo with no path to snaps
5917.body:
The trend line is brutal. After landing in Buffalo, Hardman logged just two games in 2025 on a 3.5% snap share, ran two routes, drew one target and caught nothing, posting a ghastly minus-0.508 EPA per play. That followed a 2024 in Kansas City where he salvaged 12 grabs for 90 yards across 12 games (1.32 yards per route run, 0.308 EPA) but a 2.4% target share and zero touchdowns. His last semblance of a role came in 2023: 24 targets, 15 catches, 124 yards over seven games.

He re-upped with the Bills off the practice squad, which tells you all you need to know about his standing. There is no projectable target volume here and no clear lane on a crowded depth chart. Ranked outside the top 500 and rostered in essentially no leagues, Hardman is a deep dynasty afterthought until he forces meaningful snaps that have not arrived.

5927.name: Terry McLaurin
5927.headline: Injury-marred 2025 dims a still-dangerous deep threat
5927.body:
McLaurin endured a frustrating, abbreviated 2025: just 10 games and 76.2 fantasy points, a steep fall from his 2024 breakout when he ranked WR12 with 1,096 yards, 13 touchdowns and 185.8 points across 17 games. The volume cratered with availability, his target share slipping to 13.9 percent from 23.2, and routes dropping from 478 to 228. Yet the per-rep efficiency stayed elite: a 0.529 EPA per play, 2.55 yards per route run and a 61.7 success rate actually topped his stellar 2024 marks.

The 2019 third-rounder (Pick 76, Ohio State) remains Washington number one and a true deep threat, with two 50-plus catches and a 56-yard long despite the injury. At 29 with Jayden Daniels feeding him, a healthy 2026 should restore double-digit touchdown upside and a 22-plus percent target share. The boom/bust label persists, but his floor and ceiling justify a confident WR2 draft slot and weekly starter status as long as he is on the field.

5938.name: Lil'Jordan Humphrey
5938.headline: Deep-roster Bronco with a usage ceiling he can't crack
5938.body:
The profile hasn't budged. Across seven games in 2025, Humphrey logged just 25 targets and a 5% target share, turning them into 13 catches for 156 yards and a score on a thin 0.95 yards per route run. His 0.317 EPA per play and 48% success rate read fine in isolation, but at a 26% route rate they're a sample, not a role. Even his 2024 high-water mark topped out at an 8.2% target share, so the ranking sitting at 130 among wideouts reflects the opportunity, not bad luck.

Denver brought him back for a familiar depth job, and the room ahead of him is crowded enough that he projects as a special-teams body more than a target earner. He's off the draft board in every format, with an ADP near pick 570 and essentially zero rostership. File him as an injury-contingency name only.

5947.name: Jakobi Meyers
5947.headline: Reliable target hog whose floor outshines his modest ceiling
5947.body:
Meyers remains one of the steadiest possession receivers in the league, and the volume backs it up. In 2025 he ran 82.4% of routes, earned a 22.3% target share, and posted a 0.554 WOPR while drawing 15 red-zone looks across 16 games. His efficiency held too: a 59.1% success rate, 1.69 yards per route run, and just two drops on 110 targets. The only blemish is finishing power, with three touchdowns dragging his line down to WR26 despite 75 grabs for 835 yards.

Now Trevor Lawrence's trusted target in Jacksonville, Meyers slid into WR1 work after arriving midseason and immediately led the team in target share. With a fresh contract locked in, he profiles as a high-floor WR3 around pick 100, more valuable in full PPR. Brian Thomas Jr. owns the bigger ceiling and the receiver room is hard to forecast, but Meyers' dependable usage and route polish make him a stable weekly play.

5967.name: Tony Pollard
5967.headline: Reliable bell-cow volume, dependable mid-range RB2
5967.body:
Pollard delivered a quietly strong 2025, playing all 17 games on a 62.5 percent snap share with 242 carries for 1,082 yards (4.47 YPC) and five scores, plus 33 catches for 206 yards, totaling 152.8 fantasy points and an RB19 finish.
