The efficiency ticked up from 2024 (4.15 YPC, RB20) and his explosive-run rate held at 11.16 percent with a 65-yard touchdown the highlight. He broke 16 tackles (one every 15.1 carries) and added 1.83 yards after contact, durable workhorse traits that anchor his weekly floor in Tennessee.

The caveats are real: a -0.118 EPA per play, four fumbles, and a receiving role that has shrunk to a 7.7 percent target share with zero receiving touchdowns and a meager 0.59 yards per route. With Tyjae Spears lurking and Cam Ward still developing, Pollard is a volume-dependent RB2 whose ceiling is capped without goal-line dominance or pass-game work. Draft him as a stable flex-to-RB2 starter, not a league-winner.

5970.name: Greg Dortch
5970.headline: Slot depth piece in Detroit, off-the-radar waiver flyer
5970.body:
Dortch has built a steady but capped profile in his ancillary role. In 2025 he played just 33.6% of snaps for Arizona, ran 209 routes, and turned 33 targets into 29 catches for 206 yards and 3 scores on a tidy 87.9% catch rate. His underneath usage is the story: 1.76 air yards per target, 6.69 yards after the catch per reception, and only 0.99 yards per route run. The advanced marks stay positive (0.129 EPA per play, 39.4% success rate), but a 5.3% target share and 0.089 WOPR cap the ceiling. His fuller 2024 line, 37-342-3 across 17 games, set his career bar.

The move to Detroit reunites him with coordinator Drew Petzing, yet he projects as the fourth receiver behind Amon-Ra St. Brown and Jameson Williams. With an ADP near 313 overall and roughly 10 projected points, he is a season-long waiver name, not a draft target. Only an injury ahead of him changes that.

5973.name: Josh Oliver
5973.headline: Blocking-first TE whose only fantasy juice is red-zone scores
5973.body:
Oliver remains a blocking specialist first, and the route data confirms it: just 151 routes across 12 games in 2025, a 27.8% route share, and a 4.1% target share that ranks him as a deep afterthought in Minnesota's passing hierarchy. His 0.073 WOPR and 1.06 yards per route are replacement-level for the position, and 19 targets simply can't sustain weekly relevance no matter how efficient he is. That said, the underlying efficiency is genuinely strong, a 0.604 EPA per play and 63.2% success rate, plus a clean 78.9% catch rate on 15 grabs.

The entire fantasy case is touchdown-dependent. He turned six red-zone looks and two end-zone targets into a career-high four scores, which is how a player with 160 yards still mattered occasionally. Strip away the variance-prone TDs and there's nothing to draft here; treat him as a touchdown-or-bust streamer only when the matchup and goal-line script align.

5985.name: Foster Moreau
5985.headline: Backup tight end in Houston with little fantasy juice
5985.body:
The 2024 tape still tells the story of what Moreau can be in volume: 16 games, 69.1% snaps, 347 routes and a 43-target, 413-yard, five-touchdown line that paired a sturdy 1.19 yards per route with a sharp 0.463 EPA per play and 62.8% success rate. Nine red-zone looks and five end-zone targets made him a genuine scoring threat when the route share was real.

That role evaporated in 2025. Across seven games his snaps cratered to 35.7%, he ran just 94 routes, drew 10 targets and salvaged a 7-59-0 stat line with negative efficiency (minus-0.374 EPA per play). Now in Houston, he profiles as pure depth behind Dalton Schultz, landing around TE117 in consensus and rostered in essentially no leagues. Without an injury ahead of him, there's nothing here to chase.

5995.name: Justice Hill
5995.headline: Pass-down change-up with a Henry-sized upside ceiling
5995.body:
Hill's profile reads as a satellite back, not a workhorse. Across 10 games in 2025 he logged just 18 carries against 27 targets, hauling in 21 catches for 169 yards and a 6.6% target share, while his snap rate slid to 37.1%. The efficiency stayed real — 5.17 yards per carry, 3.89 yards after contact per attempt, and a 71-yard burst that flashed the breakaway speed Baltimore values — but a minus-0.019 EPA per play and only 44.2 fantasy points underline how thin the volume runs.

The 2024 tape is the tell: 51 targets, 42 grabs, three receiving scores and a plus-0.151 EPA per play when he actually got fed. With the primary backup role to Derrick Henry now his alone, Hill stays a deep-league passing-down piece on standalone value. The genuine appeal is contingency — if Henry misses time, this skill set converts into instant RB2 production overnight.

6001.name: Drew Sample
6001.headline: Sample's blocking floor isn't a fantasy ceiling
6001.body:
Our numbers paint Sample as exactly what he is: a complementary piece whose receiving usage barely registers. He ran 286 routes across 12 games in 2025 yet drew only 20 targets, a 3.3% share that translated to 0.37 yards per route run and a 68th-ranked finish at the position. Fifteen grabs, 106 yards and a single score netted just 16.9 fantasy points. The efficiency markers stay quietly respectable, a 0.133 EPA per play and 40% success rate with zero drops, but volume this thin caps any standalone value.

The trend isn't encouraging either. His 2024 line carried sharper marks, 0.217 EPA and a 59.1% success rate, and his 28.3-point 2023 still towers over recent output. With Cincinnati's targets funneling elsewhere, Sample profiles as a blocking-first tight end and deep-bench afterthought, draftable only if injuries hand him a genuine route-tree role.

6011.name: Gardner Minshew
6011.headline: A backup arm chasing one more cameo as a starter
6011.body:
The trend line is unkind. Minshew's last meaningful stretch came in 2023, when he started all 16 for Indianapolis and finished QB17 on 3,305 yards and a 15-to-9 touchdown-to-pick split. Since then it has cratered: a 2024 split in Las Vegas produced a 9-to-10 ratio, a 43.8% success rate and minus-0.154 EPA per play across 10 games, then 2025 brought just 13 attempts of replacement-level noise, a minus-0.368 EPA mark and a brutal minus-20.5 CPOE in four appearances.

Now he lands in Arizona as insurance behind Jacoby Brissett, with a rookie also angling for the No. 2 job. Projections peg him near 9.7 points per game and a QB46-ish draft slot, which is to say undraftable in standard formats. Stash him only in deep superflex builds, and only if Brissett falters and the snaps actually open up.

6039.name: Ty Johnson
6039.headline: Efficient Bills passing-down back with little volume to mine
6039.body:
Johnson's profile is built on efficiency, not volume. Across all 17 games in 2025 he posted a positive 0.171 EPA per play on just a 29.6% snap share, turning 50 carries into 200 yards (4.0 per pop) and three scores while chipping in 24 catches on 33 targets for 263 yards and two more touchdowns. A 6.9% target share and 1.16 yards per route run confirm he's a complementary piece, and the 76.3 fantasy points reflect a five-touchdown ceiling that requires red-zone luck to repeat.

The underlying quality is real: his 0.365 EPA per play and 5.2 yards per carry in 2024 rank among the league's best for his usage tier. But that efficiency rarely converts to startable weeks behind Buffalo's depth chart. As a passing-downs specialist projected for roughly 71 points and an ADP outside the top 250, he's a deep-bench handcuff and bye-week dart, not a draft-day priority.

6111.name: Jake Browning
6111.headline: Buccaneers insurance behind Mayfield with little standalone value
6111.body:
Browning's 2025 cameo in Cincinnati was a warning sign: across five outings he posted a brutal minus-0.322 EPA per play, a 40.6% success rate, and six touchdowns against eight interceptions while completing just 64.8% of his throws for 771 yards. That collapse looked nothing like his 2023 fill-in stretch, when he managed a positive 0.108 EPA, a 47.3% success rate, and a 12-to-7 touchdown-to-pick mark over nine games. The drop in efficiency, paired with 26.1% pressure and a tendency toward poor throws (15% bad-throw rate), explains why his stock cratered.

Now in Tampa Bay, he sits firmly behind Baker Mayfield as a clipboard-holder buried near the back of QB draft boards. There is genuine upside in the role he'd inherit if Mayfield missed time, given Browning's history of 20-point spike weeks as a starter, but until that happens he's a monitor-only stash with zero weekly relevance.

6130.name: Devin Singletary
6130.headline: Veteran insurance back stuck behind Skattebo with no path
6130.body:
Singletary's 2025 spine tells the whole story: 119 carries for 437 yards at a thin 3.67 per pop, a snap share that shrank to 27.6 percent, and a flat minus-0.004 EPA per play that ranked him 47th. The efficiency markers are quietly cratering too, with explosive runs down to 5 percent and just four broken tackles across 119 attempts, his lowest tackle-evaded rate in years. Even his five rushing scores lean on 10 goal-line carries rather than real burst. At 27, he is no longer creating yards on his own.

The role outlook only hardens that read. He sits behind Cam Skattebo on New York's depth chart, and the front office is shopping for additional backfield help, squeezing his touches further. Projections near 36 carries and 23 fantasy points confirm a clear handcuff ceiling. Even spot starts last season produced only single-digit-to-low-double-digit weekly returns. Leave him on the wire until an injury reshuffles the room.

6136.name: Jarrett Stidham
6136.headline: A capable insurance policy waiting behind Bo Nix
6136.body:
The last meaningful sample tells the story of a steady backup, not a breakout. Across his most recent two-game look, Stidham completed 60.6% of his 66 throws for 496 yards, two scores and one pick, posting essentially neutral production at -0.001 EPA per play and a 41.5% success rate. His -3.7 CPOE and a 19.4% bad-throw rate confirm he hovers below the league line as a passer, with a 23.7% pressure rate and 7 sacks showing the offense around him wobbled in those reps.

The outlook is purely contingency. He sits behind Bo Nix in Denver and only surfaces if Nix misses time, exactly the path that handed him an AFC title-game start. Carrying no real draft capital and a QB ranking outside the top 55, he is droppable in standard formats and a dart-throw stash only in deep Superflex and two-quarterback builds banking on an injury upstream.

6149.name: Darius Slayton
6149.headline: A deep-shot specialist buried in a crowded Giants room
6149.body:
Slayton remains exactly what the advanced numbers say he is: an efficient field-stretcher who never commands enough volume to matter weekly. In 2025 he ran 395 routes across 14 games on an 81.3% snap share, yet earned just a 12.5% target share and a 0.159 target-per-route rate, finishing WR55 with only 61.8 fantasy points. The vertical role still produces juice, 13.4 air yards per target, 22.8 air yards per reception, and a healthy 0.252 EPA per play, but a 58.7% catch rate, five drops, and a lone touchdown cap the ceiling.

The 2026 setup only tightens the squeeze. With Malik Nabers entrenched and Darnell Mooney plus Isaiah Likely added, Slayton drops to a clear fourth or fifth option, and he's working back from offseason sports hernia surgery. A modest projection near 35-520-2 fits a player drafted around WR100. Treat him as a bye-week dart and waiver-wire stash, nothing more.

6271.name: Olamide Zaccheaus
6271.headline: Falcons reunion can't outrun a sliding profile
6271.body:
Zaccheaus took a clear step back in 2025, finishing WR89 on 65 targets and a 12.2% share across 14 games in Chicago. His efficiency cratered: a minus-0.044 EPA per play, a 44.6% success rate, and just 1.0 yards per route run after posting 1.9 the year prior. The 8.0-yard average per catch and 313 receiving yards tell the story of a low-leverage role, even with 12 red-zone looks producing only two scores. The juice he flashed in 2024 (0.392 EPA, 56.2% success) simply didn't carry over.

Now back in Atlanta, he profiles as depth behind Drake London with rookie competition pushing for the same slot snaps. Mooney's exit opens a sliver of volume, but a 0.207 target-per-route rate and shrinking air-yards share cap the ceiling. He's a late-round dart and waiver watch, nothing more, until the route share actually expands.

6290.name: Scotty Miller
6290.headline: Deep-threat dart who needs a real role to matter
6290.body:
Miller arrives in Chicago as a vertical specialist whose recent profile screams fifth option. He logged just five games in 2025 with a 17.3% route share, a 2.7% target share, and a 0.14 targets-per-route-run mark, finishing WR169 on 6.2 fantasy points. The underlying efficiency cratered too: a minus-0.079 EPA per play and 42.9% success rate, with zero red-zone scores on 14 targets. There is no ambiguity here about where he sits on the depth chart.

The ceiling case rests on the field-stretching flashes. Back in 2023 he turned 16 targets into 161 yards and two touchdowns at 1.35 yards per route run and a 0.537 EPA per play, with a 56-yard long. Those splash plays are real, but his career WOPR has never cleared 0.08. Until snaps climb, Miller is a best-ball dart throw and nothing more in 2026.

6427.name: Ashton Dulin
6427.headline: Deep-shot special-teamer with no real receiving role
6427.body:
Dulin's 2025 line tells the whole story: 12 games, a 21.3% snap share, and just 73 routes run for a 12.7% route rate. He drew only 10 targets (1.9% target share), caught 5 for 106 yards and no scores, and his 0.054 WOPR confirms he sits at the very bottom of Indianapolis' pecking order. The one bright spot is efficiency on his limited usage — a 15.8 air-yards-per-target mark and 21.2 yards per reception, with three grabs of 20-plus and a 48-yard long, plus a tidy 0.576 EPA per play. He's a vertical-only chess piece, not a volume threat.

That profile leaves nothing for fantasy. He spent time on IR with a hamstring issue and projects as a back-end depth receiver and special-teamer rather than someone with a path to targets. Modest projections of roughly 22 catches and 379 yards reflect garbage-time upside at best. Leave him on the wire.

6451.name: Stephen Carlson
6451.headline: Carlson is roster depth, not a fantasy bet
6451.body:
At 29 and listed at 6-4, 248, the Princeton product sits buried on Chicago's tight end depth chart, stuck behind Colston Loveland and Cole Kmet. Expert consensus pegs him near TE116 with no meaningful ADP, and he's rostered in essentially zero leagues. His path to snaps runs entirely through injuries ahead of him; absent that, the blocking-tilted role caps any receiving upside.

The schedule offers no rescue either, with Chicago's tight ends carrying one of the league's tougher slates. Until the depth chart thins out, Carlson is a deep-bench stash at best, the kind of name you monitor only if Kmet or Loveland misses time. Leave him on waivers in standard formats and revisit only when an opportunity actually opens. There's nothing here worth a draft pick in 2026.

6768.name: Tua Tagovailoa
6768.headline: Tua's Falcons Reset Is A Stay-Away Until He Wins The Job
6768.body:
The arrow is pointing the wrong way. After ranking as the QB1 in 2023 (4,624 yards, 29 scores, 270.4 fantasy points), Tagovailoa cratered to QB22 last year, throwing for just 2,660 yards with a 20-to-15 touchdown-to-interception split across 14 games. His efficiency collapsed too: EPA per play fell from 0.195 in 2024 to a near-flat 0.004, and his success rate slid to 43.8%. That is not a quarterback you draft on reputation.

Now in Atlanta on a one-year deal, he steps into an open competition with Michael Penix Jr. and is no lock to start, even with Penix rehabbing a knee. The schedule is soft and Drake London plus Kyle Pitts give him real weapons, but the QB31-range price tag tells the story. Leave him on waivers in single-quarterback formats and treat him as a late dart only in superflex.

6770.name: Joe Burrow
6770.headline: Burrow's Elite Efficiency Is Bottled, Just Add Health
6770.body:
The 2024 tape is the real Burrow: a wire-to-wire QB1 across all 17 games, piling up 372.8 fantasy points on 43 touchdowns against nine picks while posting a 0.159 EPA per play, a 51.1% success rate and a 6.8 CPOE that screamed best-quarterback-alive. A toe injury capped 2025 at eight games, yet even compromised he completed 66.8% of his throws with 17 scores to five interceptions, a 0.114 EPA per play and a still-excellent 4.7 CPOE. The accuracy never wavered.

That efficiency baseline is why the cratered counting stats lie. A full slate alongside Ja'Marr Chase and Tee Higgins in a fast-tempo, pass-leaning attack restores a top-three QB ceiling, and he profiles as a top-five pick at the position. The toe limited his scrambling to 14 carries; a return to mobility only widens the gap. Healthy, he is a value the moment passers come off the board.

6783.name: Jerry Jeudy
6783.headline: Jeudy's Efficiency Cratered, and Cleveland Reloaded
6783.body:
The volume held but everything around it collapsed. Jeudy still ran 91.6% of routes and commanded a 20.3% target share across 17 games, yet his per-play EPA fell off a cliff to -0.305 with a 35.8% success rate, both among the worst marks at the position. He turned 106 targets into just 50 catches for 602 yards and 2 scores, a 47.2% catch rate dragged down by 10 drops and a YPRR that sank from 1.81 to 1.08. The WR6 finish of 2024 felt like a different player entirely.

The role isn't safe, either. Cleveland spent early draft capital on two more wideouts, crowding a depth chart that no longer treats Jeudy as the centerpiece. A tweaked hamstring already has him on a cautious camp ramp. With a late-round price tag, he's a deep-league dart throw who needs early production to justify a roster spot.

6786.name: CeeDee Lamb
6786.headline: Lamb stays a WR1 even with shrunken volume
6786.body:
Lamb's 2025 was a clear step down from his peak: limited to 13 games, he posted 117 targets, 75 catches, 1,077 yards and just 3 touchdowns, finishing as our WR10 with 125.9 fantasy points. That's a steep fall from the WR2 monster of 2023 (181 targets, 1,749 yards, 12 scores, 268.2 points) and even 2024's WR8 line. His target share dipped to 19.3%, yet a 0.248 EPA per play and 54.7% success rate show the efficiency held when he was on the field.

The context behind the dip matters more than the raw decline. George Pickens' arrival siphoned looks, and a stingier Dallas defense trimmed the offense's overall snap count, capping the ceiling Lamb once owned as the lone alpha. Still, with Prescott driving the passing game and a top-10 draft cost, the talent and floor remain elite. Treat him as a rock-solid WR1 whose blow-up upside is now shared, not gone.

6790.name: D'Andre Swift
6790.headline: Swift's efficiency turnaround keeps him a sturdy RB2
6790.body:
Swift answered a rough 2024 with the best efficiency season of his career, pushing his yards per carry to 4.87 across 223 totes for 1,087 yards and nine scores, plus 34 grabs on a 9% target share. The jump shows up in the underlying numbers: his EPA per play flipped from minus-0.122 to a positive 0.051, his explosive-run rate nearly doubled to 13.45%, and he finished as our RB13 with 194.6 fantasy points. He also commanded the goal-line work, logging 14 inside carries and 41 red-zone opportunities.

The one drag is the backfield split. Swift owns the passing downs and two-minute snaps, but Chicago leaned harder on Kyle Monangai down the stretch, and the committee threatens to settle into a 1A/1B arrangement. Still, drafting near RB22 inside Ben Johnson's projected top-10 offense buys a high-floor back with weekly upside whenever the volume tilts his way.

6794.name: Justin Jefferson
6794.headline: Elite usage intact, just waiting on a real quarterback
6794.body:
The volume that built Jefferson's reputation never left. In 2025 he ran 96.3% of routes, commanded a 30.1% target share and a 0.72 WOPR on 141 looks, with 18 red-zone and 10 end-zone targets. The collapse was purely downstream: his EPA per play cratered to -0.091 with a 49.6% success rate, and just two touchdowns despite that target diet. Yards per reception sank to 12.48 and catch rate to 59.6%, the fingerprints of broken quarterback play rather than a fading receiver.

Compare 2024, when the same workload produced 1,533 yards, 10 scores, a 0.417 EPA mark and 214.5 fantasy points. The role is identical; only the trigger man changed. With Kyler Murray or J.J. McCarthy stepping in, the touchdown and efficiency regression points strongly back up. Drafting him around WR6 and inside the top ten is a bet on quarterback competence, and the alpha usage makes that bet an easy one.

6797.name: Justin Herbert
6797.headline: McDaniel offense and healthy line could unlock a QB1 ceiling
6797.body:
Herbert finished QB9 in 2025 with 286.9 fantasy points, yet the underlying profile screams untapped upside: a modest 0.03 EPA per play and 46.8% success rate, dragged down by 54 sacks and a 29.8% pressure rate behind a battered line. His arm wasn't the problem — a 76.4% on-target mark and 6.1 yards of completed air per completion held steady even as the offense leaned conservative, and he still chipped in 498 rushing yards across 83 carries.

The setup for 2026 is the real story. Mike McDaniel takes over the play-calling, tackles Rashawn Slater and Joe Alt project to be healthy, and Ladd McConkey headlines a deeper pass-catching group. When Alt suited up last year, Herbert averaged 25.1 points per game — a rate that, sustained, makes him a weekly difference-maker. With an ADP around QB9 and projections near 302 fantasy points, he's a value-priced bet on positive regression.

6801.name: Tee Higgins
6801.headline: Elite efficiency, touchdown machine, locked-in WR1 ceiling
6801.body:
Higgins keeps producing like a top-tier wideout on a complementary target diet. In 2025 he posted 0.356 EPA per play and a 54.1% success rate across just 15 games, turning 98 targets and a 16.1% target share into 846 yards and 11 touchdowns. The volume sits behind Ja'Marr Chase, but the efficiency is loud: 1.65 yards per route, 27.9% air-yards share, and 15 red-zone looks that fuel his scoring. He drops almost nothing, with two on the year and a 2% drop rate.

The 2024 line is the dream upside, when 0.526 EPA per play and a 63.3% success rate carried 73 catches in 12 games. As a high-end WR2 with WR1 weeks when Burrow is upright, he's a clean Round 3-4 anchor. The one flag is leaning on touchdowns to mask a modest route-run usage rate; any bump in target share unlocks a real bargain.

6804.name: Jordan Love
6804.headline: Elite efficiency, capped volume: a perennial QB2 with QB1 upside
6804.body:
Love's underlying play has never been sharper. In 2025 he posted a 0.238 EPA per dropback with a 48.9% success rate and a 5.5 CPOE, all career bests, while trimming his interceptions to six on 439 attempts and pushing his on-target rate to 77.4%. The problem isn't the throwing; it's the math. That workload finished as QB13 with 235.1 fantasy points, and his three-year scoring rank has bounced from QB7 (2023) to QB19 (2024) almost entirely on volume swings.

Green Bay's run-leaning, ball-control approach keeps games tidy and keeps Love off the QB1 board. He chips in modest rushing (199 yards, zero scores in 2025) but no longer provides the four-touchdown ground spike from his breakout year. Treat him as a high-end QB2 you can grab late: the efficiency and 23-plus touchdown floor are bankable, and any uptick in attempts unlocks legitimate top-12 weeks.

6806.name: J.K. Dobbins
6806.headline: Efficient runner, fragile body, touchdown-dependent ceiling
6806.body:
Dobbins keeps producing yards the moment he touches the ball. Across 10 games in 2025 he ripped off 772 rushing yards on 153 carries at 5.05 per pop, with a 13.7% explosive-run rate and four scores before another foot injury ended his year at RB33. His 2.44 yards after contact per attempt and nine broken tackles show the burst is intact; the problem has never been talent. It has been staying on the field.

The concern is volume and pass-game floor. A 2.4% target share and 51.1% snap rate point to an early-down, committee role behind a passing-down complement, so his fantasy value leans heavily on finding the end zone. He flashed a near-neutral -0.005 EPA per play on a thin rushing context, and the 2024 line (905 yards, nine TDs, RB24) is the realistic upside. Treat him as a touchdown-reliant RB2 or flex with real injury risk baked in.

6813.name: Jonathan Taylor
6813.headline: Workhorse anchor coming off a 316-point RB3 finish
6813.body:
Taylor's 2025 was a bell-cow masterclass: 316.3 fantasy points (RB3) on an 82.6% snap share, 323 carries for 1,585 yards at 4.91 a pop, and 18 rushing scores. His efficiency was driven, not lucky -- 2.35 yards after contact per attempt, 27 broken tackles, and an 11.2% explosive-run rate that produced nine runs of 20-plus and three over 50. The +0.074 EPA per play marked a hard rebound from his -0.098 mark in 2024, when a 14-game season still cleared 1,400 yards.

The receiving leap is the new wrinkle: 55 targets and 46 catches on a 10.4% target share, up from 18 grabs the year prior, which broadens his floor in PPR. The lone caveat is quarterback play -- his ceiling tracks closely with Daniel Jones' availability as Jones returns from an Achilles injury. With a top-six ADP and 74 red-zone touches plus 23 goal-line carries fueling the volume, Taylor is a locked-in early first-rounder.

6819.name: Michael Pittman
6819.headline: Veteran target earner brings stable floor to Pittsburgh
6819.body:
Pittman bounced back in 2025, finishing WR29 with 122.4 fantasy points after a down 2024. On an 85.5 percent snap share he ran 512 routes (89.2 percent route rate) and commanded a 20.9 percent target share, converting 111 targets into 80 catches for 784 yards and a career-best 7 touchdowns. The 0.209 EPA per play and 55.9 percent success rate marked a clear efficiency rebound from 2024s 0.02 EPA, though his depth of target stayed modest at 8.17 air yards.

The red flags are a sliding yardage profile (1,152 yards in 2023 down to 784) and a move to Pittsburgh, where target volume and quarterback play are less certain than his Colts peak. Still, a 2020 second-round USC product with a 16.3-game durability record and proven 20-plus percent target shares offers a reliable floor. The touchdown spike may regress, but as a high-volume possession receiver, Pittman is a PPR-leaning WR3 with weekly flex appeal rather than a true difference-maker.

6826.name: Cole Kmet
6826.headline: Kmet's Volume Vanished, And It Isn't Coming Back
6826.body:
The arrow points down, and the route data tells you why. Kmet's 2025 line of 30 catches for 347 yards and 2 scores ranked him just 33rd among tight ends, a steep slide from the 90 targets, 719 yards and TE9 finish he posted in 2023. His target share cratered to 9 percent on a 62.6 route share, and at 0.93 yards per route run he simply isn't earning enough work to matter weekly.

There are flickers worth noting: 0.257 EPA per play and a 47.9 percent success rate mean he was efficient on the limited looks he saw, and he drew six red-zone targets. But the rookie ahead of him has claimed the lead role, capping the ceiling. Treat Kmet as a touchdown-dependent dart throw and a deep-league streamer only, nothing more until the depth chart shifts.

6828.name: AJ Dillon
6828.headline: Dillon's role has vanished, and so has his value
6828.body:
Dillon's 2025 line tells the whole story: six games, an 11.9% snap share, 12 carries, and a 0.6% target share that left him with just 6.1 fantasy points. The efficiency wasn't there to justify more, either, with a brutal minus-0.29 EPA per play. Even the surface 5.0 yards per carry is noise off a 12-attempt sample, and a fumble on only three rushing chances per broken tackle hardly screams trusted back.

Go back to his last real workload in 2023, when 178 carries produced only 3.44 yards a pop and two scores across 15 games, and the ceiling looks just as capped. Now in Carolina, he projects as a touchdown-dependent committee piece buried on the depth chart, with usage that may not even clear RB2 duties. There's no realistic path to fantasy relevance here unless an injury ahead of him forces volume his way. Leave him on waivers.

6847.name: Devin Duvernay
6847.headline: Return Specialist, Not a Fantasy Asset
6847.body:
Duvernay's offensive footprint has all but vanished. Across 17 games in 2025 he logged just a 3.5% snap rate and 4.2% route share, drawing five targets for a 0.9% target share and turning two catches into 26 yards. His marks sat firmly underwater: a minus-0.062 EPA per play, a 40% success rate, and 1.04 yards per route run, all good for just 2.6 fantasy points and a WR192 finish. That follows a 2024 in Jacksonville where 16 targets yielded 79 yards, confirming the trend rather than bucking it.

Now in Arizona on a modest one-year deal, he profiles as a kick-returner and special-teams piece rather than a rotational receiver. With no real path to offensive volume, he carries no standalone value, slotting near WR249 in dynasty and going undrafted in redraft. Leave him on the wire unless an injury forces a snap-count shift that simply isn't projected.

6865.name: Colby Parkinson
6865.headline: Touchdown-fueled 2025 breakout meets a crowded Rams tight end room
6865.body:
Parkinson posted career bests in 2025: 43 grabs on 56 targets for 408 yards and eight touchdowns across 14 games, a 76.8% catch rate that finished him 29th at the position. The efficiency markers were legitimate too, with 0.224 EPA per play, a 64.3% success rate and 1.31 yards per route run, all leaps over his 2024 line. The scoring spike leaned heavily on volume in tight quarters: 22 red-zone looks and 10 end-zone targets fueled those eight scores, a rate unlikely to fully repeat given his modest 9.6% target share and 4.43 air yards per target.

The bigger drag is the depth chart. Tyler Higbee still commands starter snaps, and rookie-level additions Terrance Ferguson and Max Klare crowd the multi-tight-end packages. With an ADP outside the top 220 and only a single-digit target share, Parkinson profiles as a touchdown-dependent stash and in-season streamer rather than a draft-day target. Monitor the role; if the snaps consolidate, he carries genuine spike-week appeal.

6869.name: Adam Trautman
6869.headline: A blocker first, Trautman stays off fantasy radars
6869.body:
Trautman's profile screams in-line blocker. He ran only 270 routes on a 42.5% route share in 2025, drawing 23 targets for a 3.9% target share and a tiny 0.085 targets per route run. That funneled into 20 catches for 195 yards and one score, good for just 25.5 fantasy points and a TE51 rank. He was efficient when used (87% catch rate, 65.2% success rate, 0.469 EPA per play), but the volume simply isn't there, and his 0.72 yards per route run confirms it.

Nothing in the snapshot suggests a breakout. A fresh three-year extension locks in his blocking role, but Evan Engram owns the receiving work and two rookie tight ends add depth. His target share has actually shrunk from 7.3% in 2023, and red-zone looks dried up to a single trip last year. He's a deep-league afterthought at best.

6904.name: Jalen Hurts
6904.headline: Buy the dip on a rushing-fueled QB1
6904.body:
Hurts finished QB16 in 2025 with 301.1 fantasy points, his quietest line in years, and the advanced profile explains the cooling. His EPA per play slid to 0.074 from 0.153 the prior season, success rate dipped to 44.3%, and CPOE fell to 3.2 from 7.5. Most telling for fantasy: his ground game shrank to 421 rushing yards and eight scores on 105 carries, down from 630 and 14 the year before. He still threw 25 touchdowns against just six picks and hit 40 passes of 20-plus yards, so the arm held up even as the legs went quiet.

The rushing dropoff tracks with offensive-line injuries that derailed the back half of the season; healthy bookends should restore the designed-run volume that anchors his ceiling. Losing A.J. Brown stings, but DeVonta Smith, Dallas Goedert and a reshuffled corps keep enough around him, and the schedule grades out as the league's softest. At a QB6-7 price near pick 72, the rebound math is favorable.

7002.name: Juwan Johnson
7002.headline: Volume's there for Johnson, but the touchdowns won't follow
7002.body:
Johnson finally hit a true featured role in 2025, ranking third among tight ends with 102 targets, an 18.1% target share, and a 0.384 WOPR. He converted that into 77 grabs for 889 yards on a tidy 75.5% catch rate, and his efficiency backs the workload up: 0.345 EPA per play, a 55.9% success rate, and 1.7 yards per route run across 522 routes. The volume is real and earned.

The problem is the scoring. Just three receiving touchdowns and only eight red-zone looks capped him at 102.9 fantasy points despite all that usage, leaving him a TE2. Now New Orleans has imported pass-catching tight ends to crowd the room, and at 28 turning 29 the three-year climb is likelier to flatten than extend. Projections near 67 catches and 750 yards keep him a steady streamer, drafted late as a back-end starter rather than a difference-maker.

7021.name: Rico Dowdle
7021.headline: Proven 1,000-yard grinder lands in Pittsburgh committee
7021.body:
Dowdle has quietly been a back-to-back 1,000-yard rusher: 1,076 yards at 4.56 per carry with six scores in 2025 (177.3 points, RB14) after 1,079 yards (4.59 YPC, RB17) in 2024. The 2025 tape shows a complete back, with a 10.17 percent explosive-run rate, 14 broken tackles, 2.03 yards after contact and a tidy single fumble on 236 carries, plus pass-game utility on 39 catches for 297 yards and a 10.4 percent target share. The -0.008 EPA per play is essentially neutral on a heavy workload.

The undrafted South Carolina product (rookie 2020) now joins Pittsburgh, where the snag is a likely timeshare rather than the bell-cow role that fueled his Carolina numbers. If he wins the early-down and goal-line work, his three-down profile and 39 red-zone opportunities translate to RB2 output; if it splits, he settles as a flex. Draft him as a high-floor RB2/flex with locked-in efficiency but a workload that depends on beating out the depth chart.

7049.name: Jauan Jennings
7049.headline: Proven slot grinder buried in a crowded Vikings room
7049.body:
Jennings is a more capable receiver than his new depth chart suggests. In 2024 he posted a 22% target share, a 0.503 WOPR, and 2.49 yards per route run on 113 looks, then followed it with a 9-touchdown 2025 on a 16.4% share despite a dip to 1.47 yards per route. The red-zone profile travels: 19 red-zone and 8 end-zone targets last year explain those scores, and a 3.3% drop rate shows reliable hands. His efficiency cratered, though, with EPA per play sliding from 0.383 to -0.016 as the volume and air yards thinned.

The move to Minnesota caps the upside hard. Slotted third behind Justin Jefferson and Jordan Addison, plus Hockenson and a new quarterback in Kyler Murray, he projects for roughly 49 catches and 557 yards. That puts him in late-round, touchdown-dependent territory: a WR60-ish flier whose value spikes only if an injury opens snaps ahead of him.

7075.name: Charlie Woerner
7075.headline: Blocking Specialist, Not A Fantasy Asset
7075.body:
Woerner's 2025 line tells the whole story: 8 catches on 9 targets for 48 yards across 7 games, a 1.7% target share that ranked him 84th among tight ends. His 50.8% snap rate looks respectable until you see it came mostly as a blocker, and a 35.9% route participation that produced just 0.24 yards per route run. The -0.022 EPA per play and 33.3% success rate confirm he moves the needle on the ground, not through the air.

Nothing about the profile points to a 2026 breakout. The 88.9% catch rate is empty efficiency on near-zero volume, with 1.33 air yards per target signaling a checkdown and short-area role. Atlanta values him as an in-line run blocker, and that ceiling caps any fantasy upside. Projections in the 14-reception, one-touchdown neighborhood say it plainly: leave him on the wire in every format.

7083.name: Tyler Huntley
7083.headline: Efficient In Relief, But Buried Behind Lamar
7083.body:
Huntley's 2025 sample back in Baltimore was sharp even if tiny: across five games he completed 77.6% of his throws with a 12.9 CPOE, posted a 49.5% success rate and a positive 0.132 EPA per play, and protected the ball with two touchdowns against zero picks for 40.1 points and a QB49 finish. That marked a clean reversal from his messy 2024 Miami stint, where he managed a minus-0.127 EPA, a 39.7% success rate, three interceptions, and five fumbles.

The efficiency is real, yet the opportunity is not. He sits firmly behind Lamar Jackson on a two-year Baltimore deal and projects for minimal snaps in an offense built around its franchise quarterback. With an ADP near pick 329 and roughly zero rostership, he is a non-factor in standard formats. Stash him only in deep two-quarterback leagues as injury insurance, because any standalone value hinges entirely on Jackson missing time.

7090.name: Darnell Mooney
7090.headline: Bounce-back upside buried behind Nabers in New York
7090.body:
The two-year trend is jarring. In 2024 Mooney was a legitimate weapon: 992 yards and five scores on a 19.7% target share, with a 0.414 EPA per play, a 53.8% success rate and 1.92 yards per route run that ranked him 23rd. An injury-marred 2025 erased all of it, as his catch rate cratered to 44.4%, his EPA flatlined at 0.051, his success rate fell to 37.5%, and his yards per route halved to 1.00 (WR64). He still flashed the deep speed that produced a 14.2 air-yards-per-target mark, but the efficiency disappeared.

A one-year Giants deal reunites him with Matt Nagy and plugs him into Jaxson Dart's offense, but the path is narrow behind Malik Nabers, with Darius Slayton and Malachi Fields also in the mix. Consensus pegs him near 535 yards and three touchdowns, a WR3/WR4 line at best. His clearest route to relevance is a Nabers absence; otherwise he's a late-round dart throw.

7496.name: Nick Westbrook-Ikhine
7496.headline: Field-stretching Colts WR with TD-dependent boom-bust profile
7496.body:
Westbrook-Ikhine signed with Indianapolis after a brutal 2025 in Miami, where his role cratered: 13 games, a 44.2 percent snap share, just 20 targets and a 4.3 percent target share for 89 yards and zero touchdowns on a thin 0.42 yards per route. That collapse followed his career year in Tennessee in 2024, when he turned a 69.9 percent snap rate and 60 targets into 497 yards and a remarkable nine touchdowns, posting 103.7 fantasy points and a 71 positional rank.

The gap between those seasons exposes the profile: a deep-ball, low-volume specialist whose value lives almost entirely on touchdowns. His 21.88 air yards per reception in 2024 shows the big-play role, but 1.20 yards per route that year against 0.42 in 2025 shows how usage-dependent he is. In a crowded Colts receiver room he projects as a rotational field-stretcher, not a target earner. He is a waiver-wire name and a touchdown-chasing bye-week dart at best, not a startable weekly option.

7523.name: Trevor Lawrence
7523.headline: Volume QB1 floor, but efficiency still lags
7523.body:
Lawrence delivered his best fantasy season in 2025, ranking QB6 with 338.2 points across a full 17 games, a huge bounce from the injury-shortened 10-game, 145.2-point 2024. The counting stats carried it: 4,007 passing yards, 29 touchdowns against 12 picks, plus a career-best rushing line of 359 yards and nine scores on 82 carries that adds a sturdy floor. That ground production is the engine here, because the passing efficiency remains middling, a 60.9 percent completion rate, a negative 1.9 CPOE, and just a 0.089 EPA per play.

The peripherals temper the optimism: a 14.4 percent bad-throw rate, 78 poor throws, and 41 sacks taken behind shaky protection at a 21.8 percent pressure rate. He is more a product of volume and rushing equity than elite accuracy, but nine rushing scores and 560 attempts give him a dependable ceiling. Draft him as a low-end QB1 with upside if Jacksonville upgrades the line; a solid floor in superflex.

7525.name: DeVonta Smith
7525.headline: Brown's exit hands Smith the alpha role he's earned
7525.body:
Even sharing the field, Smith just posted a WR16 line: 1,008 yards on 77 grabs across all 17 games, a 24.4% target share, and 0.49 EPA per play on a 54% success rate. His 2.11 yards per route run and 0.577 WOPR confirm he was already operating as a true volume hub, not a complementary piece. The only thing capping his ceiling was four touchdowns on 11 red-zone looks, a number begging to climb.

Now the depth chart belongs to him. With A.J. Brown out of the building, Smith inherits the No. 1 chair and the target vacuum that comes with it, having flashed a 32.9% share in past samples without Brown. A rookie addition trims some upside, but a scheme built around his after-the-catch chops plus regression in the scoring column point toward career-best volume. Draft him as a high-floor WR2 with legitimate WR1 paths.

7526.name: Jaylen Waddle
7526.headline: Waddle's Efficiency Finally Lands in a Pass-First Offense
7526.body:
Don't let the modest box score fool you: Waddle's 2025 was quietly elite by efficiency. He posted a 0.345 EPA per play and a 56% success rate while commanding a 21.6% target share and a 0.597 WOPR across 16 games. His 2.33 yards per route run and 13.06 air yards per target show a downfield threat who simply lacked enough looks in Miami's run-leaning attack, turning 100 targets into 64 grabs for 910 yards and six scores.

Now he's the centerpiece of Denver's passing game, and that volume jump is the whole thesis. Bo Nix tilts short and intermediate, which fits Waddle's separation profile even if it caps the air-yard ceiling. His three prior 21-plus target-share campaigns all cleared 18.9%, so the role is proven. Injuries remain the lone caveat, but a true No. 1 workload makes a WR2 finish the floor and a breakout entirely live.

7527.name: Mac Jones
7527.headline: Purdy's backup whose 2025 spot starts proved genuinely playable
7527.body:
Jones spent 2025 as San Francisco's contingency and quietly delivered his cleanest tape in years: 11 games, a 69.6 completion rate, 13 touchdowns against 6 picks, and a positive 0.096 EPA per play with a 50.4 success rate. Both numbers flipped from his 2024 Jacksonville stint, where he posted negative EPA and a 42.4 success rate. A 3.7 CPOE and 79.1 on-target rate say the efficiency was real, not noise, even if the 5.9 air yards per completion confirms a short, controlled passing diet.

The catch is opportunity. He sits behind Brock Purdy again, so standalone value is roughly nil at a #311 overall draft cost. But the upside is concrete: filling in for an injured Purdy in 2025, Jones cleared 19.8 fantasy points in five of eight starts. A current shoulder issue limited him in OTAs, though he should be ready for camp. Stash-and-monitor only.

7529.name: Gary Brightwell
7529.headline: Roster fringe in Cincinnati with no path to touches
7529.body:
Brightwell's profile is built on the thinnest possible sample. Our last meaningful look came in 2023, when he played just four games and logged only nine carries for 19 yards at a dismal 2.11 per attempt, with an EPA of minus-0.115 per play that screams replacement-level. He flashed marginally as a checkdown outlet, hauling in five of seven targets for 47 yards and a 1.96 yards-per-route-run mark, but a 1.4 percent target share and 6.6 fantasy points underscore how invisible he was. That RB105 finish is the ceiling, not the floor.

Now with the Bengals after bouncing across practice squads, he sits at the very bottom of the depth chart with essentially no draftable value. Ranked outside RB160 and rostered in roughly zero percent of leagues, Brightwell is a special-teams body and emergency depth, not a fantasy asset. Leave him on waivers until injuries force a real conversation.

7535.name: Hunter Long
7535.headline: Efficient backup tight end stuck behind Strange in Jacksonville
7535.body:
Long's 2025 advanced profile is a tale of efficiency wasted on volume. Across seven games he posted a strong 0.457 EPA per play and a 58.8% success rate, and his catchable-target rate held up enough to score two touchdowns on just 17 looks. But the underlying usage is microscopic: a 23.1% route share, 3.1% target share, and a 0.056 WOPR all scream backup. A 23.5% drop rate on four total drops further caps trust, and at 39.9% snaps he never controlled the formation.

The path forward stays narrow. Long sits behind Brenton Strange on Jacksonville's depth chart and profiles as the next-man-up only if Strange misses time. Projections of roughly five catches, 44 yards and one score for the full season confirm there's no standalone value here. Hip and knee issues have already clouded his availability. He's a desperation streamer at best until injury opens real snaps.

7537.name: Jaret Patterson
7537.headline: Buried fourth on the depth chart with no clear path
7537.body:
The 2025 tape tells a sobering story. Across six games Patterson logged just 41 carries for 159 yards on a thin 3.88 per attempt, adding a single touchdown and three catches for eight yards. A 28 percent snap share, minus-0.068 EPA per play, and only 2.29 yards after contact per carry confirm a back who neither broke open defenses nor created on his own, with one broken tackle all year. The 22.7 fantasy points he managed reflect deep-bench fringe usage, nothing more.

Los Angeles handed him a contract extension, but the backfield ahead is crowded: Omarion Hampton, Kimani Vidal, and Keaton Mitchell all sit higher on the chart, leaving Patterson a fourth option fighting just to make the final 53. Expert ranks bury him outside the top 120 backs, and projections are essentially nonexistent. Outside of a dynasty stash on an injury bet, there's no draftable case here in standard formats.

7543.name: Travis Etienne
7543.headline: Bounce-back workhorse changes scenery, locked-in RB1
7543.body:
Etienne rebounded emphatically in 2025, playing all 17 games with 260 carries for 1,107 yards (4.26 YPC) and seven scores, plus 36 catches for 292 yards and six receiving touchdowns, totaling 217.9 fantasy points and an RB12 finish. That reversed a dismal 2024 (3.72 YPC, 91.2 points, RB35) and leaned on home-run ability: a 71-yard rush, three carries of 40-plus, a 10 percent explosive-run rate and 2.1 yards after contact, with zero fumbles all season. The 13 total touchdowns and 64 red-zone opportunities show restored goal-line and pass-game equity.

The 2026 twist is a move to New Orleans, where the depth chart lists him as RB1 ahead of Alvin Kamara, a backfield that could split work and cap his ceiling. Still just 27 and fresh off a three-down, every-game season, Etienne carries the draft capital and proven volume to anchor a lineup. Treat it cautiously, but he profiles as a back-end RB1 with weekly-starter upside if he holds the lead role.

7547.name: Amon-Ra St. Brown
7547.headline: Detroit's volume monster offers WR1 floor with red-zone equity
7547.body:
St. Brown is one of the surest target machines in football, and 2025 only widened the moat. He commanded 172 targets on a 31.3% target share with a 90.8% route rate, turning that volume into 117 grabs for 1,401 yards and 11 scores. A 0.73 WOPR and 2.49 yards per route run confirm he is the centerpiece, not a complementary piece, and his 35 red-zone and 21 end-zone looks anchor the touchdown floor that fantasy managers crave.

The efficiency dipped slightly from 2024's elite 0.684 EPA per play to a still-strong 0.323, with success rate sliding to 53.5%, partly explained by 10 drops on a heavier workload. Jared Goff keeps feeding him, and three straight 1,250-plus-yard campaigns make this the rare profile with almost no bust risk. Draft him confidently as a first-round WR1 whose role and route volume guarantee a high weekly floor.

7553.name: Kyle Pitts
7553.headline: Pitts Reclaims TE2 Status As Atlanta's Clear No. 2
7553.body:
The breakout finally arrived. Pitts ranked second among our tight ends in 2025, posting 88 grabs on 118 targets for 928 yards and five scores across all 17 games. His 22.7% target share and 0.217 targets per route run dwarfed the 13.7% and 0.161 marks from a year prior, and the efficiency followed: a 0.216 EPA per play, 55.9% success rate, 1.71 yards per route run, and a 0.487 WOPR that screams featured weapon. He ran a route on 95.8% of dropbacks, so the volume is locked in.

With thin receiver depth behind Drake London, Pitts profiles as the clear second option, and a new tight-end-friendly scheme should funnel even more short, efficient looks his way. The only red flags are touchdown ceiling, where five remains a career best, and lingering trade chatter after the franchise tender. At a Round 6 to 7 cost and a TE8 consensus, the value is plain.

7562.name: Tutu Atwell
7562.headline: Speedster joins Miami chasing a fragile target share
7562.body:
Atwell has never converted his draft pedigree into stable production, and 2025 was a low point: a 27.9 percent snap share, just 15 targets, 6 catches for 192 yards, and a 2.6 percent target share over 8 games, ranking WR120. The lone bright spot was a 32.0 yards-per-reception, boom-or-bust profile built on a 20.13 air yards per target. That regressed hard from his 2024 line of 42 catches, 562 yards, and an 11.5 percent target share across 15 games.

Now in Miami after five years as a Rams role player, the 2021 second-rounder profiles as a vertical complement rather than a volume target, with a 40 percent catch rate in 2025 underscoring the inconsistency. Tyreek Hill and the Dolphins pecking order leave little room for a steady weekly role. He carries deep-league dart appeal on speed alone, but for redraft purposes he is a waiver-wire flier, not a draftable starter.

7564.name: Ja'Marr Chase
7564.headline: Ja'Marr Chase: elite WR1 floor, Burrow-driven ceiling
7564.body:
Chase still graded as our WR4 in 2025, posting 125 catches on a commanding 185 targets for 1,412 yards and eight scores across 16 games, good for 188.6 fantasy points. The dip in efficiency was telling, though: his EPA per play fell from 0.44 to 0.224 and his success rate slid from 61.7% to 53%, almost entirely a function of unstable quarterback play rather than any decline in his game. His 30.4% target share actually rose, confirming he remains the unquestioned engine of this passing attack.

The blueprint sits one year back, when a healthy Joe Burrow fueled a 276-point, 17-touchdown campaign that finished as our overall WR1. Restore that battery and Chase's red-zone volume and yards-after-catch translate into league-winning weeks again. Consensus already treats him as a top-three overall pick and the position's premier option, and that price is justified. Draft him without hesitation as a foundational WR1 whose only real variable is Burrow's availability.

7567.name: Kenneth Gainwell
7567.headline: Pass-catching insurance behind Bucky Irving's shaky health
7567.body:
Gainwell's 2025 in Pittsburgh was the proof of concept: 148.3 fantasy points and an RB23 finish built almost entirely on receiving usage. He logged 85 targets, hauled in 73 of them for 486 yards and three scores, ran 344 routes at a 1.41 yards-per-route clip, and commanded a 16.3% target share, all career bests. As a runner he was efficient too, 537 yards at 4.71 per carry with five touchdowns, 14 broken tackles and a 55-yard long. That dual-threat profile is real, and it travels.

The catch is opportunity. In Tampa he slots behind Bucky Irving on a two-year, $14M deal, with Sean Tucker also in the mix, so standalone value is thin while Irving is healthy. But Irving is recovering from shoulder surgery and carries an injury history, which gives Gainwell a clear contingency path to RB2 numbers. Draft him as a PPR-friendly upside dart in the later rounds, not a reach.

7568.name: Brevin Jordan
7568.headline: Buried Houston tight end returns from injury as deep flier
7568.body:
The flashes are real but the sample is tiny. Across 2023's 10 games Jordan turned 21 targets into 17 catches for 219 yards and 2 scores, posting a sterling 76.2% success rate, 0.833 EPA per play, and 1.45 yards per route run on a 31.6% snap share. Those efficiency marks tease upside, yet his 2024 was a footnote: two games, three targets, seven yards, a 47.5% snap rate that never translated into volume. The production has never matched the athletic profile.

Now 25, Jordan missed all of 2025 with a torn patellar tendon and signed a one-year extension in December. He is being eased back into practice, but with no established ADP and roughly 0% roster rates, he sits at the bottom of dynasty boards. Treat him as a watch-list dart throw who needs an injury ahead of him to matter.

7569.name: Nico Collins
7569.headline: Elite alpha Texans target earner, back-end WR1
7569.body:
Collins remains one of the most efficient high-volume receivers in football, and 2025 reinforced his alpha status: 15 games, a 21.7 percent target share, 120 targets, 71 catches for 1,117 yards and 6 scores, 155.2 fantasy points and a WR8 finish by our rank. His 2.48 yards per route and 0.55 WOPR confirm he is the unquestioned focal point in Houston, while a microscopic 0.8 percent drop rate (one drop on 72 catchable balls) shows elite hands.

The only knock is touchdown variance and quarterback play: he scored 8 in 2023 and just 6 in 2025 despite more targets, and his 180.4-point WR8 ceiling in 2023 shows the upside when everything clicks. Three straight seasons at or near top-20 production, with two top-10 finishes, make him a remarkably stable foundation piece. Draft him confidently as a back-end WR1 and locked weekly starter, with legitimate top-five upside if the touchdowns and target volume both peak together.

7571.name: Rashod Bateman
7571.headline: Bateman's 2025 Cratered, And The Numbers Are Brutal
7571.body:
The advanced profile fell off a cliff in 2025. Bateman managed just 224 yards and two scores across 13 games, his target share collapsing to 9.3% on 38 looks while his catch rate dropped to a dismal 50%. His efficiency cratered alongside the volume: a minus-0.072 EPA per play, a 44.7% success rate, and a meager 0.76 yards per route run that ranked him WR110. That is a near-total erosion of the WR38 form he flashed in 2024.

The 2024 baseline is the reason to keep watching: 756 yards, nine touchdowns, a 1.83 YPRR, and a 0.625 EPA per play on a 15.8% target share. But that ceiling now sits behind Zay Flowers, with rookies pushing for snaps and the role looking like a downfield complement rather than a featured target. He is a deep-league dart throw until the volume returns.

7585.name: Davis Mills
7585.headline: Capable bridge starter, but only a fantasy QB when Stroud sits
7585.body:
Mills profiles as exactly what his profession demands of a backup: steady in a pinch, replaceable when the franchise quarterback returns. His 2025 spot duty produced a 39.4% success rate and minus-0.093 EPA per play across six games, with a 6.6-point negative CPOE that underlines how far below average his accuracy graded. He threw five touchdowns to one pick on 159 attempts, but at 5.75 yards per completion and only 10.2% deep attempts, the upside ceiling stays low. The lone redeeming arrow is improvement over a brutal 2024 line (minus-0.281 EPA, minus-11.7 CPOE).

For 2026, the math is simple. With C.J. Stroud healthy, Mills returns to the bench and carries no standalone value, sitting around QB60 in consensus rankings and rostered in roughly half a percent of leagues. He merits a streaming look only if Stroud misses time, where a projection near 153 passing yards and under one touchdown per game caps him as a desperation bye-week play.

7587.name: Dyami Brown
7587.headline: Field-stretcher with no role: a deep-bench dart at best
7587.body:
Brown's profile keeps screaming complementary deep threat without the volume to matter. His 2025 stint in Jacksonville produced just 37 targets across 11 games (6.8% target share), and the efficiency cratered: a minus-0.056 EPA per play, a 40.5% success rate, and five drops on only 25 catchable balls (a 13.5% drop rate). The 14.0 air yards per target confirm the vertical usage, but he turned that into 20 grabs for 227 yards and one score, finishing as the WR108.

Now back in Washington for a second tour on a one-year, $3 million deal, he projects as a depth piece behind an established receiver room. Even the rosier outside projection (roughly 30-394-3) caps him near 55 fantasy points, and his expert rank sits around WR141 with an ADP outside pick 350. Until an injury opens snaps, there's nothing here to draft. Watch the role, not the talent.

7588.name: Javonte Williams
7588.headline: Williams secures Dallas backfield after career-best Cowboys debut
7588.body:
Williams reset his value in 2025, rumbling for 1,201 yards on 252 carries at 4.77 per tote with 11 rushing scores across 16 games, good for 207.8 fantasy points and an RB15 finish. The volume was elite: 69% snaps, 66 red-zone opportunities, 20 goal-line carries, plus 35 grabs on 51 targets. He forced 25 broken tackles, gained 2.39 yards after contact, and ripped six runs of 20-plus with a long of 66, a leap from his 3.69 YPC Denver swan song.

His -0.035 EPA per play still trails the position's best, and the 8.4% target share caps full-PPR appeal, but Dallas stood pat behind him and handed him a new deal. As the locked-in lead back drafted around the third-fourth round turn, Williams profiles as a sturdy RB2 with weekly RB1 ceiling if the early-season touchdown pace holds.

7591.name: Justin Fields
7591.headline: Mahomes insurance in Kansas City with rushing-floor upside
7591.body:
Fields lands in Kansas City as the highest-leverage backup in football, and his profile remains exactly what it has always been: legs first, arm second. Across nine 2025 starts he threw for just 1,259 yards on a 62.7% completion rate, with a negative minus-0.06 EPA per play, a 44.4% success rate, and a CPOE of minus-1.9 -- bottom-tier through-air efficiency. He still salvaged 142.7 fantasy points by adding 383 rushing yards and four scores on 71 carries, the same formula that produced 289 rush yards over 10 games in 2024.

The rushing equity is the entire case here. If Mahomes opens the year limited, Fields becomes a streamable QB1 on volume alone, having posted multiple 29-plus-point outings as a starter. But the floor is real: he can disappear behind a clean passing line, and he sat at QB37 last year. He is a late-round, contingency-only stash -- buy the upside only if you can absorb the silence.

7594.name: Chuba Hubbard
7594.headline: The 2024 breakout cratered; can Hubbard rebuild value?
7594.body:
The 2025 tape is hard to defend. Hubbard slid to RB41 on 95.4 fantasy points across 15 games, with his yards per carry collapsing from 4.78 to 3.81 and his snap share falling from 77.3% to just 45.8%. He managed one rushing touchdown after scoring 10 the year prior, broke a single tackle on 134 carries, and posted zero breakaway runs while his EPA per play stayed underwater at -0.059. Yards after contact dipped to 1.81, and red-zone work dried up to 24 opportunities from 56.

There is a path back. The 2024 baseline showed a genuine bell-cow capable of 26 carries of 10-plus yards and double-digit scores, and the receiving role held steady at 30 grabs and three touchdowns. But job security is the real question, with younger backs pushing for snaps. Treat him as a mid-round dart throw whose ceiling depends entirely on reclaiming volume.

7595.name: Tylan Wallace
7595.headline: Special-teams body with a deep-ball lottery ticket attached
7595.body:
The profile speaks for itself: across 11 games in 2025, Wallace ran just 78 routes, drew 8 targets, and converted a 2% target share into 4 catches for 45 yards and a score. A 16.8% route rate and 0.58 yards per route run confirm a player who barely sees the field on offense, even if his 0.189 EPA per play kept him modestly efficient on tiny volume.

The ceiling lives in 2024, when 11 grabs turned into 193 yards, a 17.6 yards-per-reception clip, an 84-yard explosive, a 75% success rate, and a sparkling 1.057 EPA per play. That field-stretching upside is real but unreliable. Now in Cleveland after his Baltimore run, he profiles as a return specialist and depth flier rather than a roster lock. With no projectable target volume, he stays off fantasy radars outside the deepest dynasty stashes.

7597.name: Kenny Yeboah
7597.headline: Yeboah's Cardinals Audition Starts From The Bottom
7597.body:
Yeboah has barely cracked the field across his career, logging just 19% of snaps over seven games in 2024 and running only 41 routes. The flashes exist when he plays: a 0.34 EPA per play, a 50% success rate, and 1.15 yards per route run all hint at a competent role player when targeted. But the sample is microscopic, anchored by a 1.4% target share, five receptions, 47 yards, and one touchdown. A 25% drop rate on his limited looks is the glaring red flag, and his 0.195 targets per route run can't be trusted at that volume.

Now in Arizona, he arrives as a tryout signing fighting for a reserve job in a crowded tight end room. There's no defined path to snaps, no ADP, and effectively zero roster relevance. Treat him as a deep-league dart throw only if camp news points toward a real special-teams or blocking role.

7600.name: Pat Freiermuth
7600.headline: Steelers tight end fades into TD-dependent streamer
7600.body:
Freiermuth slid backward in 2025, catching 41 of 54 targets for 486 yards and four touchdowns over 15 games, good for just 72.6 fantasy points and a TE24 rank. That is a sharp drop from his TE12 2024, when a 79.6 percent route share and 17 percent target share fueled 65 grabs, 653 yards and seven scores worth 105.3 points. His snap share collapsed from 69.3 to 50.4 percent and routes fell from 434 to 326, gutting the volume that made him a back-end starter.

The efficiency was actually elite, a 0.432 EPA per play and zero drops on 41 catches, but the role shrank around him. As a 2021 second-rounder now 26, his floor still rests on red-zone work (nine red-zone targets, four end-zone looks), yet a 10.3 percent target share makes weekly reliability tenuous. Treat him as a matchup-based streaming TE2, not a set-and-forget option.

7602.name: Kylen Granson
7602.headline: Depth signing in Tennessee with no clear fantasy path
7602.body:
Granson's trajectory has been steadily shrinking, and the numbers leave little to argue with. After a 50-target, 299-route season in 2023 that posted a respectable 0.229 EPA per play and 48% success rate, his usage collapsed: 31 targets on a 44.6% route share in 2024, then a near-invisible 2025 with Philadelphia. That final season produced just seven catches for 40 yards across 12 games, a 23.4% snap rate, a 1.9% target share, and negative efficiency at minus-0.207 EPA per play. The volume simply isn't there anymore.

He lands in Tennessee on a one-year deal as a depth piece in the tight end room, not a starter. There are no 2026 projections to lean on, and dynasty value sits in the TE90s with essentially zero roster presence. Unless an injury or scheme shift hands him real route volume, he stays off the radar. Pure deep-league monitor at best.

7607.name: Michael Carter
7607.headline: Pass-catching Titans back stuck in a timeshare
7607.body:
Carter has settled into a clear identity: a receiving-down complement, not a lead back. In 2025 he played 13 games on a 45.2 percent snap share, running 265 routes and turning 45 targets into 33 catches for 267 yards, while his ground game cratered to 92 carries for 333 yards at a weak 3.62 per carry. The advanced marks confirm the limits: 1.35 yards after contact per attempt, a 5.43 percent explosive-run rate and a -0.036 EPA per play. The 66 fantasy points ranked RB45.

That receiving role is his only real tether to value, mirroring 2024s tiny 3-game sample and a career that never topped the 6.23 YPC flash of his 2023 part-time year. A 2021 fourth-rounder out of North Carolina now in Tennessee, he profiles as a passing-down specialist with a thin standalone floor. Carter is a PPR-only deep flex and bye-week fill-in, best left on waivers until an injury opens early-down work.

7610.name: Trey Lance
7610.headline: Career backup with no fantasy pulse
7610.body:
Lance remains one of the leagues great what-ifs, and the 2025 numbers are grim: four games, 57 attempts, a 47.4 percent completion rate, 226 yards, zero touchdowns and a pick, totaling 13.5 fantasy points as QB60. The efficiency is alarming, a minus 0.478 EPA per play, a 28.7 percent success rate, and a minus 9.8 CPOE that ranks among the worst marks for any passer with meaningful snaps. His 2024 cameo in Dallas was nearly identical, four games and 12.7 points with no scores.

The lone redeeming trait is mobility, a 10 percent scramble rate at 10.7 yards a pop and 85 rushing yards on 17 carries, but that cannot offset the passing collapse. Now in Los Angeles behind Justin Herbert, the former third overall pick is a clipboard-holder with no standalone path to snaps barring injury. He carries zero redraft value and only the faintest dynasty lottery ticket; leave him entirely on the waiver wire.

7611.name: Rhamondre Stevenson
7611.headline: Bruising back rebounds on efficiency, mid-range RB2 value
7611.body:
Stevenson bounced back in 2025 despite playing 14 games, climbing to 146.8 fantasy points (RB25) on far better per-touch work: 4.64 yards per carry versus 3.87 in 2024, with explosive-run rate up to 11.54 percent and 2.77 yards after contact. He forced a broken tackle every 8.1 carries (16 total) and scored a career-best seven rushing plus two receiving touchdowns, while adding 32 catches for 345 yards at 10.78 per reception. His 31 red-zone opportunities and 11 goal-line carries underline genuine scoring equity.

The 2021 fourth-rounder (Pick 120, Oklahoma) remains New England's lead back, though three fumbles and a -0.044 EPA per play keep him short of elite, and added backfield competition could trim the workload. With a three-down role, goal-line work and PPR usage intact, his volume floor and touchdown upside make him a back-end RB2 with weekly-starter weeks. Target him in the RB20-25 range as a stable, if unspectacular, contributor.

7670.name: Joshua Palmer
7670.headline: Buffalo's Field-Stretcher Hunts a Bigger 2026 Slice
7670.body:
Palmer's first Buffalo season was a quiet one on paper, but the underlying work hints at upside. Across 12 games he ran routes on just 39.7% of dropbacks, drew a 7.7% target share, and turned 37 looks into 22 grabs for 303 yards. The downfield role survived the move: 11.46 air yards per target and a 19.3 air-yards-per-reception mark rank among the deepest on the roster, and he posted a healthy 0.182 EPA per play with a 43.2% success rate despite zero touchdowns.

The story is opportunity, not ability. His 2024 baseline in Los Angeles, a 13.2% target share, 0.388 EPA per play, and 52.3% success rate, shows what fuller usage unlocks. With six red-zone targets already and consistent vertical separation, even a modest snap bump pushes him into flex relevance. He is a cheap late-round dart with real spike-week equity in a high-volume passing attack.

7694.name: Tommy Tremble
7694.headline: Efficient on a sliver of the targets
7694.body:
Tremble finished as our TE47 in 2025, and the profile reads like a part-time tight end who does the little he gets well. Across 15 games he ran 283 routes but drew just 37 targets, a 7.7% share and a thin 0.131 targets per route run, turning that volume into 27 grabs for 249 yards, 2 scores and 36.9 fantasy points. The efficiency is real: a 0.223 EPA per play, a 56.8% success rate and a 73% catch rate all rank well, but at 0.88 yards per route run the ceiling is capped.

The blocking-first usage hasn't budged in three seasons, with route rates stuck near 50% and target shares hovering around 6 to 8%. Carolina splits tight end snaps, so meaningful volume only arrives if the room thins out. Draft him as a deep-league dart, not a weekly starter.

7716.name: John Bates
7716.headline: Blocking specialist with negligible target volume; deep waiver-wire afterthought
7716.body:
Bates is paid to block, and the receiving numbers say exactly that. Across 2025 he ran just 171 routes on a 34.2% route share, drawing only 16 targets for a 3.7% team target share and a microscopic 0.064 WOPR. He turned that into 11 grabs for 103 yards and a score, and the efficiency was actually sharp where it counted: 0.488 EPA per play, a 62.5% success rate, and 6.18 yards after the catch per reception. The problem is the funnel, not the hands. With a 0.6 yards-per-route mark and three air yards per target, there is no path to fantasy volume.

The broader picture confirms it. He sits buried on the depth chart with no expanded role on the horizon, and projections topping out near 18 catches, 174 yards, and a couple of scores leave him as a low-end TE3 going essentially undrafted. He stays a blocking-first body to leave on the wire until something opens up.

7720.name: Kene Nwangwu
7720.headline: Return-game specialist with no real backfield path in New York
7720.body:
Nwangwu's profile is a special-teams story, not an offensive one. His 2025 advanced line lays it bare: 13 carries for 49 yards (3.77 per attempt) on a 4.4 percent snap share across 10 games, with a single target, a -0.288 EPA per play, and just 10.9 fantasy points. He created almost nothing on his own, logging 1.92 yards after contact per attempt, zero broken tackles, and only one explosive run. That mirrors his thin 2023 sample in Minnesota, when he managed 2.6 yards a pop on five totes.

The Jets brought him in on a modest one-year deal explicitly for return duties, and nothing about the depth chart points to a scrimmage role. Ranked outside RB179 in consensus drafts and rostered in essentially no leagues, he carries zero seasonal value. Only an injury ahead of him would change the math; until then, leave him on waivers and revisit only if New York's backfield collapses.

7757.name: Ben Skowronek
7757.headline: Special-teamer who barely sniffs the route tree
7757.body:
The advanced profile is the story here, and it's a stark one: Skowronek logged just 58 routes across 16 games in 2025, a 10% route share that translated to a 1.1% target share and a 0.03 WOPR. He turned six looks into four catches for 69 yards and a score, and the per-snap efficiency is actually fine -- 0.901 EPA per play, a 66.7% success rate, 17.3 yards per reception -- but the volume is a rounding error. His snap rate has stalled near 15% for three straight seasons, and the routes have only shrunk.

At 28, the 6-foot-3 Notre Dame product is a blocking-first depth piece behind Pittsburgh's established pass-catchers, not a target earner. Projections peg him around 5 catches for 51 yards and minimal scoring, with no meaningful draft value and a lingering hand issue that doesn't move the needle. He's a roster afterthought in all standard formats.

7812.name: Simi Fehoko
7812.headline: Blocking Specialist With No Path To Targets
7812.body:
Fehoko's 2024 profile screams deep-roster afterthought: 16 targets across six games for a 3.3% target share, a 0.132 rate per route run, and just six catches on 37.5% efficiency. The 106 yards came on a vertical diet, 14.75 air yards per look, yet only 0.88 yards per route run and a WOPR under 0.09 confirm he never commanded volume. A 0.168 EPA per play and 37.5% success rate read fine in a tiny sample, but two of his eight catchable balls hit the ground.

Now in Arizona on a one-year deal, the 6-foot-4 Stanford product profiles as a blocker and special-teams body rather than a rotational pass-catcher. He logged zero scores in 2024 after his lone 2023 receiving touchdown, and carries no meaningful ADP or ranking. Until injuries clear a path, treat him as roster filler with negligible fantasy upside in any format.

7828.name: Noah Gray
7828.headline: Chiefs blocking TE2 craters without 2024 red-zone luck
7828.body:
Gray's 2025 was a sharp regression. He played 14 games on a 52.1 percent snap share, ran 265 routes, and turned a 6.7 percent target share into just 21 catches for 178 yards and zero touchdowns, good for 17.8 fantasy points and a TE56 finish. That collapse is stark against 2024, when he caught 40 of 49 targets for 437 yards and five scores on a 0.473 EPA per play to land TE29. His efficiency cratered: 0.67 yards per route run versus 1.34, a -0.16 EPA, and a 35.1 percent success rate.

The profile is entirely matchup- and touchdown-dependent behind Travis Kelce, with red-zone work the only ceiling driver. He saw only two red-zone targets in 2025 after 10 in 2024, and three drops on 24 catchable balls did not help. Now 26, Gray is a blocking-first TE2 whose relevance evaporates when the scores dry up. Leave him on the waiver wire in all redraft formats; he is a desperation bye-week streamer at best.

7842.name: Luke Farrell
7842.headline: Efficient blocker stuck behind Kittle and Tonges
7842.body:
Farrell is the kind of tight end whose advanced profile flatters his fantasy ceiling without raising it. On just 14 targets across 13 games in 2025, he posted a 0.253 EPA per play and a 57.1% success rate, caught 11 of those looks cleanly with zero drops, and turned four red-zone targets into two scores. The catch is volume: a 2.5% target share, 25.3% route rate, and 0.56 yards per route run confirm a blocking specialist who barely runs pass patterns. His efficiency is real (a 0.534 EPA back in 2023 says he can finish plays), but it lives on a sliver of usage.

The depth chart kills any draftable case. He sits third behind George Kittle and Jake Tonges, and even if Kittle's Achilles costs him early-season time, the targets funnel to Tonges first. With a projected line near five catches and 41 yards, Farrell stays off fantasy rosters in every format.

7891.name: Brock Wright
7891.headline: Blocking-first TE2 with no path to fantasy volume
7891.body:
Wright remains a depth piece in Detroit's offense, and his profile leaves little room for fantasy interest. Across eight 2025 games he ran just 170 routes on a 27.5% route share, drawing 22 targets for a 4% target share and turning them into 14 catches, 108 yards, and two scores. The peripherals confirm the limited role: a 0.064 WOPR, 0.64 yards per route run, and a negative EPA per play (-0.088), down sharply from his efficient -- but equally low-volume -- 2024 (0.192 EPA, 68.8% success). He's a blocker first who lives near the line, with airless usage (one air yard per target).

The path forward looks no clearer. He projects out around 19 catches, 175 yards, and a couple of touchdowns over a full season, the kind of line that never cracks weekly lineups. With Sam LaPorta locked into the receiving snaps and a neck injury clouding his availability, Wright stays a stash-only name. There's nothing here to draft.

8110.name: Jake Ferguson
8110.headline: Ferguson's volume floor is real, but the ceiling is capped
8110.body:
Ferguson turned a healthy 17 games and a 77.5% route rate into 102 targets, 82 catches, and 8 scores in 2025, his TD output rebounding from a goose egg the year prior. The efficiency, though, tells the cap story: 7.32 yards per reception, just 1.19 yards per route run, and an EPA per play of 0.177 that trailed his 2023 peak of 0.321. A 16.8% target share and 24 red-zone looks anchor the floor, but a 47.1% success rate and microscopic 4.66 air yards per target frame him as a possession piece, not a field-stretcher.

Behind CeeDee Lamb and George Pickens, he stays third in the pecking order, leaning on red-zone work and Dak Prescott's availability. His one collapse came when Prescott missed time. With elite hands (just two drops, 80.4% catch rate) and consistent six-target volume, treat him as a high-end TE2 worth a double-digit-round flier.

8111.name: Cade Otton
8111.headline: Steady volume tight end with thin scoring upside
8111.body:
Otton has quietly become a fixture in Tampa Bay's passing game, playing 93.5% of snaps and running a route on 89.2% of dropbacks in 2025. He drew 81 targets for a 15% target share, hauled in 59 balls, and dropped just one. The problem is the ceiling: only one touchdown despite six red-zone looks, a 5.17 air-yards-per-target figure, and 1.08 yards per route run. His EPA per play slipped from 0.144 in 2024 to 0.05, and his fantasy output cratered from 81.6 points to 63.2 as the scoring dried up.

There's a path back toward his 2024 form. With Mike Evans gone, vacated red-zone work could land on Otton, who tallied three scores across four games when Tampa's top wideouts missed time in 2024. But he's a high-floor, low-spike piece in a crowded room, finishing TE25 last season. Treat him as a streaming or bye-week answer, not a weekly starter.

8112.name: Drake London
8112.headline: London's Target Hog Profile Survives Any Quarterback
8112.body:
Even in an injury-shortened 2025, London soaked up a 21.6% target share and ran 0.294 targets per route, posting a 0.212 EPA per play and a 53.6% success rate across 12 games. The fuller picture is the 2024 ceiling: 158 looks, a 29.3% target share, 0.71 WOPR and nine scores while finishing the WR4 in advanced rank. He buys you 15 red-zone targets in a partial season and barely drops anything, with a 0.9% drop rate on 69 catchable balls.

The volume travels regardless of who lines up under center, with Tagovailoa now pushing Penix and London entrenched as the unquestioned focal point. Drafters are paying up around the WR7 range and a top-20 overall slot, which is fair for a player carrying a near-30% air-yards share. Health is the only real variable; the usage and efficiency are already WR1-caliber when he's on the field.

8116.name: Pierre Strong
8116.headline: Packers depth back marooned behind Jacobs workload
8116.body:
Strong remains a low-snap change-of-pace flier whose most recent tape, 2024 in Cleveland, shows the ceiling: 26 carries for 108 yards at 4.15 per tote, plus 14 catches on 22 targets for 104 yards across 13 games, totaling a meager 21.2 fantasy points and an RB84 rank on a 19.2 percent snap share. His 2023 line was busier but not better, 63 carries for 291 yards and a touchdown at RB61, with a minus-0.171 EPA per play that underscores limited efficiency.

Now in Green Bay, he sits fourth on the depth chart behind workhorse Josh Jacobs, Chris Brooks, and MarShawn Lloyd, leaving no standalone path to touches. His receiving chops (0.75 yards per route run, 123 receiving YAC in 2024) give him a sliver of upside only if injuries cascade ahead of him. For 2026 redraft he is a waiver-wire handcuff at best, not a draftable asset.

8117.name: Jalen Tolbert
8117.headline: Veteran flier in Miami's wide-open, rookie-heavy receiver room
8117.body:
Tolbert's 2025 was a step backward across the board: a 51.3% snap rate, a 5.6% target share, and just 18 catches for 203 yards and a single score over 12 games left him at WR116. The volume erosion is stark next to his 2024 breakout, when he ran routes on 79.8% of snaps, drew 79 targets, and turned a 12.7% share into 610 yards and seven touchdowns on a 50.6% success rate and 0.319 EPA per play. The 2025 efficiency cratered too, with yards per route run sliding from 1.14 to 0.80.
