Now in Miami on a one-year deal, he profiles as the most experienced body in a room stacked with three drafted rookies, which could earn him early snaps under a coaching staff that values proven hands. But a Malik Willis-led offense and a projected five-target ceiling keep him firmly in deep-PPR flier territory. Treat him as a late-camp watch, not a draftable asset.

8119.name: Jahan Dotson
8119.headline: Dotson lands in Atlanta hunting a real target share
8119.body:
Two years buried behind A.J. Brown and DeVonta Smith left almost nothing on Dotson's profile. In 2025 he ran 376 routes for just 36 targets, a 7.8% share, and 18 catches for 262 yards and a single score. The efficiency was brutal: 0.7 yards per route run, a 33.3% success rate, and minus-0.418 EPA per play, ranking him 104th. He stayed on the field for 15 games but the role never materialized.

The upside lives in 2023, when Washington gave him 83 targets, a 13.8% share, and 49 grabs for 518 yards and four touchdowns. A two-year Atlanta deal reopens that door, but he is one of several bodies brought in to replace Darnell Mooney inside a run-leaning offense. Until he separates from that committee, treat him as a late-round dart and bench depth, not a weekly starter. ADP near WR125 reflects exactly that.

8121.name: Romeo Doubs
8121.headline: Patriots slot addition brings steady target floor, capped ceiling
8121.body:
Doubs posted a career-best stat line in 2025: 16 games on a 77.6 percent snap share, 386 routes, an 18.5 percent target share, and 85 targets turned into 55 catches for 724 yards and six scores, totaling 110.4 points at WR33. The underlying efficiency was excellent, a 0.395 EPA per play, 1.88 yards per route, a 0.455 WOPR, and 17 red-zone targets, building on his 2024 WR54 (601 yards) and 2023 WR46 (674 yards, eight TDs) seasons. He has been remarkably durable, averaging 15.3 games over three years.

The move to New England reshapes his outlook: he opens as the starting slot receiver but behind A.J. Brown on the outside, which likely trims the 18-plus percent target share he enjoyed in Green Bay. A 12.76 air-yards-per-target profile gives him modest big-play equity, but the touchdown-dependent ceiling and a new, lower-volume role make him a back-end flex and bye-week fill-in rather than a confident weekly starter in PPR formats.

8125.name: Calvin Austin
8125.headline: Speedy slot flier lands behind Nabers in New York
8125.body:
Austin's profile is built for splash plays, not volume. Across three Pittsburgh seasons he peaked at a 12.6% target share, an 84.8-point campaign, and 548 yards in 2024, then slipped in 2025 to 31 catches on 55 targets for 372 yards and three scores over 14 games. The downfield role is real — 11.0 air yards per target, an 18.4% air-yards share, and a 19.5-yard average depth on his catches — but the efficiency cratered to a 43.6% success rate and minus-0.075 EPA per play, a steep drop from the plus-0.416 he posted the year before.

A one-year Giants deal slots him into the slot vacated by Wan'Dale Robinson, but he opens behind Malik Nabers in a crowded room. With a 53.5% snap rate and 0.184 targets per route as his recent ceiling, he's a deep-league dart at best — useful only if injuries open the field in front of him.

8126.name: Wan'Dale Robinson
8126.headline: Volume monster changes scenery, target hog in Tennessee
8126.body:
Robinson broke out in 2025, ranking WR13 with 125.9 fantasy points: 140 targets at a hefty 27.8 percent share, 92 catches for 1,014 yards and four scores on a 90.8 percent snap rate. The leap was real, his 2.04 yards per route run nearly doubled 2024s 1.28, his +0.146 EPA per play flipped a -0.173 mark, and per-catch output jumped from 7.52 to 11.02 yards as his air yards per target rose from 4.69 to 8.48. A 0.603 WOPR is genuine alpha usage, even in a PPR-skewed role.

The move to Tennessee is the swing factor. He brings a proven ability to command 140 targets, but a downgrade at quarterback and an unproven supporting cast cloud whether that volume and the efficiency gains travel. Expect him to operate as the slot-based target leader with a strong floor and a touchdown-dependent ceiling. He profiles as a back-end WR2 in PPR and a solid WR3 in standard, a high-floor pick whose value hinges on retaining his outsized target share.

8127.name: Charlie Kolar
8127.headline: Efficient blocking tight end with too little volume to matter
8127.body:
The advanced profile screams efficiency without opportunity. Across 13 games in 2025, Kolar ran routes on just 24.4% of dropbacks, drew 15 targets (a 3.7% share), and turned 10 catches into 142 yards and two scores. He was excellent on contact, posting a 66.7% success rate and 0.344 EPA per play, and the prior two seasons were even sharper (0.744 and 0.854 EPA). But a 0.133 target-per-route rate and 0.077 WOPR confirm he is a part-time receiver, not a featured one.

The move to the Chargers does nothing to unlock that ceiling. He profiles as a blocking-first body buried behind Oronde Gadsden, with modest projections of roughly 16 catches, 200 yards and a couple of touchdowns. ADP near pick 270 reflects the reality: he is a deep-league afterthought whose efficiency never gets enough snaps to translate into fantasy points.

8129.name: Dameon Pierce
8129.headline: Buried in Philadelphia's backfield with no clear path to touches
8129.body:
Pierce's spine has rotted since his promising rookie debut. His 2025 cameo with Kansas City was a five-game blip: 14 carries for 36 yards at 2.57 per pop, an 8.4% snap share, and a brutal -0.301 EPA per play that ranked him 118th. Even the flashes of 2024 in Houston were schedule-quirk noise built on a 92-yard touchdown that single-handedly inflated his 7.33 yards per carry across just 40 attempts. The underlying tackle-breaking has faded too, and he hasn't cleared a 32% snap rate since 2023.

Now he lands in Philadelphia behind Saquon Barkley, with Tank Bigsby and Will Shipley also ahead of any meaningful workload. That's a third- or fourth-string slot with no realistic route to volume. The modest projection of roughly 35 carries, 161 yards and one score reflects pure handcuff-depth status. He's undraftable in standard formats and only worth a passing glance if Barkley's situation changes.

8130.name: Trey McBride
8130.headline: The undisputed TE1, even with touchdown regression looming
8130.body:
McBride isn't a tight end so much as a featured No. 1 receiver who happens to line up inline. He posted a 27.4% target share on 169 looks in 2025, ran a route on 94.1% of dropbacks, and turned that volume into 126 catches for 1,239 yards. The underlying efficiency backs the workload: a 0.436 EPA per play, a 60.4% success rate, and just two drops on 128 catchable balls. He's been a target magnet for three straight years, and at 25 the usage floor is rock solid.

The only real caution is the 11 touchdowns, which more than quintupled his 2024 total of two and won't fully repeat. Even halved, his receiving profile keeps him in the elite tier, and his 33 red-zone targets argue he'll stay involved near the goal line. A new coaching staff and added backfield investment could trim Arizona's league-leading pass volume, but no tight end pairs this target share with this efficiency. Draft him as the position's clear top option.

8131.name: Isaiah Likely
8131.headline: Free from Andrews, Likely inherits a genuine starter's role
8131.body:
For four years Likely was a part-time piece behind Mark Andrews, and the underlying numbers still hint at what's been bottled up. In 2024 he posted a 0.478 EPA per play with a 63.8% success rate and turned 58 targets into six scores; even a banged-up, 12-game 2025 yielded a 0.15 EPA mark, 75% catch rate and a tidy 1.29 yards per route. He's never crossed 60 targets in a season. That ceiling is gone now.

The Giants paid $40 million over three years to make him the centerpiece tight end, and outside Malik Nabers (rehabbing an ACL) the pass-catching depth is thin. The path to a career-high target share is obvious, even if a run-leaning scheme caps the absolute volume. Projections near 66 catches and 790 yards make him a back-end TE1 worth a late-round shot around pick 130. The talent has always been there; finally so is the opportunity.

8132.name: Tyler Allgeier
8132.headline: Touchdown vulture chasing carries in a crowded Arizona backfield
8132.body:
Allgeier's 2025 line tells the story of a goal-line specialist, not a featured back. He punched in eight rushing scores on just 143 carries while averaging a modest 3.59 yards per attempt, and his EPA per play sat underwater at -0.055. The volume was concentrated near the stripe: 33 red-zone opportunities and 10 goal-line carries propped up a profile that managed only a 30.2% snap share and a 3.1% target share. That formula finished RB44 with 109 points, and the 1.61 yards after contact per attempt confirms the efficiency ceiling is low.

The move to Arizona muddies things further. He now sits behind James Conner, rookie Jeremiyah Love, and Trey Benson, with passing downs spoken for. Drafters are treating him as a late dart at RB48 ADP. Bank on touchdown poaching and injury-contingent upside rather than steady weekly snaps, and keep him on the bench until the depth chart clears.

8134.name: Khalil Shakir
8134.headline: Reliable Floor, Capped Ceiling as Moore Crowds the Room
8134.body:
Shakir's 2025 line tells a clear story: 72 catches on 95 targets for 719 yards and four scores across 16 games, a 75.8% catch rate, and a tidy 2.1% drop rate. The underlying marks softened from his 2024 peak, with EPA per play falling from 0.442 to 0.099 and success rate sliding from 57% to 44.2%. His 19.8% target share and 0.27 targets per route held steady, but a 3.67 air-yards-per-target number confirms a short-area role doing most of its damage at 7.51 yards after the catch.

That profile screams dependable possession option, not breakout. D.J. Moore's arrival threatens Shakir's standing as Buffalo's target leader and likely trims his volume closer to five looks a game. With limited downfield and red-zone equity, the ceiling stays modest. Treat him as a steady WR4 with a high floor, drafted around the WR53-54 range, rather than chasing untapped upside.

8135.name: Treylon Burks
8135.headline: Former first-rounder still searching for a role
8135.body:
Burks landed in Washington still chasing the promise of his 2022 first-round billing, and 2025 offered little, just eight games, a 5.1 percent target share, and 19 fantasy points as WR136. He caught only 10 of 22 targets for 130 yards and a score on a 27.2 percent route share, and the efficiency cratered to a minus 0.401 EPA per play with a 45.5 percent catch rate. The downfield usage is there, a 13.45 air-yards-per-target and 29.6 air yards per reception, but he simply has not stayed on the field or earned volume.

Availability is the through-line of his career, averaging eight games a year over three seasons, and his best campaign still peaked at just 3.6 points per game. Buried behind Terry McLaurin and Deebo Samuel in a crowded Commanders room, he profiles as a deep-bench flier at best. For 2026 he is a waiver-wire afterthought with only a thin dynasty-stash case if a depth-chart injury finally hands him snaps; in redraft, ignore him.

8136.name: Rachaad White
8136.headline: Fading committee back changes teams, flex ceiling capped
8136.body:
White slid further in 2025: across 17 games his output fell to 103 points and RB35, down from 148.6 (RB25) in 2024 and a workhorse 203.9 (RB4) in 2023. His carry share has cratered as snap rate dropped from 78.4 to 51.1 percent, and the pass-game value that once defined him eroded too, with six receiving scores and 393 yards in 2024 becoming zero TDs and 218 yards on a 0.63 yards-per-route mark. He ran efficiently at 4.33 per carry with a positive 0.054 EPA, but only 1.51 yards after contact flags a back living on his blocking.

Now in Washington, the former third-round Buccaneer (Pick 91, 2022) joins a new backfield where his early-down role is hardly guaranteed and a receiving rebound is the swing factor. His 8.3 percent target share and eight goal-line carries hint at usable PPR volume if he wins the passing-down job, but the trajectory is clearly downward. Treat him as a low-end flex and bench-depth dart, not a weekly starter.

8137.name: George Pickens
8137.headline: Pickens' Dallas Breakout Is Real, Not a Mirage
8137.body:
Pickens turned his first year in Dallas into a top-3 finish in our WR model, hauling in 93 of 137 targets for 1,429 yards and nine scores across all 17 games. The efficiency drove it: a 0.64 EPA per play and a 63.5% success rate dwarfed his Pittsburgh marks (0.27 and 41.7% in 2024), while 2.46 yards per route and a 0.235 target-per-route rate confirmed he was no longer a boom-or-bust deep threat. A 22.6% target share and 23 red-zone looks, even sharing a backfield-style workload with CeeDee Lamb, gave him a real floor.

The questions are off the field. Pickens is angling for a new deal and skipped voluntary work, and a sturdier Dallas defense could trim pass volume. Still, he profiles as a back-end WR1 going around the second-round turn, and his per-game ceiling justifies that price.

8138.name: James Cook
8138.headline: Cook's career-best volume makes him a locked-in RB1
8138.body:
Cook took a leap in 2025, finishing as our RB4 on 269.2 fantasy points while leading the league with 1,621 rushing yards on 309 carries at a punishing 5.25 per tote. His efficiency held under that heavy workload: a 12.62% explosive-run rate, nine carries of 20-plus yards, 21 broken tackles and 2.22 yards after contact per attempt. He also tucked away 12 rushing scores plus 16 goal-line carries, so the touchdown equity is real even if 2024's gaudy 16 TDs was always going to settle down.

The lone soft spot is the passing game. A 1.17 YPRR, 8.4% target share and just 33 catches leave value on the table for a back this dynamic in space. If Buffalo finally leans into him on third downs, his ceiling climbs. With no new competition in the backfield and elite rushing volume guaranteed, Cook is a safe early-round anchor.

8142.name: Alec Pierce
8142.headline: Field-stretcher inherits WR1 role, but the profile screams boom-bust
8142.body:
Pierce broke through in 2025: 1,003 yards on just 47 grabs, a 21.3-yard average, 6 scores and a WR19 advanced finish. The efficiency is real and rare, a 0.664 EPA per play, 53.6% success rate, 2.22 yards per route and a 36.4% air-yards share that ranked him among the league's premier vertical threats. His hands held up too, one drop on 48 catchable balls. The catch: it was built on an 18.96 air-yards-per-target diet, the most volatile way to score fantasy points.

With Pittman gone, he's the nominal top target, yet the role demands underneath work he's never logged, and Tyler Warren plus Josh Downs will eat short-area volume. A 15.8% target share and 56% catch rate cap the floor, and his ceiling rides on Daniel Jones throwing deep. An offseason ankle procedure may cost early camp reps. Treat him as a sixth-round dart with WR2 upside and weekly variance.

8144.name: Chris Olave
8144.headline: Healthy Olave is a target-monster WR1 worth a Round 3 swing
8144.body:
Olave's 2025 line is the case for him: a WR7 finish on 156 targets, a 27.6% target share, and a monstrous 38.5% air-yards share that screams alpha. He turned that into 100 catches for 1,163 yards and nine scores while running a route on 86.9% of dropbacks. The advanced markers back the volume up — a 0.228 EPA per play, a 50% success rate, and a 0.683 WOPR that ranked him among the league's true featured receivers. The downfield usage (11.8 air yards per target, 18.41 per catch) gives him week-winning upside.

The profile clears once Tyler Shough's late-season takeover stuck; Olave was a per-game top-five producer in those starts. Rookie Jordyn Tyson trims the ceiling slightly and may sharpen his efficiency rather than gut his role. The real watch items are health — a lung blood clot expected to resolve by late June plus a worrying concussion history. At a #28 overall ADP, he's a borderline WR1 priced as a value.

8145.name: Jeremy Ruckert
8145.headline: Efficient blocker, but the targets just aren't there
8145.body:
Ruckert's 2025 line tells the whole story: 23 catches on 29 targets across 14 games for 179 yards and a single score, good for just 23.9 fantasy points and a TE54 finish. The peripherals are quietly competent, a 79.3% catch rate, 0.157 EPA per play and a 44.8% success rate, but the volume cap is brutal. A 6.1% target share, 38.8% route rate and 0.84 yards per route run mean he simply isn't being asked to do much in the passing game, and three red-zone looks won't change that.

The profile here is a blocking tight end who occasionally chips in, not a fantasy contributor. Outside projections peg him near 20 catches, 146 yards and barely 22 points, reinforcing a TE85-ish draft tag. Until New York carves out real route volume for him, he stays off redraft boards in every format and only matters in the deepest two-tight-end leagues.

8146.name: Garrett Wilson
8146.headline: Healthy Wilson reclaims target hog role with Geno upgrade
8146.body:
A knee injury wrecked Wilson's 2025 after just seven games, but the underlying usage stayed elite while he played: 12.5% target share looks soft until you see he ran routes on barely a third of snaps, yet still drew 0.303 targets per route. The healthy version is the 2024 monster who posted a 26% target share, 0.624 WOPR, 101 grabs for 1,104 yards and a 52.3% success rate at +0.142 EPA per play. He has anchored this passing game for three years running, and three straight seasons north of 150 targets when on the field tells the real story.

The context finally improves. Geno Smith and Frank Reich replace a broken quarterback room, and Wilson remains the clear first read despite added complementary weapons. He's a strong WR2 with WR1 ceiling around an early-Round-4 ADP. Buy the volume; the floor and the upside both point up.

8147.name: John Metchie
8147.headline: Metchie's Carolina move can't outrun his career ceiling
8147.body:
Four years in, Metchie still hasn't cleared the role-player tier, and 2025 confirmed it: 13 games, a 46.1% snap share, 48 targets on a 10.3% target share, and just 274 yards on 33 catches. The efficiency markers are the bigger red flag. His 8.3 yards per reception and 1.16 yards per route run are replacement-level, his minus-0.049 EPA per play dipped back below water after a brief 2024 uptick, and a 43.8% success rate offers nothing to dream on. Two touchdowns and 38.9 fantasy points across a full slate is the production floor of fantasy irrelevance.

Carolina signed him to a one-year deal, and the depth-chart math is unforgiving behind the established top of their receiver room. A WR125 consensus rank and an ADP outside the top 150 receivers price him correctly. Without a target-share leap he has never shown, Metchie is a late-camp watch, not a draftable asset.

8148.name: Jameson Williams
8148.headline: Explosive WR2 whose vertical role finally pays off
8148.body:
Williams turned his draft pedigree into production last season, ranking ninth among receivers with 1,117 yards and seven scores across all 17 games. The efficiency markers are loud: 0.551 EPA per play, a 55.9% success rate, 17.2 yards per catch, and a 34.5% air-yards share that confirms he is Detroit's downfield engine. He ran a route on 95.5% of dropbacks while seeing an 18.5% target share, so the volume is real even if it trails the alpha ahead of him.

The ceiling is obvious, but so is the volatility. Eleven drops and a 10.8% drop rate keep weekly floors shaky, and his deep-leaning usage produces feast-or-famine lines. Detroit broadened his menu with intermediate work, which should stabilize targets, though St. Brown still commands the top look. Treat him as a high-upside WR2 in the mid-rounds, drafting the boom and stomaching the busts.

8150.name: Kyren Williams
8150.headline: Touchdown-rich workhorse worth a Round 3 RB2 bet
8150.body:
Williams keeps stacking RB1 seasons on the strength of usage and scoring, not breakaway speed. Across 17 games in 2025 he turned 259 carries into 1,252 yards at 4.83 a pop, added 36 grabs, and finished RB8 with 227.3 fantasy points. The real engine is the goal line: 63 red-zone opportunities, 18 goal-line carries, and 10 rushing scores plus three through the air. His EPA per play flipped positive to 0.044 after sitting at -0.074 in 2024, and his explosive-run rate climbed to 10 percent even as his snap share dipped to 68 percent.

The ceiling cap is Blake Corum, who now splits early-down work and pushes this toward a committee. Even so, Williams retains the lead role and the high-value touches that make backs fantasy-relevant. With an ADP near RB16 and a Round 3 price, he profiles as a steady low-end RB1, and pairing him with Corum protects the investment.

8151.name: Kenneth Walker
8151.headline: Explosive but inefficient back lands in a new backfield
8151.body:
Walker's tape screams big-play juice and screams volatility in equal measure. In 2025 he ripped off a 14.93% explosive-run rate with 33 carries of 10-plus yards, 10 of 20-plus, and a 55-yard touchdown, while forcing 23 broken tackles. Yet the efficiency lagged: 4.65 yards per carry papered over a minus-0.029 EPA per play, just 1.84 yards after contact per attempt, and 26 carries stuffed for negative yardage. He finished RB17 on 160.9 points across 17 games.

The receiving role stayed thin at 36 targets and a 7.9% target share, and his red-zone equity shrank to eight goal-line carries and five rushing scores after eight in 2023. A move off Seattle resets his outlook entirely: the breakaway speed travels, but the snap rate (46.9%) and contact balance need a friendlier scheme to push him back toward a weekly RB1 ceiling.

8154.name: Brian Robinson
8154.headline: From feature back to forgotten man
8154.body:
The 2025 collapse is hard to overstate: Robinson cratered to RB56 on just 92 carries across 17 games, his snap share gutted to 16.7% after a midseason move. The 400 rushing yards and two scores barely cleared 54 fantasy points, and a minus-0.046 EPA per play confirmed he was a passenger, not a driver. Even his efficiency markers thinned, with 1.77 yards after contact per attempt and a 4.35-yard average that flattered light volume.

Rewind only one year and the contrast stings. In 2024 he was an RB27 workhorse on 187 carries, 51.6% of snaps, eight touchdowns and 46 red-zone looks. That early-down, goal-line profile still exists, but his receiving role has shriveled from a 1.36 YPRR peak in 2023 to 0.37 last season. He needs a clear path to opportunity, and right now Atlanta's backfield does not promise one. Treat him as a late-round bench dart.

8155.name: Breece Hall
8155.headline: Workhorse volume, fading pass game, still an RB2 with juice
8155.body:
Hall handled 243 carries for 1,065 yards and 4 scores across 16 games last year, and the burst that built his name is intact: a 10.7% explosive run rate, eight runs of 20-plus, and a 59-yard touchdown all point to a back who can break a game open. His 1.84 yards after contact per attempt and 12 broken tackles confirm he still creates on his own. The drag is the surrounding math, with a slightly negative EPA per play (-0.047) reflecting how little the Jets offense did to set him up.

The receiving role is the swing factor. Targets fell to 48 last season from 95 in 2023, and that passing-down work looks likely to keep eroding, which caps his PPR ceiling. Even so, the line ranks among the league's best in yards before contact and his bell-cow rushing share is secure. Draft him as a high-end RB2 and bank on volume plus explosiveness rather than the three-down monster he once profiled as.

8160.name: Kenny Pickett
8160.headline: A Veteran Insurance Policy Behind Bryce Young
8160.body:
Pickett's 2025 cameo in Las Vegas confirmed what his profile keeps suggesting: a steady hand who doesn't move the needle. Across six appearances he completed 62.2% of his throws for just 188 yards, one touchdown against two picks, and a brutal -0.552 EPA per play with a 29.3% success rate. That finished him 61st among quarterbacks at 7.7 fantasy points, a far cry from the QB29 ranking and 39.8% success rate he posted as Pittsburgh's starter in 2023.

Now on his third team in three years, Pickett lands in Carolina as the clear backup behind Bryce Young on a roster trending upward. The one-year deal is depth insurance, nothing more. There's no draftable path here outside Superflex or two-quarterback formats, and even then only as a contingency stash should Young miss time. Leave him on the waiver wire and monitor the situation.

8161.name: Malik Willis
8161.headline: Backup with electric splits now MIA's QB1 wildcard
8161.body:
Willis has been remarkably efficient in tiny spot-duty samples. In 2025 across 4 games he completed 85.7 percent of 35 passes for 422 yards and 3 TDs with zero picks, posting a sparkling 0.486 EPA per play, a 22.0 CPOE and a 91.4 on-target rate while adding 123 rush yards and 2 scores on 22 carries (51.2 fantasy points). His 2024 Green Bay cameo was similar: 6 games, 74.1 percent completions, 3 TDs, 0 INTs, 0.246 EPA per play. The dual-threat profile is real, with rushing usage that props up his floor.

The catch is the leap from clean-pocket relief work to a full starting load; that efficiency will regress hard against starter-level defenses and tougher game scripts. But Miami's depth chart now lists him as QB1, which turns a career clipboard option into a streaming target with a rushing-driven ceiling. If the job holds through camp, he is a late-round dart and bye-week streamer; standalone QB1 value hinges entirely on volume he has never carried.

8167.name: Christian Watson
8167.headline: Big-play WR1 with vacated targets and breakout volume
8167.body:
Even on a part-time route share, Watson's 2025 tape pops. In 10 games he posted a 0.587 EPA per play and a 61.8% success rate while running only 43.7% of routes, turning 55 targets into 611 yards and six scores at 17.5 per catch. His 22.8% air-yards share and 17.76 air yards per target confirm a true downfield role, and 2.7 yards per route run ranks among the league's best. The drop rate sat at 1.8%, with 11 grabs of 20-plus yards in a half-season.

The path to a leap is volume. With Romeo Doubs gone in free agency and Dontayvion Wicks traded, the Packers' target hierarchy clears for Watson as Jordan Love's primary read and designed red-zone option. His route share lived near 44%; pushing that toward a full workload on his efficiency profile makes him a genuine WR2 with WR1 ceiling. He's worth a Round 6 swing, with the only real caveat being the injury history that cost him seven weeks last year.

8168.name: Skyy Moore
8168.headline: Camp body in Green Bay, off the redraft radar
8168.body:
The former second-round pick (54th overall, 2022) has never delivered on his draft slot, and 2025 in San Francisco was rock bottom: a 10.1 percent snap share, 66 routes, and seven targets for a 1.3 percent target share over 17 games. He caught five balls for 87 yards, no scores, and just 9.8 fantasy points, ranking 153rd at the position. His best season remains a modest 2023 in Kansas City, when 263 routes and 38 targets still netted only 244 yards and one touchdown on a 6.3 percent target share and 0.93 yards per route.

Now in Green Bay, Moore lands behind Jayden Reed, Romeo Doubs, Christian Watson and rookie capital in a crowded room, with no clear path to even a fourth-receiver role. The lone bright spot was a 0.674 EPA per play on tiny volume, but that is noise, not signal. There is nothing here for redraft or even deep dynasty rosters. Leave him on the waiver wire.

8172.name: Greg Dulcich
8172.headline: Healthy at last, Dulcich could finally own Miami's TE room
8172.body:
Dulcich's 2025 efficiency numbers are the reason to care. Across nine games he turned 33 targets into 26 grabs (78.8% catch rate, zero drops) for 335 yards and a 12.88 average, posting 2.36 yards per route and a sterling 0.559 EPA per play with a 60.6% success rate. That's a wide leap from his disastrous 2024 split between Denver and New York, where he managed a minus-0.634 EPA and an 8.3% success rate on 12 targets. The talent flashes; staying on the field has been the obstacle.

The opportunity now lines up. He sits atop the depth chart for an offense thin on proven receiving options, and a tight-end-friendly staff plus a strong spring with Malik Willis point toward real volume. His 7.1% target share and 0.232 targets per route still leave room to climb, and a projection near 53 catches and 600 yards would be a career high. Durability is the only thing standing between him and a streaming-plus role.

8180.name: Jalen Nailor
8180.headline: Free-agent payday hands Nailor his first real volume
8180.body:
Nailor's 2025 line tells the story of a buried but efficient field-stretcher: 53 targets on a 72.7% route share, 444 yards and four scores, with 12.38 air yards per target and a 17.5% air-yards share that outran his 11.3% target share. The efficiency was real too, a 0.198 EPA per play and 50.9% success rate, with 12 red-zone looks signaling more scoring upside than his snap count allowed. He simply never commanded the ball, posting a 0.292 WOPR and just 1.12 yards per route run.

Vegas paid him three years and $35 million to fix exactly that. With minimal receiver competition added and Nailor slotting as a primary perimeter option, the projected jump to roughly 42-566-4 is well within reach if his target rate climbs. He flashed top-seven weekly WR ceilings whenever he saw five-plus looks last year. At an ADP near 151, he's a sensible late dart with weekly WR3 equity.

8183.name: Brock Purdy
8183.headline: Healthy Purdy and a revamped receiver room make him a value QB1
8183.body:
Even hobbled by turf toe in 2025, Purdy posted a 0.229 EPA per play, a 54% success rate, and a 7.2 CPOE across nine starts -- elite efficiency that ranks among the best in the league when he plays. The problem was availability: nine games capped him at 2,167 yards and 177 fantasy points, and a 12.3% bad-throw rate hints at lingering discomfort. Across 2023-24 he cleared 290 fantasy points twice while throwing for 30-plus combined scores, so the ceiling is established when his body cooperates.

The weaponry got an overhaul, with Mike Evans and Christian Kirk joining McCaffrey and Pearsall, though Kittle's Achilles recovery clouds the early schedule. Projections call for roughly 4,150 yards, 28 touchdowns, and 300-plus fantasy points, and his 33 carries and three rushing scores in 2025 add a usable floor. At an ADP near pick 96 and a back-end QB1 tag, he's a profit play -- buy the efficiency and bet on health.

8188.name: Tyquan Thornton
8188.headline: Field-stretching boom-bust dart joins loaded Chiefs
8188.body:
Thornton finally flashed in 2025, posting career bests of 438 yards and three scores on just 19 catches, ranking WR65. The how is extreme: a 26.89 air-yards-per-target and 52.37 air yards per reception are pure vertical usage, fueling a gaudy +0.709 EPA per play and 1.89 yards per route run on only a 6.7 percent target share. Seven catches of 20-plus yards and a 61-yard long underline the boom, but a 51.4 catch rate, three drops, and a 39 percent snap share on 12 games expose the bust. It dwarfs his 138 combined yards across 2023-2024.

The move to Kansas City is a fit on paper but a usage minefield. He profiles as the designated deep threat behind Rashee Rice, Xavier Worthy, and Travis Kelce, meaning a thin target diet tethered to Patrick Mahomes hitting low-probability shots. That means a handful of spike weeks buried among zeros. For redraft, Thornton is a late-round dart and best-ball stash, not a lineup option you can trust weekly.

8195.name: Ronnie Rivers
8195.headline: Rams third-string back with no path to volume
8195.body:
Rivers remains a deep-roster Ram whose usage keeps shrinking: 2025 brought just six games, a 2.5 percent snap share, nine carries for 46 yards (5.11 per pop), zero targets, and 4.6 fantasy points. That is a clear step down from 2024, when he managed 22 carries, five catches, and 13.3 points across six games on a 7.4 percent snap share, and from his 2023 line of 32 rushes for 129 yards. The 2025 efficiency was empty calories, a -0.121 EPA per play with no explosive runs and only 2.22 yards after contact per attempt.

He sits third on the Los Angeles chart behind workhorse Kyren Williams and Blake Corum, with Jarquez Hunter also in the mix, leaving no standalone role and no receiving outlet (two routes all of 2025). His value is entirely contingent on injuries to the two backs ahead of him. For 2026 he is a waiver-wire handcuff at best and holds no startable redraft value in any format.

8204.name: Bo Melton
8204.headline: Full-time at receiver now, but the production isn't there
8204.body:
Melton enters 2026 with a settled job description: LaFleur has committed him to wide receiver full-time after he spent prior stretches bouncing between offense and defensive back, and Green Bay's offseason departures at the position opened the door. That clarity is the only thing working in his favor right now. The projection sheet pegs him at roughly 3 catches, 38 yards and a fraction of a touchdown across a full slate, totaling around 8.6 fantasy points — bench-clinger territory.

The rankings back up the skepticism, with expert consensus near WR218 and dynasty value around WR197, and he sits in essentially zero leagues. His late-season tape was thin too: about 0.3 grabs per game over his final three weeks, with a 34-yard outing against Baltimore as the lone flicker. A friendly schedule doesn't matter without targets. Leave him on the wire until snaps and usage actually materialize.

8205.name: Isiah Pacheco
8205.headline: Montgomery's old role awaits, but the explosiveness has vanished
8205.body:
Pacheco's tape has trended the wrong way for two straight years. After a 4.56 yards-per-carry, seven-touchdown 2023 on 205 attempts, he managed just 3.92 per carry across 13 games in 2025, with a -0.127 EPA per play and only 1.6 yards after contact. His explosive-run rate cratered from 9.76% to 8.47%, his breakaway rate fell to 0.85%, and he broke a single tackle on 118 carries. Injuries shrank his 2024 and 2025 workloads to seven and 13 games, and the burst that once produced 169.9 fantasy points is gone.

In Detroit he inherits the vacated complementary role behind Jahmyr Gibbs, the physical between-the-tackles job. That caps his ceiling, but the path to RB2 value exists if Gibbs misses time. He's a late-round dart and contingency stash, not a target before the double-digit rounds, and the recent efficiency decline tempers any optimism about a bounce-back.

8207.name: Tyler Goodson
8207.headline: Deep handcuff with no path to volume
8207.body:
Goodson's profile screams emergency depth, not draftable asset. In 2025 he logged just 10 games on a 5% snap share, turning nine carries into 24 yards at 2.67 per rush with a brutal -0.327 EPA per play and only 3.2 fantasy points. Even his stronger 2024 line, 153 rushing yards on 4.78 a pop with a positive 0.085 EPA across 14 games, came with a 12.6% snap rate and a 3% target share, the ceiling of a clear backup.

The 2026 setup does nothing to change that math. He sits behind Jonathan Taylor with a role projected to stay minimal, an ECR around 456 overall and an RB rank near 119, and he is rostered in roughly zero leagues. A groin issue has trended the right way, but there is no realistic standalone value here. Goodson is purely a contingency stash, only relevant if the workhorse ahead of him misses extended time.

8210.name: Chig Okonkwo
8210.headline: Steady move tight end heads to Washington seeking a target bump
8210.body:
Okonkwo arrives in Washington as a known commodity who profiles as a stable, low-ceiling TE2. Across the last three seasons in Tennessee he hovered in the same lane: 70 to 79 targets, a 14 to 16 percent target share, and finishes between TE17 and TE22. His 2025 line tells the story plainly, 56 catches on 79 looks for 560 yards and just 2 scores, with a 70.9 percent catch rate and only 68 fantasy points. The efficiency was respectable, a 0.16 EPA per play and 1.3 yards per route run, but the volume never spiked.

The red-zone usage is the real anchor on his outlook, with five red-zone targets and three inside the 20 producing minimal touchdown equity. He plays a lot, a 63.7 percent snap share on a 69.3 percent route rate, yet generates yardage on short, secure throws averaging 4.63 air yards per target. A new offense could nudge his role, but until the target count and scoring climb, he stays a streaming-tier option.

8220.name: Sincere McCormick
8220.headline: Efficient but injury-limited depth back changes coasts to San Francisco
8220.body:
McCormick's last meaningful action came in 2024 with the Raiders, where he packed real production into four games: 39 carries for 183 yards at 4.69 per tote, a 15.38 percent explosive-run rate, and 21.2 fantasy points (RB83). He flashed home-run ability with a 29-yard long and added six catches on seven targets, but the sample is tiny and his -0.01 EPA per play plus a modest 1.69 yards after contact reflect a back yet to prove durability or a defined role.

The move to San Francisco places him deep on the depth chart behind Christian McCaffrey and Jordan James, with Isaac Guerendo also around. There is no clear runway to touches while McCaffrey is upright, and McCormick's own availability has been a recurring question. The Shanahan scheme can elevate a zone-fit runner, which gives him a faint contingency-value upside if injuries strike ahead of him. For now he is a waiver-wire watch and at most a deep-dynasty stash, not a 2026 redraft asset.

8225.name: Daniel Bellinger
8225.headline: Efficient blocker-first tight end with no real target floor
8225.body:
Bellinger's 2025 with the Giants flashed quiet efficiency without anything resembling fantasy volume. On just 26 targets across 13 games he posted a 0.869 EPA per play, a 73.1% success rate and 1.23 yards per route run, catching 19 balls for 286 yards and two scores. The problem is everything underneath that: a 40.7% route share, a 5.2% target share and a 0.112 target-per-route-run rate cap his ceiling regardless of how clean the per-snap math looks. He moves the chains when used, but he simply isn't used enough to matter in lineups.

The move to Tennessee reunites him with Brian Daboll, now the offensive coordinator, which is the one thread worth tracking given their Giants history. Even so, the projected role is blocker-first, with the upside hinging on a target spike that hasn't materialized in three seasons. With an ADP outside pick 360 and TE56-type ranking, he stays a deep-bench dart at best until volume actually shows up.

8228.name: Jaylen Warren
8228.headline: Warren broke out as a lead back, but Dowdle clouds the path
8228.body:
Warren's 2025 was the breakout the advanced numbers had been hinting at: 211 carries for 958 yards at 4.54 per pop, six rushing scores, and an RB18 finish worth 177.1 fantasy points across 16 games. The contact balance is real — 23 broken tackles, 2.36 yards after contact per attempt, and a 10.9% explosive-run rate — and his 0.04 EPA per play flipped positive after two negative seasons. He also stayed busy through the air, turning 45 targets into 40 grabs, 333 yards and two touchdowns while logging 234 routes.

The catch is opportunity, not talent. Pittsburgh added Rico Dowdle, who profiles as a genuine threat for early-down and goal-line work under a new staff, and Warren's 14 goal-line carries from a year ago aren't guaranteed to hold. The receiving floor is his insurance: with Kenneth Gainwell's vacated targets up for grabs, Warren can absorb passing-down volume that props up his PPR value. Draft him as a back-end RB2 with committee risk priced in around the RB24-29 range.

8230.name: Ty Chandler
8230.headline: Aging backup changes scenery without changing his ceiling
8230.body:
Chandlers value has cratered. In 2025 he managed just 3 games, 17 carries for 47 yards (2.76 YPC), a -0.302 EPA per play, and 5.8 fantasy points on a 22.3 percent snap share, ranking RB108. That continued a downward slide from 2024 (14 games, 56 carries, 3.25 YPC, 13.9 snap percent, RB77) and is a far cry from his 2023 high of 102 carries, 461 yards at 4.52 YPC, 3 touchdowns, and 80 fantasy points. The explosive juice is gone: zero runs of 10-plus yards in 2025 versus an 8.82 explosive-run rate in 2023.

The move to New Orleans is a fresh backfield, but the 27-year-old former fifth-rounder enters as a depth piece with no clear path to volume and just 1.82 yards after contact per attempt last year. Availability has also been a problem, averaging 9.3 games over three seasons. Absent an injury ahead of him, there is no standalone fantasy case. He is a waiver-wire afterthought and a handcuff worth monitoring only in deep leagues.

8249.name: Lucas Krull
8249.headline: Depth Tight End Without A Path To Volume
8249.body:
Krull's profile screams roster filler. His healthiest stretch came in 2024, when 10 games and 200 routes produced just 23 targets, a 4.2% target share, and 152 yards on 19 catches. The efficiency markers were quietly fine, an 82.6% catch rate, +0.195 EPA per play, a 52.2% success rate, and 0.76 yards per route run, but the volume never scaled. His 2025 was a non-event: two games, four targets, a 0.7% share, and 1.5 fantasy points before disappearing entirely.

Nothing about the role suggests a leap. He re-signed in Denver on a one-year deal buried in the tight end room, sitting outside the top 150 in early consensus and rostered in essentially zero leagues. The career arc, one touchdown across three seasons and a peak of 152 yards, caps the ceiling hard. Until injuries above him force snaps, Krull is a deep-dynasty stash and nothing more in redraft.

8254.name: Julius Chestnut
8254.headline: Deep-roster Titans back with no path to volume
8254.body:
The advanced profile spells out why Chestnut stays a roster afterthought: just 11 carries on a 3.9% snap rate across 16 games in 2025, turning into 40 yards at 3.64 a pop and a single broken-tackle-free season. His -0.361 EPA per play was actively negative, and the 4 fantasy points he produced sat him at RB116. Even his stronger 2024 sample (22 rushes, 4.64 YPC, RB99) came on a 7.1% snap share, so the trend is shrinking opportunity, not growing it.

Tennessee brought him back on a one-year deal strictly as backfield depth, and he enters 2026 ranked outside the top 180 in redraft with no projections or ADP attached. With two career receptions and 2.55 yards before contact masking just 1.09 after, there's no efficiency hook to bet on either. He's a watch-list special-teamer who only matters if injuries clear the entire depth chart ahead of him.

8408.name: Jordan Mason
8408.headline: Physical early-down hammer with handcuff upside behind Aaron Jones
8408.body:
Mason proved his first Minnesota season was no fluke, churning out 758 yards on 159 carries at 4.77 per tote with six scores across 16 games, finishing as the team's leading rusher despite a 42.8% snap share. The profile is built on contact balance: 2.42 yards after contact per attempt, 13 broken tackles, and a 13.84% explosive-run rate. His -0.059 EPA per play and modest 16-target receiving role cap the ceiling, but 30 red-zone opportunities and seven goal-line carries point to durable touchdown equity.

He enters 2026 as the early-down grinder in a timeshare with Aaron Jones, who handles passing downs. That setup makes Mason a touchdown-dependent flex with genuine RB2 upside the moment Jones misses time. With an ADP near RB42 and goal-line workload baked in, he's a sturdy late-round value whose scoring profile could spike if Minnesota's offense moves the chains efficiently.

8414.name: Britain Covey
8414.headline: Special-teams specialist with no path to fantasy snaps
8414.body:
The on-field profile makes the case plainly: Covey logged just six games in 2025 at a 10.3% snap rate, running only 26 routes and drawing four targets for two catches and 20 yards. His 0.9% target share and 0.014 WOPR are roster-edge numbers, and they are not anomalies. Across 2023-2025 his target shares read 1.1%, 1.9%, and 0.9%, with route participation never clearing 6%. He has never scored a receiving touchdown in this sample and has cleared 42 yards in a season only once.

The usage ceiling is purely cosmetic for fantasy. He failed to lock down a 53-man spot elsewhere and carries a flat zero-reception projection for the year, leaving him essentially unrostered. Covey's value lives on returns and gadget snaps, not in the passing game, so there is no realistic redraft or dynasty angle here. Leave him on waivers in every standard format.

8423.name: Brittain Brown
8423.headline: Big-Play Flashes Buried On Chicago's Practice Squad
8423.body:
Brown's 2025 sample was tiny but eye-catching: 5 carries for 37 yards, a 7.4 average, a touchdown, and a 22-yard burst that accounted for one explosive run on 20% of his attempts. His 0.405 EPA per play and 4.6 yards after contact per attempt signal a back who creates when handed space, and he forced two missed tackles on five totes for a 40% elude rate. The catch: just two games, a 6.7% snap share, and one target.

The profile is intriguing, the path is not. Brown sits on Chicago's practice squad purely for depth, and at 27 he carries no real draft value, landing outside the top 120 backs in consensus and rostered in essentially zero leagues. Until injuries clear the rotation ahead of him, this is a watch-list name whose efficiency markers only matter if opportunity ever arrives. For now, leave him on waivers.

8484.name: Ko Kieft
8484.headline: Blocking specialist with no fantasy pulse in Tampa
8484.body:
Kieft's profile is that of a pure in-line blocker, and the numbers leave no ambiguity. Across 10 games he ran 67 routes on a 19.7% snap rate yet drew only five targets, a 0.9% target share and a 0.022 WOPR that sit at the very bottom of the position. He caught one of those looks for two yards, and his -0.267 EPA per play with a 20% success rate confirms the passing game simply does not run through him. The lone redeeming note: two red-zone targets, one inside the 10, and a touchdown that salvaged 6.2 fantasy points.

Nothing about the 2026 setup changes the math. He returns as roster depth behind Tampa's established tight ends, carries no meaningful draft or ADP value, and a brutal positional schedule offers no streaming hook. He is a real-football contributor whose value lives in run blocking, not box scores. Leave him on the wire in every format until usage forces a second look.

8489.name: Drew Ogletree
8489.headline: Blocking tight end stuck behind Tyler Warren breakout
8489.body:
Ogletree is a 2022 sixth-round pick (192 overall, Youngstown State) who has never been more than a complementary, blocking-first tight end. His career peaked at a meager 3.0 points per game in 2023 and cratered to roughly 0.8 per game in 2025, and his fantasy value has always been TD-dependent rather than volume-driven. He has been durable, averaging 14.7 games over the last three seasons, but durability without targets does not move the needle.

The 2026 situation is bleaker still: the Colts drafted Tyler Warren to be a focal-point tight end, with Mo Alie-Cox also ahead of Ogletree on the depth chart, leaving him third on the TE ladder. Daniel Jones spreading the ball to Josh Downs, Alec Pierce and Jonathan Taylor leaves almost nothing for a part-time blocker. Ogletree is a deep-league streaming dart at best on a rare red-zone week, and a clear waiver-wire hold in all standard formats.

8527.name: Dareke Young
8527.headline: Special-teams body with no offensive path to fantasy value
8527.body:
The numbers leave no room for optimism. Across eight 2025 games Young ran just 17 routes on a 3.4% route rate and a 9.4% snap share, drawing only three targets all year. He turned those into two grabs for 48 yards, and while the per-touch efficiency reads loud on paper (24 yards per catch, 2.82 yards per route, 0.657 EPA per play, a 66.7% success rate), that's noise off a microscopic sample, not a signal. His 0.7% target share and 0.021 WOPR confirm he was never part of the passing plan.

Now in Las Vegas after trailing coordinator Klint Kubiak over from Seattle, Young projects as a core special-teamer and depth piece rather than a rotational receiver. There's no ADP, no ranking, and no realistic route to volume on a crowded wideout depth chart. Leave him on the wire in every format; any fantasy buzz here would require a cascade of injuries ahead of him.

8583.name: Stone Smartt
8583.headline: Blocking-tier Eagles depth with no fantasy pulse
8583.body:
The undrafted Old Dominion convert has bounced from quarterback to receiver to tight end, and the production has never followed. His 2025 stint with the Jets was negligible: six games, a 9.9 percent snap share, 60 routes, and nine targets turned into seven catches for 52 yards, no touchdowns, and 5.2 fantasy points. That was a step down from his 2024 high in Los Angeles, when 111 routes and a strong 1.87 yards per route run produced 16 grabs for 208 yards across nine games, still without finding the end zone.

Now in Philadelphia, Smartt sits firmly behind Dallas Goedert's successor situation and any pass-catching tight ends ahead of him, profiling as a blocking-and-special-teams body. He has caught zero career touchdowns in three seasons and has never cleared a 4.4 percent target share. There is no redraft or dynasty case here. He is a waiver-wire afterthought.

8676.name: Rashid Shaheed
8676.headline: Field-stretcher carved bigger role, boom-bust WR3 in Seattle
8676.body:
Shaheed posted career-high volume in 2025, playing 18 games on a 90.7 percent route rate and turning a 16.3 percent target share into 59 catches for 687 yards and two scores (97.6 points, WR38). The profile remains explosive and downfield: 11.45 air yards per target, a 22 percent air-yards share and a 0.398 WOPR, with a perfect 0.0 percent drop rate on 92 looks. But efficiency dipped, as a -0.047 EPA per play and 45.7 percent success rate trailed his 2023 Saints form (719 yards, five TDs, 0.161 EPA).

The undrafted Weber State product (rookie 2022) now works a deep Seattle perimeter, where his 11.6 yards per catch and big-play knack give weekly spike potential but a thin floor on a 64.1 percent catch rate. With only nine red-zone targets, his scoring stays volatile. He is a WR3/flex and best-ball favorite whose splash games carry roster-altering ceiling, but inconsistent target quality keeps him out of the must-start tier.

8698.name: Jake Tonges
8698.headline: Efficient Kittle insurance with real upside when starting
8698.body:
Tonges quietly delivered when handed snaps in 2025, and the underlying numbers back up the production. Across 10 games he posted a 0.309 EPA per play and a 60.9% success rate while drawing 46 targets on just 240 routes (0.192 targets per route run). The red-zone usage stands out: seven red-zone looks, five end-zone targets, and five touchdowns on only 34 catches. He didn't drop a single ball (0% drop rate) and converted at a 73.9% catch rate, making him a trustworthy chain-mover whenever San Francisco leaned on him.

The catch is the depth chart. He sits behind George Kittle, so standalone value hinges entirely on Kittle's health, which already cost San Francisco seven games last year. When Kittle sat, Tonges saw six-plus targets in multiple outings and posted double-digit PPR scores. As a late-round dart and waiver stash, he's a legitimate streaming TE1 the moment Kittle misses time.

8799.name: Zaire Mitchell-Paden
8799.headline: Saints depth tight end offers nothing for redraft
8799.body:
An undrafted Florida Atlantic product who entered the league in 2022, Mitchell-Paden remains a blocking-first afterthought in the New Orleans passing game. The 2025 sample is microscopic: just 3 games, a 22.9 percent snap share, and only 26 routes for a 4.1 percent route rate. He drew 3 targets all year for a 0.7 percent target share and a 0.014 WOPR, catching 2 for 17 yards and a 14-yard long. The lone bright spot is efficiency on noise-level volume, a 0.382 EPA per play and 0.65 yards per route run, but 1.7 fantasy points tell the story.

At 26 with no draft capital and a route share that has never cracked single digits, there is no realistic path to relevance barring catastrophic injuries ahead of him. He logged 1 red-zone target and 1 end-zone look, so the only conceivable spike is a fluke goal-line score. This is not a streamer or a TE-premium dart; it is a roster-clogging depth body. Leave him on the waiver wire in all formats and skip the dynasty stash.

8800.name: Malik Davis
8800.headline: Deep-bench flier whose efficiency outran his volume
8800.body:
Davis touched the ball just 52 times on the ground across 10 games in 2025, yet he made those carries count: 250 rushing yards at 4.81 per tote, two scores, and a 43-yard breakaway that doubled as his longest touchdown. Roughly 8 percent of his runs went for explosive or breakaway gains, and his 2.96 yards before contact hints the blocking, not the back, capped his ceiling. The drag was a 20.7 percent snap share, a 0.8 percent target share, and a minus-0.07 EPA per play that pulled his fantasy total to 38.6.

The 2026 path is opportunity, not talent. Behind Javonte Williams, Davis is fighting for backup reps that could vanish to younger legs, and a late-season calf and eye injury didn't help his standing. He's a final-round dart in deep leagues, worth a flier only if camp anoints him the clear handcuff.

8917.name: KaVontae Turpin
8917.headline: Big-play juice, but the volume just isn't there
8917.body:
Turpin's 2025 efficiency pops in isolation: a 0.44 EPA per play, a 52.6 percent success rate and a 15.2-yard average on his 26 grabs, capped by an 86-yard burst. The problem is everything underneath. He ran a route on just 35.9 percent of Dallas dropbacks, drew a 6.3 percent target share and turned 38 looks into only 396 yards and two scores, finishing 70th at the position. His 1.69 yards per route run actually slid backward from 2024's 2.09 as the snaps thinned out.

That profile screams big-play gadget, not weekly starter. The route count caps his ceiling, and the back-end ECR and triple-digit WR ADP reflect a player buried on the depth chart. Unless the role meaningfully expands, his real fantasy value lives in return-yardage formats, where the field-flipping speed finally translates to points. Leave him on waivers in standard leagues.

9220.name: Evan Hull
9220.headline: Camp body in Houston after another lost, inefficient season
9220.body:
The advanced numbers explain why Hull keeps bouncing around rosters. Across six games in New Orleans last year he managed just 19 carries for 48 yards, a brutal 2.53 per attempt, with only 1.05 yards after contact and a -0.378 EPA per play that ranked him 111th. He cleared 19.2 percent of snaps just once and posted a lone reception, finishing at 5.4 fantasy points all season. There is no breakaway juice here: zero runs of 10-plus yards, zero broken tackles, and a long gain of eight.

Now on his third team in three years, Hull arrives in Houston after an April waiver claim and projects as a depth piece battling for table scraps behind the established backfield. He carries no real ADP and sits near the bottom of the running back board. Until an injury opens a path to touches, he is a preseason watch-list name and nothing more for redraft purposes.

9221.name: Jahmyr Gibbs
9221.headline: Gibbs profiles as a true bell-cow ascendant
9221.body:
Gibbs has built one of the steadiest profiles at the position, ranking RB5 in our 2025 model after posting RB3 (310.9 points) in 2024. Last season he stacked 289.9 fantasy points across 17 games, splitting work as a runner and receiver: 243 carries for 1,223 yards at 5.03 a pop with 13 rushing scores, plus 77 grabs on 94 targets for 616 yards and five more touchdowns. The receiving floor is rare, and his red-zone opportunity total anchors the touchdown equity.

If the early-down committee that has capped his carries thins out, a path opens toward a true workhorse share in a high-powered Detroit attack. His passing-down role already spiked down the stretch, pushing a 17.1% target share that should climb further. Goal-line and red-zone volume both project up, and the dual-threat usage makes him a defensible first-overall selection in PPR formats.

9224.name: Chase Brown
9224.headline: Three-Down Bengal Workhorse Primed For A Full-Season Leap
9224.body:
Brown's 2025 ledger reads like a foundational fantasy asset: an RB9 finish on 213.6 points across 17 games, with 232 carries for 1,019 yards (4.39 per pop), six rushing scores, and a genuine receiving floor of 69 grabs for 437 yards plus five touchdowns. The 14.5% target share and 47 red-zone opportunities, including 12 goal-line carries, confirm Cincinnati funnels its highest-value touches through him.

The efficiency tells the only cautionary tale, a slightly negative minus-0.046 EPA per play and modest 1.97 yards after contact, but Brown's profile spiked dramatically once Joe Burrow returned, and his usage never wavered. Drafters are paying up accordingly, with a top-20 overall ADP and an RB11 price tag. A full slate behind a healthy Burrow points squarely at career-best volume and clear top-tier upside in 2026.

9225.name: Tank Bigsby
9225.headline: Efficient Eagles handcuff stuck behind Barkley's bell-cow role
9225.body:
Bigsby's 2025 move to Philadelphia tanked his volume but not his efficiency. Behind Saquon Barkley he managed just 63 carries over 15 games on a 12.6 percent snap share, yet ripped off 356 yards at a career-best 5.65 per carry with a 20.63 percent explosive-run rate, 10 broken tackles (one every 6.3 carries) and 2.67 yards after contact. That is a stark drop in opportunity from his 2024 Jacksonville workload, when 168 carries produced 766 yards, seven touchdowns and 122 fantasy points on a 36.1 percent snap share.

The talent is evident; the path is not. Barkley is the unquestioned workhorse, capping Bigsby at roughly 4-5 carries a game with spike potential only if injury strikes. His 50.8 fantasy points ranked 61st, and the near-zero receiving role (four targets) limits PPR appeal. He is one of the league's better handcuffs and a contingency-value stash, but only a desperation flex play while Barkley stays healthy.

9226.name: De'Von Achane
9226.headline: Elite chunk-play back facing a thinner receiving menu
9226.body:
Achane's 2025 spine is loud: 1,350 rushing yards on 5.67 a carry, an explosive-run rate near 17% and 22 broken tackles, plus 3.02 yards after contact per attempt across 16 games. He finished RB6 with 255.8 fantasy points, and the dual-threat profile is what separates him — 67 catches, 488 receiving yards and a healthy 18.4% target share on 381 routes. A 75.4% snap share confirms he's the unquestioned engine of this backfield.

The regression case is real but narrow. A new play-caller and mobile quarterback Malik Willis figure to siphon checkdowns, trimming the receiving volume that anchors his PPR floor, and goal-line work has never been a strength. An offseason shoulder procedure shouldn't cost camp time. He's a back-end RB1 — mid-second-round value around the RB8-9 range — with a thinner ceiling than his talent suggests.

9228.name: Bryce Young
9228.headline: Career highs, but the efficiency still says backup
9228.body:
Young set personal bests in 2025 with 23 touchdowns, 3,011 yards and a 63.6% completion rate across 16 games, yet the underlying profile barely budged. His EPA per play sat at -0.03 for the second straight year, his success rate ticked up only to 42.6%, and a finish as our QB21 tells you the volume never arrived. A 7.7% deep-attempt rate and 4.8 completed air yards per completion confirm an offense that lived underneath, and 11 picks against modest production cap the ceiling.

There are flickers of growth: CPOE climbed to +1.0, the bad-throw rate fell to 16.4%, and on-target percentage rose to 77.9%. But 216 rushing yards and two scores add little, and the half-season of sub-25-attempt games signals a coaching staff still managing him. Treat Young as a superflex-only stash with real bust risk, not a one-quarterback option.

9229.name: Anthony Richardson
9229.headline: Buried behind Jones, a rushing-upside dynasty stash only
9229.body:
The profile that once made Richardson tantalizing remains unresolved on the page. His fullest sample, 2024, produced 499 rushing yards and six scores on the ground across 11 games, yet the passing line stayed alarming: a 47.7% completion rate, a minus-10.9 CPOE, a 39.6% success rate, and minus-0.056 EPA per play. The 2025 season barely happened, just two appearances and nine passing yards before the job slipped away entirely.

Now he sits behind Daniel Jones after requesting a trade, with Riley Leonard also in the backup mix, so there is no standing role to draft. His arm has yet to stabilize, but the legs still carry real weekly ceiling, and he has cleared 22 fantasy points in six of 11 starts where he played meaningful snaps. Leave him on waivers in single-QB formats and treat him purely as a Superflex dynasty hold awaiting a starting opening.

9230.name: Tanner McKee
9230.headline: Backup behind Hurts with intriguing spot-start upside
9230.body:
McKee remains stuck behind Jalen Hurts in Philadelphia, but his limited sample shows real arm talent for a 2023 sixth-rounder (Round 6, Pick 188, Stanford). His 2024 cameo was electric: in two games he completed 66.7 percent for 323 yards, four touchdowns, zero picks and a stellar 0.225 EPA per play across 45 attempts. The 2025 expansion to four games told a tougher story, with completion dipping to 55.8 percent, EPA falling to -0.141, a 42.6 success rate and a 26.3 bad-throw rate as defenses got real film on him.

The profile is a pocket passer who lives and dies by efficiency and TD volume, evidenced by his 28.8 fantasy points in 2024 versus just 13.8 in a larger 2025 role. With Hurts entrenched and durable, McKee has no standalone path to weekly relevance, but his four-TD flash makes him a sneaky two-QB and superflex stash. In single-QB redraft he is a pure waiver name, only ownable as a contingency lottery ticket if Hurts misses time.

9479.name: Darnell Washington
9479.headline: Efficient on a budget, but the target ceiling stays capped
9479.body:
The arrow is pointing up. Washington's snap rate climbed to 57.2% in 2025 while his target share nearly doubled year over year, from 5.4% to 8.2%, and 43 looks turned into 31 catches for 364 yards. The efficiency underneath is real: 1.43 yards per route run, a 0.316 EPA per play, a 60.5% success rate, and 7.39 yards after the catch per reception from a 6-foot-7, 264-pound frame that breaks tackles. He even drew eight red-zone targets despite playing only 13 games.

The problem is the math above him. A new four-year extension locks in his role, but Pat Freiermuth, DK Metcalf, and Michael Pittman all sit ahead in the pecking order, which keeps his weekly volume thin and one touchdown is hard to bank on. Projected for roughly 33 catches and 345 yards, he's a TE3 worth stashing only in tight-end-premium or deep formats where his per-route production can quietly pay off.

9480.name: Brenton Strange
9480.headline: Locked-in Jaguars TE1 with breakout efficiency, capped only by scoring
9480.body:
Strange's 2025 tape backs the leap: in 12 games he ran 318 routes (52.9% of dropbacks), drew 60 targets at an 11% share, and turned them into 46 catches for 540 yards on a 76.7% catch rate. The efficiency markers are loud for the position, a 0.523 EPA per play, a 63.3% success rate, and 1.70 yards per route run, all career bests that pushed him from TE31 to TE18 despite missing time. He's the undisputed top tight end in Jacksonville, third on the team in routes, and a genuine pillar of this passing game alongside Lawrence.

The ceiling hinges on touchdowns. Just three scores on 10 red-zone looks last year, and only six across his career, keeps the floor honest, especially with Jacksonville's loaded receiver room and a pair of rookie tight ends added. Still, his late-season surge and improving year-over-year usage make him a sturdy mid-round TE2 with sleeper upside if the scoring rate finally catches the volume.

9481.name: Luke Musgrave
9481.headline: Efficient when on the field, but buried and snap-starved
9481.body: The efficiency case is real and the volume case is broken. Across 13 games in 2025 Musgrave posted a 0.327 EPA per play and a 58.1% success rate while catching 77.4% of his targets, yet he ran routes on barely 40% of snaps and drew just 31 looks for a 6.7% target share. One red-zone target and zero touchdowns underline the problem: when the ball comes his way he produces, but Green Bay rarely points it there.\n\nThat snap profile has trended the wrong direction since his promising 2023 rookie line (46 targets, 65% snap rate), with injuries repeatedly stalling momentum. A crowded tight end room and a hard projected schedule cap the ceiling further, and modest 2026 forecasts near 20 catches and 200 yards reflect a clear backup role. He is a deep-league dart throw at best until the usage says otherwise.

9482.name: Michael Mayer
9482.headline: Raiders tight end shows pulse but caps low
9482.body:
Mayer posted the best counting season of his career in 2025, yet it still landed him at TE35. Over 13 games on a 61.8 percent snap share he ran 249 routes, drew a 10.1 percent target share and a strong 0.201 targets-per-route rate, catching 35 of 50 targets for 328 yards and a touchdown with just one drop. The problem is depth of usage: a thin 4.8 air yards per target, a -0.197 EPA per play and a 46 percent success rate reveal a checkdown role, even as his 5.6 yards after the catch per reception added juice.

The trend is up from 2024s 15.6 points but still shy of his 2023 rookie pace (44.4 points, 0.418 EPA). A 2023 second-round Notre Dame pick, he profiles as a blocking-first complement whose fantasy value is TD-dependent and matchup-driven. With 6 red-zone targets hinting at scoring upside but no volume foundation, Mayer is a streaming-only tight end and waiver option, not a player to roster through quiet weeks.

9484.name: Tucker Kraft
9484.headline: Ascending Packers tight end is a top-five breakout
9484.body:
Krafts 2025 was a half-season tease: in just 8 games he posted a monster 0.889 EPA per play, a 2.19 yards per route run, and 15.28 yards per reception while turning 44 targets into 32 grabs and 6 touchdowns on 86 percent of snaps. That efficiency built on his 2024 every-down emergence, when he played all 17 games, ran 442 routes, drew a 15.1 percent target share, and finished TE7 with 707 yards, 7 scores, and 113.3 fantasy points.

The lone caveat is durability after the injury-shortened campaign, but the role is locked: he is Jordan Loves clear top tight end with red-zone equity (12 red-zone targets in 8 games). His 10.75 yards after catch per reception and 18.8 percent avoided-tackle rate make him a YAC weapon defenses must account for. If he plays a full slate at 2025 efficiency, he is a fringe top-three TE. Draft him as a weekly starter and one of the best values at the position.

9486.name: Dontayvion Wicks
9486.headline: Talented depth piece buried in a crowded Eagles room
9486.body:
Wicks has flashed efficiency without ever earning steady volume. His 2025 line in Green Bay tells the story: just 46 targets and a 10% target share over 13 games, yet a 0.308 EPA per play and a 56.5% success rate that point to a sharper player than the box score. Back in 2023 he posted a 60.3% success rate, 2.11 yards per route run and 0.503 EPA on 58 targets, so the talent is real when the looks come. The problem has always been the looks.

Now in Philadelphia, the math gets harder. DeVonta Smith, A.J. Brown, Dallas Goedert and a pair of drafted rookies sit ahead of him on a run-leaning offense where Jalen Hurts rarely clears 3,000 passing yards. An ADP near 197 and a sub-30-catch projection frame him correctly: a late-round dart, draftable only in the deepest formats and otherwise a waiver watch.

9487.name: Parker Washington
9487.headline: Breakout slot man earns flex floor in Jacksonville
9487.body:
Washington took a real third-year leap, turning 95 targets into 58 catches for 847 yards and five scores across 16 games while posting 126.7 fantasy points and a WR24 rank. That nearly doubled his 2024 line of 51 targets, 390 yards and a WR87 finish, and the underlying usage backed it up: target share jumped from 9.8 to 17.4 percent, WOPR from 0.238 to 0.431, and yards per route run from 1.07 to 2.24. He also stretched the field, ranking high in air yards per target (12.49) while posting a 0.333 EPA per play.

The 2023 sixth-round Penn State pick is now Jacksonville's clearest perimeter option, though seven drops and a 61.1 percent catch rate flag inconsistent hands. With a 17.4 percent target share already established and 14 red-zone looks, he carries a startable floor but a ceiling capped by the offense's pass-volume swings. Draft him as a low-end flex with WR3 upside, not a locked weekly starter.

9488.name: Jaxon Smith-Njigba
9488.headline: League's target king cashes in as a fantasy cornerstone
9488.body:
Smith-Njigba's 2025 wasn't a step forward so much as a leap into the elite tier. He commanded a 35.8% target share, the highest in football, turning 163 looks into 119 catches, 1,793 yards and 10 scores while posting a 0.879 WOPR and 3.81 yards per route run. The efficiency backs the volume: a 0.556 EPA per play and 63.2% success rate, both massive jumps from his 2024 marks (0.361, 56.2%). At a 93.3% route rate with a 48.8% air-yards share, he is the offense, not a piece of it.

That earned him the richest receiver extension in league history and locks in his every-down centerpiece role. Adding Rashid Shaheed and losing his coordinator could trim a few targets at the edges, but a 35.8% share leaves enormous cushion. Drafting in the top five overall is the correct price for a true WR1 with three straight years of climbing usage.

9493.name: Puka Nacua
9493.headline: Puka Nacua's elite efficiency makes him a foundational WR1
9493.body:
Nacua sits as the No. 2 receiver in our advanced model after a dominant 2025: 129 catches on 166 targets for 1,715 yards and 10 touchdowns across 16 games, anchored by a 28.6% target share and a league-leading 3.71 yards per route run. The supporting metrics are absurd, a 0.697 EPA per play and a 70.5% success rate, and his 246 fantasy points led the position. Even his injury-shortened 2024, just 11 games and 990 yards, came with a career-high target rate, proving the volume share never wavers.

The Rams funnel everything through him, and with Matthew Stafford back under center he projects as the clear primary read once again. That floor explains why he carries a top-four consensus value and belongs in the opening tier of any draft. Health is the only real variable; the per-game production is too elite to fade, so treat him as a cornerstone pick.

9494.name: Marvin Mims
9494.headline: Big-Play Gadget Talent Still Stuck Behind a Crowded Room
9494.body:
Mims took a step back in 2025, finishing as our WR86 with a tepid 0.069 EPA per play and a 41.2% success rate while logging just a 36.9% snap rate across 15 games. His 8.7% target share and 0.199 targets per route run translated to 37 catches for 322 yards and one score, and the 1.26 yards per route run badly undersold the explosive profile he flashed a year earlier (0.449 EPA, 2.76 YPRR, six touchdowns). The efficiency cratered as the role stayed part-time.

Denver's offseason did him no favors. Adding Jaylen Waddle behind Courtland Sutton pushes Mims into a target competition with Troy Franklin and Pat Bryant for whatever scraps remain. His return work keeps him relevant in special-teams scoring leagues, but as a pure receiver he profiles as a late-round dart and bench stash whose ceiling hinges on an injury opening reps ahead of him.

9500.name: Josh Downs
9500.headline: Pittman's exit hands Downs a full-time target spike
9500.body:
Downs has been quietly efficient since arriving, but 2025 was a step back on paper: 58 catches for 566 yards and 4 scores across 16 games, with his EPA per play sliding to 0.098 and yards per route run dipping to 1.73 after sitting at 2.33 the year prior. The encouraging signs are his 16.6% target share, a healthy 50% success rate, and 15 red-zone looks despite a crowded room. His age-23 ceiling is the real benchmark: 72-803-5 on 21.7% of targets and a 0.421 WOPR.

The math changes now that Michael Pittman is gone. Downs profiles as the clear top option, and his depth-of-target history suggests he can absorb Pittman's volume rather than stay boxed into the slot. He needs more snaps in two-receiver sets to fully cash in, but the path to leading Indianapolis in targets is open. With WR40-50 draft value, he's a sensible upside bet who only needs the role to follow the opportunity.

9501.name: DeMario Douglas
9501.headline: Buried on the depth chart, Douglas slides off the radar
9501.body:
Douglas's 2025 line tells the story of a shrinking role: his snap share cratered to 25.9% from 62.6% the year prior, route participation fell to 37.6%, and target share dipped to 9.5% (46 targets across 16 games). When he was on the field he was efficient, posting a 0.612 EPA per play, a 56.5% success rate, and 2.16 yards per route run on just two drops. But the volume evaporated, and 64.8 fantasy points reflect a part-time contributor rather than a building block.

New England's offseason additions only worsen the outlook. Bringing in A.J. Brown and Romeo Doubs pushes Douglas further down the pecking order, and his per-game targets have steadily slid toward the low single digits. The projections agree, pegging him near 23 catches for 271 yards and under 40 fantasy points. He's a deep-bench dart at an ADP outside the top 300, useful only as an in-season waiver flier if injuries open snaps ahead of him.

9502.name: Tank Dell
9502.headline: Returning from devastating knee injury, a dynasty bet
9502.body:
Dell's last action came in 2024, when 81 targets over 14 games yielded 51 catches for 667 yards, three scores and 89 fantasy points on a 14.8 percent target share and 0.375 WOPR. That was a step back from his electric 2023 rookie line of 709 yards and seven touchdowns in just 10 games, where his 2.35 yards per route and 0.414 EPA per play flashed star upside. A severe knee injury in December 2024 wiped out his entire 2025 season, the reason his advanced data stops there.

The questions are entirely about health and burst. If the knee allows him to recover his separation quickness, Dell could reclaim a complementary downfield role opposite Nico Collins in Houston, with real WR3 upside given his proven 14-plus percent target share. But coming off a year-plus layoff with no 2025 tape, he is a high-variance late-round dart in redraft and a buy-low dynasty bet, not a player to draft as a weekly starter.

9504.name: Kayshon Boutte
9504.headline: Efficient deep threat squeezed out by New England's offseason haul
9504.body:
Boutte quietly turned in a hyper-efficient 2025: a 0.801 EPA per play and 71.7% success rate on 46 targets, with six touchdowns and 16.7 yards per catch across 14 games. He functioned as a vertical specialist, averaging nearly 17 air yards per target and producing 1.65 yards per route run while dropping just one ball. The production was real, but the volume never followed his snaps, as his target share sat at only 9.5% despite a 67.5% snap rate.

That thin usage is the problem heading into 2026. New England imported A.J. Brown and Romeo Doubs, crowding the pecking order and shoving Boutte toward the roster bubble. Modest projections near 30 grabs, 462 yards and four scores reflect that crunch, and an ADP outside the top 85 receivers tells the story. He is a touchdown-or-bust deep flier and a bye-week patch, not someone to count on weekly.

9506.name: Sean Tucker
9506.headline: Goal-line vulture with no volume path behind Bucky Irving
9506.body:
Tucker's 2025 was a touchdown-fueled mirage: he scored eight total times (seven rushing) on just 86 carries, posting RB64 and 83.4 fantasy points despite a feeble 3.72 yards per carry and -0.07 EPA per play. The efficiency collapsed from his 2024 cameo, when he ran for 6.16 a pop on 18 percent explosive runs and 2.58 yards after contact; in 2025 those marks fell to 8.14 percent explosive and an alarming 0.97 yards after contact. His 11 goal-line carries and 24 red-zone chances show his value was scripted scoring.

The role caps the ceiling hard. At a 15.8 percent snap share behind Bucky Irving and Kenneth Gainwell, Tucker is the clear RB3 with no passing-down work, shown by 11 targets and a 2 percent target share. His fantasy line lives and dies on touchdown variance that is unlikely to repeat, and any Irving health scare is the only realistic route to standalone value. Treat him as a deep-bench handcuff and late-round dart, not a startable asset.

9508.name: Tyjae Spears
9508.headline: Titans passing-down back carved into a PPR floor
9508.body:
Spears has settled into a clear receiving-back identity. In 2025 he played 13 games and set career highs in catches (45 on 50 targets) and routes (261), turning that into 264 receiving yards and 361 yards after catch, while his rushing slipped to 72 carries for 283 yards at 3.93 YPC. The 45.9 percent snap share and 9.4 percent target share gave him 66.7 fantasy points (RB50), trailing his 2023 rookie peak of 101.8 points (RB38).

The efficiency dip is real: his yards after contact per attempt fell to 1.35 from 2.19 in 2024, and broken tackles cratered from 13 to 4. With the early-down rushing role limited and only 2 rushing scores, his value is tied almost entirely to PPR passing-down volume. That makes him a stable PPR flex with weekly RB2 upside if injuries open the backfield, but a touchdown-dependent, low-ceiling option in standard formats.

9509.name: Bijan Robinson
9509.headline: Bijan Robinson is a locked-in 2026 RB1 anchor
9509.body:
Robinson graded as our overall RB1 in 2025, banking 291.8 fantasy points across all 17 games on a near-bell-cow snap share. He paired a heavy rushing workload with a 19.8% target share, 79 catches and 820 receiving yards, layering elite receiving production onto the ground game. That caps a steady climb from RB6 (188.3) as a rookie to RB4 (280.7) in 2024 to the top of the board in 2025.

The only real knock is touchdown variance; his rushing scores dipped even as total volume rose, a number that should normalize so long as the goal-line work stays largely his. The dual-threat floor is bulletproof regardless of game script, which is exactly what you want from a foundational pick. Drafted around the top two overall league-wide, he carries genuine RB1-overall upside if Atlanta finds the end zone more often.

9510.name: Lew Nichols
9510.headline: Practice-squad holdover fighting for a Steelers roster spot
9510.body:
Nichols is a developmental back, not a draftable asset. He spent all of 2025 on Pittsburgh's practice squad and re-signed on a future contract this offseason, which keeps him in the building but says nothing about a defined role.
