The market reflects that reality: he sits around RB #211 in expert rankings and RB #207 in dynasty formats, with no ADP, no projection set, and effectively zero roster share across leagues.

The profile is worth noting only as a deep-stash flier. At 5'10" and 220 pounds, the 24-year-old Central Michigan product has the build to handle short-yardage and goal-line work if the depth chart thins out. But until he earns a 53-man roster spot and carves out touches ahead of the established backfield, there is no fantasy case to make here. Leave him on the wire and revisit only if camp news or an injury opens a lane.

9511.name: Keaton Mitchell
9511.headline: Big-play handcuff in a crowded Chargers backfield
9511.body:
Mitchell's calling card is detonating, not grinding. Across 13 games in 2025 he turned just 59 carries into 341 yards at 5.78 per tote, with a 16.95% explosive-run rate and four runs of 20-plus, including a 55-yarder. His rookie 2023 tape was even louder: 8.43 yards a carry, 25.5% explosive runs, and a 0.345 EPA per play on 47 attempts. The problem is volume and efficiency under pressure, only 2.17 yards after contact in 2025 and a slightly negative -0.041 EPA mark, signaling a player who feasts in space but rarely creates it himself.

Now in Los Angeles, he sits behind Omarion Hampton and battles Kimani Vidal for the change-of-pace role. Projections peg him near 85 carries, 395 yards, and roughly 59 fantasy points, modest standalone value that spikes only if Hampton misses time. Treat him as a deep-league speculative stash and Hampton's lottery-ticket handcuff.

9753.name: Zach Charbonnet
9753.headline: Touchdown-fueled Seahawks back is a high-end RB2
9753.body:
Charbonnet posted a career year in 2025 largely on scoring volume: 16 games, 184 carries for 730 yards, and a massive 12 rushing touchdowns off 54 red-zone opportunities and 18 goal-line carries, finishing RB30 with 161.4 fantasy points. His contact balance stands out—14 broken tackles, a tackle every 13.1 carries, 1.83 yards after contact—even as his 3.97 YPC and -0.007 EPA per play show he leaned on usage over explosiveness versus his 4.21 YPC in 2024.

The receiving role shrank to a 5.3 percent target share and 20 catches, down from 42 grabs and a 9.1 percent share in 2024, so this profile rides early-down and goal-line work over PPR floor. The risk is touchdown regression: 12 scores on that yardage is hard to repeat, and his value is tightly tethered to the SEA red-zone hammer role. Still, with proven volume and goal-line equity, the second-round UCLA product is a sturdy RB2 and weekly starter in standard and half-PPR leagues.

9754.name: Quentin Johnston
9754.headline: Chargers wideout posts WR3 line with TD ceiling
9754.body:
Johnston delivered a steady third year, turning 84 targets into 51 catches for 735 yards and a career-high eight touchdowns over 13 games, banking 120.2 fantasy points at WR31. That edged his 2024 (55-711-8, WR42) on a per-game basis despite a dip in target share from 18.5 to 15.4 percent and avoided-tackle rate falling from 14.5 to 7.8. The big-play juice is real: he logged 13 catches of 20-plus yards and three of 50-plus, with a 19.39 air yards per reception that anchors a downfield role.

The 2023 first-rounder out of TCU sustained his touchdown output two straight years (eight each), and 15 red-zone targets plus seven end-zone looks point to continued scoring equity. The 60.7 percent catch rate and 44 percent success rate cap his floor, making him touchdown-dependent week to week. Draft him as a back-end WR3 or flex with legitimate upside if his target share rebounds.

9756.name: Jordan Addison
9756.headline: Downfield burner bets on a quarterback upgrade
9756.body:
Addison's 2025 line tells a story of wasted juice: 0.177 EPA per play and a 48.1% success rate on 79 targets, but only 42 catches on a 53.2% catch rate that cratered from 63.6% the year prior. The air-yards profile stayed elite (28.8% air-yards share, 13.71 air yards per target, an 81-yard long), yet 4 interceptions on his throws and six drops gutted the efficiency. A three-game suspension capped him at 14 games and 93.1 fantasy points after he posted 149.5 in 2024.

The bull case is regression in reverse. His 2024 baseline (9 TDs, 19.1% target share, 1.82 yards per route) is the real player, and a quarterback bump back toward competence should restore that downfield connection. He carries red-zone equity (13 red-zone targets, 5 end-zone looks) and a top-tier schedule. Volume is the catch, so treat him as a high-variance WR3 with upside if the passing game stabilizes.

9757.name: Kendre Miller
9757.headline: Talented but buried, and rehabbing an ACL
9757.body:
Miller's three seasons in New Orleans tell the same frustrating story: flashes without volume. He has never topped 47 carries or seven games in a year, and his efficiency has slid from a respectable 3.8 yards per attempt as a rookie to negative EPA territory in both 2024 (-0.127) and 2025 (-0.063). The contact balance is real — seven broken tackles and 2.57 yards after contact per rush in 2025 — but a 24.3% snap share and microscopic 0.9% target rate mean the production never scaled. Across three years he has exactly three rushing touchdowns.

Now he is working back from a torn ACL, and the depth chart is genuinely crowded. Devin Neal holds the early edge for backup work and has earned more passing-down looks, so Miller's standalone value hinges almost entirely on Alvin Kamara's future. If Kamara stays, Miller is undraftable; a vacated role would make him a late-round dart. Projections of roughly 210 rush yards and two scores reflect that uncertainty — monitor camp before committing anything.

9758.name: C.J. Stroud
9758.headline: Efficiency rebound makes Stroud a value QB target
9758.body:
Stroud's 2025 looked like a quiet bounce-back under the hood: his EPA per play climbed to 0.114 with a 46.2% success rate, both nearly matching his QB8 rookie campaign and erasing the negative-EPA, 41.2% slog of 2024. He posted just eight picks against 19 touchdowns over 14 games, with a 0.4 CPOE and 74.6% on-target rate signaling cleaner ball placement. The catch was volume and pressure: 423 attempts, 23 sacks, and only 209 rushing yards capped his ceiling, dropping him to QB20 at 208.5 points.

The efficiency was real, but the offense leaned conservative, completing passes barely six air yards downfield. With Nico Collins anchoring the receivers and the line improving, a healthier 16-17 games should push him back toward the 24-touchdown, 3,900-yard range. Going outside the top-22 quarterbacks off the board, Stroud is a sturdy late-round streamer with genuine QB1 upside if the volume returns.

9997.name: Zay Flowers
9997.headline: Flowers Blooms Into a Locked-In WR1 Target
9997.body:
Flowers graded out as our WR6 in 2025, and the workload backs it up: a 29% target share, a 0.681 WOPR, and 118 looks across all 17 games. He converted that into 86 grabs for 1,211 yards on a 72.9% catch rate, while his 2.75 yards per route run and 0.401 EPA per play put him among the most efficient receivers in football. His 57.6% success rate confirms this wasn't volume noise.

The one cap is the end zone. Just 11 red zone targets and 5 end zone looks held him to five scores, and he's never topped that mark. That keeps the ceiling tethered to Baltimore's run-leaning script and Lamar Jackson's touchdown rate. But the route volume, the air-yards share north of 35%, and a third straight rising target tree make him a steady early-round WR2 with genuine WR1 weeks baked in.

10210.name: Cameron Latu
10210.headline: Buried fourth tight end with special-teams role only
10210.body:
Latu enters 2026 as the fourth option on Philadelphia's tight end depth chart, which tells you everything about his fantasy floor: there isn't one. His value lives almost entirely on special teams, not in the passing game, and expert rankers reflect that with a TE158 consensus tag and rostership hovering near zero percent. With no 2026 projections on the board and a season stat line still reading zeros across the board, there is simply no production case to make here.

A stinger has already kept him out of recent practice work, adding another hurdle for a player who needs injuries ahead of him just to sniff snaps. The lone bright spot is a soft tight end schedule that grades among the easiest in the league, but matchups mean nothing without a role. Leave him on the wire until meaningful targets materialize, which may never come.

10212.name: Josh Whyle
10212.headline: Buried behind Musgrave with no route share to dream on
10212.body:
Whyle's profile peaked in 2024 with Tennessee, when he turned 37 targets into a 7.3% target share across 15 games, posting 28 catches for 248 yards, 1.33 yards per route run, and 32.8 fantasy points on a healthy 0.215 EPA per play. That hinted at a usable streaming floor. The move to Green Bay erased it. In 2025 he played just six games, and despite a respectable 39.9% snap rate his route participation cratered to 14.7%, yielding six targets, a 1.3% target share, and only 36 yards. The snaps are blocking reps, not passing-game volume.

The path forward is blocked too. He sits behind Luke Musgrave on the depth chart and carries an afterthought TE109 expert rank with essentially no roster ownership. The 6-foot-7 frame and a career red-zone history keep him on touchdown-vulture watch, but without a meaningful uptick in routes he has no standalone fantasy value in 2026.

10213.name: Tre Tucker
10213.headline: Field-stretching ascent makes him a real WR3
10213.body:
Tuckers third year was a genuine breakout: he climbed to WR37 with 104.7 fantasy points, locking down a near-every-down role at a 94.8 percent snap share and 96.5 route share. His target share jumped to 18.6 percent with a 0.483 WOPR and a massive 29.1 percent air-yards share, turning 57 catches into 696 yards and five scores. The vertical juice is the draw, a 16.4 air-yards-per-reception mark, a 61-yard long, and a leap in yards per route run from 0.87 in 2024 to 1.25.

The risk is efficiency volatility: a 62 percent catch rate and a 44.6 percent success rate flag the boom-bust profile of a deep threat, and his 0.237 EPA per play, while solid, rests on big plays rather than steady chains. As the Raiders clear alpha in target share he carries weekly WR3 value with spike-week ceiling, especially in best-ball and big-play formats. Draft him as a flex with WR2 upside if the volume holds.

10214.name: Davis Allen
10214.headline: Touchdown-dependent depth piece buried in a crowded room
10214.body:
Allen's 2025 line tells the whole story: 24 grabs for 208 yards and three scores on a 50.3% snap share, finishing TE50 with just 38.8 fantasy points. He ran routes on barely 37% of dropbacks, drew a 5.7% target share, and posted a feeble 0.143 targets per route run with 3.94 air yards per look. The efficiency markers were respectable for the volume he saw -- a 0.164 EPA per play and 54.5% success rate, plus seven red-zone targets that fueled the touchdowns -- but the WOPR of 0.102 underlines how little of the passing pie reaches him.

The path forward is blocked. He sits behind Higbee, Parkinson, rookie Ferguson, and Klare on the Rams' tight end ladder, and without an injury ahead of him the snaps simply aren't there. Consensus rankings near TE87 reflect that reality. He's a touchdown-or-bust dart with no floor -- leave him on waivers in standard formats.

10216.name: Kenny McIntosh
10216.headline: ACL recovery and a crowded depth chart bury his 2026 value
10216.body:
There's real on-field juice buried in McIntosh's tiny sample. Across 11 games in 2024 he turned 31 carries into 172 yards at a robust 5.55 per pop, broke off a 25-yard run, and posted a 12.9% explosive-run rate while forcing a broken tackle every 10.3 attempts. His 3.87 yards before contact hint at decent blocking and burst, and a positive 0.031 EPA per play with zero fumbles shows he didn't waste touches. The problem was volume: a 7.5% snap share, four targets, and zero touchdowns over those 19.4 fantasy points.

Now a torn ACL wipes out his runway. He's working back from the injury while Seattle reshapes the room around Jadarian Price, Emanuel Wilson and George Holani, with Charbonnet also rehabbing and Walker gone. That stacks at least three backs ahead of him entering the year. With an RB-189 expert rank and no usable ADP, he's a watch-list dynasty stash, not a draftable asset.

10218.name: Xavier Hutchinson
10218.headline: Texans depth receiver breaks out as efficient flex sleeper
10218.body:
Hutchinson finally carved a role in 2025: 16 games, a 57.5 percent snap share, 382 routes, and 57 targets for 35 catches, 428 yards and three touchdowns, good for WR67 and 63 fantasy points. The leap from his 2024 line (26 targets, 117 yards, WR132) is dramatic, and the efficiency backs it—his 0.226 EPA per play, 54.4 percent success rate, 1.12 yards per route run, and a tidy 3.5 percent drop rate all marked career bests for the former 6th-round Iowa State pick.

The ceiling is capped by opportunity rather than play. A 10.3 percent target share and 0.24 WOPR place him third in the Houston pecking order, and his 8.06 air yards before the catch show a possession role rather than a downfield one. He is a solid bet to repeat as a reliable depth piece, but standalone WR3 numbers require an injury ahead of him to unlock more volume. For 2026, draft him as a late-round dart and bye-week flex with clear contingency upside, not a weekly starter.

10219.name: Chris Rodriguez
10219.headline: Short-yardage hammer flashes efficiency, but workload remains capped
10219.body:
Rodriguez built a real case in 2025: 500 rushing yards on 112 carries (4.46 per attempt), six touchdowns, and a goal-line specialty role with 14 carries inside the five and 34 red-zone opportunities across just 12 games. The contact balance is the selling point. He forced 13 broken tackles, churned 2.97 yards after contact per attempt, and popped an explosive run rate of 11.6 percent with a 48-yard long. At a 31.2 percent snap share, his slightly positive EPA per play (0.025) and clean ledger (zero fumbles) marked him as a trustworthy finisher.

The ceiling is the catch. He's a non-factor in the passing game (four targets, 0.3 yards per route run), which caps him as a two-down grinder rather than a bell cow. A new home in Jacksonville resets the depth chart, so his fantasy value hinges on whether he wins the early-down and goal-line snaps that fueled his touchdown equity. Bench stash with standalone touchdown upside.

10222.name: Jayden Reed
10222.headline: Post-hype slot ace primed for a healthy bounce-back
10222.body:
Reed's profile screams bounce-back. Across 2023-24 he ran a real route load (370-plus routes both years), posting a 0.561 EPA per play and a 54.7% success rate in 2024 while finishing WR32 on 142 fantasy points. He's a target magnet from the slot, with a 16-17% target share both seasons and a healthy 2.3 yards per route run in 2024. A 2025 wrecked by injury limited him to five games, but the underlying efficiency held: 86.4% catch rate, 0.525 EPA per play, 50% success rate on a tiny 22-target sample.

The path forward is clean. Green Bay let Romeo Doubs and Dontayvion Wicks walk, thinning the room and reaffirming Reed with a three-year, $50.25M extension. He still competes with Matthew Golden and Christian Watson for volume, but the slot snaps are his. With projections near 61 catches and 730 yards, he's a draftable value going outside the top 80.

10223.name: Eric Gray
10223.headline: Buried depth back with no path to fantasy snaps
10223.body:
Gray remains a deep-roster afterthought, and the underlying numbers explain why the Giants keep him glued to the bench. Across his last meaningful action he played just 9.5% of snaps, turned 14 carries into 31 yards (2.21 per tote) with a longest gain of six, and posted a brutal -0.49 EPA per play. He broke zero tackles, generated no runs of even 10-plus yards, and managed only one yard after contact per attempt. The receiving profile is thin too: 10 grabs for 82 yards, a 2% target share, and no touchdowns of any kind.

There's nothing in the data suggesting a breakout is coming. He carries no real draft value, sits deep in dynasty pecking orders, and offers production only if injuries vault him into a role he hasn't earned. Until he commands consistent offensive snaps, treat him as a watch-list name rather than a draftable asset.

10225.name: Jonathan Mingo
10225.headline: Mingo's collapse leaves nothing for fantasy to chase
10225.body:
The arrow points straight down. Mingo's positional finish has cratered from 76th in 2023 to 124th in 2024 to 194th this past season, when his role evaporated to a 20.7% snap share across just five games. He drew five targets total, an 0.8% team share, and converted them into one catch for 25 yards. His -0.84 EPA per play and 20% success rate weren't small-sample noise so much as confirmation of a 2024 that already bottomed out at a 28.6% success rate.

The context is just as bleak. He sits at the back of a crowded Dallas receiver room with no path to meaningful snaps, and a restructured deal stripping his workout bonus signals how the team values him. He's effectively unrostered league-wide and projects for minimal volume. There is no reason to draft him in any standard format.

10226.name: Andrei Iosivas
10226.headline: Steady WR3 snaps, but the touchdowns dried up
10226.body:
Iosivas locked down the No. 3 role in 2025, playing 76.2% of snaps across 15 games and running a route on nearly 80% of dropbacks. The volume was real but capped: a 9.5% target share, 58 looks, 0.81 yards per route, and a 13.18 average produced just 33-435-2. His 0.201 EPA per play and 50% success rate flag an efficient complementary piece, yet that WR66 finish reflects a clear ceiling behind Cincinnati's top two.

The alarming dip is in the end zone. After six scores on 10 red-zone and 10 end-zone targets in 2024, those high-value looks collapsed to seven and four, dragging his touchdowns to two. With Chase and Higgins commanding the bulk of work, Iosivas remains a snap-secure but low-equity bench stash whose weekly value spikes only when an injury opens targets ahead of him.

10227.name: Payne Durham
10227.headline: Buried Bucs blocker offers nothing for redraft
10227.body:
Durham has never carved out a receiving role, and 2025 was his quietest yet: one catch on four targets for 11 yards across eight games, just 1.1 fantasy points and a TE109 rank on a 31.8 percent snap share. Even his modest 2024 ceiling, 11 grabs for 115 yards and two scores worth 23.5 points at TE61, came on only a 2.5 percent target share. The arrow points down, with route share sliding from 28 to 17 percent and a 0.7 percent target share that signals pure blocking duty.

The 2023 fifth-round Purdue pick is a competent in-line blocker, but Tampa Bay's passing game flows elsewhere and his minus-0.299 EPA per play on a tiny sample offers nothing for fantasy. With no volume and no red-zone equity beyond the occasional look, there is zero startable value here. He is a waiver-wire afterthought in every 2026 format, including deep dynasty.

10228.name: Charlie Jones
10228.headline: Special-teamer with no offensive runway in Cincinnati
10228.body:
Three seasons in, Jones still hasn't carved out an offensive role, and the trend is going the wrong direction. He played 14 games in 2025 but logged just 11 routes on a 2.1% snap share, drawing a single target for a 0.2% target share and a brutal -0.534 EPA per play. The 2024 line was nearly identical: 16 routes, one catch for five yards across seven appearances. His career-best workload remains 2023, when he ran 30 routes and turned nine targets into 64 yards at 2.13 yards per route run.

That 2023 efficiency hints at usable hands, but it came on a sample too thin to project anything. An ankle issue ended his 2025 early, and he spent the bulk of the year on special teams behind a crowded Bengals receiver room. Until injuries or departures clear a path, Jones is a roster-clogging zero with no draftable value in any format.

10229.name: Rashee Rice
10229.headline: Elite efficiency meets availability risk, draft the upside
10229.body:
When healthy, Rice is one of the NFL's most efficient receivers. Across eight 2025 games he posted a 0.192 EPA per play, a 2.32 yards-per-route mark and 7.81 yards after the catch per reception, turning a 14.2 percent target share into 53 catches for 571 yards and five scores (97.1 points despite the missed time). His red-zone gravity is elite, with 18 red-zone and 14 end-zone targets in half a season. That mirrors his 2023 breakout (79-938-7, WR28 in 16 games) and the absurd 0.568 EPA flash over four 2024 games.

The catch is availability: the 2023 second-rounder (Pick 55, SMU) has played just 8, 4 and 16 games the last three years, and any setback would gut the volume that makes him a top-15 ceiling in the Mahomes offense. With a clear path to alpha targets in Kansas City, he is a high-upside WR2 worth a slight discount, a weekly starter when active. Draft a contingency and accept the boom-bust games-played risk.

10231.name: Elijah Higgins
10231.headline: McBride's Shadow Keeps Higgins A Deep-League Lottery Ticket
10231.body:
Higgins quietly logged his most productive season in 2025, suiting up for all 17 games and turning 37 targets into 30 grabs for 301 yards while securing 81.1% of his looks and posting a clean zero-drop sheet. The volume stayed thin, though: a 6% target share, 0.128 targets per route, and 1.04 yards per route run dragged his EPA per play to a flat 0.039 and capped him as the TE38 in fantasy scoring. The encouraging wrinkle is his 6.97 yards after the catch per reception and a 16.7% avoided-tackle rate, signaling he can create when fed.

The ceiling problem is the man in front of him. Higgins remains the clear backup behind Trey McBride, and as long as the starter stays upright there simply aren't enough routes to matter. He's a touchdown-light, low-floor name buried near the end of draft boards. Stash him only in the deepest formats as injury insurance.

10232.name: Michael Wilson
10232.headline: Wilson's 2025 leap leans hard on Harrison's absences
10232.body:
Wilson's 2025 was a genuine ascension on paper: 17 games, 126 targets, a 20.4% target share and a 0.532 WOPR that vaulted him to WR17 with 142.6 fantasy points. The efficiency backs it up, with 1,006 yards, a 1.68 yards-per-route-run mark, 0.212 EPA per play and a 50.8% success rate. He commanded volume too, drawing a 32.2% air-yards share and 16 red-zone looks while converting seven scores and dropping just one of 79 catchable balls.

The catch is context. Much of that production stacked up when Arizona's top option was sidelined, and across 2023-2024 his target shares sat at 10.7% and 13.6% with far thinner output. A healthy pecking order, plus McBride and a new ground-game investment, caps his ceiling. The 11.92 air yards per target and downfield role keep him relevant, but treat the WR17 finish as a soft projection, not a floor, and draft accordingly.

10234.name: Derius Davis
10234.headline: Return specialist with no offensive path in a crowded room
10234.body:
Davis remains a special-teams piece first, and his receiving line keeps shrinking. After a modest 2024 (66 routes, 17 targets, 13 catches, 112 yards, two scores, plus a 1.7 yards-per-route-run flash and 0.319 EPA per play), 2025 cratered to three targets across 11 games, two grabs for 10 yards, 11.1% snaps, and a brutal minus-0.342 EPA per play with a 0% success rate. That is not a profile trending toward offensive snaps.

The depth chart makes the case airtight: Ladd McConkey, Quentin Johnston, Tre Harris, and rookie competition stand ahead, leaving Davis fighting for scraps. His only real value lives in the return game, where he logged punt and kickoff duties in 2025, and even that is threatened by Keaton Mitchell on kickoffs. Watch training camp, but in standard and most deep formats he holds no fantasy weight.

10235.name: Roschon Johnson
10235.headline: Buried Behind Monangai With No Path To Volume
10235.body:
Johnson's trajectory has gone the wrong direction every season. After a promising rookie year (81 carries, 4.35 yards per clip, a 34-catch receiving role and an RB48 finish), his usage cratered to a 2.73 mark on 55 totes in 2024, then collapsed entirely in 2025: six games, two carries, 1.7 fantasy points and a rank of 128. The 8.5 yards-per-carry and 0.679 EPA on that microscopic sample mean nothing without volume.

The job he was hyped to inherit went to seventh-round rookie Kyle Monangai, who locked down the clear backfield No. 2 while Johnson battled injuries. That leaves Johnson a distant third option with no realistic route to touches behind an entrenched, younger back. His goal-line work was the only fantasy hook (six rushing scores in 2024), and even that has dried up. He is waiver-wire depth at best and undraftable in standard formats this year.

10236.name: Dalton Kincaid
10236.headline: Elite Efficiency, Capped Volume: Kincaid's Frustrating Middle Ground
10236.body:
Kincaid was wildly efficient in his 12 games last season, posting 0.823 EPA per play and a 63.3% success rate while finishing TE16. On just 49 targets he caught 39 balls (79.6%) for 571 yards at 14.64 per grab, and his 2.97 yards per route run jumped from 1.68 the year prior. Five touchdowns and eight red-zone looks show the scoring chops are real when he's healthy and on the field.

The problem is the snaps: a 37.4% rate and 10.2% target share that both slid from 2024. With Knox and a rookie eating into the rotation, a target-hungry receiver room, and a knee Buffalo plans to manage, volume is the ceiling-killer here. The per-route metrics scream league-winner, but until the route share climbs back toward 50%, Kincaid is a high-upside TE2 you draft for spikes, not weekly floor.

10444.name: Cedric Tillman
10444.headline: Deep-field flier whose numbers keep saying no
10444.body:
Tillman's 2025 line is a flashing warning light: a 7.5% target share, just 0.13 targets per route, and a 0.90 yards-per-route-run mark that ranks him near WR102 in our model. He converted only 53.8% of those looks into catches for 270 yards and two scores across 12 games, with a 0.198 WOPR that screams complementary, not featured. The lone positive is a 0.167 EPA per play, his best in three years, but a 41% success rate undercuts any efficiency case.

The trajectory hasn't budged either, with his target share parked between 7.4% and 7.7% every season since 2023. A crowded Cleveland room only got tighter after two drafted rookies, capping his snap and air-yards ceiling further. Treat him as a touchdown-dependent dart throw in the deepest formats, nothing more, until a clear path to volume actually opens.

10859.name: Sam LaPorta
10859.headline: Injury-shortened 2025 dents elite TE, still a top-five bet
10859.body:
LaPorta's 2025 was a tale of missed time: nine games, a TE23 finish, and just 66.9 fantasy points after he ranked TE6 (114.6) in 2024 and TE5 (153.3) as a rookie. The volume cratered with the absence, his target share sliding to 8.9 percent and WOPR to 0.186 from 15.9 percent and 0.355 the year prior. The efficiency, though, was loud: a position-best 0.743 EPA per play, a 67.3 percent success rate, 81.6 percent catch rate, and zero drops on 40 catches.

When healthy, LaPorta is a locked-in starter. He played a 90.7 percent snap share when active, and Detroit funnels red-zone work his way, shown by 20 red-zone targets and seven scores in 2024. The 12.22 yards per reception and 6.85 yards after the catch confirm the playmaking remains. At 24 with three years of TE5-or-better-rate production, the only real risk is health. Draft him as a weekly TE1 with top-five upside the moment he is back to a full slate.

10867.name: Jake Bobo
10867.headline: Buried depth piece whose 2025 footprint shrank to nothing
10867.body:
Bobo's trajectory is moving the wrong direction. After flashing in 2023 with 19 grabs, 196 yards, two scores and a healthy 1.36 yards per route on a 23.6% route share, his role cratered to 144 routes and 13 catches in 2024, then collapsed entirely in 2025: five games, a 17% snap rate, 35 routes, and just two targets for 20 yards. A target share of 0.4% and 0.009 WOPR are the numbers of a player barely on the field. The lone bright spot is efficiency on a tiny sample, with a 100% catch rate and 0.57 EPA per play.

Seattle re-signed him on a modest two-year deal after matching an outside offer sheet, but he projects as the fourth or fifth receiver behind Jaxon Smith-Njigba, Cooper Kupp, Rashid Shaheed and Tory Horton. With essentially no path to volume, he stays off draft boards and belongs only on deep-league watch lists if injuries open snaps.

10871.name: Luke Schoonmaker
10871.headline: Buried Behind Ferguson, Schoonmaker Stays Off The Radar
10871.body:
Schoonmaker's 2025 line tells the whole story: 14 games, a 32.9% snap rate, and just 23 targets for a 3.8% share that left him 62nd at the position. He ran only 167 routes, turning them into 14 catches for 132 yards and zero touchdowns at a thin 0.79 yards per route. The 0.158 EPA per play and 43.5% success rate were a clear step back from 2024, when his 0.186 targets per route, 1.24 YPRR, and 5.8% share hinted at more.

The efficiency dip matters because the volume isn't coming. As long as Jake Ferguson owns the position, Schoonmaker is a blocking-tier afterthought drafted around TE67, well outside startable range. He's a Ferguson-injury handcuff and nothing more right now. Until a real route share materializes, there's no reason to roster him in any standard format heading into 2026.

11068.name: Mitch Tinsley
11068.headline: A Deep-Ball Flier With Red-Zone Equity
11068.body:
Tinsley's 2025 line reads like a true field-stretcher: 14.75 air yards per target and a massive 44.25 air yards per reception fed a 0.11 EPA per play across his 24 looks. The problem was conversion. A 33.3% catch rate and matching 33.3% success rate left him at just 116 yards and 0.79 yards per route run, and his WR140 finish reflects a profile that lived on volatile downfield shots rather than reliable separation. Eight catches over eight games with one drop is thin, even by deep-threat standards.

The sliver of intrigue is usage near the goal line. Five red-zone targets and one end-zone look turned into two touchdowns and 23.6 fantasy points, so the touchdown equity is real if his snap share climbs from a modest 24.6%. With only a 3.9% target share and a 0.112 WOPR, Tinsley remains a speculative depth piece who needs an opportunity bump to matter in fantasy lineups this season.

11082.name: Ben Sims
11082.headline: Depth tight end with no measurable fantasy footprint
11082.body:
Sims has been a body in the tight end room, not a pass-catcher, and the tracking bears it out. Across 2023-2025 he never cleared a 21.3% snap share, ran 35 routes in three 2025 games, and posted a 6.4% route rate with a 1.3% target share. His 0.86 yards per route run and three catches for 30 yards last season are the kind of numbers that don't move fantasy lineups. Eleven career receptions in three years tell the whole story.

A one-year deal in Miami slots him behind the depth chart as a blocking-first reserve, and a projected line near 21 catches for 200 yards and a touchdown would actually be a career year by a wide margin. Ranked outside the top 90 at the position, he carries no real draft value and stays on waiver wires until injuries force snaps his way.

11113.name: Joel Wilson
11113.headline: Reserve/future flier with no fantasy footprint yet
11113.body:
Wilson enters 2026 as a roster-edge tight end after inking a reserve/future deal with Tennessee, and the profile reflects exactly that standing: he logged zero game action, carries no measurable projections, and sits at essentially 0% ownership across redraft formats. There is no production to build a case on and no defined role on the depth chart, which makes him a name to monitor rather than a piece to draft.

Until he earns snaps in camp and climbs into the rotation, treat him as a watch-list deep stash only. The schedule offers no real lift either, as Tennessee's tight ends grade out middle-of-the-pack for positional matchups. For now, Wilson is purely a training-camp story whose value hinges entirely on carving out a job, so leave him on the waiver wire and revisit only if the depth chart opens up.

11168.name: Xavier Smith
11168.headline: Explosive Rams gadget receiver is a boom-bust dart
11168.body:
Smith’s 2025 jump came on tiny volume but eye-popping efficiency: a 21.4 percent snap share and 149 routes yielded 24 targets, 18 catches and 303 yards at a sterling 16.83 yards per reception. The 2.03 yards per route run and a 0.914 EPA per play are elite per-route marks, fueled by a deep role—16.88 air yards per target, four catches of 30-plus and two of 40-plus—after just 6 yards in 2024. He still finished only WR92 with 30.3 fantasy points because the target tree is so thin.

That is the whole story: spectacular rates, negligible volume. With a 4.1 percent target share and zero touchdowns despite the big plays, Smith is entirely matchup- and variance-dependent in a crowded Los Angeles receiver room. The deep-ball role gives him real spike-week potential if a target ahead misses time, but the 27-year-old offers no reliable floor. He is a best-ball and deep-league dart only, not a standard redraft starter.

11199.name: Emari Demercado
11199.headline: Big-play handcuff buried on Kansas City's depth chart
11199.body:
Demercado's 2025 line tells a familiar story: explosive in flashes, thin on volume. On just 44 carries he ripped off 312 yards at 7.09 per tote, with a 20.5% explosive-run rate, a 71-yard long, and nine runs of 10-plus. But the negatives matter for projection — a minus-0.133 EPA per play, zero rushing touchdowns, and a 23.2% snap share confirm he only mattered when Arizona's backfield got hurt. He added 13 grabs for 101 yards and a score as a passing-down outlet.

Now in Kansas City, he profiles as a deep-bench dart at best, sitting third behind Kenneth Walker III with Emmett Johnson and Brashard Smith crowding the reserve reps. ADP near pick 265 and roughly 1% rostership reflect that reality. His path is purely contingent: a Walker injury would unlock standalone value, much like 2023-25 in Arizona. Until then, monitor camp and stash only in the deepest formats.

11201.name: Blake Whiteheart
11201.headline: Whiteheart is a snap-count blocker, not a fantasy bet
11201.body:
The numbers leave little room for optimism. Across nine games in 2025, Whiteheart ran just 109 routes on an 18% route share, drawing six targets for a 1.1% target share and converting only two of them into four total yards. His -0.621 EPA per play and 0% success rate were among the worst at the position, and a fumble on such limited volume only deepens the concern. The 2024 sample was thinner but cleaner: six grabs for 51 yards and a red-zone touchdown on a 44.4% success rate.

Nothing here points to a usable role. With a sub-24% snap rate and essentially zero air-yards involvement, he profiles as a blocking specialist rather than a receiving threat. Cleveland's tight end pecking order keeps him buried, and his depressed ranking reflects it. Leave him on the wire in every format and revisit only if injuries open real route volume ahead of him.

11237.name: Jacob Saylors
11237.headline: Special-teams body with no offensive runway in Detroit
11237.body:
Saylors logged 11 games in 2025 but his 80% snap rate came almost entirely on special teams, not the backfield. His offensive footprint was a rounding error: two carries for 11 yards, zero targets, and a single broken tackle, which added up to 1.1 fantasy points on the year. The 5.5 yards per carry and 3.0 yards after contact per attempt are fun to cite, but on a sample of two rushes they tell you nothing. A minus-0.529 EPA per play underlines how little the offense trusted him with the ball.

Nothing about his 2026 standing has changed. He sits buried on a crowded depth chart, is essentially undrafted in redraft formats, and carries no projected volume worth chasing. The favorable schedule means nothing without touches. He is a roster-clogging name to ignore in every standard league unless an injury cascade hands him real snaps.

11256.name: Tyson Bagent
11256.headline: Bagent stays a clipboard handcuff behind Caleb Williams
11256.body:
Bagent's 2025 line is pure garbage time: two appearances, four attempts, 47 yards and 2.6 fantasy points. The peripherals flatter him in that tiny sample, a 0.322 EPA per play with a plus-9.6 CPOE, but four throws tell you nothing actionable. You have to rewind to his 2023 rookie spot start run for real volume, and that stretch was rough, 143 attempts at a minus-0.119 EPA per play with six picks against three scores while finishing the QB44 in points.

With Caleb Williams entrenched as the starter, Bagent stays glued to the bench, going essentially unrostered with no draft cost attached. The only path to relevance is a Williams injury, which could unlock back-end QB2 value, maybe a flash of QB1 upside, inside Ben Johnson's offense. Until then he is a pure contingency stash, not a draftable asset in any standard format.

11292.name: Tommy DeVito
11292.headline: Career clipboard arm, irrelevant outside emergency two-QB
11292.body:
DeVito remains a deep backup with a thin, inefficient body of work. His 2024 Giants cameo was two games, 31-of-44 for 257 yards, zero touchdowns and zero picks, a tidy 70.5 completion mark and 4.7 CPOE but a -0.185 EPA per play and 36.2 percent success rate that scream conservative dink-and-dunk (4.5 completed air yards per completion, just 2 percent deep attempts). His fuller 2023 sample, nine games, 1,101 yards, eight touchdowns and a -0.209 EPA per play with a brutal 37 sacks taken, confirmed a checkdown profile that does not move the needle.

Now buried behind Drake Maye on the Patriots depth chart, DeVito has no path to snaps barring injury, and even as a starter he offers little, modest rushing (36 carries, 195 yards, one score in 2023) cannot offset the lack of passing volume or downfield juice. He is strictly a name to ignore in redraft and a non-factor in superflex unless Maye misses time. Waiver wire, full stop.

11299.name: Zavier Scott
11299.headline: Vikings change-of-pace back stuck behind crowded depth
11299.body:
A 2023 UDFA out of Maine, Scott carved out a situational role in Minnesota during 2025 but the numbers cap his ceiling. Across 11 games on a thin 10.2 percent snap share he turned 32 carries into just 114 yards, a punchless 3.56 per attempt with a -0.203 EPA per play and a longest run of only 11 yards. His value tilts to the passing game: 16 targets, 14 catches for 98 yards and a touchdown, a healthy 1.53 yards per route run and 105 yards after the catch on a 3.4 percent target share. Two fumbles on limited touches are a red flag for a back fighting for snaps.

The 25.2 fantasy points and rank of 76 reflect a deep-bench role, not a usable one. Behind a committee of higher-pedigree options, Scott profiles as a passing-down change-of-pace at best, and his sub-4.0 yards per carry gives him no early-down equity. The receiving chops keep him alive in deeper dynasty PPR formats, but for 2026 redraft he is a waiver-wire afterthought barring an injury bump.

11320.name: Nikko Remigio
11320.headline: Deep Chiefs depth piece with no fantasy role
11320.body:
Remigio has been on the field for three NFL seasons in name only, logging tiny snap shares behind Kansas City`s established pass catchers. The 2025 line is a rounding error: 14 games but a 3.3 percent snap share, eight routes, one target, one catch for 21 yards and 3.2 fantasy points. His 2024 was similarly empty, five games and a 20.6 percent snap rate that produced six targets and two grabs for 48 yards. The advanced metrics, however flashy on a one-catch sample, mean nothing at this volume.

Buried behind Xavier Worthy and the rest of the Chiefs` receiver and tight end depth, plus his return-game role, Remigio has no realistic path to offensive targets in 2026. There is simply no scenario short of a cascade of injuries in which he becomes fantasy-relevant. He is a clear waiver-wire ignore in all redraft formats and carries no dynasty value beyond a camp-body roster spot. Do not draft him in any league.

11370.name: Chris Brooks
11370.headline: Depth-chart afterthought whose snaps and efficiency are both fading
11370.body:
Brooks has spent three seasons as a low-volume rotational back, and the 2025 tape shows the role shrinking rather than growing. He played 17 games but logged just a 20.1% snap share, turning 27 carries into 106 yards at 3.93 a pop with zero touchdowns, and his -0.136 EPA per play was the worst mark of his career outside his 2023 cameo. The burst that flashed earlier is gone: he managed one broken tackle on the season and just 1.85 yards after contact per attempt, down sharply from 2.44 in 2024.

The receiving profile offers the only sliver of upside, with 13 grabs on 17 targets for 91 yards, but a 3.7% target share doesn't move the needle. Until he climbs Green Bay's backfield pecking order, there's no path to fantasy relevance. He's a roster-clogging dart throw at best and belongs on the waiver wire in every format heading into 2026.

11433.name: Nate Adkins
11433.headline: Denver's Blocking Tight End Stays Off the Radar
11433.body:
Adkins is a fantasy non-factor, and our numbers spell out why. His 2025 campaign was a step backward: six games, a 32.8% snap share, and just 11 targets that produced a 1.9% target share, six catches, 48 yards and one score. The efficiency cratered, too, as he posted a minus-0.057 EPA per play, a 36.4% success rate, and a brutal 27.3% drop rate while ranking 85th at the position. Even his 0.72 yards per route run flagged him as a blocker first.

The one tease came in 2024, when 14 receptions on 0.73 YPRR carried a 73.3% success rate, a 0.605 EPA, three touchdowns and zero drops. That spike was tiny and tough to bank on. A one-year extension keeps him in Denver, but the role is clear: in-line blocker, not a pass-game asset. Leave him on waivers in all formats this season.

11435.name: Emanuel Wilson
11435.headline: Volatile change-of-pace flier in a crowded Seattle backfield
11435.body:
Wilson's 2025 tape in Green Bay tells the story of a useful committee piece rather than a lead back. Across 17 games he turned 125 carries into 496 yards at just 3.97 per rush, with a negative -0.055 EPA per play and a meager 9.6% explosive-run rate. The bigger flag is his work as a passing-down option: only 15 receptions on 17 targets, a 3.7% target share, and 0.65 yards per route run. He forced 7 broken tackles and ground out 2.17 yards after contact per attempt, so the toughness is real, but the efficiency and creation just aren't.

His new home complicates everything. Seattle paid him for a role, then drafted Jadarian Price in the first round and still has Zach Charbonnet working back from an ACL tear. Wilson profiles as an early-season flex while Price acclimates, with an ADP near pick 217 (RB66) reflecting the murk. Modest projections around 290 rushing yards make him a speculative late-round dart, not a foundation.

11439.name: Jaleel McLaughlin
11439.headline: Efficient Spark Plug Buried On Denver's Crowded Depth Chart
11439.body:
McLaughlin keeps producing whenever he touches the ball, he just rarely gets the chance. Across eight 2025 games he turned 37 carries into 187 yards at a robust 5.05 per tote, popping a 13.5% explosive-run rate while playing only 16% of snaps and finishing RB75. That tracks his career: a 5.39 mark and RB47 finish back in 2023, plus 4.39 yards a clip over a full 16-game 2024 slate. The juice is real even if the volume never is.

The problem is opportunity, not talent. A slightly negative EPA per play (-0.014) and a 1% target share reflect a clear fourth-back role behind Dobbins, Harvey, and Coleman in Sean Payton's loaded backfield. Consensus pegs him outside the top 90 backs, and that's fair until an injury opens the door. Stash him only in deep dynasty formats as a contingency-value insurance piece, nothing more.

11474.name: Dylan Drummond
11474.headline: Deep on the depth chart, off the radar
11474.body:
Drummond's 2025 rookie sample reads like a developmental redshirt. Across six games he ran just 71 routes for a 12.5% route share, drawing 11 targets and a 2.1% target share while playing only 21% of snaps. The efficiency underneath was alarming: a minus-0.556 EPA per play, a 36.4% success rate, and a brutal 0.59 yards per route run. He turned seven catches into 42 yards, never finding the end zone despite three red-zone looks.

The path forward is opportunity, not production. Atlanta's receiver room dealt with injuries, yet Drummond still couldn't carve out a defined role, and he projects as a back-end depth piece entering 2026. With essentially no ADP and zero roster relevance, he's a watch-list name at best. Until he earns real snaps and route volume, there's nothing here to draft in any format.

11557.name: Joe Milton
11557.headline: Milton's flashes are real, but the runway isn't
11557.body:
The arm talent shows up in the data, but the sample is microscopic. Across four Dallas appearances in 2025, Milton threw for 183 yards on 24 attempts with a 62.5% completion rate, one touchdown against two picks, and a -0.05 EPA per play paired with a 44.8% success rate. His 26.1% bad-throw rate flagged the rough edges, though a 1.6 CPOE and five carries for 50 rushing yards hinted at a higher ceiling. The 2024 New England tape was the dream: 22-of-29 for 241 yards, a 92.6% on-target rate, 17.1 CPOE, and 19.2 fantasy points in one start.

That upside collides with reality in Dallas. Milton is locked in a QB2 fight with Sam Howell behind a healthy Dak Prescott, leaving no clear path to snaps. With an overall ADP near 383 and QB54 positioning, he's a pure deep-dynasty stash, not a 2026 fantasy asset.

11559.name: Michael Penix
11559.headline: Arm Talent Is Real, Efficiency Has to Catch Up
11559.body:
Penix enters his first full season as Atlanta's starter carrying a thin but loud nine-game sample. He finished QB29 in 2025 with 1,982 yards, nine touchdowns and just three picks across nine starts, posting a modest 0.016 EPA per play and a 44.8% success rate. The accuracy still lags: a 60.1% completion rate, minus-3.6 CPOE and a 24% bad-throw rate say his deep-shot profile (12.1% of attempts) outran his ball placement.

The upside lives in the arm. He stacked 26 throws of 20-plus yards and a 69-yard long despite the truncated workload, and his sturdier 2024 cameo flashed a 0.131 EPA per play. With 13 sacks and four fumbles, ball security and protection are the swing factors. Project a full 17, and the volume alone lifts him into low-end QB2 territory with weekly ceiling games baked in. A leap in efficiency is the difference between streamer and starter.

11560.name: Caleb Williams
11560.headline: The Year Two Leap Is Real
11560.body:
Williams turned a shaky rookie campaign into a genuine QB1 breakout, climbing to a seventh-place finish on 316.2 fantasy points across 17 games. The efficiency jump tells the story: his EPA per play flipped from minus-0.057 to a positive 0.074, success rate rose to 43.8 percent, and his touchdown total nearly tripled the value of his rookie year at 27 scores against just seven picks for a franchise-record 3,942 yards. Crucially, sacks collapsed from 68 to 24 as the pocket finally held.

The dual-threat element keeps the floor sturdy, with 383 rushing yards and three scores layered on top of that passing production. A 58.1 percent completion rate and a 13.9 percent deep-attempt mark leave obvious room to grow, but with an elite play-caller and a loaded receiving room around him, betting on another step forward is the easy call here. Draft him as a high-upside QB1.

11562.name: Spencer Rattler
11562.headline: Sophomore leap is real, but a low-end QB2
11562.body:
Rattler's second season showed legitimate growth. Across nine starts he completed 67.7 percent for 1,586 yards, eight touchdowns and five picks, posting a positive 2.4 CPOE after a brutal -9.1 as a rookie. His success rate climbed from 38.1 to 46.4 percent and his EPA per play improved from -0.262 to -0.087, while his bad-throw rate fell from 18.2 to 11 percent. He added 167 rushing yards on 31 carries, and his 98.1 fantasy points in nine games extrapolate to a respectable full-season pace.

The caveats are real: the EPA is still negative, he took 18 sacks behind a leaky line (17.5 percent pressure), and he fumbled four times. With Kellen Moore's offense and a healthier receiving corps, Rattler profiles as the clear 2026 starter in New Orleans, but the modest deep-ball volume (12.4 percent) and middling supporting cast cap the ceiling. He is a streaming QB2 and superflex depth, not a weekly starter in one-quarterback leagues.

11563.name: Bo Nix
11563.headline: Volume-Soaked QB1 Floor, Capped Ceiling in Denver
11563.body:
Nix piled up 612 attempts in 2025, the league lead, yet the efficiency stayed modest: a 0.089 EPA per play, 43.9% success rate, and a minus-1.2 CPOE that placed him eighth among our advanced-stat tracking. He cleared 304.8 fantasy points behind 25 passing scores, 11 picks, and just 11.4% of his throws traveling deep, so the ceiling is propped up by sheer pass count rather than splash plays.

The rushing layer keeps him steady, 356 yards and five touchdowns on 83 carries, the kind of floor that survives cold passing stretches. He absorbed only 22 sacks behind a 19.1% pressure rate, and a refreshed receiving corps led by Courtland Sutton plus the addition of Jaylen Waddle should sharpen the completion math. Monitor the offseason ankle, but a back-end QB1 finish remains the realistic baseline.

11564.name: Drake Maye
11564.headline: Year-two leap is real: a locked-in top-five QB
11564.body:
Maye's 2025 jump wasn't noise, it was a full rebuild of his profile. His EPA per play swung from -0.03 to plus-0.265, his success rate climbed to 53%, and his CPOE hit a league-best-tier 10.8 on a 72% completion clip. He threw 31 touchdowns against just 8 picks over 17 games while finishing fourth among quarterbacks in fantasy points, and the dual-threat floor held with 450 rushing yards and 4 scores on the ground. The deep ball arrived too: 67 throws of 20-plus yards after only 25 the year prior.

The surrounding cast now matches the talent. New England added A.J. Brown and Romeo Doubs and shored up the line, giving Maye real outlets behind one of last year's most efficient passing attacks. A tougher schedule is the only legitimate worry, but with the rushing volume and red-zone usage intact, he profiles as a set-and-forget QB1 who can challenge the position's elite.

11565.name: J.J. McCarthy
11565.headline: Murray addition clouds McCarthy's path after shaky rookie tape
11565.body:
McCarthy's debut season offered little to build excitement on. Across 10 games he completed just 57.6% of his throws for 1,632 yards with 11 touchdowns against 12 interceptions, and the underlying numbers were uglier than the box score: a -0.151 EPA per play, a 41% success rate, and a -5.7 CPOE that pegged him as a clear negative as a passer. His 21.3% bad-throw rate and 51 poor throws underscore the accuracy concerns, though 181 rushing yards and four scores on 37 carries added a thin floor.

The bigger problem is opportunity. Minnesota brought in Kyler Murray, who profiles as the Week 1 favorite, pushing McCarthy toward QB36 territory with an ADP outside pick 270 and roster rates near 2.5%. The franchise still believes in him long-term, so he's a stash worth monitoring, but until the depth chart clears he's an afterthought in standard formats.

11566.name: Jayden Daniels
11566.headline: Elite rushing ceiling, but 2025 dip and durability cloud the case
11566.body:
The rookie version was a fantasy cheat code: 0.162 EPA per play, a plus-3.5 CPOE, 48.3% success rate, 891 rushing yards on 148 carries and six scores, good for QB16 in points while finishing top-tier in efficiency. Then 2025 cratered. Across just seven games his EPA per play went negative (-0.012), CPOE flipped to -2.7, completions sank to 60.6%, and yards per attempt collapsed to 6.7 as elbow, knee and ankle issues piled up. The rushing fell off too, with the long passing dimension thinning out.

The rushing floor is what keeps him a QB1: even a partial 2025 produced 278 ground yards and two scores, and a full year approaches 700-plus on the ground. The risk is real, though. The pass-catching corps behind Terry McLaurin is thin, the schedule grades brutally, and the body must hold up. Draft him as a rebound bet around the QB4-6 tier, not the locked-in star of a year ago.

11569.name: Jarquez Hunter
11569.headline: Buried behind Williams and Corum, strictly a handcuff dart
11569.body:
Hunter enters 2026 fighting for the No. 3 job in a crowded Rams backfield, slotted behind workhorse Kyren Williams and Blake Corum, with Ronnie Rivers also in the mix for scraps. He didn't log a snap during the 2025 campaign, and his current consensus is unambiguous: a back near the very bottom of the position, going around 358th overall and rostered in well under one percent of leagues. That tells you everything about his standalone value right now.

Projections peg him at roughly 32 carries for 148 yards and a score, or about 22 fantasy points across a full season, which is bench-clogging territory in standard formats. The investment case is purely contingency. If Williams or Corum miss time, Los Angeles's run-heavy scheme could funnel real volume his way overnight. Until then, monitor camp reps and treat him as a late-summer stash, not a draft-day commitment.

11570.name: Rasheen Ali
11570.headline: Deep stash banking on a pass-game role
11570.body:
Ali's 2025 line reads like a true backup's: 15 games but just 13% of snaps, seven carries for 24 yards at 3.43 per pop, and a brutal minus-0.25 EPA per play that flags how little space he created. The flicker of intrigue is through the air. He drew 11 targets, caught nine for 68 yards and a score on 0.79 yards per route run, and his 83 receiving yards after the catch hint at usable hands when given a route tree. With a 2.7% target share, though, the receiving profile is more proof of concept than production.

The path forward runs entirely through Derrick Henry's workload, and Ali has barely 17 career carries to argue he's the next man up. Going near the back of drafts, he's a deep-league handcuff lottery ticket: ignore him in standard formats and only speculate if Henry's availability wobbles.

11571.name: Isaiah Davis
11571.headline: Efficient change-of-pace back stuck behind a crowded Jets backfield
11571.body:
Davis has done plenty with the limited work he's gotten. Across 43 carries in 2025 he ripped off 236 yards at 5.49 per tote, with a 50-yard burst and explosive runs on nearly 14% of his attempts. He also flashed as a receiver, turning 28 targets into 21 grabs for 186 yards on a tidy 0.92 yards per route run. The catch: a 28% snap share and just 10 red-zone touches cap his ceiling, and his 0.005 EPA per play says the efficiency came on low-leverage volume.

The path forward is narrow. Breece Hall and Braelon Allen sit ahead of him, leaving Davis as a passing-downs piece rather than a true handcuff. New coordinator Frank Reich has historically fed backs in the passing game, which could nudge his target floor, but projections near 135 rushing and 78 receiving yards keep him a deep-league dart only.

11573.name: Frank Gore
11573.headline: Legacy name, fourth on Buffalo running back ladder
11573.body:
This is Frank Gore Jr., not the Hall of Fame father, and he arrives in Buffalo with no NFL advanced stats to his name. The depth chart is the whole story: he sits fourth among Bills running backs behind James Cook, change-of-pace back Ty Johnson and 2024 draftee Ray Davis. That is a hard ceiling in a backfield where Cook commands the early-down and goal-line work and Johnson owns the passing-down role.

Without a single carry of measurable production to project from, Gore is purely a camp-body and practice-squad-caliber flier whose only path to touches runs through multiple injuries ahead of him. The compact, elusive running style that made him productive in college does not translate to fantasy relevance behind this trio. He is undraftable in redraft and barely a name to file away in the deepest dynasty formats. Leave him on the wire.

11574.name: Dylan Laube
11574.headline: Special-teams role keeps Laube off fantasy radars
11574.body:
Laube's 2025 line tells the whole story: 17 games but just a 2.9% snap share, seven carries for nine yards (1.29 per attempt), and three catches for 18 yards. That added up to 2.7 fantasy points and a brutal minus-0.56 EPA per play, leaving him 122nd among backs. There simply weren't enough touches to evaluate, and the few he got produced almost nothing on the ground.

The depth chart explains the silence. He sits third in Las Vegas behind Ashton Jeanty and Mike Washington Jr., a buried role that draws an RB124 expert rank and essentially zeroed-out projections. Where he earns his roster spot is the kicking game, where he logged real volume returning kickoffs. Outside of return-yardage formats, there's no path to relevance unless injuries clear two bodies ahead of him. Leave him on the wire.

11575.name: Ray Davis
11575.headline: Cook's Handcuff With Quiet Efficiency Under The Hood
11575.body:
Davis spent 2025 buried behind James Cook, drawing just a 14.3% snap share and 58 carries, yet the underlying work held up. He averaged 4.74 yards per attempt, posted a 0.099 EPA per play, and forced enough missed tackles to keep his explosive-run rate north of 15%. The receiving role is real too: 13 targets, 10 grabs, 1.34 yards per route run, and two scores through the air despite a microscopic 2.7% target share. The touchdown variance is the catch, as he reached the end zone only via receptions across a 17-game slate.

His 2024 ceiling, 99.1 fantasy points on 113 carries with three rushing touchdowns, shows what a workload bump unlocks. As the primary backup, Davis is a pure contingency play, going around RB60 at pick 198. He is worth a final-round dart only for Cook managers buying insurance, with standalone value tethered entirely to the starter's health.

11576.name: Braelon Allen
11576.headline: Talented handcuff stuck behind Hall until opportunity opens
11576.body:
Allen's 2025 was a four-game blip cut short by injury, and the underlying numbers were rough: 4.22 yards per carry on just 18 totes, a brutal -0.379 EPA per play, only 1.83 yards after contact, plus a fumble and two tackles for loss. His fuller 2024 sample tells the real story of where he is as a runner - 92 carries for 334 yards (3.63 per pop), two scores, and a passable 2.09 yards after contact across 17 games while logging 19 catches on 27 targets.

The profile is a young, powerful back with three-down tools who simply cannot find volume behind an entrenched starter. He sat around 23-26 percent of snaps the past two years, and his fantasy value is almost entirely contingent on an injury or trade clearing the lead role. Until then he is a late-round stash and pure handcuff, valuable mostly to managers already holding Hall.

11577.name: Will Shipley
11577.headline: Backup Eagles back stuck behind Barkley, deep stash
11577.body:
Shipley’s 2025 line confirms a marginal role: 14 games, a 12.5 percent snap share, just 14 carries for 49 yards at 3.5 per tote, and 9 catches on 12 targets for 56 yards. The efficiency cratered with a -0.334 EPA per play and a 0.67 yards per route run, leaving him at 10.5 fantasy points and RB91 in our rank. That is even thinner than his 2024 rookie season (30 carries, 2.73 YPC, -0.178 EPA, RB98), so the second-year jump never materialized behind a healthy Saquon Barkley.

The profile is volume-starved by design. With a 2.6 percent target share, a lone red-zone opportunity, and zero broken tackles forced across his 14 carries, Shipley offers no standalone value while Barkley owns the Eagles backfield. His 4th-round draft capital keeps him the clear next-man-up, which is the entire case. Treat him strictly as a handcuff and deep-dynasty stash whose redraft relevance lives and dies with a Barkley injury, not a draftable asset on his own.

11581.name: MarShawn Lloyd
11581.headline: Talented but invisible RB3 buried behind Jacobs
11581.body:
Lloyd's NFL career is still essentially theoretical. A 2024 third-round pick (88th overall) out of USC, he has logged just one game, totaling 6 carries for 15 yards (2.5 per attempt), one catch and 1.8 fantasy points on a 14 percent snap share, with a -0.291 EPA per play that tells you nothing in so small a sample. Injuries, not ineffectiveness, have kept him off the field, but the result is a near-blank resume entering year three.

The role is the bigger problem: he sits third on Green Bay's depth chart behind workhorse Josh Jacobs and Chris Brooks, leaving no standalone touches while Jacobs stays healthy. The draft capital keeps him interesting as a contingency, and an injury ahead of him would unlock real change-of-pace and passing-down upside given his college receiving chops. For 2026 redraft he is a late-round dart and a high-value handcuff stash; in dynasty he remains a hold, but he must finally prove he can stay available.

11583.name: Jonathon Brooks
11583.headline: High-upside lottery ticket if the knee holds
11583.body:
Our profile on Brooks is built on a sliver of data: a 3-game cameo in 2024 where he logged just 9 carries for 22 yards (2.44 per rush), 3 catches on 3 targets, and an 11% snap share before his second ACL tear wiped everything out. The efficiency markers were ugly small-sample numbers — a minus-0.11 EPA per play, 1.56 yards after contact per attempt, and a single broken tackle — but the volume was far too thin to read anything into. He finished as RB111 on the back of 4.5 fantasy points.

So the bet here is talent and opportunity, not production. Brooks reportedly returned to 11-on-11 work and projects fully healthy entering 2026, slotting behind Chuba Hubbard in a thin Carolina backfield. With an ADP near pick 108, he's a cheap swing at a potential lead role. The downside is real after consecutive knee surgeries, but the ceiling justifies the late-round stab.

11584.name: Bucky Irving
11584.headline: Bounce-back bet on a discounted lead back
11584.body:
Irving's 2025 was a tale of regression: his rushing EPA cratered to minus-0.129 per play after a sterling plus-0.141 as a rookie, and his yards per carry collapsed from 5.42 to 3.4 across just 10 games. The explosive juice vanished too, with breakaway rate halving to 2.31 percent and yards after contact dropping nearly a full yard to 1.64. A shoulder injury that required offseason surgery clearly sapped him, and the one rushing touchdown on 22 red-zone chances reflects a back never quite right.

The rookie tape is the real Bucky, though, and that ceiling keeps him a top-32 fantasy back. The concern is a crowded committee: Sean Tucker has stolen goal-line work, Rachaad White handled third downs, and free-agent addition Kenneth Gainwell threatens receiving snaps. With an early-Round-5 price tag, he's a discounted lead back whose pass-catching floor and bounce-back upside make him worth the gamble if the shoulder holds.

11586.name: Blake Corum
11586.headline: Explosive, efficient handcuff one Kyren injury from fantasy gold
11586.body:
Corum's 2025 leap is the real story: 145 carries became 746 yards at 5.14 a pop with six scores and zero fumbles, a 0.063 EPA per play that flipped his ugly -0.108 from 2024. He cleared 10-plus yards on 23 runs, broke off five 20-plus and a 48-yard touchdown, and posted a 15.9% explosive-run rate among the league's best. That efficiency came on just a 29.1% snap share, so the volume ceiling is obvious if usage climbs.

The catch is the depth chart. Kyren Williams still owns this backfield, leaving Corum a complementary back with negligible passing-game work (14 targets, 0.22 yards per route). That caps his standalone value, but he's among the premier handcuffs available, going around RB34 near pick 93. He flashed three-down upside down the stretch, and any Williams absence instantly turns him into a weekly starter behind a favorable schedule.

11588.name: Jawhar Jordan
11588.headline: Explosive flashes can't overcome a crowded Houston backfield
11588.body:
Jordan's 2025 sample was tiny but tantalizing: 43 carries for 193 yards at 4.49 a pop, headlined by a 50-yard rip and a 16.3% explosive-run rate that hints at real burst. He forced eight broken tackles (one every 5.4 totes) and added 2.09 yards after contact, so the wiggle is legitimate. The problem is everything around it. He played just four games on a 30% snap share, his -0.173 EPA per play reflects how little the offense trusted him, and zero touchdowns across 43 rushes and seven targets caps the ceiling.

The roster math is brutal. Houston added David Montgomery and still has Woody Marks ahead of him, leaving Jordan fighting for the No. 3 job rather than touches that matter. At 27 with no dynasty equity and rostership near half a percent, he's a deep-league dart at best. Projections of roughly 220 rushing yards and a single score confirm he belongs on the waiver wire in standard formats.

11589.name: Trey Benson
11589.headline: Explosive on tape, buried on the depth chart
11589.body:
Benson's 2025 was a four-game tease: 5.52 yards per carry on just 29 totes, a 52-yard burst, and a 13.79% explosive-run rate that flashed real juice. The problem was efficiency under the hood and volume on top of it. His -0.184 EPA per play and a meager 2.6% target share (0.9 yards per route run) signal a back who hasn't earned passing-down trust, and his snap share never translated into goal-line work. The 2024 baseline (4.62 YPC, one touchdown over 12 games) tells the same story: bursty, but boom-or-bust and light on scoring equity.

Now the runway has vanished. Arizona reshaped the room this offseason, pushing Benson toward the back of the rotation behind fresher additions. An ankle issue limiting his spring work only deepens the hole. The talent flashes are real, but without injuries ahead of him, he's a deep-stash dart throw, not a draftable asset in standard formats.

11592.name: Erick All
11592.headline: Promising profile stalled by a second torn ACL
11592.body:
All's 2024 tape flashed the chops that made Cincinnati's brass excited. Across eight games he caught 20 of 22 looks for a 90.9% catch rate, posted a 50% success rate, and generated 0.239 EPA per play on a 41.9% snap share. His 0.188 targets per route run and 1.35 yards per route run were efficient for a part-time role, even with a tiny 3.5% target share and shallow 3.27 air yards per target. The arrow pointed up.

That arrow snapped. A second career ACL tear lands him on PUP and likely costs the entire 2026 season, gutting any near-term value. His draft cost has collapsed to roughly TE47, around pick 291, and he sits rostered in almost no leagues. Treat him as a deep dynasty stash on talent alone; in redraft formats there is no reason to spend a pick until he proves the knee and reclaims real snaps.

11593.name: Brevyn Spann-Ford
11593.headline: Blocking Tight End With No Path To Targets
11593.body:
The advanced profile screams afterthought. Across 16 games in 2025, Spann-Ford ran a route on just 14.7% of dropbacks and saw a 2.1% target share, turning 13 looks into 9 grabs for 90 yards and one score. His 0.94 yards per route run and -0.049 EPA per play landed him 74th among tight ends, and a 24.9% snap rate confirms a blocking specialist rather than a pass-catcher.

There's no growth signal underneath. His route share actually shrank from 19.5% in 2024, and across two seasons he has never cleared 14 targets or topped 90 receiving yards. With Dallas already set ahead of him on the depth chart, his volume stays capped barring injuries. He sits outside the top 110 at the position in early rankings for good reason. Leave him on the wire in every format until usage meaningfully changes.

11595.name: Jared Wiley
11595.headline: Buried depth tight end with no statistical case yet
11595.body:
The numbers leave nothing to project. Across 2024 and 2025, Wiley logged a combined three games, one snap-heavy enough to register only a 19.3% snap share at its peak, and exactly two targets total. He turned those into two catches for 11 yards, a 0.2% target share in each season, and a WOPR near zero. The lone bright spot is a freakish 2.566 EPA per play in 2025 on a single reception, a sample so small it carries no predictive weight. For a 6-foot-6, 249-pound tight end entering his age-25 season, the opportunity simply has not materialized in Kansas City.

The draft market agrees, slotting him around TE82 with an ECR rank in the 500s and essentially no roster presence. Modest projections of 11 catches, 95 yards and a score reflect hope, not a defined role. Until he climbs the depth chart, he is a deep-stash dynasty flier at best.

11596.name: Ben Sinnott
11596.headline: Efficient on a sliver of snaps, but buried on the depth chart
11596.body:
Sinnott's 2025 tape is quietly efficient: across nine games he posted a 0.249 EPA per play and a 61.5% success rate, catching 11 of 13 targets (84.6%) for 114 yards and a score with zero drops. His 1.15 yards per route run and 5.73 yards after the catch per reception show real ability whenever Washington bothers to use him. The problem is volume: a 36.1% snap share, a 19.8% route rate, and just a 3% target share leave him generating fewer than two looks a game.

Until the depth chart cracks open, that production cap holds. He saw only one red-zone and one end-zone target all year, and the projected line of roughly 12 catches, 116 yards, and one or two touchdowns reflects a clear backup role rather than a health issue. The age-23 efficiency is intriguing for dynasty stashes, but in redraft he's a watch-list name who needs a snap jump before he matters.

11597.name: Theo Johnson
11597.headline: Ascending Giants TE with real red-zone target equity
11597.body:
Johnson took a clear step forward in 2025, ranking TE19 with 82.8 fantasy points across 15 games on an 86.7 percent snap share. The 2024 fourth-rounder (Pick 107, Penn State) more than doubled his target share to 14.7 percent from 7.8 and lifted yards per route run to 1.18 from 0.84, hauling in 45 of 74 targets for 528 yards and five scores. The biggest leap was usage near the goal line: 14 red-zone and six end-zone targets, up from one each in 2024, the engine behind his touchdown production.

The concerns are catch quality, a 60.8 percent catch rate, five drops and a 6.8 drop rate, plus a modest 0.119 EPA per play that caps his ceiling. Still, as a near-every-down option in a thin Giants pass-catching corps, his volume and red-zone role give him a stable streaming floor with TE1 spike weeks. For 2026 he is a low-end starter and strong dynasty hold, draftable as a back-end TE1 with room to grow.

11600.name: Ja'Tavion Sanders
11600.headline: Sanders stuck in a committee that caps his ceiling
11600.body:
Sanders went the wrong way in 2025, slipping to TE52 after a TE34 finish the year prior. His efficiency cratered alongside the workload: yards per route run fell from 1.09 to 0.85, EPA per play sagged from 0.271 to 0.13, and his target share dipped to 7.1% across just 12 games before a December ankle break ended things. He did catch 85.3% of his looks and saw 10 red-zone targets, but with one touchdown and 190 yards, the volume simply isn't there.

The deeper problem is the timeshare with Tommy Tremble, who has matched him snap for snap and kept both from carrying weekly value. Sanders is moving well in his ankle rehab and projects for a healthy training camp, yet a TE39 ADP near pick 252 reflects the reality. He owns the better long-term profile, but until Carolina commits to him, he's a bench-stash dart throw, not a startable tight end.

11603.name: AJ Barner
11603.headline: Efficient red-zone weapon whose snap surge unlocks TE2 upside
11603.body:
Barner's 2025 leap is the whole story: his snap share climbed from 45.1% to 77.3%, and the efficiency held up at scale. He posted 0.411 EPA per play and a 64.7% success rate on a 14.9% target share, turning 68 looks into 52 grabs for 519 yards and 6 scores. Critically, he was trusted near the goal line, drawing 13 red-zone and 6 end-zone targets, and he barely wasted possessions with a 2.9% drop rate. At 1.26 yards per route run, he earned the volume rather than inheriting it.

The path forward is clean. Seattle added no notable pass catchers, so behind Jaxon Smith-Njigba's massive target share, Barner is the obvious secondary option if the team throws more. Rookie Elijah Arroyo is the lone threat to the workload, and offseason surgeries warrant camp monitoring. Around an ADP near TE24, he's a sensible late-round TE2 with weekly touchdown equity.

11604.name: Brock Bowers
11604.headline: Bowers primed to reclaim the TE1 crown in 2026
11604.body:
Even compressed to 12 games by injury, Bowers finished as our TE10 in 2025, turning 86 targets into 64 grabs for 680 yards and seven scores while posting a stout 0.259 EPA per play and 112.2 fantasy points. Lean on the rookie blueprint instead: a TE1 finish in 2024 on 153 targets, 112 receptions, 1,194 yards and a commanding 25.8% target share that fueled 150.7 points. His 2025 share dipped to 17.4%, a function of missed time and a stalled attack rather than ability.

The roster math points back up. A thin receiver room leaves him the clear funnel for whoever wins the quarterback job, and nothing on the depth chart threatens his alpha role. Drafters are treating him as the position's top option around the second-third round turn, and that price is justified: healthy, he carries the highest weekly ceiling at a scarce position and a realistic path to overall TE1.

11605.name: Jaheim Bell
11605.headline: Deep-roster Steelers tight end with a long climb ahead
11605.body:
Bell's NFL profile is still a blank slate, and the numbers underline how little he has done. Across four 2024 games in New England he logged just 34 routes on an 8.6% snap share, drew three targets, and turned them into two catches for 20 yards. His 0.6% target share, 0.59 yards per route run, and slightly negative EPA per play (-0.002) all scream backup, and a 33.3% success rate gives nothing to build on. He finished as roughly the TE98, the kind of line that reflects a player fighting for any field time at all.

A fresh one-year deal in Pittsburgh hands him a clean reset, but he steps into a crowded tight end room competing for snaps rather than touches. Modest projections in the 10-catch, 99-yard, one-touchdown range fit a deep-league afterthought. He is a watch-list dynasty stash and nothing more until he carves out a defined role.

11608.name: Isaiah Williams
11608.headline: Depth-chart filler whose 2025 role never threatened fantasy relevance
11608.body:
Williams carved out his first real workload in 2025, playing 15 games at a 36.1% snap rate and drawing 36 targets on a 7.6% share. The production stayed thin: 26 catches for 193 yards and zero touchdowns, with a 3.39 air-yards-per-target line that pins him as a short-area possession piece. His 0.96 yards per route run and 44.4% success rate are replacement-level, and a 0.033 EPA per play confirms a finish (WR119) that offered nothing for lineups.

The profile screams field-position specialist rather than passing-game weapon, with his value tied largely to a kick-return and special-teams role. A draft slot buried near WR174 reflects that reality, and there's no projected target bump or red-zone usage (one red-zone look all year) to chase. Until an injury ahead of him forces meaningful snaps, he stays off rosters in every format.

11610.name: Malik Washington
11610.headline: Underneath slot grinder lacks the volume to matter
11610.body:
Washington took a real step up in playing time in 2025, jumping from a 44.7 percent snap share to 57 percent and from 237 to 340 routes across a full 17 games. The target share climbed to 14.1 percent (65 targets, 46 catches) and he scored 3 times, but the production was empty: just 317 receiving yards at 6.89 per catch and a thin 0.93 yards per route run. A 1.39 yards-before-catch average confirms a pure underneath, YAC-dependent role, and a -0.151 EPA per play marks him as a drag on efficiency.

His 70.7 fantasy points (WR88) reflect a glorified possession option, not a fantasy contributor. With an air-yards share of only 10 percent, there is no realistic path to weekly relevance unless injuries clear Miami's depth chart. The 5.5 yards after catch and 10.9 percent avoided-tackle rate are the only flashes worth monitoring. For 2026 redraft he is a deep-PPR waiver name at most; leave him undrafted in standard formats.

11616.name: Jacob Cowing
11616.headline: Buried on a crowded depth chart, draft-irrelevant for now
11616.body:
Cowing's profile is built on a tiny sample: 6 targets across 15 games in 2024, a 10.4% snap rate, a 10.7% route share, and a 1.2% target share. The flashes were real -- a 0.709 EPA per play, a 66.7% success rate, 20.0 yards per reception, and a 14.33 air-yards-per-target mark that pegs him as a downfield option -- but four catches for 80 yards and a 0.031 WOPR is noise, not signal. There's nothing here to project a fantasy role from.

The situation only narrows it further. A hamstring injury wiped out his 2025, and he returns to a San Francisco receiver room stacked ahead of him, leaving him fighting for reserve snaps rather than targets. Coaches have praised his conditioning, but conditioning doesn't beat out a full depth chart. He's undraftable in every format and belongs only on a deep-league watch list -- a name to revisit if an injury opens a lane in front of him.

11618.name: Jalen McMillan
11618.headline: Injury wrecked 2025, but the talent flashes are real
11618.body:
McMillan's 2025 was a four-game blip, yet the efficiency inside it was loud: 0.833 EPA per play, a 66.7% success rate, 80% catch rate, and 2.31 yards per route run on a tiny 12.9% route share. That mirrors the breakout tease from 2024, when he turned 58 targets into 8 touchdowns, five of them from the end zone, on a 10.3% target share across 13 games. The red-zone nose for the football (8 red-zone looks, 8 scores in 2024) is the real hook here.

The path matters more than the profile. With Mike Evans gone, McMillan slots behind Emeka Egbuka and Chris Godwin and must beat out depth bodies for the WR3 job in three-receiver sets. Projections of roughly 47-607-4 fit a part-time role, and the injury question looms large after last year. A late-round dart with genuine touchdown equity if the snaps arrive.

11619.name: Ja'Lynn Polk
11619.headline: Fresh start in New Orleans, but the bar starts on the floor
11619.body:
The rookie tape was rough by any measure. Across 14 games in 2024, Polk ran just 234 routes, drew 33 targets and reeled in only 12 of them for 87 yards and two scores. A 36.4% catch rate, a -0.6 EPA per play and a 27.3% success rate all point the same direction, and his 0.37 yards per route run ranked among the worst at the position. Four drops on 16 catchable balls didn't help the trust.
