A shoulder injury wiped out his second year before a trade sent him from New England to New Orleans, where he gets a clean slate but no guaranteed role. ADP sits near pick 390, deep into draft-and-stash territory. Until he carves out snaps and proves the hands have improved, he's a watch-list flier rather than anything you draft with intent. Monitor the depth chart in camp; the talent is there, the production hasn't been.

11620.name: Rome Odunze
11620.headline: Big-Play Profile, Stalled Catch Rate: Odunze's Bounce-Back Case
11620.body:
Odunze's 2025 sample is loaded with downfield juice: 13.91 air yards per target, a 25.3% air-yards share, and 0.243 EPA per play across 12 games. He commanded a 16.9% target share and 12 red-zone looks, turning that into six touchdowns and 102.1 fantasy points. The yardage stayed deep too, with 13 catches of 20-plus yards. The hole in the profile is a 48.9% catch rate dragged down by three interceptions on his targets rather than his hands, since he dropped just two of 46 catchable balls.

That catch-rate dip screams positive regression, especially with another year of Caleb Williams rapport ahead. The counter is volume: a crowded route tree featuring Loveland and Burden caps his ceiling, and his draft cost near the WR29 range bakes that in. Treat him as a touchdown-leveraged WR2 whose efficiency, not target count, drives the payoff.

11623.name: Jordan Whittington
11623.headline: Buried behind Nacua and Adams, a deep stash only
11623.body:
The 2025 tape underlines a player still fighting for snaps: 40.8% snap share across 15 games, a 36% route rate, and just 25 targets for a 4.3% target share. He turned that sliver into 18 catches, 171 yards and no scores, with a 0.77 yards-per-route mark and a 0.087 WOPR that scream tertiary option. His 0.103 EPA per play and 52% success rate were efficient enough, but the volume ceiling is the story, and his 2024 sample (2.42 YPRR, 0.59 EPA) shows the upside is real only if targets ever arrive.

That unlock is unlikely. Slotted third in line behind Puka Nacua and Davante Adams, he projects for roughly 15 grabs and 172 yards, leaving him near WR126 and off the draft board at a 301 ADP. He is a contingency play, not a roster asset, worth a flier only if injury thins the room ahead of him.

11624.name: Xavier Worthy
11624.headline: Speedster Worthy dips in injury-shortened year, buy-low WR3
11624.body:
Worthy’s sophomore season slid the wrong way: 14 games, a 70.6 percent snap share and 379 routes produced 73 targets, 42 catches, 532 yards and just one touchdown, landing him WR56 with 67.9 fantasy points. That trails his rookie 2024 (98 targets, 638 yards, six scores, WR48, 128.2 points) almost entirely on touchdown regression—his 13.3 percent target share and 0.345 WOPR held a strong route role, and his 0.203 EPA per play matched rookie efficiency. His 1.4 percent drop rate was a major cleanup from a shaky 7.1 percent in 2024.

The underlying usage is encouraging for a 22-year-old: a 20.8 percent air-yards share and 12.93 air yards per target confirm a real downfield role. The lone touchdown is the obvious bounce-back lever, and first-round draft capital signals continued investment. Treat the down year as a buy-low window: Worthy is a WR3 with WR2 upside if scoring regresses toward his volume, a mid-round pick rather than a fade.

11625.name: Adonai Mitchell
11625.headline: Big-play traits, but a Wilson-shaped ceiling caps the upside
11625.body:
Mitchell's 2025 line is a study in contrasts: a 14% target share and 25.2% air-yards share on a vertical role (14.89 air yards per look) that produced explosive flashes, including a 75-yard catch and six grabs of 20-plus yards. Yet the efficiency underneath was alarming. He converted just 44.6% of targets into catches, drew five interceptions when thrown to, and posted a brutal minus-0.218 EPA per play with a 35.1% success rate over 16 games. The 453 yards and two scores on 33 catches translated to only 54.9 fantasy points.

The trade to New York gave him a half-season audition while Garrett Wilson was hurt, but Wilson's return plus two first-round rookie pass-catchers crowds the room. Projections of roughly 39-564-3 reflect that ceiling compression. With an ADP near 189 and WR61 consensus, he's a speculative late dart whose volume-dependent past leaves little margin if the deep-ball variance regresses again.

11626.name: Xavier Legette
11626.headline: Legette's efficiency collapse buries any draft-day case
11626.body:
Year two went backward across the board. Legette posted a 13.3% target share on a 0.171 target-per-route rate, both down from his rookie marks, and his 0.97 yards per route run barely cleared replacement level. The advanced profile is ugly: minus-0.064 EPA per play, a 39.1% success rate, and a 54.7% catch rate that turned 64 looks into just 35 grabs for 363 yards. Even 12 red-zone targets yielded only three scores and 54.3 fantasy points across 15 games.

The 2024 baseline (16.2% target share, 1.26 YPRR, a 0.405 WOPR) shows the talent isn't gone, but the trend and the context both cut against him. Carolina threw for the league's third-fewest yards, and he projects no better than third in a WR2 race with Jalen Coker and rookie Chris Brazzell. Drafting at a WR104 ADP, he's a late-round dart, not a target.

11627.name: Troy Franklin
11627.headline: Waddle trade buries a quietly efficient breakout
11627.body:
Franklin's sophomore leap was real: he climbed from WR107 to WR35, posting 65 grabs, 709 yards and six scores on 104 targets across 17 games. The underlying work backed the box score, with a 17.8% target share, a healthy 0.473 WOPR, 1.55 yards per route and a positive 0.064 EPA per play after he sat at minus-0.348 as a rookie. A 45.2% success rate and 12.7 air yards per target show a genuine downfield role, not empty volume.

The problem is opportunity. Denver's Jaylen Waddle acquisition shoves Franklin behind Courtland Sutton and Waddle into a crowded No. 3 job, where he splits leftover looks with Marvin Mims and Pat Bryant. Projections crater toward 36 catches and 396 yards, making him a late-round dart that only matters if an injury opens snaps. The talent earns a stash; the depth chart caps the ceiling.

11628.name: Marvin Harrison
11628.headline: The Talent Is Loud, The Usage Went Quiet
11628.body:
Don't let a noisy 2025 fool you: when Harrison ran routes, the efficiency was elite. He posted 0.412 EPA per play and a 54.8% success rate, both jumps over his rookie marks, and that points to a player whose per-snap impact never cratered. The problem was volume. Across only 12 games he drew just 73 targets and an 11.8% target share, well off the 22.2% and 116 looks he commanded as a rookie, and his WOPR collapsed from 0.629 to 0.32.

The profile screams positive regression if the opportunity returns. He still averaged nearly 13 air yards per target and stayed a red-zone presence with 10 red-zone and five end-zone targets, finishing with four scores on a thin 41 catches. Restore a full route share and the downfield usage, and the WR45 finish becomes a floor, not a forecast. Buy the dip with conviction.

11629.name: Devontez Walker
11629.headline: Big-play flashes, but the runway just got crowded
11629.body:
Walker's 2025 line is a study in efficiency without volume: six grabs on eight looks across eight games, a 75% catch rate, 136 yards, and three touchdowns on just a 2% target share. The per-route work pops at 2.43 yards per route run and a 0.143 target-per-route rate, while a 2.242 EPA per play and 75% success rate confirm that nearly every touch turned into a win. With a 24.5-yard average depth of target, he is purely a field-stretcher, not a chain-mover.

That profile is the problem and the promise. Baltimore added a pair of rookie receivers this offseason, pushing Walker's path behind Zay Flowers and Rashod Bateman to a situational deep role he must re-earn. He carries an ADP near pick 295 and belongs only in the deepest formats. Bank the explosiveness, but the snaps and consistency simply are not there yet to chase him sooner.

11630.name: Roman Wilson
11630.headline: Steelers slot flier flashes deep upside, needs volume
11630.body:
Wilson's first real action came in 2025: nine games, a modest 33.1 percent snap share, 154 routes, and 21 targets converted into 12 catches for 166 yards and two scores, good for 26.6 points and a WR127 finish. The efficiency was intriguing despite the thin volume, a 0.194 EPA per play, 1.08 yards per route, 13.19 air yards per target, and three catches of 20-plus including a 45-yard long, hinting at the field-stretching role the 2024 third-rounder (pick 84, Michigan) was drafted to fill. Availability remains the flag, with just seven games per year over two seasons.

The 2026 setup is mixed: he sits second on the Pittsburgh chart behind DK Metcalf, with Aaron Rodgers under center, but a low 4 percent target share leaves him reliant on a usage jump to matter weekly. He is a late-round dart and dynasty stash with real big-play juice, but until the target volume climbs he is a bench piece, not a startable flex in redraft.

11631.name: Brian Thomas
11631.headline: Bounce-back bet after a noisy, drop-plagued sophomore dip
11631.body:
The numbers scream regression in both directions. As a rookie, Thomas posted a top-three season: 1,282 yards, 10 scores, a 0.392 EPA per play and 2.58 yards per route on a 25.5% target share. Year two cratered to 707 yards and two touchdowns across 14 games, with EPA falling to 0.189, route productivity halved to 1.63, and target share sliced to 16.6%. The volume evaporated.

Much of that collapse is fixable. A 52.7% catch rate and 10 drops on 58 catchable balls (11% drop rate, up from 5.3%) point to poor ball security and shaky quarterback play rather than lost talent. The downfield profile held firm, 14.45 air yards per target and a 26.9% air-yards share, so the alpha role and field-stretching usage remain intact. At 22 with a proven ceiling, he's a clear buy-low whose price should reflect the down year, not the rookie breakout.

11632.name: Malik Nabers
11632.headline: Elite target hog returns from lost season, smash WR1
11632.body:
Before injury wiped out his 2025, Nabers was a true alpha: as a rookie in 2024 he ran 526 routes across 15 games, commanding a monster 30.7 percent target share, a 0.741 WOPR and 40 percent of the air yards while turning 170 targets into 109 catches, 1,204 yards and 7 scores for WR7 (164.6 fantasy points). His 0.323 targets per route run and 0.268 EPA per play were genuinely special for a 21-year-old.

2025 lasted just four games before a season-ending injury, but the usage held even in that tiny sample: 79.5 percent snaps, a 15.86 air-yards-per-target downfield role and 2.2 yards per route run. The Giants funnel everything through him, and his rookie production gives a top-three positional ceiling if healthy. The only red flag is the availability noted in his profile (9.5 games per year over two seasons). Draft him as a locked-in weekly WR1 with league-winning upside, accepting the injury risk as the cost of admission.

11635.name: Ladd McConkey
11635.headline: Bounce-back bet as McConkey inherits the alpha role
11635.body:
McConkey's 2025 was a step back hiding in plain sight. He still ran a route on 83.2% of dropbacks and commanded a 19.5% target share, but his efficiency cratered: 1.52 yards per route run and a 0.068 EPA per play, down from a monster 2.55 and 0.577 in 2024. His catch rate slid from 73.2% to 62.3%, and five interceptions on his targets point to context that should normalize. The raw line of 66-789-6 finished WR28 after a WR9 rookie campaign.

The path back is clear. Keenan Allen is gone, freeing the underneath and red-zone work that capped McConkey's ceiling, and Mike McDaniel arrives to scheme him open. He already drew 15 red-zone looks in 2025, so target volume isn't the question, finishing is. With Herbert and a thinned-out target tree, his rookie-year form is the realistic baseline. A Round 4 ADP makes that upside a value.

11636.name: Johnny Wilson
11636.headline: Size and reps tease, but production remains nonexistent
11636.body:
The profile is built on potential rather than output. Across 10 games in 2024, Wilson ran just 151 routes on a 33.6% snap share and drew only 15 targets, a 3.6% team share that left his WOPR at a negligible 0.083. The efficiency tells the same story: a 33.3% catch rate, negative EPA per play (-0.077), a 33.3% success rate, and 38 receiving yards on the season. His one redeeming thread is air yards, with 29.4 per reception and three end-zone looks hinting at a downfield, contested role he never converted, finishing WR172.

After sitting out all of 2025, Wilson has reportedly drawn first-team practice reps this spring, a flicker of opportunity in Philadelphia's offense. Still, there is no ADP, no projection, and consensus boards bury him near WR263. Until he carves out real snaps and shows he can finish the catches his frame promises, he stays a deep-bench dart throw with nothing tangible to bet on yet.

11637.name: Keon Coleman
11637.headline: Make-or-break year for a stalled Bills receiver
11637.body:
Year two went sideways. Across 12 games Coleman commanded just a 12.3% target share on a 0.205 targets-per-route mark, turning 59 looks into 38 grabs for 404 yards and four scores. His 1.4 yards per route run slipped from 1.84 as a rookie, and the explosive element vanished: after notching five catches of 50-plus yards in 2024, he managed zero last season with a 17.3 air-yards-per-reception drop. The efficiency wasn't all bad (a 59.3% success rate, 0.224 EPA per play), but a 64.4% catch rate and 1.4 WOPR scream complementary role.

Now the runway shrinks further. Buffalo imported a proven veteran to occupy the same intermediate areas Coleman lived in, squeezing an already thin target tree. He's drifting toward pick 228 with WR4/WR5 projections, and nothing in the usage suggests a leap. Treat him as a late-round dart only if the depth chart clears.

11638.name: Ricky Pearsall
11638.headline: Big-play efficiency flashes, breakout-or-bust WR4 in San Francisco
11638.body:
Pearsall showed real growth in his nine 2025 games, posting an outstanding 0.504 EPA per play and 62.3 percent success rate while averaging 14.67 yards per catch on a deep 14.11 air yards per target. He turned a modest 9.6 percent target share into 36 grabs for 528 yards, with 25 receptions of 10-plus yards proving the downfield juice, though zero touchdowns on just four red-zone looks capped his 52.6 points (WR57). The leap over his rookie year (0.184 EPA, 1.47 yards per route) signals an ascending player.

The 2024 first-rounder (Pick 31, Florida) sits in a crowded 49ers passing tree, so his fantasy fate hinges on volume that has not yet arrived (under 10 percent target share both seasons) and on positive touchdown regression. The talent and draft capital point up, and an injury ahead of him would unlock WR2 upside. For now he is a high-ceiling WR4 and dynasty buy, a flex dart with breakout equity rather than a reliable starter.

11643.name: Jaylen Wright
11643.headline: Buried behind Achane, Wright stays a contingency stash
11643.body:
Two pro seasons in, Wright hasn't forced the issue. Across nine games in 2025 he carried 70 times for 288 yards at 4.11 a pop with two scores, but the efficiency picture stays grim: a minus-0.093 EPA per play, just 21.5 percent of snaps, and 2.51 yards after contact per attempt. His receiving role is essentially nonexistent at nine targets and a 1.9 percent target share. The flashes are real, including a 32-yard run and a breakaway, yet six rushes for negative yards and two fumbles in limited work undercut the upside.

The path is the problem. De'Von Achane owns this backfield, leaving Wright as the clear RB2 with no standalone value. Projections around 300 rushing yards and roughly 42 fantasy points reflect that ceiling, and an ADP near pick 253 confirms the market. He's a pure handcuff, and a worthwhile one given Achane's injury history, but draft him only as insurance, not for weekly production.

11644.name: Cody Schrader
11644.headline: Practice-squad depth back chasing a Denver roster spot
11644.body:
The ledger on Schrader is thin by design. His only NFL exposure came in a single 2024 appearance, where he logged a 3 percent snap share, one carry for three yards, and a lone target he turned into a six-yard catch. That line produced 0.9 fantasy points and a No. 127 positional rank, with a modest 0.038 EPA per play across a sample far too small to read into.

He enters 2026 on a future contract with Denver after spending last season on their practice squad, which frames him as a camp body fighting for the back end of the depth chart rather than a draftable asset. The backfield ahead of him is crowded, the schedule grades out as one of the league's tougher run environments, and no ADP exists because no market does. File him as a deep stash worth tracking only if injuries thin the room.

11646.name: Jalen Coker
11646.headline: Efficiency Says Coker Earns The No. 2 Job
11646.body:
The underlying numbers love Coker even when the box score stays quiet. Across 11 games in 2025 he posted a 0.506 EPA per play and a 62.8% success rate, catching 33 of 43 targets at a 76.7% clip with zero drops. The volume cap is real, though: a 9% target share, 0.16 targets per route, and just 1.47 yards per route mean he needs more routes to matter weekly. His 2024 tape was sharper still, with 1.85 yards per route and a 0.632 EPA, signaling the talent is there once snaps climb.

Behind Tetairoa McMillan, Coker profiles as Carolina's clear second receiver, but he must fend off rookie Chris Brazzell for the 80%-plus snap rate that unlocks his value. Health has been the recurring obstacle. With a fresh extension and an ascending finish, he is a late-round flier worth the dart if Bryce Young takes a step.

11647.name: Kimani Vidal
11647.headline: Proven LAC handcuff with standalone juice if Hampton sits
11647.body:
Vidal already showed he can carry a backfield when called on. Across 13 games in 2025 he turned 155 carries into 643 yards at 4.15 a pop with three scores, added 16 catches for 136, and banked 101.9 fantasy points on a 54.5% snap share. The explosiveness is real: 13 runs of 10-plus, four of 20-plus, a 59-yard touchdown, and 2.37 yards after contact per attempt behind a line that left him 23 negative-yardage carries. His -0.075 EPA per play is below average but a clear step up from 2024's -0.128 on far heavier volume.

The ceiling is gated by Omarion Hampton, who locks down the LAC starting job, with Keaton Mitchell pushing for the No. 2 role. As a re-signed exclusive-rights back drafted around pick 227, Vidal is a late-round handcuff. But he flashed RB2 weeks while Hampton's foot healed, so any injury makes him an immediate weekly play.

11649.name: Kendall Milton
11649.headline: Milton waits behind Brown as deep insurance
11649.body:
There is almost nothing on the ledger yet. Milton logged a single game in 2025, took just two carries for two yards, and never crossed a 6% snap share. A 137th positional rank and 0.2 fantasy points tell the whole story, even if the lone bright spot, a 0.429 EPA per play across that tiny sample, hints at the burst that made the 230-pound Georgia product an intriguing developmental back.

The path to relevance runs entirely through opportunity, not pedigree. With Chase Brown entrenched as the lead back and Khalil Herbert the likely next man up, Milton sits as a third-string insurance piece carrying no meaningful draft value and a dynasty number outside the top 175. He is a watch-list stash only, worth a roster spot in the deepest formats if an injury ahead of him suddenly cracks the depth chart open.

11650.name: Luke McCaffrey
11650.headline: Efficient flashes, but the volume never showed up
11650.body:
McCaffrey's 2025 numbers read like a tease: across nine games he turned just 15 targets into 11 grabs for 203 yards and 3 scores, posting a glittering 18.45 per catch, 2.16 yards per route run, a 0.56 EPA per play and a 66.7% success rate. The catch is the denominator. He ran routes on only 18.8% of dropbacks, drew a 3.5% target share and carried a 0.09 WOPR, so the efficiency rode a microscopic sample before a broken collarbone in Week 9 ended things early.

The deeper concern is that 2024 already gave a fuller picture: 24 targets, 168 yards, zero touchdowns and a 0.79 yards-per-route mark over 13 games. Buried on the depth chart at 24 and stuck near a 32% snap rate, he needs a real route to volume before he matters. Until that opportunity arrives, he is a late-round dart and a watch-list name, nothing more.

11651.name: Isaac Guerendo
11651.headline: Explosive McCaffrey handcuff dimmed by a torn pec
11651.body:
Guerendo's 2024 tape is the reason to care: on just 84 carries he ripped off 420 yards at 5.0 per tote with four scores, and the explosiveness was real -- a 76-yard burst, two runs of 30-plus, a 10.7% explosive-run rate, and a healthy 0.114 EPA per play across 16 games. He chipped in 15 grabs for 152 yards on limited routes, so the receiving floor exists. The catch is opportunity: a 22.9% snap share and 3.1% target share confirm he was strictly a complementary piece.

He's again penciled in to back up Christian McCaffrey, but a torn pectoral suffered in May threatens most of camp and clouds his early availability, while Jordan James and Kaelon Black push for the No. 2 job. With McCaffrey turning 30 off a 450-touch season, the handcuff appeal is genuine, yet a sub-100 RB ADP and uncertain timeline make him a late-round lottery ticket, not a target.

11655.name: Tyrone Tracy
11655.headline: Committee back with PPR floor, ceiling capped by usage
11655.body:
Tracy held a meaningful role in 2025 but slid from his 2024 baseline: 124.8 fantasy points across 15 games on a 54.5 percent snap share, with 176 carries for 740 yards at 4.2 per pop and two scores, down from 839 yards, 4.37 per carry, and five touchdowns over 17 games the year prior. The receiving work is the anchor, 36 catches on 48 targets for 288 yards and two touchdowns at a 9.5 percent target share, plus 314 yards after the catch. A -0.044 EPA per play and a thin 1.71 yards after contact flag efficiency limits.

The profile is steady rather than spiky: a 10.8 percent explosive-run rate and one broken tackle every 35.2 carries point to a back reliant on blocking and volume. With the Giants backfield muddied by rookie competition and Tracy fumbling five times across two seasons, his grip on lead duties is not secure. He lands as a PPR-leaning flex with weekly RB2 upside if he secures three-down work, a middle-round volume bet.

11716.name: Tip Reiman
11716.headline: Blocking Tight End Stuck Behind McBride's Shadow
11716.body:
Reiman's profile is a blocker's profile, and the numbers leave no ambiguity. Across three games in 2025 he saw just five targets on a 0.8% target share, turning 40 routes into 18 yards and 1.8 fantasy points before an ankle issue ended the year. His 0.45 yards per route run and 0.125 targets per route run confirm he is rarely a read in the passing game, and a 0.001 EPA per play means he added essentially nothing when the ball came his way.

2024 told the same story: 41.2% snaps but a 1.3% target share and 0.24 yards per route run over eight games. The role is in-line muscle, not receiving volume. With Trey McBride entrenched and Elijah Higgins competing for the No. 2 job, there is no realistic path to fantasy relevance. Leave him on the wire in every format until the depth chart changes.

11729.name: Sione Vaki
11729.headline: Special-teamer with no offensive role behind Detroit's stars
11729.body:
Vaki has been a non-factor on offense across two seasons: one carry for four yards in 2025 (a 0.1 percent snap share, RB136, 0.4 points) after six carries and three catches in 2024. The 2024 line showed a faint pulse with a 2.47 yards per route run on 15 routes, but the overall body of work is negligible, and his -0.882 EPA per play in 2025 underscores how little he has contributed when on the field. The fourth-round 2024 pick out of Utah has been primarily a special-teams and defensive-package value to this point.

The roster math is brutal. Vaki sits as Detroit's RB3 behind All-Pro Jahmyr Gibbs and veteran Isiah Pacheco, leaving no early-down, passing-down, or goal-line role to mine. Barring multiple injuries ahead of him, there is no standalone fantasy path in 2026. He is a clear waiver-wire name in redraft and only the thinnest of dynasty depth stashes given his draft pedigree and a possible hybrid role down the line.

11762.name: Anthony Gould
11762.headline: Buried Colts depth piece with no fantasy footprint
11762.body:
Gould's profile is a flatline. Across 11 games in 2025 he logged just an 8.7% snap rate, ran 28 routes, and was targeted twice, hauling in both for a grand total of 6 yards and 0.6 fantasy points. The underlying marks are damning: a 0.4% target share, a 0.005 WOPR, a 0.21 yards-per-route figure, and a -0.495 EPA per play with a 0% success rate. There is simply no role to build a fantasy case around, and his 2024 cameo (one catch, 23 yards in eight games) tells the same story.

The outside view matches the numbers. He sits around WR197 in expert rankings and is rostered in essentially no leagues, with a lingering foot issue further muddying any path to snaps. Consensus projections top out near 7 catches and 74 yards for the entire season. Until he carves out actual playing time in a crowded Indianapolis receiver room, there is nothing here for managers to chase.

11783.name: Ryan Flournoy
11783.headline: Elite efficiency, but the targets just aren't there yet
11783.body:
Flournoy's 2025 leap was real where it counts: 0.511 EPA per play and a 57.1% success rate ranked among the most efficient marks for any wideout, a massive jump from his -0.2 EPA rookie season. He turned 56 targets into 40 catches (71.4%), 475 yards, and 4 touchdowns across 15 games, posting a healthy 1.83 yards per route run with 7 red-zone looks. The talent and the per-snap production are undeniable.

The ceiling is capped by volume, not ability. A 9.2% target share and 44.8% snap rate place him third in line behind CeeDee Lamb and George Pickens, and his biggest outings came when Lamb sat. With a WR66 draft profile, he's a dynasty stash and an injury-contingent bench piece rather than a weekly redraft option. If a spot opens ahead of him, the efficiency suggests he'd run with it.

11806.name: Casey Washington
11806.headline: Deep-field flier whose snaps still haven't arrived
11806.body:
The profile reads like a player still searching for a foothold. Across five games in 2025 Washington ran just 94 routes, a 16.5% route rate that capped his target share at 2.7% and a 0.149 targets-per-route mark that never translated into volume. He hauled in 6 of 14 looks for 94 yards and no scores, a 42.9% catch rate dragged down by a sky-high 18.71 air yards per target. The efficiency flickers were real, though: a 0.266 EPA per play and a 1.00 yards-per-route-run hint at downfield juice on a tiny sample.

The role is the problem. Stuck deep on the Atlanta depth chart and recently a healthy scratch, he carries no guaranteed targets and an ECR near pick 574. A favorable late-season schedule offers nothing if he can't crack the rotation. Treat him as a watch-list dart, not a draftable asset, until the snap share climbs out of the teens.

11834.name: Devaughn Vele
11834.headline: Efficient deep threat buried on the depth chart
11834.body:
The numbers say Vele can play when given a route tree. Across nine games after the midseason move to New Orleans in 2025 he posted a 0.507 EPA per play and a 61.5% success rate, hauling in 25 of 39 looks for 293 yards and 2 scores. His 2024 Denver tape was even sharper: 0.638 EPA per play, a 74.5% catch rate on 55 targets, and 1.66 yards per route run. At 6-foot-5 he wins down the field, with 11-plus air yards per target and a 17.3 air yards per reception mark that screams vertical role.

The catch is opportunity. A 50.2% snap share and 36.8% route rate kept him from ever consolidating volume, and a season-ending shoulder injury on IR clouds his timeline. Projections land near 35-423-3, and his ADP sits in the WR97 range. He is a late flier whose efficiency profile only pays off if the Saints carve out real snaps.

11959.name: Bryce Oliver
11959.headline: Deep-roster Titans WR still chasing snaps before fantasy matters
11959.body:
Oliver's profile remains microscopic. He logged just 2 games in 2025 on a 17.3% snap share, running 21 routes for a single target and one 8-yard catch. The 2024 sample was slightly larger but still negligible: 8 games, 29 routes, 9 targets, 6 grabs for 95 yards and a 1.8% target share. The flashes are real on a per-snap basis, his 3.28 yards per route run and 0.482 EPA per play in 2024 hint at efficiency when given a route, but the volume simply isn't there to build a fantasy case.

Tennessee tendered him, so he sticks on the roster, yet the expectation is another season buried in the depth chart. With an expert ranking outside the top 200 wideouts and effectively zero roster percentage, there's nothing actionable here. He's a watch-list name only if injuries open a path to consistent playing time.

11963.name: Chris Collier
11963.headline: Deep-roster Raider with a long climb from the practice squad
11963.body:
The numbers spell out exactly where Collier sits on the ladder. Across six 2024 appearances he logged just a 4.2% snap share, turning five carries into 12 yards at 2.4 a pop with a longest run of four. Two targets produced a single catch for negative four yards, and the efficiency markers are brutal: minus-0.658 EPA per play and a grand total of 0.8 fantasy points. There is no breakaway speed on tape (zero runs of 10-plus) and no broken tackles to point to.

The situation hasn't changed his standing. He's back on a reserve/future deal heading into the offseason program, carrying no ADP, no consensus ranking, and a projection sheet of zeros. Facing the league's 27th-toughest back schedule, he profiles as camp-body depth who must beat out bodies just to dress. Leave him on the waiver wire unless an injury cascade forces Las Vegas's hand.

11994.name: Kameron Johnson
11994.headline: Buried depth piece with no path to fantasy snaps
11994.body:
Johnson's 2025 ledger tells the story of a roster fringe player: 17 games but only a 9.7% snap share, 41 routes, and seven targets that produced four catches for 64 yards and one score. A 1.3% target share and 0.033 WOPR confirm he was an afterthought in the Tampa passing tree, even if the surface efficiency (16.0 yards per catch, 1.56 yards per route) looked tidy on a tiny sample. The lone interception when targeted and a 14.3% drop rate underscore how little margin a player this far down the depth chart gets.

The role projection for 2026 is bleak. He logged under two targets in eight of nine appearances late in the year, and forward outlooks peg him near WR-200-plus in dynasty value with essentially zero standard projection. Until injuries or attrition force him into real volume, he's a watch-list name only, not a draftable asset in any format.

12048.name: George Holani
12048.headline: Deep-roster Seahawks back with a thin path to touches
12048.body:
Holani's 2025 tape spells out the ceiling problem: 22 carries for 73 yards at 3.32 per pop, a brutal -0.395 EPA per play, and zero runs of even 10-plus yards across 10 games on a 5.5% snap share. He forced just three broken tackles and gained only 1.68 yards after contact, so there's no efficiency signal arguing he deserves a bigger workload. One short touchdown and 18.8 fantasy points is the whole season's footprint.

The depth chart only muddies things further. With Kenneth Walker gone and Charbonnet recovering from a knee injury, Seattle still slots a rookie and Emanuel Wilson ahead of Holani, leaving him fourth in line. Projections of roughly 39 carries, 164 yards and a touchdown match that backup reality. Draft him only as a final-pick dart, betting on a camp surprise rather than any established role.

12062.name: Cam Grandy
12062.headline: Deep-roster Bengals tight end still chasing snaps
12062.body:
Grandy remains a developmental piece who has yet to crack Cincinnati's rotation. His 2025 sample barely registered: two games, a 9.2% snap share, just five routes run, and two targets turned into two catches for seven yards. A 1.4 yards-per-route-run mark looks tidy, but a minus-0.176 EPA per play, a single fumble, and a 0.7% route share confirm how little he was trusted on the field.

The lone encouraging note traces to 2024, when 34 routes produced a 0.556 EPA per play, a 60% success rate, and two red-zone looks including an end-zone target. That flash of efficiency is the only reason to monitor him. With negligible roster rates and a tight-end ranking buried outside the top 100, he is a watch-list name, not a draftable asset, until the depth chart opens real opportunity ahead of him.

12374.name: Brenden Bates
12374.headline: Browns' depth tight end with a buried fantasy role
12374.body:
Bates barely registered in 2025, drawing five targets across five games while logging just a 27% snap share and a 6.9% route rate. The flashes were real but tiny: he caught four of those looks (80% catch rate), posted a 0.61 EPA per play and an 80% success rate, and ran routes at 1.14 yards per route run. With a 0.9% target share, a 0.119 target-per-route-run mark, and zero touchdowns, none of it translated to usable scoring — 4.8 fantasy points all year.

The arrow points sideways. He returns to Cleveland for another season buried in a crowded tight end room, where his snaps were rotational at best and his 42 routes left no path to volume. Drafted around TE142 in early rankings with no measurable ADP, he is a name to know only in the deepest dynasty formats. Until the depth chart clears, Bates stays off redraft boards entirely.

12455.name: Brashard Smith
12455.headline: Pass-catching specialist buried behind a rebuilt Chiefs backfield
12455.body:
Smith's rookie line is the story of a depth piece, not a runner to build around. Across 17 games he played just a 16.7% snap share, turning 44 carries into 151 yards at a thin 3.43 per attempt with a -0.131 EPA per play and only 1.34 yards after contact. He never reached a 20-plus rush and broke zero tackles, so the ground game isn't the path. The encouraging signal is on routes: 25 catches on 35 targets for 172 yards, a receiving touchdown, and 1.76 yards per route run hint at a usable third-down skill set.

The 2026 picture got crowded fast. Kansas City brought in Kenneth Walker III to lead, then added Emari Demercado and rookie Emmett Johnson, pushing Smith toward the back of the depth chart. His value is contingent and narrow, essentially a receiving-role flier and a Walker handcuff. Outside an injury ahead of him, treat him as a deep-league name to monitor in camp, not a draftable asset.

12457.name: Jaydon Blue
12457.headline: Explosive flashes, but Blue's rookie efficiency was thin
12457.body:
Blue's rookie tape was a study in contradiction. Across five games he handled just 38 carries on a 25.6% snap share, turning them into 129 yards at a meager 3.39 per pop with one score. The efficiency underneath was alarming: a minus-0.195 EPA per play, only 1.16 yards after contact per attempt, and seven carries swallowed for negative yardage. Yet the burst is real, with a 13.16% explosive-run rate, a 27-yard long, and a breakaway gear most backs his age lack.

The path forward is opportunity, not talent. He enters camp battling for the clear backup job behind Javonte Williams, and his late-season Week 18 start showed he can shoulder a real load when handed one. At an RB65-ish ADP, he's a cheap dart with standalone upside the moment Williams misses time. Until that backup role is locked, Blue is a speculative bench stash with league-winning handcuff equity.

12467.name: Jordan James
12467.headline: Buzzed-about handcuff stuck behind McCaffrey's heavy workload
12467.body:
James enters 2026 as a deep-bench lottery ticket, drafted around pick 210 (RB63) and rostered in just 3.5% of leagues. The path to relevance runs entirely through Christian McCaffrey, who turns 30 in June and absorbed 450 touches last year. James spent his first 14 weeks of 2025 as a healthy scratch before earning special-teams snaps, then logged six carries for 28 yards and a catch in the playoff loss to Seattle. The 49ers reportedly came away impressed this offseason.

The catch is a crowded depth chart: Kaelon Black, Isaac Guerendo, and veteran Brian Robinson all contest the No. 2 job, so a McCaffrey absence may trigger a committee rather than a clear bell cow. Projections of 102 carries, 416 rush yards, 16 catches, and roughly four touchdowns peg him as a late-round dart throw. Camp will decide whether he's the contingency to own or just another body.

12469.name: Dylan Sampson
12469.headline: Sampson's receiving spark is the late-round PPR play
12469.body:
The rookie tape screams pass-catcher. Across 15 games on just a 26.5% snap rate, Sampson turned 40 targets into 33 catches and a 1.82 yards per route run mark that ranked among the best at the position, all while drawing a 7.7% target share. The ground game lagged badly: 65 carries netted 2.69 yards a pop with zero rushing scores, a minus-0.095 EPA per play, and only 1.54 yards after contact, leaving him at RB54 on 54.6 fantasy points.

At 20, the path to volume runs through the backfield's health. With Jerome Ford gone, the third-down job is open, and Sampson already worked first-team reps while Quinshon Judkins rehabs a leg injury. Going around RB52 with an RB50 consensus rank, he's a sensible late-round dart in PPR, with real handcuff upside if Judkins misses time.

12471.name: DJ Giddens
12471.headline: Stashed behind Jonathan Taylor with thin standalone value
12471.body:
Giddens barely cracked the rotation as a rookie, logging just a 12% snap share across five games with 26 carries for 96 yards (3.69 per tote) and zero touchdowns. The efficiency markers were rough: a negative EPA per play (-0.058), only 1.85 yards after contact per attempt, zero broken tackles, and a single rush over 10 yards. He cleared 30% of snaps only once, in Week 18, and the six red-zone opportunities never converted. This was a backup buried behind a healthy lead back, finishing RB94 with 9.6 fantasy points.

The path forward is purely contingent. Taylor played all 17 games, and Giddens is still battling Seth McGowan just for the RB2 job. His college profile (six yards per carry, nearly 12 per catch at Kansas State) hints at upside, and projections of roughly 33 carries and nine catches reflect a deep stash. With an ADP around 257, he is a late-round handcuff who only matters if Taylor misses time.

12472.name: Raheim Sanders
12472.headline: Deep-League Dart Behind Cleveland's Crowded Backfield
12472.body:
Sanders' rookie tape was a small, inefficient sample: 27 carries for 92 yards (3.41 per pop) and one short touchdown across four games, with a modest 25.8% snap share. The advanced numbers hint at a runner who grinds rather than pops, churning 2.22 yards after contact per attempt against just 1.19 before it, with only one carry of 10-plus and zero breakaways. His 0.067 EPA per play and four targets on a 0.8% target share leave little for fantasy to grab right now, which is how he lands at RB88 in our rankings.

The path to relevance is purely about opportunity. Quinshon Judkins owns the early-down work, and Dylan Sampson is the other body fighting for the backup role, so Sanders enters camp as a No. 3 with a draft cost near pick 371 and roughly half a percent rostered. He's a handcuff-of-a-handcuff: only worth a deep-league flier, and only if injuries reshuffle the depth chart ahead of him.

12473.name: Mitchell Evans
12473.headline: Touchdown Lottery Ticket Waiting On A Real Role
12473.body:
Evans graded out as the TE57 last season, and the underlying numbers explain why: 14 games, a 37.4% snap rate, and just 142 routes that produced 25 targets and a 5.2% target share. The volume is thin, but the efficiency flickers. He posted a 68% success rate, 0.128 EPA per play, a 76% catch rate, and 1.2 yards per route run, turning limited looks into 19 grabs for 171 yards and two scores. Four red-zone targets, three of them inside the ten, hint at where his value lives.

That scoring upside is the entire case right now. With a 0.176 targets-per-route mark and a sub-0.10 WOPR, he is a touchdown-dependent flier rather than a weekly play. Carried at roughly TE60 in early drafts, Evans is purely a deep-league dart. If the snaps and route volume climb in Carolina's pecking order, the red-zone profile gives him a real path; until then, hold the pick.

12474.name: Woody Marks
12474.headline: Texans workhorse-lite back is a steady PPR flex
12474.body:
Marks turned his rookie year into real volume: 16 games, a 51.5 percent snap share, 196 carries for 703 yards and 304 routes run, finishing RB27 with 121.1 fantasy points. The receiving role is the hook—24 of 36 targets for 208 yards and three scores, with 223 yards after the catch, props up his PPR floor. He also handled goal-line work with 9 goal-line carries and 41 red-zone opportunities, a usage profile that punches above his 4th-round draft slot out of USC.

The concern is efficiency. A 3.59 yards-per-carry mark, a -0.095 EPA per play, just 1.77 yards after contact, and a broken tackle only every 32.7 carries point to a grinder rather than an explosive runner, and 28 carries stopped for negative yards underline the lack of creation. With every-down work but thin big-play juice, Marks profiles as a high-floor, low-ceiling RB2/flex—a dependable PPR starter for steady touches, not league-winning spikes.

12476.name: Devin Neal
12476.headline: Efficiency-starved rookie buried behind Kamara until the depth chart shifts
12476.body:
Neal's rookie tape reads like a committee back hunting for a role: 57 carries for 206 yards at just 3.61 a pop, a brutal minus-0.133 EPA per play, and only a 5.26 percent explosive-run rate across nine games before a Week 15 hamstring injury ended his year. He created next to nothing on his own, averaging 1.93 yards after contact with a single broken tackle on the season. The one bright spot was passing-game involvement: 17 catches on 19 targets for 104 yards, with 138 yards after the catch showing soft hands out of the backfield.

The path forward is entirely situational. As long as Alvin Kamara holds the lead role, Neal is a dart throw drafted near pick 322 and rostered in barely one percent of leagues. If Kamara is moved, a camp battle with Kendre Miller decides the handcuff, and Neal's receiving chops give him a real lane. Until then, treat him as a late-round speculation only.

12481.name: Cam Skattebo
12481.headline: Bell-cow workload returns once the ankle clears
12481.body:
Skattebo's rookie tape is a workhorse profile cut short: 101 carries for 410 yards (4.06 per tote) plus 24 grabs on a 6.4% target share across just eight games before a Week 8 ankle and fibula fracture ended things. The receiving chops are real, with 1.56 yards per route run and 209 yards after the catch, and the goal-line trust shows up in nine goal-line carries, 27 red-zone touches and seven scores. The -0.074 EPA per play and 1.9 yards after contact flag a grinder more than a creator, but volume covers that.

The Giants declined to add a back, leaving him atop the depth chart over Tyrone Tracy with only a rookie lineman bolstering the front. He's reportedly tracking toward training-camp readiness. An easy early schedule and a projected 234-carry, 1,000-yard role make him a sturdy RB2 with RB1 upside if the leg holds.

12482.name: Savion Williams
12482.headline: Athletic gadget WR flashed efficiency but needs a real route role
12482.body:
Williams barely played as a rookie, logging an 11.5 percent snap share over 12 games with just 37 routes and a 7.2 percent route rate, which capped him at WR159 and 15.5 fantasy points. The microscopic sample was efficient, though: 10 catches on 10 targets for a 100 percent catch rate, 78 yards, a score, and a strong 2.11 yards per route run. Green Bay used him as a manufactured-touch weapon, shown by a tiny 1.7 air yards per target and four red-zone looks.

The path forward is the issue. Williams sits as the LWR2 behind Christian Watson in a deep Packers receiver room that also features Matthew Golden and Jayden Reed. A 2.2 percent target share gives him no standalone value, and Green Bay spreads the ball too thin for a low-volume gadget piece to reach weekly relevance. His athleticism and red-zone usage make him a dynasty stash worth monitoring for a role expansion, but in redraft he is a waiver-wire flier at best.

12483.name: Jack Bech
12483.headline: Buried in the pecking order, but the talent's real
12483.body:
Bech's rookie line tells the story of a player who never got off the bench: 39.8% snaps, a 32.5% route share, and just 29 targets across 14 games for a thin 5.9% target share and a 0.14 WOPR. The efficiency followed the usage down, with -0.07 EPA per play, a 48.3% success rate, and a meager 1.2 yards per route run. He caught 69% of his looks for 224 yards and zero scores, hardly the breakout TCU hype suggested.

The path forward runs through volume he doesn't yet have. He sits behind Brock Bowers, Tre Tucker, and Jalen Nailor in the target queue, and the quarterback picture, Kirk Cousins or a rookie, is unsettled under Klint Kubiak. Drafted around WR100 with projections near 41-483-2, he's a late-round dart worth a flier. Just understand you're betting on a role he has to win, not one he holds.

12484.name: Jayden Higgins
12484.headline: Efficient rookie flashes scoring upside behind Houston's pecking order
12484.body:
Higgins' rookie tape graded out better than his counting stats suggest. Across 17 games he posted a 0.26 EPA per play and a 51.5% success rate on just 68 targets, turning a modest 12.3% target share into six touchdowns and 525 yards. The downfield role is real: 12.3 air yards per target, a 17.6% air-yards share, and eight red-zone looks point to a player the staff trusts near the goal line. His 1.54 yards per route run is respectable for a second-rounder still earning snaps.

The ceiling hinges on volume. At 57.2% snaps he split work with Jaylin Noel, Xavier Hutchinson and Tank Dell, and Houston's run-leaning offense caps the passing pie. Win that camp battle and the No. 2 job behind Nico Collins is his, with C.J. Stroud feeding a proven scorer. Until the snap rate climbs, treat him as a late-round dart with genuine touchdown equity.

12485.name: Tez Johnson
12485.headline: Tiny rookie sleeper rode TDs to thin output
12485.body:
Johnson, a 2025 seventh-round flier (Pick 235, Oregon), carved a rotational role in Tampa Bay, playing 15 games on a 47.1 percent snap share. His efficiency belied his draft slot: a 0.371 EPA per play, 50 percent success rate and 63.6 catch rate on 44 targets, turning 28 grabs into 322 yards. The headline was five touchdowns, which inflated his 64.4 fantasy points well past what his 8.1 percent target share and 1.16 yards per route run would normally support.

That TD dependence is the warning: with seven red-zone targets driving the scoring, regression looms if usage does not climb. As an undersized complementary piece behind Tampa Bay top options, his fantasy value is situational and format-dependent. The upside case is an expanded route share in year two unlocking PPR appeal, but the realistic 2026 outlook is a deep-league dart and bye-week streamer. Leave him on the waiver wire in standard redraft until volume proves real.

12487.name: Terrance Ferguson
12487.headline: Big-play rookie TE buried in crowded Rams rotation
12487.body:
Ferguson, the Rams second-round rookie (Pick 46, Oregon), flashed vertical juice rarely seen at tight end: in 11 games on a thin 36.7 percent snap share he turned 25 targets into 11 catches for 231 yards, three scores and a sparkling 21 yards per reception. His 18.64 air yards per target and 0.295 EPA per play scream big-play threat, but a 44 percent catch rate and 17.36 yards before the catch per grab reveal a feast-or-famine, downfield-only role with little underneath volume.

That profile produced just 41.1 fantasy points and a 4.3 percent target share, the mark of a complementary piece behind Los Angeles veterans. The path to fantasy relevance runs through expanded snaps and red-zone work, where he saw three targets and two end-zone looks. Year two could bring a leap if the Rams clear the depth chart, making him a worthwhile dynasty stash. For 2026 redraft, treat him as a TD-dependent streaming dart, not a startable weekly tight end.

12489.name: RJ Harvey
12489.headline: Harvey's Receiving Chops Carry Crowded Backfield Risk
12489.body:
Harvey's rookie line tells a split story: 540 rushing yards on just 3.7 per carry across 146 attempts, but a far stickier receiving profile with 47 catches on a 9.9% target share, 356 yards and five receiving scores. His efficiency as a pure runner was middling, evidenced by a slightly negative -0.014 EPA per play, 1.79 yards after contact, and 17 carries stuffed for negative yardage. The juice came in chunks, three runs of 30-plus and a 50-yard touchdown, plus seven rushing scores and 39 red-zone opportunities that pushed him to 159.6 fantasy points and an RB29 finish over 17 games.

The 2026 picture is murkier. Dobbins is back healthy and rookie Jonah Coleman crowds the goal-line work, capping the volume that fueled Harvey's late-season RB13 surge. He profiles as a passing-down asset and PPR flex rather than a featured back, with a labrum surgery worth tracking through camp. Draft him as a Round 7 upside swing.

12490.name: Bhayshul Tuten
12490.headline: Etienne's exit hands Tuten a real lead-back runway
12490.body:
Tuten's rookie tape is more efficient than the box score suggests. On just 83 carries and a 21.1% snap share, he turned 27 red-zone opportunities and seven goal-line carries into five rushing scores, plus two more through the air, banking 78.6 fantasy points across 15 games. The raw 3.7 yards per carry is dragged down by contact he wasn't creating himself: only 1.65 yards before contact versus 2.05 after, with five broken tackles and a tidy success-rate profile. The -0.039 EPA per play and 6.0% explosive-run rate are the warning signs that volume, not bursts, will drive him.

That volume is now in play. With Etienne gone to New Orleans, Tuten projects as the early-down lead, with Chris Rodriguez contesting goal-line work and LeQuint Allen owning third downs. He goes around RB25 with mid-round redraft cost. Win camp decisively and the standalone upside jumps; lose the goal-line battle and he's a touchdown-dependent flex.

12491.name: Corey Kiner
12491.headline: Efficient on a sliver of snaps, buried on the depth chart
12491.body:
The 2025 sample is thin but flattering: 12 carries for 58 yards at 4.83 a pop, a tidy 0.097 EPA per play, and 2.42 yards after contact per attempt across just four games on a 13.2% snap share. He even flashed receiving value with two grabs on three targets, posting a 0.5 target share when on the field. Nothing here screams future bell cow, but the per-touch numbers say he didn't waste his looks.

The problem is opportunity, not talent. Kiner sits deep in a crowded Arizona backfield, stuck behind a wall of backs and unlikely to climb without injuries ahead of him. With no rushing scores and a ranking outside the top 130 at the position, he's a roster-clogger in standard formats. File him as a deep-dynasty stash whose efficiency earns a watch if the depth chart thins.

12492.name: Pat Bryant
12492.headline: Efficient rookie flashes, but the volume isn't there
12492.body:
Bryant's rookie tape rewards a second look. Across 13 games he ran 291 routes on a 45.8% route share, drawing 49 targets for 31 grabs, 378 yards and a score. The efficiency markers pop: a 0.29 EPA per play, a 57.1% success rate, and eight red-zone looks with four trips inside the 10. He wasn't asked to do much downfield (8.4% target share, 1.3 yards per route run), yet he converted what came his way.

The problem is the opportunity ceiling, not the player. Denver added another mouth to feed, slotting Bryant behind Courtland Sutton and into a crowded battle with Troy Franklin and Marvin Mims for the No. 3 role. At a WR85 draft cost he's a late-round dart, not a roster lock. Bank the per-snap quality and the goal-line involvement as the upside; a midseason injury ahead of him is the realistic path to relevance.

12493.name: Oronde Gadsden
12493.headline: Breakout rookie TE locks in weekly starter status
12493.body:
Gadsden's rookie year was an outright breakout for a fifth-round pick. Over 15 games on a 62.6 percent snap share, the 22-year-old ran 381 routes and converted a 12.7 percent target share into 49 catches for 664 yards (13.55 per reception) and three touchdowns, totaling 82.4 fantasy points and a TE11 finish. The efficiency is excellent: 1.74 yards per route run, a 0.286 WOPR, and a 0.239 EPA per play with a 63.8 percent success rate. He was a true downfield seam threat, with 11 grabs of 20-plus yards and a 53-yard long.

The red-zone usage cements the ceiling: 15 red-zone targets and five end-zone looks, room to grow the three-score output. Six drops on 55 catchable balls is the lone blemish. With an entrenched role in the Chargers' offense and rising target volume, he profiles as a low-end TE1 with upside for more. He is a weekly starter in all formats and a strong buy in dynasty given his age and trajectory.

12495.name: Ollie Gordon
12495.headline: Power back stuck behind Achane, redraft non-starter
12495.body:
Gordon's rookie tape was rough on efficiency. Across 17 games on a thin 22.4 percent snap share, he managed 70 carries for 199 yards, a brutal 2.84 yards per carry, plus seven catches for 32 yards, totaling 47.1 fantasy points and an RB71 rank. His -0.165 EPA per play, 16 tackles-for-loss absorbed, and 1.16 yards before contact per attempt point to a runner who struggled to find daylight. The redeeming notes: three rushing touchdowns on six goal-line carries, six broken tackles, and 1.69 yards after contact show short-yardage value.

The role is the problem. In Miami he sits behind explosive lead back De'Von Achane with Jaylen Wright also in the mix, capping Gordon as a goal-line and early-down complement rather than a volume play. His standalone profile is touchdown-dependent with no receiving role to lean on. He is a late-round dart and handcuff stash whose value spikes only if Achane misses time; otherwise leave him benched in redraft.

12496.name: Tai Felton
12496.headline: Buried Vikings rookie carries deep-dynasty intrigue only
12496.body:
Minnesota spent a third-round pick (102nd overall) on the Maryland product in 2025, but his rookie year produced almost nothing: a 4.4 percent snap share, 21 routes, and three targets across 12 games. He caught all three for 25 yards and 2.5 fantasy points, ranking 195th at the position. The microscopic sample did flash efficiency, a 0.461 EPA per play and 1.19 yards per route, but on volume this thin those numbers carry no predictive weight.

The 2026 path is blocked. Justin Jefferson and Jordan Addison headline the room, with T.J. Hockenson commanding tight-end targets, leaving Felton fighting for the third or fourth receiver job. Mid-round draft capital and youth (he turns 23 this season) keep a flicker of dynasty upside alive if an injury opens snaps, but for redraft there is nothing to roster. Treat him as a deep-dynasty stash and nothing more in 2026.

12497.name: Tory Horton
12497.headline: Big-play rookie flash earns sleeper dynasty buy
12497.body:
Horton packed a lot of upside into a tiny rookie sample. The 2025 fifth-rounder played eight games on a 48.1 percent snap share, running 136 routes for 22 targets and 13-161-5, with an eye-popping five touchdowns on just a 4.8 percent target share. The downfield profile is the draw: 15.32 air yards per target, 25.92 air yards per reception, and a 0.466 EPA per play with a 54.5 percent success rate and zero drops on 13 catchable balls, elite efficiency for a Day 3 receiver buried behind Jaxon Smith-Njigba.

The touchdown rate (five scores on 13 grabs) is wildly unsustainable, and his 0.135 WOPR and 1.18 yards per route flag how thin the volume was. Still behind JSN and Jake Bobo on the Seahawks chart, Horton needs a target bump to matter weekly, but the 22-year-old vertical separator offers breakout equity if Seattle expands his role. He is a late-round dart in redraft and, more compellingly, a worthwhile dynasty stash with WR3 upside.

12498.name: Mason Taylor
12498.headline: Ascending Jets tight end carries real PPR upside
12498.body:
Taylors rookie season delivered the rare thing for a young tight end: a real role. The 21-year-old played 13 games on an 81.1 percent snap share, ran 350 routes (63.5 percent route rate) and commanded a 13.8 percent target share, turning 65 looks into 44 catches for 369 yards and a score. His 0.133 EPA per play and 56.9 percent success rate signal efficient usage, and a 0.186 targets-per-route mark plus 8 red-zone targets show a player the Jets trust. The 44.9 fantasy points ranked TE30, but that undersells the trajectory.

The blemish is reliability: 8 drops on 52 catchable targets (12.3 percent) capped his output, and 3.02 yards after the catch per reception is merely adequate. A 2025 second-round LSU product, he profiles as a volume-driven target earner whose floor should climb in year two as the drops regress. With route and target volume already locked in, Taylor is a back-end PPR starter and high-upside streamer worth a mid-round dart.

12499.name: Elic Ayomanor
12499.headline: Big target share, broken efficiency: profit hinges on cleaner hands
12499.body:
Ayomanor's rookie volume was real: an 80.6% snap rate across 488 routes, a 16.8% target share, and a hefty 28.1% air-yards share that pushed his WOPR to 0.448. The Titans aimed deep with him, averaging 12.65 air yards per target, but the production cratered on contact with the ball. A 46.1% catch rate, five drops (5.6%), and a brutal -0.108 EPA per play with a 40.4% success rate left him at just 515 yards and 1.06 yards per route. The downfield role plus eight red-zone and six end-zone looks netted only four scores.

The path forward is murky. Tennessee's added pass-catchers, dropping him into a crowded WR3 mix rather than the de facto target hog injuries made him in 2025. His near-undraftable cost reflects that, with redraft value confined to the deepest formats. The talent and route volume are there, but until the catch rate and drops improve, he's a dart throw, not a target.

12500.name: Quinn Ewers
12500.headline: Seventh-round flier on Miami bench, deep stash only
12500.body:
Ewers got a four-game cameo as a rookie and the tape was rough: 55-of-83 (66.3 percent) for 622 yards, three touchdowns against three picks, a -0.103 EPA per play and a meager 39.4 percent success rate. He pushed the ball almost nowhere, with completed air yards of just 4.9 per completion and a 12.1 percent deep-attempt rate, while absorbing eight sacks and fumbling three times behind a 22.3 percent pressure rate. The 32.2 points and QB46 rank confirm replacement-level output.

As a 2025 seventh-rounder (Pick 231 out of Texas), Ewers profiles as a developmental arm buried on Miami depth chart rather than a 2026 fantasy asset. His pocket-only skill set offers no rushing floor (eight carries, 19 yards) to bail out shaky efficiency, so even an injury-forced start would be a streaming gamble. Leave him on the wire in redraft and treat him only as a speculative dynasty stash.

12501.name: Matthew Golden
12501.headline: Big-play Packers rookie still hunting consistent target volume
12501.body:
Goldens rookie year flashed the explosive profile that made him the 23rd pick out of Texas, but Green Bays committee buried his volume. He played 14 games on just a 53.2 percent snap share with a 48.2 percent route rate, drawing a thin 9.6 percent target share. The efficiency, however, popped: 12.55 air yards per target, a 1.45 yards-per-route mark, a 0.382 EPA per play and only one drop on the season (2.3 percent). On 44 targets he caught 29 for 361 yards, with 4 grabs of 30-plus, but found the end zone zero times.

That touchdown drought and the lack of red-zone usage (2 targets) are the ceiling killers, as is a crowded Packers receiver room that suppresses any single players workload. The talent and first-round draft capital argue for a year-two leap if route share expands, making him a tantalizing dynasty hold. For 2026 redraft, the wide-open target tree keeps him a boom-or-bust WR4 and bench stash rather than a trusted weekly flex.

12502.name: Gunnar Helm
12502.headline: Titans starting TE with a clear path to volume
12502.body:
Helm's rookie tape sketches a real foundation. Across 16 games he ran 270 routes, drew a 10.4% target share and turned 55 looks into 44 catches for 357 yards and two scores, hauling in 80% of what came his way with a clean 3.6% drop rate. The 1.32 yards per route run and modestly positive 0.07 EPA per play are solid markers for a 22-year-old playing 52.6% of snaps, and eight red-zone targets hint at scoring upside if the role grows.

Chig Okonkwo's exit to Washington hands Helm the primary pass-catching tight end job, with Daniel Bellinger projected mainly as a blocker. Cam Ward's late-season strides matter here, and the projected jump to roughly 50 catches and 427 yards reflects that bigger workload. Going around TE25 at an ADP near 176, he's a low-cost streamer with breakout equity rather than a set-and-forget starter.

12503.name: Isaiah Bond
12503.headline: Boom-or-bust field-stretcher buried on a crowded depth chart
12503.body:
Bond's rookie tape reads like a deep-shot specialist still searching for a role. Across 15 games he played just 48.1% of snaps, drew a thin 8.4% target share, and converted only 18 of 44 looks for 338 yards and no scores. The 40.9% catch rate is ugly, but it's a byproduct of usage: his 17.07 air yards per target ranked among the most aggressive in the league, and he posted a 18.78 yard average with a 0.265 WOPR. The efficiency cratered, though, with a negative EPA per play and a 31.8% success rate that landed him at WR82.

The path forward is narrow. Cleveland spent early draft capital on two rookie receivers, stacking competition in front of a player who never locked down a snap count. His 1.03 yards per route run doesn't force the issue, and there's no clear projection for volume until he earns it. Treat him as a late-round dart with big-play upside, nothing more.

12504.name: Kaleb Johnson
12504.headline: Buried rookie must show more before fantasy matters
12504.body:
Johnson's rookie tape was a cautionary file: just 28 carries for 69 yards across 10 games, a brutal 2.46 per attempt and a -0.249 EPA per play that ranked among the worst at the position. His 9.1% snap share and zero rushing scores tell the story of a back the staff barely trusted, and the underlying contact numbers offer little hope of a hidden gem, with only 1.21 yards after contact per attempt and two broken tackles all year.

The path forward is just as crowded. He sits behind Jaylen Warren and Rico Dowdle in a three-man committee, projecting as no better than an RB3 with negligible standalone value in standard leagues. The 2024 college profile (1,537 yards, 21 touchdowns, 22 catches) hints at upside if injuries open a lane, but until he earns real volume he is a dynasty stash and watch-list name, not a draftable asset.

12505.name: Jalen Royals
12505.headline: Deep-bench Chiefs flier whose path runs through camp reps
12505.body:
Royals' rookie line barely registers: two appearances, 34 routes, a 5.4% route share, and three targets that produced two grabs for four yards. The advanced markers are as cold as the box score, with a minus-0.542 EPA per play, a 0% success rate, and 0.12 yards per route run. Snap rate sat at 19% and his WOPR (0.012) confirms he was an afterthought in the passing game, finishing 216th at the position.

The 2026 case is entirely about opportunity, not production banked so far. With the room thin behind the top options and Andy Reid praising his camp work after first-team reps, there's a real lane to climb the rotation. Projections still peg him as a deep flier near pick 281 (roughly WR116) at around 28.9 PPR points. Don't spend draft capital here; stash him on the watch list and pounce off waivers if the targets and Mahomes chemistry finally arrive.

12506.name: Harold Fannin
12506.headline: Fannin's rookie usage screams breakout in year two
12506.body:
As a 21-year-old rookie, Fannin already commanded a 20.5% target share across 16 games, drawing 107 looks on a 72.2% route rate and an 0.244 targets-per-route-run mark that reads like a featured pass-catcher, not a part-time tight end. He turned that volume into 72 grabs for 731 yards and six touchdowns, posting a 0.427 WOPR and just a 3.7% drop rate. The efficiency lagged the workload at 1.67 yards per route and 10.15 per catch, but a 42.1% success rate and positive EPA per play signal a player the offense trusts.

The profile is built on opportunity, and there is room to climb. Ten red-zone targets and three end-zone looks hint at touchdown upside that should expand with more snaps than last year's 75.9%. With elite YAC ability shown by 4.89 yards after the catch per reception, Fannin projects as a TE1 ascending into a larger role at age 22.

12507.name: Omarion Hampton
12507.headline: First-round bell-cow flashed RB1 upside in nine games
12507.body:
Hampton's rookie season was a strong, injury-shortened sample. In nine games on a 63.4 percent snap share, the 22nd overall pick ran 124 times for 545 yards (4.4 YPC) and four scores while adding 32 catches on 35 targets for 192 yards and a touchdown, banking 103.7 fantasy points and an RB40 finish despite the missed time. The explosiveness was real: an 11.29 percent explosive-run rate, a 54-yard touchdown, 12 broken tackles, and 245 receiving yards after the catch. The pass-game role, 207 routes and 0.93 yards per route run, gives him a three-down floor.

That receiving usage elevates the profile from volume runner to genuine PPR weapon, and a full Chargers workload projects comfortably inside RB1 range. The 2025 availability concern that capped him at nine games is the chief risk, alongside a modest 1.53 yards after contact. Health permitting, he is a confident every-week starter and a top-12 dynasty asset; draft him as a fantasy RB1.

12508.name: Jaxson Dart
12508.headline: Dart's legs carry QB1 upside as Year 2 arrives
12508.body:
Dart's rookie tape is a runner's profile wearing a passer's jersey: 487 rushing yards and 9 rushing touchdowns on 86 carries powered a QB26 finish (241.6 points) across just 14 games, with a healthy 7.7 yards per scramble. The arm was steadier than flashy — 63.7% completions, 15 touchdowns to 5 picks, and a barely-positive 0.065 EPA per play on a 45.2% success rate. A -0.2 CPOE and 12% deep-attempt rate confirm he won and won't yet on volume and his legs, not pinpoint accuracy.

The surrounding math finally cooperates. A healthier Malik Nabers, plus Isaiah Likely, Darnell Mooney and an upgraded line, should lift the 6.7 yards-per-attempt floor, and projections near 3,600 passing yards with 23 scores plus 540 rushing yards point to weekly QB1 weeks. The catch is durability — five concussion checks and two missed games last season. If he slides sooner, his Round 7-8 ADP is a value.

12509.name: Tre' Harris
12509.headline: Rookie flashes efficiency but buried in pecking order
12509.body:
Harris closed his rookie year as WR85 with a meager 39.4 fantasy points, a function of role rather than talent: the Ole Miss product ran routes on just 43.5 percent of dropbacks and saw a 7.9 percent target share across 16 games. When targeted he was sharp, catching 30 of 43 for 324 yards on a 69.8 percent catch rate, a 0.356 EPA per play, and a 60.5 percent success rate, with a clean 2.3 percent drop rate that hints at reliable hands on a 9.26 air-yards-per-target diet.

The problem is opportunity. A 48.5 percent snap share and a 0.18 WOPR leave him a clear depth piece behind Ladd McConkey and Quentin Johnston in a Chargers offense that spreads it around. A full-time route share would unlock real upside given the efficiency, but that is a projection, not a present reality. For 2026 redraft he is a late-round dart and bench stash with breakout equity if injuries open snaps; otherwise leave him on the wire in shallow leagues.

12512.name: Quinshon Judkins
12512.headline: Workhorse usage masks a rookie efficiency problem
12512.body:
Judkins handled 230 carries across 14 games as a rookie and finished the RB24, but the surface counting stats hide some warts. He averaged a thin 3.6 yards per carry, posted a negative -0.126 EPA per play, and forced a broken tackle just once every 20.9 attempts, gaining only 2.2 yards after contact. The volume was real, though: 33 red-zone opportunities and 11 goal-line carries turned into seven scores, and four runs of 30-plus yards show the home-run gear is there.

The receiving role barely exists, with a 6.9% target share, 26 grabs, and 0.96 yards per route run capping his PPR ceiling. He's recovering from a Week 16 ankle and fibula fracture but tracking toward the opener. A rebuilt line should lift the efficiency floor. Bank on a 300-touch projection and draft him as a high-volume RB2 with real touchdown equity.

12514.name: Emeka Egbuka
12514.headline: Evans gone, Egbuka inherits Tampa's alpha target share
12514.body:
The rookie year already screamed WR1 in the making. Egbuka ran a route on 82.9% of dropbacks, commanded a 23.5% target share and a 34.9% air-yards share, and posted a 0.596 WOPR on 127 looks across 17 games. He turned that into 938 yards and six scores at 14.89 per catch, with 14 red-zone targets confirming he was already trusted near the goal line. The blemishes are fixable: a 49.6% catch rate, nine drops, and a modest 41.7% success rate dragged the efficiency.

With Mike Evans gone, the competition for targets thins and Egbuka steps into the clear alpha role in a soft receiver schedule. The volume and downfield usage are the foundation, and a healthier Baker Mayfield raises the ceiling toward top-10. Projections near 1,100 yards and seven-plus touchdowns are realistic. Draft him as a high-end WR2 in the WR20 range and bet on the target floor.

12515.name: Donovan Edwards
12515.headline: Deep Dolphins depth piece with no path to touches yet
12515.body:
Edwards joined Miami off Washington's practice squad and sits at the very bottom of a crowded backfield, buried behind De'Von Achane with Jaylen Wright and Ollie Gordon also ahead of him. The consensus pegs him as roughly the 140th running back, ECR draft rank #502, and rostered in essentially zero leagues. His arrival was a roster-shuffle move tied to Darren Waller landing on IR, not a signal of any expanded role, and the projections reflect it: about 31 carries for 130 yards and a score plus two catches, totaling under 21 fantasy points for the year.

There is no realistic standalone value here right now. The 23-year-old former Michigan back is a watch-list name only, someone whose situation could shift if an injury opens snaps in front of him. Until that happens, leave him on waivers and revisit only if the depth chart thins out.

12517.name: Colston Loveland
12517.headline: Loveland's Rookie Spike Points to a TE1 Leap
12517.body:
The rookie tape was loud. Across 16 games Loveland posted a 0.591 EPA per play and a 59.8% success rate on his targets, elite efficiency for a 21-year-old learning the position. He turned 82 targets into 58 grabs, 713 yards and six scores while running routes on 63% of dropbacks, and his 0.218 targets per route run plus a 15.4% target share signal a player the offense already trusts. He dropped exactly one catchable ball.

The red-zone usage is what pushes the projection higher: 14 red-zone looks and six end-zone targets at age 21 hint at a scoring floor that climbs with volume. At 1.9 yards per route run he is still rounding out as a downfield weapon, but the late-season target surge and a tightening rapport with Caleb Williams make this an obvious year-two ascent. He profiles as a top-five tight end with genuine top-three upside.

12518.name: Tyler Warren
12518.headline: Elite rookie TE arrives as a weekly TE1 anchor
12518.body:
Warren validated his No. 14 overall pedigree immediately, ranking TE5 by our metric with a monster 17-game debut: 112 targets on a 21.1 percent share, 76 catches for 817 yards and four scores, on an 89.2 percent route share. His 0.219 targets per route run and 0.413 WOPR are alpha-level usage for a tight end, and a +0.158 EPA per play with a 49.1 percent success rate confirm real production. The 21 red-zone and 11 end-zone looks signal touchdown upside.

The yards profile leaves room to grow: just 10.75 yards per reception and a modest 5.38 air yards per target paint him as a high-floor underneath chain-mover, and only two drops on 78 catchable balls shows reliable hands. At 23 with Daniel Jones now steering Indianapolis, the target volume should hold and the four touchdowns look like a low-water mark. Warren is a set-and-forget weekly TE1, a top-five draft option at a position where his floor is a genuine roster-builder.

12519.name: Luther Burden
12519.headline: The efficiency is real, the volume isn't yet
12519.body:
Burden's rookie tape screams breakout the moment you account for the workload. On just 225 routes and a 37.8% route share, he posted a 0.501 EPA per play and a 58.3% success rate -- elite marks that usually belong to entrenched starters, not 21-year-olds buried at 39.7% snaps. He turned 60 targets into 47 catches (78.3% catch rate), 652 yards, and 2.9 yards per route run, flashing real after-catch juice at 7.1 YAC per reception.

The ceiling case is obvious; the floor is the problem. An 11.3% target share and 0.234 WOPR tell you the volume simply wasn't there, and Rome Odunze plus Colston Loveland complicate any leap in Ben Johnson's offense. If the slot role expands to match the per-route efficiency, Burden is a league-winner. Draft him as the high-variance upside swing he is -- pay a round early or pass.

12521.name: Elijah Arroyo
12521.headline: Rookie flashes downfield, but a backup role caps the ceiling
12521.body:
Arroyo's rookie line tells a clear story: 15 grabs for 179 yards and a score on 26 targets across 13 games, with a 39.5% snap share that pinned him to a part-time role. The efficiency numbers are ugly enough to demand patience, a minus-0.625 EPA per play and a 42.3% success rate, dragged down by three interceptions thrown his way. Yet the seam usage is intriguing, 8.46 air yards per target and a 14.67 average depth on receptions, plus five red-zone looks that hint at scoring upside if the volume ever arrives.

The problem is the depth chart. He sits behind A.J. Barner and earned just a 5.7% target share, so the path to relevance runs entirely through expanded snaps. With an ADP outside the top 40 tight ends and projections near 17-190-1, he's a speculative late-round dart, worth a stash in deep leagues but nothing you draft expecting weekly production.

12522.name: Cam Ward
12522.headline: Rookie efficiency was ugly, but the arrow finally points up
12522.body:
The rookie tape was rough: a minus-0.165 EPA per play, a 37.9% success rate, and a CPOE of minus-2.9 across 540 attempts, with just 15 touchdowns against 7 picks for 186.7 fantasy points and a QB17 finish. The pressure profile explains a lot, as he absorbed a league-high 55 sacks behind a porous line, took 50 knockdowns, and bailed on plays with 37 throwaways. That kept his completed air yards at a meager 5 per completion despite a deep-ball appetite worth 40 passes of 20-plus yards.

Year two brings real reasons for optimism. Brian Daboll arrives to run the offense, the line could add two new starters to cut into that sack rate, and Wan'Dale Robinson plus rookie Carnell Tate finally give him perimeter help he lacked. He averaged 15.5 points over his final four starts and lost weight for added mobility. Still only a superflex or two-QB target at a 137 ADP, but the breakout setup is genuine.

12523.name: Jimmy Horn
12523.headline: Efficient rookie flashes await a bigger Year 2 route share
12523.body:
Horn closed his rookie year ranked 143rd among receivers, but the underlying work hinted at more than the box score showed. Across 10 games he ran routes on just 13.5% of snaps, yet still posted 0.203 targets per route run and a tidy 1.46 yards per route run. A 73.3% catch rate, 0.231 EPA per play and a 46.7% success rate all point to a runner who delivered when the ball found him, even with a microscopic 3.1% target share.

The problem was volume, not production: 15 targets and zero touchdowns leave plenty of runway. At age 22 with an 18.2% avoided-tackle rate and yards-after-catch chops, Horn profiles as a Year 2 breakout candidate the moment the snap count climbs. If the route rate doubles, his per-route efficiency suggests a real flex ceiling. For now, treat him as a cheap upside dart with the underlying metrics on his side.

12524.name: Shedeur Sanders
12524.headline: Efficiency Red Flags Cloud Sanders' Browns Audition
12524.body:
The 2025 tape is hard to spin. Across eight games Sanders posted a brutal minus-0.232 EPA per play and a 36.7% success rate, with a minus-5 CPOE confirming he completed fewer passes than expectation. He threw 10 picks against just seven scores, completed only 56.6% of his attempts, and averaged a tiny 4.8 air yards per completion while leaning on dink-and-dunk volume. The protection cratered too: a 40.3% pressure rate, 23 sacks and a 3.24-second time to throw that invited the rush.

There are slivers of upside. He flashed deep, hitting four passes of 50-plus yards and a 66-yard long, and added 169 rushing yards at 9.3 per scramble. Cleveland is still an open competition with Watson, and a new staff plus fresh line and receiver investments could lift the floor. But until the efficiency turns, he is a Superflex-only dart at QB34 ADP, not a one-QB roster piece.

12526.name: Tetairoa McMillan
12526.headline: Volume monster ready to crash the WR1 tier
12526.body:
McMillan's rookie résumé screams alpha. He commanded a 25.4% target share and a staggering 40.9% air-yards share on 122 looks, posting 70 grabs for 1,014 yards and seven scores across all 17 games. The underlying numbers back the workload: 0.236 targets per route, a 0.667 WOPR, 1.96 yards per route run, and a plus-0.207 EPA per play with a 50% success rate. That production landed him at WR14 despite an offense that gave him little margin.

The path to a true breakout is paved by sheer opportunity. Fifteen red-zone targets and an 11.6-yard average depth signal a complete, every-down role with year-two upside. The catch rate (57.4%) and eight drops leave obvious room to climb, and any quarterback stability lifts his ceiling further. An ankle issue bears monitoring, but a healthy camp keeps him squarely in the early-Round-3 conversation as a high-floor WR1 bet.

12527.name: Ashton Jeanty
12527.headline: Elite usage met a broken offense; the talent breaks out next
12527.body:
