The rookie line reads like a disappointment on the surface, but the workload says everything: 266 carries, 73 targets, a 77.9% snap rate across 17 games, and 44 red-zone chances including 10 goal-line carries. That bell-cow role produced just RB16 fantasy production (190.1 points) only because the offense around him collapsed, dragging his EPA to -0.225 per play and his average to 3.67 a pop. Yet he forced 24 missed tackles and churned 2.09 yards after contact per attempt, meaning the blocking, not the back, was the problem.

That blocking gets fixed. A reworked line, a real quarterback upgrade, and Brock Bowers commanding coverage should unlock the efficiency his contact metrics promise. With 55 receptions already banked and locked-in volume near the goal line, even modest blocking improvement vaults him toward a top-five ceiling. Draft the role, bet on the touchdowns regressing upward, and treat the rookie ranks as a floor.

12529.name: TreVeyon Henderson
12529.headline: Explosive rookie ready to seize lead back role
12529.body:
Henderson was electric as a rookie, finishing RB21 with 171.2 fantasy points despite splitting work on a 45.9 percent snap share. The Ohio State second-rounder averaged a stout 5.06 yards per carry on 180 totes for 911 yards and nine scores, and the explosiveness leaps off the page: a 10 percent explosive-run rate, four carries of 40-plus, a 69-yard touchdown, and a broken tackle every 16.4 attempts with 2.01 yards after contact. He chipped in 35 catches on 42 targets for 221 yards, showing the three-down skill set New England drafted him for.

The 40 red-zone opportunities and eight goal-line carries signal the staff already trusts him near the stripe, and his 0.05 EPA per play is respectable for a back behind a rebuilding line. With a path to a workhorse role in year two, the ceiling is sizable if the snap share climbs from that base. Draft him as a high-upside RB2 with league-winning equity, a clear buy in redraft and dynasty.

12530.name: Travis Hunter
12530.headline: Two-way phenom WR usage still TBD, dynasty cornerstone
12530.body:
Hunter, the No. 2 overall pick, flashed receiver chops in a seven-game, injury-shortened rookie year while also playing cornerback. On offense he ran 209 routes for 45 targets and 28-298-1, an 8.2 percent target share with a strong 0.215 targets per route and 1.43 yards per route, plus a 44-yard catch among five 20-plus grabs. The catch rate (62.2 percent) and -0.051 EPA per play reflect a raw, split-attention role, and the WR93 finish (35.8 points) understates the per-route efficiency he showed when on the field.

The entire case rests on his 2026 snap allocation: if Jacksonville commits him to a fuller offensive workload alongside Brian Thomas Jr., the target share and route volume should climb sharply. The two-way usage is a genuine risk to his ceiling, but the talent and draft pedigree are elite. He is a high-variance WR3/flex gamble in redraft and a premier dynasty asset to acquire while uncertainty suppresses his price.

12531.name: Trevor Etienne
12531.headline: Buried in Carolina's backfield, but the burst is real
12531.body:
Etienne barely cracked the rotation as a rookie, logging a 5.6% snap share across 17 games and finishing 90th at the position with just 10.7 fantasy points. The volume was minuscule, 20 carries and three targets, yet the efficiency flashed: 4.7 yards per attempt, a 15% explosive-run rate, a 22-yard long, and 2.3 yards after contact per carry on a roster that simply never fed him. The -0.094 EPA per play and 0.72 yards per route run reflect the tiny sample more than the talent.

The problem is the depth chart, not the player. He sits behind Chuba Hubbard and Jonathon Brooks, a logjam that keeps him off redraft radars at an ADP near 341 overall. Still just 21, he profiles as a worthwhile dynasty stash. Brooks' injury history offers a believable path to real touches, and the contact balance suggests he'd hold up if that door opens.

12533.name: Jacory Croskey-Merritt
12533.headline: Explosive early-down runner crowded by a deeper Washington backfield
12533.body:
Croskey-Merritt's rookie tape was genuinely explosive: 805 yards on 175 carries (4.6 per attempt) with eight scores, and the chunk plays jump off the page with 21 runs of 10-plus, three of 20-plus, and a 72-yard touchdown. He forced 13 broken tackles and ran behind a clean blocking picture, generating 2.53 yards before contact per attempt. The warning signs are real too: a -0.024 EPA per play, 17 carries for negative yardage, 17 stuffs at or behind the line, and three fumbles point to boom-or-bust volatility rather than steady efficiency.

The bigger concern for 2026 is opportunity. He opens camp atop the depth chart but enters a crowded room after Washington added Rachaad White, Jerome Ford, and rookie Kaytron Allen. With only nine catches and a 3% target share last year, he offers little passing-down value, and White or Allen could siphon goal-line work. Treat him as a Round 8 upside dart, not a guaranteed starter.

12534.name: Kyle Monangai
12534.headline: Monangai's Goal-Line Role Makes Him a Premium Swift Handcuff
12534.body:
Monangai's rookie line was quietly efficient: 169 carries for 783 yards at 4.63 per pop, five scores, and an RB26 finish worth 128.7 fantasy points across 17 games. He cleared 4 yards per carry while operating on just 40.9 percent of snaps, with 39 red-zone opportunities and 10 goal-line totes signaling Chicago already trusts him near the stripe. The -0.029 EPA per play and modest 1.64 yards after contact temper the ceiling, but an 8.88 percent explosive-run rate shows the burst is real.

The passing-game work stays thin, with 30 targets, a 5.6 percent target share, and a 0.67 YPRR that won't fuel PPR weeks on its own. That keeps him behind D'Andre Swift as a complementary early-down piece and back-end flex. The appeal is leverage: if Swift misses time, Monangai's proven goal-line volume hands you weekly RB1/2 upside, making him a draft-and-stash worth a mid-round dart.

12535.name: Isaac TeSlaa
12535.headline: Red-zone touchdown magnet eyeing Detroit's vacant No. 3 role
12535.body:
TeSlaa's rookie line is a study in efficiency without volume. On just 27 targets and a 4.9% target share across 14 games, he turned 16 catches into 239 yards and six touchdowns, posting a robust 0.66 EPA per play and a 55.6% success rate. The usage was deliberately downfield and concentrated near the goal line: 22 air yards per reception, 11 red-zone looks and three end-zone targets. The touchdown rate won't hold at six scores on 16 grabs, but the role is real.

The path forward hinges on snaps, not talent. He played under 40% of them while St. Brown and Jameson Williams soaked up the targets, yet Kalif Raymond's exit opens the No. 3 job for a near-6-foot-4 vertical threat. Projections around 25 catches, 350 yards and four scores frame him as a late-round dynasty stash and bench upside, one injury ahead of weekly WR2 relevance.

12536.name: Jaylin Noel
12536.headline: Year-two leverage, but the math still favors patience
12536.body:
Noel's rookie tape is a study in efficiency without volume. He logged just a 26% snap share and ran only 189 routes, yet posted a 0.405 EPA per play and a 48.6% success rate on 35 targets, hauling in 74.3% with a single drop. His 1.54 yards per route run and 7.42 air yards before the catch say the production was real when the offense looked his way; the 6.3% target share says it rarely did.

The opportunity argument is straightforward: Christian Kirk is gone, opening a path to a bigger role, and Noel flashed when handed snaps. But Tank Dell's return clouds that runway, and the modest projection near 22 catches and 239 yards reflects it. With an ADP outside WR90, he's a dynasty stash on age and upside more than a redraft target. Monitor the depth chart; the talent is there, the volume isn't yet.

12539.name: Arian Smith
12539.headline: Deep-only flier with no target share to mine
12539.body:
Smith's rookie tape reads like a track athlete still learning the position. Across 13 games he ran 219 routes yet drew only 16 targets, a microscopic 3.4% target share and 0.073 targets per route run, turning that into seven catches for 52 yards and zero touchdowns. The efficiency numbers are brutal: a 43.8% catch rate, just 0.24 yards per route run, a 31.2% success rate and -0.396 EPA per play, which ranked him WR173. His one identifiable trait is vertical juice, with 13.5 air yards per target and a 30.9-yard average depth on his receptions.

That field-stretching role is real but it is the lowest-value job in fantasy, and he sits buried on a Jets depth chart with clearer options ahead of him. He logged zero red-zone and zero end-zone targets, so there's no scoring equity to dream on. Until he climbs the rotation and earns volume, he stays off rosters in every format.

12540.name: Chimere Dike
12540.headline: Rookie flashes, but a crowded room caps the ceiling
12540.body:
Dike's rookie tape was more useful than the box score: 48 grabs on 74 targets across 17 games, a 14% target share, and a healthy 14 red-zone looks (five into the end zone) that produced four scores. The advanced profile is mixed, though. He ran routes on 62.9% of dropbacks but managed just 1.08 yards per route run and 8.81 yards per catch, with an air-yards-per-target of 8.09 signaling a short, low-leverage role. A 0.021 EPA per play and 41.9% success rate confirm he wasn't yet a needle-mover.

The path to volume is the problem. Tennessee's depth chart stacks Calvin Ridley, Wan'Dale Robinson, Carnell Tate and Elic Ayomanor ahead of or alongside him, leaving Dike fighting for WR3 snaps. Projections of roughly 25 catches, 240 yards and three scores reflect that squeeze. His standalone appeal lives in return-yardage formats, where his elite special-teams output matters. Dynasty stashers can wait on the development; redraft managers can pass.

12541.name: Dont'e Thornton
12541.headline: Field-stretching deep threat who must fix catch-rate problems
12541.body:
Thornton's 2025 tape reads like a one-dimensional vertical specialist: 16.6 air yards per target and a staggering 49.7 air yards per reception, yet only 10 grabs on 30 looks across 11 games. That 33.3% catch rate is alarming, and the production behind it was thin at 135 yards and zero touchdowns. A -0.401 EPA per play and 30% success rate confirm the targets sent his way actively hurt the offense, compounded by three drops and three picks thrown into his coverage.

The usage hints at a real role, 45.3% of snaps and 41.8% route participation, but a 6.1% target share and 0.20 WOPR cap any meaningful fantasy ceiling. At 22, the raw deep speed gives him a path, and if the hands and route nuance tighten, the air-yards profile could spike for big plays. As constructed, though, he's a boom-or-bust dart throw, not a startable weapon. Treat him as a late-round flier only.

12543.name: Tahj Brooks
12543.headline: Buried in Cincinnati's backfield until the workload shifts
12543.body:
Brooks barely cracked the rotation as a rookie, logging just a 4.5% snap share across 12 games and turning 16 carries into 45 yards at 2.81 a pop. The advanced profile is brutal: a minus-0.672 EPA per play, 1.25 yards after contact per attempt, zero touchdowns, and a 0.3% target share that erases any passing-down floor. A line of 5.4 fantasy points and an RB112 finish tells you everything about his current leverage in the offense.

The receiving usage simply isn't there, with one catch on two looks and a 0.39 yards-per-route-run mark that won't earn him third-down snaps. A late-season concussion only muddied his standing further. Until an injury ahead of him or a depth-chart shift hands him real volume, he's a deep-stash dart throw at an RB95 valuation and a draft cost near pick 308, not a player worth chasing on draft day.

12544.name: LeQuint Allen
12544.headline: Pass-pro specialist needs a bigger backfield slice to matter
12544.body:
Allen's rookie tape reads like a situational role, not a feature back: 16 games, just a 22.6% snap share, 23 carries for 94 yards (4.09 per pop) and 10 catches on 11 targets for 54 more. The efficiency markers underline the limited ceiling, with a -0.209 EPA per play, only 8.7% of runs going for explosive gains, and a meager 1.52 yards after contact per attempt. He found the end zone zero times and saw just six red-zone opportunities all year.

The value sits in dynasty patience rather than 2026 production. At 21, Allen earned trust on third downs for his pass protection, which keeps him on the field but caps the fantasy payoff since blocking does not score. Until his touch count climbs meaningfully, he projects as a waiver-wire flier in redraft. Dynasty managers can stash him cheaply and bet on the role expanding.

12545.name: Tyler Shough
12545.headline: Saints young passer flashes a streaming-QB floor
12545.body:
Shoughs rookie year offered modest promise: the 2025 second-rounder (pick 40, Louisville) played 11 games, completed 67.6 percent for 2,384 yards, 10 touchdowns, and 6 interceptions, adding 186 rush yards and 3 scores for 158 fantasy points (QB25). The efficiency was league-average at best, with a -0.018 EPA per play, a 43.7 percent success rate, and a 10.9 percent deep-attempt rate pointing to a short, conservative passing profile.

The rushing element is the fantasy hook: 45 carries, 3 scores, and 27 red-zone attempts give him a weekly floor that pure pocket passers lack. But a 31-sack, 19.6 percent pressure rate behind a shaky New Orleans line capped the upside, and the 6 picks flag developmental risk. With a full season and continued mobility he could push toward back-end QB1 weeks. For now he is a late-round dart and a streaming option in superflex, not a set-and-forget starter.

12547.name: Kyle Williams
12547.headline: Explosive rookie flashes deep-ball upside in limited Patriots role
12547.body:
Williams arrived as a vertical specialist in 2025, and the numbers back the profile: 19.05 air yards per target and a 40-yard average depth on his catches made him New England's designated field-stretcher. On just 21 looks across 14 games, he turned 10 grabs into 209 yards and three scores, posting a robust 20.9 yards per reception with a 72-yard long. The efficiency markers are loud for a 22-year-old: 0.468 EPA per play, a 42.9% success rate, and 1.31 yards per route run despite seeing only 30.8% of the snaps.

The ceiling is real, but so are the volume limits. A 4.4% target share and a 0.126 WOPR mean he was barely involved in the offense, and zero red-zone targets cap his touchdown dependability. For 2026, the bet is on an expanded snap rate converting that big-play juice into steady production. Until the route volume climbs, he stays a boom-or-bust bench dart with genuine upside if the role grows.

12634.name: Jordan Watkins
12634.headline: Deep-roster flyer with no role and tiny 2025 sample
12634.body:
There is almost nothing to build on here. Watkins logged just two games as a rookie, running 14 routes on a 10.2% snap share while drawing three targets for two catches and 26 yards. That is a 0.5% target share and a 0.015 WOPR, the profile of a player who barely sees the field. The lone encouraging crumb is efficiency on microscopic volume: a 0.331 EPA per play and 14.3 air yards per target, suggesting he was used downfield when he did play. None of it is predictive at this sample size.

The outlook for 2026 is buried depth. He enters camp fighting for a back-end spot in a crowded San Francisco receiver room, and Shanahan's praise for his conditioning is the kind of note that fills a roster, not a target tree. With no projections, no ADP, and roughly 0% rostered, he is a watch-list name only, relevant if injuries clear a path.

12641.name: Jaylin Lane
12641.headline: Deep-shot rookie flash, but a crowded room caps the ceiling
12641.body:
Lane's rookie tape reads like a vertical specialist who never got a real on-ramp: 16 grabs on 32 targets across 15 games, with 13.3 air yards per look and a 26.7 air-yards-per-catch number that screams downfield usage. The problem is everything underneath it. A 36.2% snap share, 7.4% target share, and a -0.177 EPA per play with a 40.6% success rate point to a low-volume role where the offense bet on splash plays rather than steady work. Three catches of 20-plus and a 41-yard long show the juice; two interceptions thrown his way and a 50% catch rate show the volatility.

The 2026 path is narrow. An ankle injury that landed him on injured reserve clouds the offseason, and Washington's receiver room now includes Chig Okonkwo, rookie Antonio Williams, and Treylon Burks all chasing the same targets. Until Lane wins a defined route tree beyond punt-return duty, he's a waiver-wire stash, not a draft-day asset.

12658.name: Jackson Hawes
12658.headline: Hyper-efficient on tiny volume, Hawes needs the snaps
12658.body:
Hawes was one of the most ruthlessly efficient tight ends nobody used in 2025, posting a 0.781 EPA per play and a 73.7% success rate while finishing as the TE53. The catch: that came on just 19 targets across 13 games, a 22.7% route share, and a 4% target share. When he was thrown to, he delivered, hauling in 16 of 18 catchable balls at an 84.2% clip for 187 yards, 11.7 per grab, and three scores. The 1.55 yards per route run is real; the opportunity is not.

The profile is a blocking-first TE2 buried behind Dalton Kincaid, and the path to relevance runs entirely through a Kincaid absence. Until the snap count climbs past 44%, the receiving production stays a rounding error. He is an end-of-draft dart and a deep-league injury stash, not a weekly asset, but the efficiency floor means any volume bump would play up fast.

12670.name: KeAndre Lambert-Smith
12670.headline: Deep-ball flyer buried behind a crowded Chargers depth chart
12670.body:
The 2025 profile screams afterthought: 10 games, an 18.8% snap share, and just 12 targets that translated to five catches for 51 yards and one score. The underlying marks are even harsher, with a -0.41 EPA per play and a 33.3% success rate, while his 41.7% catch rate and a 0.176 target-per-route figure leave no foundation to build on. The one intriguing wrinkle is usage as a vertical threat, evidenced by 17.6 air yards per target and 42.2 air yards per reception, but volume that thin caps any real value.

The 2026 outlook does nothing to clear the runway. He profiles as roughly the fifth wideout in Los Angeles behind Ladd McConkey, Quentin Johnston, Tre Harris, and Brenen Thompson, with a Week 18 hamstring clouding his offseason. Modest projections near 10 grabs and 121 yards confirm he is undraftable in standard formats and only matters if injuries thin the room ahead of him.

12699.name: LaJohntay Wester
12699.headline: Return specialist with a long shot at offensive snaps
12699.body:
Wester arrives in Baltimore as a return man first and a receiver second. The front office has openly floated him as a potential impact returner, and his college resume backs the projection: 66 punt returns spread across five seasons at FAU and Colorado give him real reps in the role the Ravens are penciling him into for 2026. At 5-foot-9 and 170 pounds, he profiles as a twitchy slot type whose fastest path to value runs through special teams rather than the wide-receiver rotation.

For fantasy purposes, the arrow points down for now. He carries a roughly 360 overall ADP, sits around WR151, and is essentially unrostered across the market. Until he carves out offensive snaps behind an established Baltimore pass-catching group, treat him as a deep-league dart and waiver stash whose ceiling depends entirely on injuries opening targets ahead of him.

12718.name: Konata Mumpfield
12718.headline: Buried behind Nacua and Adams, deep-stash only
12718.body:
Mumpfield's rookie tape tells the story of a player drowning in low-value usage. Across 10 games he ran just 175 routes (28.4% route share) and drew 23 targets for a 4% target share, converting only 10 catches at a 43.5% catch rate for 92 yards and a score. The efficiency markers are alarming: a -0.381 EPA per play, a 34.8% success rate, and a microscopic 0.53 yards per route run. With an air-yards-per-target of 12.87 he was used as a downfield flier, but the 8.7% drop rate and an interception on his targets undercut even that role.

The path forward is blocked. He sits third on the depth chart behind Puka Nacua and Davante Adams, leaving almost no organic target runway in 2026. The numbers crystallized his fantasy standing as a fringe WR149, undraftable in standard formats. He's a pure injury-contingent stash who only matters if one of the two stars above him misses time.

12735.name: Caleb Lohner
12735.headline: Towering Broncos project with no usable fantasy floor yet
12735.body:
Lohner is the definition of a developmental swing: a 6-foot-7, 250-pound athlete out of Utah who arrives in Denver at 24 with essentially no professional production to lean on. The market has noticed the lack of a track record, parking him around pick 402 overall and TE72 in redraft, with a dynasty valuation buried near TE124 and a roster rate hovering at zero. There are no projections to anchor to and no prior-season snaps to grade, which is exactly why he belongs on no standard-league radar.

The one practical wrinkle is health: an offseason cleanup procedure ends his spring early, though he should be cleared in time for training camp. Even a clean ramp-up leaves him buried behind Denver's established tight end options and fighting for a role rather than targets. The schedule grades out friendly, but that only matters once a player is on the field. For now, he is a watch-list name in the deepest dynasty formats and nothing more.

12738.name: Phil Mafah
12738.headline: Mafah's lone cameo flashed efficiency, but the runway is short
12738.body:
Mafah's rookie ledger is a single Week 18 snapshot, yet the underlying marks intrigue. On just five carries he averaged 3.6 yards a pop with a goal-line touchdown, chipped in two grabs for an absurd 3.67 yards per route run, and posted a 0.133 EPA per play across a tiny 18 percent snap share. The 8.9 fantasy points in one game read as noise, but a back gaining 2.2 yards before contact on a thin sample at least hints the burst is real.

The problem is the depth chart, not the talent. Mafah enters camp battling for the backup job behind Javonte Williams, and at an RB ADP buried near pick 316 the market sees a year-two flier, not a workload. He carries late-round dart appeal only if he wins the No. 2 role outright; until that clarity arrives, he is a watch-list stash rather than a draftable name.

12787.name: Josh Williams
12787.headline: Deep-roster Tampa back with no fantasy footprint yet
12787.body:
Williams remains a fringe piece in the Buccaneers backfield, and the 2025 ledger says as much: just two games, a 4.3% snap share, four carries for 11 yards at 2.75 per attempt, and a single route run. That production amounted to 1.1 fantasy points and an RB rank of 130, with an EPA per play of -0.464 that underscores how little impact he had on the field when he did touch it.

There's nothing here that points to a standalone fantasy role. No red-zone or goal-line work, zero targets, and no broken tackles or explosive runs to suggest untapped upside. At 25, Williams profiles as emergency depth rather than a draftable asset, and he'd need a string of injuries ahead of him to matter. Leave him on waivers until usage actually changes.

12797.name: Ahmani Marshall
12797.headline: Reserve/Future Flier Stuck Deep On Cleveland's Bench
12797.body:
Marshall enters 2026 on the very edge of the roster picture, having inked a reserve/future deal with Cleveland after spending time on the practice squad. The Appalachian State product brings genuine size for the position at 6-foot-1 and 225 pounds, and at 24 he is young enough to develop, but nothing about his current standing suggests a defined role is waiting. He sits behind the established Browns backfield with no clear path to touches as offseason work begins.

The deep-league ranking near RB210 and essentially zero roster rate tell the story: this is a watch-list name, not a draftable asset. Cleveland's schedule grades out roughly middle-of-the-pack for backs, so opportunity, not matchup, is the only thing holding him back. Monitor camp and preseason for any sign he climbs the depth chart, but until that happens he belongs nowhere near redraft boards.

12826.name: Ulysses Bentley
12826.headline: Deep depth-chart flier with no standalone path
12826.body:
Bentley remains a roster afterthought, with a 2025 sample of a single game, one carry for zero yards, three percent snaps, and a -0.795 EPA on his lone touch, ranking RB139 with zero fantasy points. There is simply no production to evaluate, and nothing in the advanced data suggests latent upside, the one rush went nowhere and he logged just one route. He is a body, not a contributor, at this stage of his career.

The role context is equally bleak: he sits fourth on the Indianapolis depth chart behind workhorse Jonathan Taylor, change-of-pace DJ Giddens, and Seth McGowan, leaving no realistic touch share absent a cascade of injuries ahead of him. For 2026 he carries no redraft value and is not worth a bench spot in any standard format. He is strictly a waiver-wire watch, relevant only if camp attrition vaults him up the chart, and even then his ceiling is deep-league handcuff at best.

12851.name: Rivaldo Fairweather
12851.headline: Camp body buried at the bottom of Arizona's depth chart
12851.body:
Fairweather lands as pure roster filler in Arizona after the Cardinals plucked him off the Cowboys practice squad late last year. At 23, the 6-3, 249-pound Auburn product profiles as a developmental depth piece rather than a target earner, and the expert board reflects that reality: he sits at TE170 in consensus rankings, essentially undraftable in standard formats and rostered in almost no leagues.

The projection math is brutal for fantasy purposes, with the field forecasting roughly a dozen catches, a little over 100 yards, and a single score across the full season. A 28th-ranked strength of schedule only deepens the hole. Until injuries clear a path or he wins a real role in the tight end room, there is no startable case here. Leave him on the wire and revisit only if the depth chart collapses in front of him.

12897.name: Nathan Carter
12897.headline: Flashes on a tiny sample, buried on Atlanta's depth chart
12897.body:
Carter's 2025 rookie ledger is microscopic: two games, nine carries, 60 yards. The efficiency, though, popped. He averaged 6.67 yards a tug, ripped off two runs of 10-plus for a 22.2 percent explosive-run rate, and reached 12 yards on his longest scamper. Those are encouraging flashes from a 23-year-old who barely touched the field.

Temper the optimism, because the rest of the profile screams depth piece. His -0.181 EPA per play, a fumble on nine carries, and a No. 107 positional finish with just four fantasy points all reflect a player still fighting for snaps. Zero red-zone or goal-line work means no touchdown equity, and zero targets cap any passing-game upside. Until injuries or attrition clear a path in Atlanta's backfield, Carter stays off draftable radars as a deep-stash dart throw rather than a roster lock.

12954.name: Kye Robichaux
12954.headline: Camp longshot in a crowded Detroit backfield
12954.body:
Robichaux arrives as a deep-roster flyer rather than a fantasy asset. The 23-year-old Boston College product checks in at 6-foot, 213 pounds, but he carries no meaningful professional production, no ADP, and no published projections heading into the season. Rostered in essentially zero leagues, he sits at the bottom of Detroit's depth chart and will spend the summer fighting just to claim a roster spot behind an established backfield.

The one tailwind is scheduling: Detroit grades out with one of the league's softest slates, including early matchups against Buffalo, the Jets, and Carolina. That matters little, though, until Robichaux earns touches he hasn't yet been promised. For now he's a name to file away and revisit only if preseason reps and injuries ahead of him open a clear path to work.

12969.name: Lan Larison
12969.headline: Undrafted UC Davis back buried on a crowded depth chart
12969.body:
Larison enters 2026 as a true long shot. The 24-year-old UC Davis product arrived in New England as an undrafted free agent, and the early practice reports already pin him as the No. 3 back behind Rhamondre Stevenson and rookie TreVeyon Henderson. With two clearly established names ahead of him and Jam Miller fighting for the same reserve snaps, there is no obvious path to meaningful touches.

At 5'11" and 209 pounds, he has the frame to stick as a depth piece or special-teamer, but the fantasy case is essentially nonexistent right now. He carries no ADP, sits rostered in roughly zero percent of leagues, and has no published projection. Treat him strictly as a camp body to monitor; he only matters if injuries thin out the backfield ahead of him.

12984.name: CJ Dippre
12984.headline: Roster-bubble Patriots tight end with no fantasy path yet
12984.body:
Dippre enters 2026 as a deep-bench afterthought, recently promoted from the practice squad to fill a Patriots roster spot rather than a defined offensive job. The 6-foot-5, 256-pound 24-year-old out of Alabama logged zero production down the stretch last season, and the late-season game logs confirm he was barely seeing the field. With an expert ranking down around TE133 and essentially 0% rostership, he carries no draft cost and no real reason to be drafted in standard formats.

The projection math underscores the problem: roughly 3.9 catches, 37 yards and a third of a touchdown pencil out to about 5 fantasy points for the year. A favorable tight end schedule means nothing without snaps, and until Dippre earns a steady role in New England's rotation, he belongs on the watch list at best. Monitor the depth chart, but there is no actionable upside to chase right now.

13013.name: Mark Redman
13013.headline: Deep Rams tight end with no fantasy pulse
13013.body:
Redman profiles as a roster-fringe tight end with no meaningful NFL production on record and no advanced-stat footprint to evaluate. He sits buried on the Los Angeles depth chart behind an established room, currently listed as a secondary option among multiple bodies that includes veterans Colby Parkinson and Tyler Higbee plus drafted youngster Terrance Ferguson. With that many ahead of him, snaps would only come through a cascade of injuries.

There is simply no path to fantasy targets here in 2026. The tight end position already concentrates volume among a tiny handful of starters, and a player this far down the chart on a run-leaning Sean McVay offense will not see the route volume needed to matter even in deep two-TE formats. Treat him as a non-factor for redraft and a name to ignore in all but the deepest dynasty taxi situations until he climbs the depth chart or changes teams.

13047.name: Jacardia Wright
13047.headline: Deep practice-squad flier with one carry of NFL tape
13047.body:
Wright's professional resume is a single appearance: five carries for 20 yards on a 9% snap share, one explosive run, and exactly 2 fantasy points. The underlying marks are tidy but tiny - a 4.0 average, 2.4 yards before contact and 1.6 after, plus a broken tackle that gave him a 20% elude rate. There's nothing here to project a real workload from; it's a cup-of-coffee sample that confirms he can hold up briefly, not that he's earned touches.

The roster math is just as sobering. The Missouri State product re-signed on a future deal after practice-squad time in Seattle, slots near the very bottom of the depth chart at a draft rank around 491, and carries no ADP or statistical projection. An easy strength of schedule is the only tailwind, and it means little behind an established backfield. Treat him as a camp body to monitor for an injury-driven path, not a draftable asset.

13075.name: Myles Price
13075.headline: Speedy slot dart buried behind Jefferson and Jennings
13075.body:
Price is a 2025 rookie out of Indiana with no NFL advanced stats logged yet, having drawn 0.0 PPG across a five-game cameo last season. His best traits are speed and slot quickness, but the path to volume in Minnesota is choked off: he sits as the SWR2 behind Jauan Jennings inside, in an offense funneling targets to Justin Jefferson and Jordan Addison. That alignment leaves him fourth or fifth in the pecking order for meaningful looks.

With no measurable production to build on, the projection leans on role and pedigree, and both temper expectations. Even a Jennings injury would only nudge Price into a rotational slot job rather than a featured one, capping his ceiling at sporadic deep shots and gadget touches. He carries some dynasty appeal as a cheap speed lottery ticket if the Vikings expand his snaps, but for 2026 redraft purposes there is nothing to start. He is a waiver-wire name and a watch-list stash, not a draftable asset in standard formats.

13079.name: Jahdae Walker
13079.headline: Tiny Sample, Loud Efficiency: A Caleb Williams Lottery Ticket
13079.body:
Walker's 2025 was a six-game cameo, but the per-route numbers pop. On just 57 routes and a 9.6% route rate, he posted 1.53 yards per route, a 0.175 target-per-route-run mark, and a sky-high 0.685 EPA per play with a 60% success rate. The volume was minimal -- a 1.9% target share and 0.047 WOPR -- yet he turned 10 looks into 6 grabs, 87 yards, and two scores, including two end-zone targets. That 20.7-point flash on 15.1% of snaps shows real juice when the ball finds him.

The ceiling depends entirely on snaps. At 6-foot-3 and 23, he profiles as a downfield, red-zone weapon for Caleb Williams, but he's buried in the rotation and undraftable at a 300-overall ADP. Treat him as a deep-league stash: the efficiency is intriguing, but until the route share climbs meaningfully, the production stays a rounding error rather than a roster lock.

13121.name: Ben Yurosek
13121.headline: Deep-bench Vikings tight end with no fantasy footprint yet
13121.body:
Yurosek's 2025 line is the entire story: six games, a 16.6% snap share, and just 36 routes run, a sliver 6.6% of Minnesota's dropbacks. He drew three targets all year, hauling in two for 14 yards, leaving a microscopic 0.6% target share and a 0.012 WOPR that essentially rounds to zero usage. The 0.39 yards per route and 1.4 total fantasy points confirm there's no role to project from here.

The one flicker worth noting is efficiency on a tiny sample: 0.116 EPA per play and a 33.3% success rate suggest he didn't actively hurt the offense when on the field. But at 23 he sits buried on the depth chart with no path to volume mapped out, and nothing about his snap or target trend points toward a 2026 breakout. He's a name to monitor only if injuries clear the room ahead of him, not a draftable asset.

13150.name: Darius Cooper
13150.headline: Efficient when targeted, but buried on Philadelphia's depth chart
13150.body:
Cooper's 2025 sample is tiny but tidy: across six games he ran just 97 routes (18.3% route share), drew 11 targets, and turned nine of them into 92 yards while catching 81.8% of what came his way. The underlying marks are encouraging for a Tarleton State undrafted type, with 0.353 EPA per play and a 54.5% success rate, yet the volume context is brutal, a 2.4% target share, 0.053 WOPR, and zero red-zone or end-zone looks. He profiles as a rotational depth piece who only spiked when starters rested in Week 18.

The roadblock is obvious: A.J. Brown and DeVonta Smith own the snaps, leaving Cooper without a defined role. That's why he sits around WR244 in expert rankings with essentially no draft cost. Modest projections near 3.3 catches and 38 yards underline the ceiling. He's a watchlist name, nothing more, until an injury or depth-chart shakeup actually opens targets.

13268.name: Elijah Sarratt
13268.headline: Deep-league sleeper buried on a crowded Ravens depth chart
13268.body:
A fourth-round rookie out of Indiana, Sarratt lands in a Baltimore receiver room with little room to breathe. Zay Flowers owns the top job, and fellow rookie Ja'Kobi Lane is slotted for outside snaps, pushing the 6-foot-2, 210-pound Sarratt inside despite a college profile built on perimeter work. Expert consensus parks him around WR96 with an ADP near 204, and projections of roughly 20 grabs, 295 yards, and under two scores tell you how thin the Year 1 path looks.

The appeal is purely upside. Sarratt wins contested balls and earns targets at a level that hints at relevance if an injury opens snaps ahead of him. Realistically, the target volume in this offense isn't there yet, so treat him as a dynasty stash or last-pick dart in very deep redraft formats rather than a roster lock.

13269.name: Fernando Mendoza
13269.headline: Top pick waits on Cousins before Vegas hands him keys
13269.body:
The 2026 first overall pick and reigning Heisman winner lands in Las Vegas with real pedigree after a 16-0 national title run and a 72% completion year worth 41 touchdowns. The catch for fantasy managers is timing: Kirk Cousins is penciled in to open the season, so Mendoza projects as a redshirt-then-takeover arm rather than a Week 1 plug-and-play. That holds his ECR near QB29 (167 overall) and stamps him a late-round dart in single-QB formats, with far more juice in superflex and dynasty rookie pools.

The supporting cast is a mixed bag. Brock Bowers and Ashton Jeanty give him two genuine difference-makers, and a Tyler Linderbaum-anchored line plus Klint Kubiak's scheme help, but the receiver room of Tre Tucker, Jalen Nailor and Jack Bech is thin. Modeled output lands around 2,844 yards, 16.6 scores and 232 rushing yards for roughly 193 fantasy points. Stash him, start him later.

13270.name: CJ Daniels
13270.headline: Polished rookie route-runner buried behind LA's elite WR duo
13270.body:
Daniels arrives in Los Angeles as a sixth-round flier whose calling card is craft over explosion. At 6-2, 202, the Miami product leans on refined footwork, route nuance, and a 62.3% contested-catch rate to win against zone, but average long speed and burst cap his ceiling. His 2025 line of 50 grabs for 557 yards and seven scores in 13 games shows production, though none of that translates to a clear NFL target share yet.

The path is the problem. Daniels lands behind Puka Nacua and Davante Adams, then must beat out Jordan Whittington, Konata Mumpfield, and Xavier Smith just to claim the No. 3 job. That math explains a #338 ADP and projections of roughly 3 catches for 43 yards per game. Treat him as a last-round dart or taxi-squad stash who only matters if an injury opens snaps ahead of him.

13274.name: Germie Bernard
13274.headline: Polished Alabama rookie stuck behind a crowded Steelers depth chart
13274.body:
A second-round investment out of Alabama, Bernard arrives with a versatile, YAC-friendly profile and coaches already raving about how quickly he's absorbed all three receiver alignments at 22 years old. The slot is his clearest runway to snaps, and the 6-1, 206-pound frame plus open-field toughness give him a sturdier floor than most Day 2 rookies. But the path to volume is choked: DK Metcalf and Michael Pittman Jr. sit ahead of him, and Aaron Rodgers will spread a passing game that finished 22nd in yards per outing a year ago.

The projection -- roughly 45 grabs, 555 yards and three scores for under 74 fantasy points -- captures the reality of a depth piece rather than a contributor. ADP near WR70 (177 overall) and just 5 percent rostered reflect that. He's a better real-life player than fantasy asset in 2026; dynasty and deep-league managers should stash and wait.

13275.name: Ty Simpson
13275.headline: Rookie QB stashed behind Stafford, no 2026 value
13275.body:
Simpson arrives in Los Angeles as a developmental rookie with no NFL snaps and therefore no advanced profile to lean on. The depth chart is unambiguous: he sits QB2 behind Matthew Stafford, with Stetson Bennett and Matthew Caldwell rounding out the room. That makes him a clipboard-holder for a contending offense with no realistic path to meaningful 2026 reps unless Stafford, who remains the entrenched starter, suffers an injury that opens the door.

For redraft, that translates to zero standalone value, he should go undrafted in every standard and most superflex formats. The intrigue is purely long-term: Stafford is in the twilight of his career, and an apprenticeship under Sean McVay carries developmental upside for a dynasty stash. Managers in deep dynasty or rookie-pick leagues can speculate on him as a future-asset lottery ticket, but for 2026 he is a waiver-wire name with no weekly relevance and belongs nowhere on a redraft roster.

13276.name: Omar Cooper
13276.headline: Rookie WR1 on paper, deep dynasty flier only
13276.body:
Cooper enters 2026 with no NFL advanced stats and no recorded draft data, signaling a rookie or undrafted free agent profile rather than a proven contributor. The intriguing wrinkle is that the Jets' depth chart currently lists him atop a thin receiver group, ahead of Isaiah Williams and Caullin Lacy. That nominal positioning likely reflects a wide-open, low-pedigree room more than any established role, and should be read with heavy skepticism until camp reps and target trends confirm it.

For redraft, an unproven receiver in an unsettled offense with no statistical track record is not a draftable asset; preseason snaps and target share will dictate whether he is even rosterable by Week 1. The realistic verdict is a watch-list name: monitor whether that depth-chart slot holds through August and whether the quarterback play supports any volume. He is a deep-dynasty flier and waiver-wire monitor for 2026, not a player to spend redraft capital on.

13277.name: CJ Donaldson
13277.headline: Undrafted power back buried on a crowded Saints depth chart
13277.body:
Donaldson arrives in New Orleans as an undrafted rookie out of Ohio State, and the path to touches is steep. At 6-1, 230 he profiles as a downhill, early-down grinder built to finish runs between the tackles, but the scouting read flags only modest burst and little juice in the open field. That skill set is replaceable, and the Saints already have it covered with Alvin Kamara, Ty Chandler and Kendre Miller ahead of him.

The near-term outlook is a roster-bubble fight rather than a fantasy proposition. Dynasty boards slot him around RB128, and he is rostered in essentially zero leagues with no ADP or projections attached. For 2026 he is a deep-stash dart throw at most, worth monitoring only if camp injuries thin the backfield and his power running earns a short-yardage and goal-line specialist role. Until that opening appears, leave him on the wire.

13279.name: Carnell Tate
13279.headline: Rookie WR with WR1 upside in Tennessee's thin room
13279.body:
The fourth overall pick lands in a Titans receiver group that lacks the elite talent he played behind at Ohio State, where Marvin Harrison Jr., Emeka Egbuka and Jeremiah Smith capped his counting numbers. The underlying profile travels: 3.17 yards per route run, a 14.6 average depth of target, and a clean sheet on drops point to a refined route-runner who wins downfield and through contact. At 6-2, 192 and just 21, he projects as the field-stretcher opposite Wan'Dale Robinson's underneath work, with a real path to the clear No. 1 role.

Projections of 66 catches for roughly 899 yards and four-plus scores frame him as a high-upside WR3, and the ceiling rises with second-year passer Cam Ward under Brian Daboll. A top-20 finish is in range if Ward develops. With an ADP near 61 and a favorable late-season slate, he's a sensible Round 6 swing and the top rookie receiver to target.

13281.name: Jordyn Tyson
13281.headline: High-ceiling rookie wideout chasing targets in a pass-happy Saints attack
13281.body:
Many evaluators tabbed Tyson as the top receiver in his class, and the landing spot helps the case: New Orleans leaned heavily on the pass down the stretch in 2025, finishing fifth in neutral dropback rate from Week 12 on. He projects as the clear second option behind Chris Olave, catching balls from Tyler Shough, and the redraft math reflects real belief—an ADP near pick 63 (WR32) with projections of roughly 64 grabs, 893 yards and 4.5 scores. His Arizona State tape backs it up, averaging 86 yards per game over his final two college seasons with 18 touchdowns across 21 contests.

The catch is health. A lingering hamstring problem followed him out of college and already limited him through spring work, with rehab expected to stretch into camp. That risk caps his floor and could slow his early ramp behind an established veteran. Treat him as a Round 7 dart with WR2 upside if the leg cooperates, and a strong dynasty target around the top of rookie drafts.

13285.name: Malachi Fields
13285.headline: Big-bodied Giants rookie stashed behind Nabers
13285.body:
Fields joins the Giants as a developmental outside receiver with no NFL advanced stats yet, and the depth chart is the key data point. He projects as the No. 2 on the left side behind star Malik Nabers, but the broader room is crowded with Darius Slayton, Darnell Mooney, slot work going to Calvin Austin, plus veterans Odell Beckham and Jalin Hyatt competing for snaps. Tight ends Isaiah Likely and Theo Johnson further compress the target tree in a Jaxson Dart-led offense.

The size profile gives Fields a contested-catch and red-zone path if he climbs the depth chart, but Nabers commands a massive target share that caps everyone behind him. With no production to model, he is a role- and upside-based dynasty stash rather than a 2026 contributor. Monitor camp for any sign he leapfrogs the veterans, but in redraft he is a waiver-wire name only, worth a bench spot solely in the deepest leagues.

13286.name: Jadarian Price
13286.headline: Rookie inherits Seattle's run-heavy backfield with lead-role upside
13286.body:
Seattle spent a first-round-caliber pick on Price to take over for Kenneth Walker III, and the path to volume is unusually clean for a rookie. With Walker shipped to Kansas City and Zach Charbonnet's knee expected to sideline him into the early season, the only obstacles between Price and lead duties are journeymen Emanuel Wilson and George Holani. In a backfield this thin, an offense that profiles to stay ground-oriented hands him real touches from Week 1.

The talent backs it up: Price ranked 25th in yards after contact and 31st in breakaway rate across his Notre Dame years, the kind of contact balance and burst that travels. The catch is the passing game, where just 15 career college receptions and three years of minimal targets cap his PPR ceiling and could cost him third downs. Projections near 950 rushing yards and 7.9 scores justify a Round 5 seasonal price, with locked-in dynasty appeal as the second rookie back off the board.

13287.name: Jeremiyah Love
13287.headline: Love's draft capital screams workhorse — if Arizona lets him cook
13287.body:
Arizona spent the third overall pick on Love, and a team doesn't burn that kind of capital to hand a back a timeshare. The Notre Dame product arrives with a monster resume — roughly 2,497 rushing yards and 35 scores on 362 carries across his final two seasons, plus 55 grabs — and steps into a featured role at just 21. Drafters are buying in, pushing his ADP up around 23rd overall and inside the top 13 at the position.

The only thing between Love and a top-12 finish is the depth chart. James Conner and Tyler Allgeier loom as touchdown vultures, and a shaky quarterback room caps the scoring environment. But the talent gap is real, and the logic is obvious: this backfield consolidates around Love sooner than later. Treat him as a high-end RB2 with legitimate top-10 upside once the workload tilts his way.

13288.name: Nicholas Singleton
13288.headline: Explosive rookie back stuck behind crowded Titans committee
13288.body:
Singleton brings a genuine athletic pedigree to Tennessee after a productive Penn State career built on home-run speed and receiving chops, the kind of profile that flashes flex upside if the door opens. The problem in 2026 is the depth chart: he sits third in the backfield behind Tony Pollard and Tyjae Spears, who already split early-down and passing-down work. With no NFL advanced stats yet, his case rests on talent and draft pedigree rather than an established role.

The path to relevance is injury-dependent. Pollard has carried heavy workloads and Spears has missed time before, so one absence could vault Singleton into a real touch share, and his pass-catching skill means he would not be a pure early-down plodder. For now he is a bench stash with upside, not a draftable contributor. Treat him as a late-round dart in deeper formats and a strong dynasty stash given the age and pedigree, but do not bank on weekly production behind two veterans.

13293.name: Ja'Kobi Lane
13293.headline: Ravens rookie is a red-zone bet for patient dynasty rosters
13293.body:
A third-round pick out of USC, Ja'Kobi Lane lands in Baltimore as a 6-4, 200-pound vertical threat whose combine measurables jump off the page, headlined by 10.5-inch hands and one of the longest wingspans in the class. The athletic profile fits exactly what Baltimore wants near the goal line, and the coaching staff has openly signaled it intends to weaponize his size in scoring situations. The route nuance and ball-tracking are real, even if an 8.6 percent college drop rate and thin yards-after-catch chops flag a developmental runway.

The path to volume is the sticking point. Rashod Bateman is locked in through 2029, and Lane must climb past Elijah Sarratt for steady snaps, which keeps his Year 1 ceiling capped. Drafting near pick 233 overall, he is a stash, not a starter. Treat him as a dynasty target in the late-second to early-third tier and let the redraft case build itself before committing a bench spot.

13294.name: Makai Lemon
13294.headline: Biletnikoff-winning rookie buried behind Smith and Goedert
13294.body:
Philadelphia traded up in the first round for Lemon, a 5-foot-11 USC product who won the Biletnikoff Award and graded out as one of college football's most efficient perimeter receivers. The pedigree is real: just three drops on roughly 175 targets over his final two seasons, with the lateral quickness and route polish to win from the slot or outside. The problem is opportunity. DeVonta Smith and Dallas Goedert already soak up the meaningful target share, and a hamstring issue that cost him practice time only complicates a rookie's path to early reps.

Projections of 58 catches for 821 yards and nearly five scores reflect that target-competition ceiling, landing him around a Round 8 redraft cost and roughly WR35 by ADP. That makes him a stash, not a starter, in 2026. The investment pays off after 2027, when Goedert could depart and vacate the volume Lemon needs to break out.

13296.name: Caleb Douglas
13296.headline: Third-round Dolphin walks into a wide-open depth chart
13296.body:
Miami spent the 75th overall pick on Douglas and then gutted its receiver room, handing the 6-3, 206-pound rookie a genuine path to early snaps with little veteran talent blocking the way. A two-year starter out of Texas Tech, he profiles as a smooth route runner with sharp change-of-direction and juke ability, and his spread-offense background should slot cleanly into the scheme. That opportunity is why he draws top-20 buzz in rookie-only drafts and late-round flier interest in deeper redraft pools.

Temper the excitement, though. The current projection lands around 35 catches for 440 yards and a couple of scores, roughly 57 fantasy points, which is bye-week filler at best. The college production backs the caution: he ranked 158th and 116th in yards per route run his final two seasons, and evaluators see a long strider rather than a burner. Draft him as a lottery ticket, not a roster lock.

13297.name: Reggie Virgil
13297.headline: Big-bodied rookie buried on a crowded depth chart
13297.body:
A fifth-round pick (143rd overall) out of college, Virgil arrives in Arizona with a prototypical 6'3", 187-pound frame but lands behind an established trio. Marvin Harrison Jr. and Michael Wilson lock down the top two roles, leaving the rookie to scrap with Kendrick Bourne for snaps on the back end. His final college campaign produced 57 grabs for 705 yards and six scores, useful tape but not the kind of profile that forces early targets.

The scouting read is a possession-style receiver: ordinary speed, modest yards after the catch, and a route tree that still needs work, though he reads zone coverage well. Projections peg him near 3-4 catches and roughly 39 yards a game, which translates to fringe relevance at best. Leave him on the waiver wire in redraft and treat him as a late-round dart only in deep rookie dynasty formats.

13298.name: KC Concepcion
13298.headline: Late-Round Browns Lottery Ticket With Real Starter Upside
13298.body:
A first-round selection out of Texas A&M, Concepcion arrives in Cleveland with the kind of explosiveness that turns short catches into chunk plays, separating after the grab and making defenders miss in space. His final college campaign produced 919 receiving yards and nine scores, a total that likely undersold him given erratic deep-ball accuracy from his quarterbacks. The fit isn't perfect: Jerry Jeudy and fellow rookie Denzel Boston will siphon looks, and the drop problems that nagged him in college could carry over.

The ceiling is what makes him worth the gamble. Among rookie receivers available well past pick 100, he profiles as the most likely to seize a starting-caliber workload, with a path to becoming Cleveland's top passing-game target. The comparison points, Ladd McConkey and Brian Thomas Jr., both blossomed into top-24 finishers. A shaky Browns aerial attack caps the floor, but the cost is minimal and the payoff could be a weekly contributor.

13299.name: Nate Boerkircher
13299.headline: Undrafted Jaguars TE2 buried with no path
13299.body:
Boerkircher enters 2026 as a pure depth piece in Jacksonville, sitting second on the tight end chart behind Brenton Strange with no draft capital to push that order. As an undrafted rookie tight end he carries the standard developmental profile: blocking-first usage, special-teams reps, and the occasional two-tight-end snap rather than any designed target role. In an offense funneling targets to Strange and the wide receiver corps in Trevor Lawrence`s passing game, the volume simply is not there for a backup to matter.

There are no NFL advanced stats to lean on, and nothing in his situation projects standalone fantasy value. The realistic 2026 ceiling is a handful of snaps and maybe a red-zone surprise if Strange misses time. For redraft purposes he is a clear waiver-wire name to ignore, and even in deep dynasty TE-premium leagues he is only worth a roster spot if his camp blocking reputation earns a bigger early-down role. Leave him on the wire.

13301.name: Antonio Williams
13301.headline: Crowded slot rookie buried on Washington depth chart
13301.body:
Williams enters Washington as a developmental slot option with no NFL production on file, and the depth chart offers him little oxygen. He sits atop the listed slot group but in a Jayden Daniels offense funneling targets to Terry McLaurin and pass-catching back Jacory Croskey-Merritt, the realistic pecking order pushes Williams behind McLaurin, Treylon Burks, Luke McCaffrey and tight end Chig Okonkwo. He must also fend off Jaylin Lane for the underneath snaps that remain.

With zero career advanced stats to lean on, the projection is purely role- and capital-based, and neither argues for redraft relevance in 2026. The path to standalone value requires multiple injuries ahead of him plus a special-teams or gadget role to earn snaps. He is a deep-dynasty name to monitor through camp, not a player to roster in standard leagues. For now, leave him on the waiver wire and revisit only if the Commanders thin out at receiver.

13302.name: Adam Randall
13302.headline: Raw Ravens convert buried behind Henry's workload
13302.body:
Randall arrives in Baltimore as a fifth-round developmental swing, a 6-foot-3, 232-pound former Clemson receiver with a single season of college running back reps under his belt. That thin resume shows up in the draft data: an overall ADP near 259 and an expert consensus rank closer to 294, which is a polite way of saying he is essentially undrafted in standard redraft formats. He profiles as a project, not a contributor, sitting third or fourth in a Derrick Henry backfield that already houses Justice Hill and Rasheen Ali.

Projections of roughly 55 carries, 280 rushing yards and a handful of catches confirm there is no standalone path to relevance while Henry stays upright. The realistic case is a deep-dynasty stash: monitor training-camp depth-chart chatter, and let someone else absorb the upside-free volume until his receiver-to-back transition shows tangible burst. For 2026, leave him on the wire.

13303.name: Cade Klubnik
13303.headline: Day-3 rookie buried behind Geno Smith, superflex-only stash
13303.body:
Klubnik lands in New York with no path to early snaps, sitting behind Geno Smith and merely fighting Brady Cook for the No. 2 line on the depth chart. The Jets spent only fourth-round capital here, and a potential first-round arm next spring could shove him further down. A back issue from OTAs has already cost him minicamp reps, and the per-game forecast of roughly 117 passing yards, under one touchdown, and only eight rushing yards reflects a clipboard role, not a fantasy asset.

The upside is purely developmental. His legs are the calling card, with 400-plus rushing yards projected annually if he ever plays, and the arm strength and compact delivery hint at a usable starter someday. But shaky reads and pocket recklessness, plus a clear college regression in 2025, keep him off standard radars. Treat him strictly as a deep superflex or dynasty rookie-draft flier, nothing more.

13305.name: Mike Washington
13305.headline: Undrafted Raider stuck behind Jeanty's bell-cow workload
13305.body:
Washington enters 2026 with no NFL advanced-stat profile and no draft pedigree in our data, an undrafted back trying to win a roster spot in Las Vegas. The depth chart lists him as the RB2 behind first-round workhorse Ashton Jeanty, but that ordering is fragile, with Dylan Laube, Chris Collier and Roman Hemby all chasing the same change-of-pace and special-teams snaps. Jeanty is built for a near-three-down role, leaving little projectable touch volume for anyone behind him.

With zero career rushing or receiving data to lean on, the redraft verdict is simple. Washington has no standalone path to relevance while Jeanty stays healthy, and even a Jeanty absence would trigger a committee scramble rather than an automatic handoff of the lead role. He is a camp-body flier whose only 2026 value is contingency-based. Leave him on the waiver wire in all formats and revisit only if he flashes in the preseason or climbs to a clear handcuff spot.

13307.name: Marlin Klein
13307.headline: Developmental Texans tight end years from relevance
13307.body:
Klein arrives as a raw developmental tight end with no NFL advanced stats to assess and a depth-chart spot that says everything: he is the No. 3 option in Houston behind veteran starter Dalton Schultz and blocking-specialist Foster Moreau, with Cade Stover also in the mix. That is a crowded room with an entrenched lead man who soaks up the receiving work, leaving Klein with virtually no projectable target volume in 2026.

With Schultz handling the pass-catching duties and the rest of the room rotating, there is no standalone fantasy case to make. Rookie and second-year tight ends rarely produce regardless of opportunity, and Klein would need an injury to Schultz simply to see the field consistently. He is purely a dynasty-taxi flier on athletic upside and nothing more; for 2026 redraft he is off the board entirely. Revisit only if he flashes in camp or the depth chart thins ahead of him.

13311.name: Chris Bell
13311.headline: Rookie ACL recovery makes Bell a dynasty stash, not redraft pick
13311.body:
A third-round investment out of Louisville, Bell arrives in a Miami receiver room that is conspicuously short on dependable target-earners, which is exactly why his long-term path matters. Before tearing his ACL last September, he piled up 72 grabs for 917 yards and six scores across just 11 college games, the production of a big, physical wideout who accelerates well for his frame and punishes defenders after the catch. The knee is the whole story for 2026, and recovery reports lean encouraging, with word that he is tracking ahead of his timeline.

That thin depth chart hands him a real runway once he is whole, and the projections sketch a modest first-year line near 40 catches, 535 yards and a pair of touchdowns. With route polish still developing and Malik Willis steering an uncertain offense, treat him as a forward-looking hold rather than a Week 1 starter. Drafted around pick 207, he belongs on dynasty and keeper rosters, not redraft boards.

13312.name: Josh Kattus
13312.headline: Kattus a developmental tight end buried in Cincinnati
13312.body:
Kattus enters 2026 as a raw, late-roster project out of Kentucky, and the market reflects it: he checks in around TE22 with an expert consensus ranking near TE156 overall, no available projections, and essentially zero presence in fantasy lineups. At 6-foot-4 and 245 pounds he carries the frame teams covet at the position, but there is no meaningful NFL production to point to yet, and the Bengals' depth chart gives him a steep climb for snaps.

For managers, this is a name to track rather than draft. The schedule offers some early softness, with one of the league's friendlier opening matchups and an easy three-game stretch through Houston, Pittsburgh and Jacksonville, but opportunity has to come first. Until an injury or role change opens the door, Kattus stays a deep-league dynasty stash whose value hinges entirely on carving out a real spot in the rotation.

13313.name: Emmanuel Henderson
13313.headline: Sixth-round rookie buried on a deep Seahawks depth chart
13313.body:
Henderson arrives in Seattle as the 199th pick of the 2026 draft, a sixth-rounder out of Kansas, and the math on early playing time is unforgiving. The Seahawks already roster Jaxon Smith-Njigba, Cooper Kupp, and Rashid Shaheed as the primary trio, with Jake Bobo and Tory Horton ahead of him for snaps. At 6-1 and 185 pounds, he is a developmental piece, not a Week 1 contributor, and his WR255 expert ranking reflects exactly that.

There is no ADP and effectively no roster share, which tells you everything about his redraft relevance: none. The favorable strength of schedule is noise behind a player who has to climb four or five bodies just to dress on game day. Treat him strictly as a deep dynasty flier, a name to monitor if injuries or attrition open the back of the room, with no expectation of fantasy production in his rookie year.

13315.name: Dean Connors
13315.headline: Undrafted Ram battling for a backfield roster spot
13315.body:
Connors arrives in Los Angeles as an undrafted rookie staring up at a crowded depth chart, currently slotted as an RB4 who must beat out Ronnie Rivers just to claim a roster spot. The Rams' backfield is stacked, so any path to meaningful snaps is narrow. He carries no ADP, landing around RB123 in dynasty circles and RB185 in redraft, which fairly reflects a fringe asset with no projectable 2026 workload yet.

The profile is more interesting than the role. He grades as a well-rounded back who finishes runs and excels in pass protection, and a 14.4% college target share hints at real third-down receiving value. Average burst and long speed cap his ceiling, but the receiving chops give him a survivable niche. Realistically he's a watch-list stash whose value spikes only with an injury ahead of him or a fresh landing spot elsewhere.

13317.name: Ted Hurst
13317.headline: Undrafted depth WR with no clear path to targets
13317.body:
Ted Hurst III enters 2026 as a deep reserve on the Tampa Bay roster, carrying no NFL advanced-stat profile and no recorded draft capital, the classic signature of an undrafted long shot fighting for a roster spot. The depth chart tells the story: he sits as the number-two left wide receiver behind Emeka Egbuka, with established names ahead of him across the formation. That places him well outside the rotation that actually commands targets in Tampa Bay passing game.

With no measurable production to lean on, his fantasy case rests entirely on training-camp standout reps and special-teams value earning him helmet status, neither of which translates to weekly scoring. Barring a string of injuries that thrusts him into real snaps, there is no startable role here in any format. For 2026 redraft and even dynasty, Hurst is a pure waiver-wire watch-list name, not a player to roster until he demonstrates a defined offensive role.

13319.name: Oscar Delp
13319.headline: Buried Saints TE3 with no redraft path
13319.body:
Delp arrives without NFL advanced stats or recorded draft data, marking him as a rookie-tier prospect rather than a producer. New Orleans' depth chart slots him third at tight end, behind Juwan Johnson and Noah Fant, with Moliki Matavao also in the room. That positioning leaves no projectable target volume in an offense that already concentrates its tight-end work near the top of the group. Without college grounding or any usage trend to anchor a forecast, his 2026 outlook is purely speculative.

For redraft purposes, a third-string tight end with no statistical track record and limited route opportunity is not rosterable in standard leagues. His path to relevance requires injuries ahead of him plus a quarterback situation willing to feed the position, neither of which is bankable. The realistic verdict is a deep-dynasty stash and watch-list name: monitor camp reps and any move up the depth chart, but leave him on the waiver wire for 2026.

13320.name: Zachariah Branch
13320.headline: Branch Brings Burst, But Targets Will Be Scarce Early
13320.body:
Branch arrives in Atlanta with rare juice and a runway to the No. 2 receiver job opposite Drake London, but the math is the catch. With Bijan Robinson and Kyle Pitts ahead of him in the pecking order, he could open the year as the fourth option in the passing game, and the early projection of roughly 21 grabs for 252 yards and a touchdown reflects that crowded reality.

The draft market agrees, parking him near pick 180 and around WR91, an end-of-draft dart in redraft. His value is real in dynasty, where the elite open-field burst and college-leading screen volume hint at a manufactured-touch role that scales. For 2026, treat him as a stash and waiver target rather than a starter, betting on a second-half snap bump if the Falcons lean into his YAC ability.

13321.name: Dan Villari
13321.headline: Deep-bench Rams tight end with no fantasy footprint yet
13321.body:
Villari is a developmental piece buried on the Rams' tight end depth chart, ranked just TE #119 in early expert boards and rostered in essentially zero leagues. The 6'4", 243-pound Syracuse product carries no 2025 game data, no statistical track record, and no published projections, which tells you everything about where he sits in Los Angeles' pecking order entering the year. There is simply no production to lean on here.

The one thing working in his favor is scheduling noise: the Rams draw the second-easiest tight end slate, with soft early matchups and a friendly playoff stretch. That's irrelevant until he earns snaps, though. Until an injury ahead of him or a clear role emerges, Villari is a watch-list name for the deepest dynasty formats only and shouldn't occupy a redraft roster spot in any standard league.

13322.name: Sam Roush
13322.headline: Roush stuck third on Chicago's tight end ladder
13322.body:
A third-round pick out of Stanford, Roush lands in Chicago with the build of a classic in-line Y (6-5, 267) and a combine workout that flashed real size-and-speed upside. The problem is the room ahead of him: Colston Loveland and Cole Kmet own the meaningful snaps, leaving Roush as a developmental piece whose early reps come mostly in heavy 12 and 13 personnel under Ben Johnson. His college profile leans blocker, with limited separation speed and modest yards after the catch.

The draft market reflects all of it. He's drifting near pick 343 overall and barely cracks the top 55 at the position, essentially undraftable in standard formats. A hard early-season schedule does him no favors. Treat 2026 as a redshirt year and a deep-dynasty stash at best, not a contributor you actively roster in seasonal leagues.

13324.name: Matt Hibner
13324.headline: Hibner Lands in Baltimore as an Andrews Heir
13324.body:
Baltimore thought enough of Hibner to trade up in the fourth round for him, sending the 49ers pick 154 plus a 2027 sixth to grab the SMU product. He arrives as a fifth-year senior who flashed late, posting 31 grabs for 436 yards and four scores across 13 games in 2025 after a quiet Michigan stint. The athletic profile and formation versatility give the Ravens a chess piece they can deploy all over.

The redraft case is thin. Mark Andrews still anchors this room, and Hibner projects as a backup in a multi-tight-end scheme, so meaningful snaps likely hinge on injury. That makes him a name to stash rather than start. The real value lives in dynasty, where he profiles as the eventual successor to Andrews and a sneaky streamer if the veteran misses time. Park him on a taxi squad and let the runway develop.

13328.name: Anthony Hankerson
13328.headline: Undrafted Oregon State back fighting for Miami depth touches
13328.body:
Hankerson arrives in Miami as an undrafted free agent out of Oregon State, where he stacked back-to-back seasons north of 1,000 rushing yards and chipped in 49 catches across that span. That production profile shows real college volume and enough pass-game involvement to suggest he isn't a one-dimensional grinder, but the leap to an NFL roster spot is the immediate hurdle. At 5'8" and 204 pounds, he projects as a compact, between-the-tackles option competing for a reserve role rather than a defined workload.

For now this is purely a roster-depth play, not a draftable fantasy asset. He carries no consensus ADP and sits at roughly zero percent rostered, with no 2026 projections to lean on. Miami's backfield strength of schedule grades out 15th, a neutral backdrop. Treat Hankerson as a camp name worth tracking only if an injury ahead of him cracks the depth chart open.

13329.name: Malik Benson
13329.headline: Speedy Raiders depth receiver behind Tucker and Bowers
13329.body:
Benson lands in Las Vegas as a vertical-speed receiver with no NFL advanced stats on record, slotted as the No. 2 on the right side behind Tre Tucker. The pecking order ahead of him is steep: Jack Bech, Dont'e Thornton and slot man Jalen Nailor all compete for outside and underneath snaps, while Kirk Cousins funnels heavy volume to elite tight end Brock Bowers and back Ashton Jeanty in the passing game. That leaves precious few targets for a fourth or fifth option.

His deep speed offers a field-stretching role if he earns playing time, but speed alone rarely translates to fantasy production without target volume, and the depth chart does not project him for any. With nothing to model statistically, Benson is a camp watch and deep-dynasty speculative stash, not a redraft asset. He needs injuries ahead of him and a defined role to matter. For 2026, leave him on the waiver wire in all standard formats.

13330.name: Kenyon Sadiq
13330.headline: Elite athlete, raw profile, crowded Jets room
13330.body:
Drafted 16th overall, Sadiq arrives with a rare athletic toolbox for the position: a 4.39 forty at 6-foot-3 and a 43-inch vertical that scream upside. The problem is everything around the traits. His Oregon production stayed modest at 1.62 yards per route run as a junior, and a 9 percent drop rate with too many body-catches points to real refinement still needed before the volume follows.

The situation does him no favors either. Geno Smith leads a passing game projected to underwhelm, and the target tree runs through Garrett Wilson, rookie Omar Cooper Jr., and Mason Taylor before it reaches him. A recent hernia surgery shouldn't cost camp, but the math caps near-term ceiling. Treat him as a high-end TE3 dart throw and a dynasty bet on the athleticism eventually winning out, not a 2026 starter you count on weekly.

13331.name: Dae'Quan Wright
13331.headline: Athletic UDFA tight end buried deep on Philadelphia's chart
13331.body:
Wright arrives in Philadelphia as an undrafted rookie out of Ole Miss, where he posted 66 catches for 1,029 yards and 9 touchdowns in his final college campaign. At 6'4" and 246 pounds with reported 4.5-4.6 buildup speed, he profiles as a yards-after-catch threat with a varied route tree, though evaluators flag real limitations at the catch point and project him as a below-average blocker entering the league. For now he sits fourth or fifth on the depth chart, with a realistic ceiling of climbing to TE2 behind Eli Stowers.

The fantasy case is purely speculative. Rostered in essentially zero leagues with no ADP or projections attached, he carries no standalone value in 2026. The longer-term hook is roster turnover: Grant Calcaterra, Dallas Goedert, and Johnny Mundt all hit free agency after this season, which could clear a path. Until then, he's a deep-dynasty stash, not a redraft asset.

13333.name: Deion Burks
13333.headline: Slot-only speedster buried on a crowded Colts depth chart
13333.body:
A seventh-round rookie out of Oklahoma, Burks arrives as a 254th-overall flier whose calling card is pure juice: a 4.30 forty and the shiftiness to turn screens, drags, and underneath looks into chunk gains. He cleaned up his hands late in college, trimming his drop rate to 3.1%, and finished second on the Sooners in catches (57), yards (520), and scores (4). The athletic profile is real; the size and a 36.8% contested-catch rate are not.

The problem is fit and footing. Burks ran 77.9% of his routes outside in 2025 but projects strictly to the slot in the NFL, and Shane Steichen's two- and three-tight-end leanings squeeze undersized inside receivers. He has to beat out Nick Westbrook-Ikhine just for WR3 reps, and his ADP near 327 reflects that. Stash him in deep dynasty formats and leave him alone in redraft until a role materializes.

13337.name: Emmett Johnson
13337.headline: Fifth-round Chief carving out the Walker handcuff role
13337.body:
Johnson arrives in Kansas City as a fifth-round rookie out of Nebraska, where he proved he can shoulder a load: 251 carries for 1,451 yards and 12 scores, plus 46 grabs for 370 yards and three more touchdowns over 12 games. That three-down profile, including 18% of his college snaps lined up in the slot or out wide and a top-six missed-tackles-forced mark, is exactly the versatility Andy Reid covets behind a workhorse.

The path is clear but capped: he has to beat out Brashard Smith and Emari Demercado to lock the job behind Kenneth Walker III, and current projections of roughly 40 carries, 165 rushing yards and a touchdown reflect that ceiling while Walker stays healthy. With an ADP around 188 and an RB59 draft tag, he's a late-round stash whose value is almost entirely contingent. Win camp, become the standalone handcuff, and the upside swings hard if Walker misses time.

13338.name: Kevin Coleman
13338.headline: Missouri rookie buried on Miami's depth chart for now
13338.body:
Coleman arrives in Miami as a 22-year-old rookie out of Missouri, listed at 5-foot-10 and 180 pounds, and the early signals point to a long climb. He carries no fantasy projections, sits essentially unrostered across leagues, and shows up as N/A in every ranking and ADP metric. That is the reality for an undersized receiver fighting for snaps in a crowded room rather than one handed a defined role.

The path forward runs through special teams and slot reps, where his quickness can earn a helmet before it earns targets. The schedule offers no early gift either, opening at Las Vegas before a manageable stretch against San Francisco, Kansas City and Minnesota. Until he forces his way into the rotation, treat Coleman as a deep-league dart and a name to monitor on the practice report, not a draftable asset.

13342.name: John Michael Gyllenborg
13342.headline: Chiefs UDFA tight end is a dynasty taxi stash, nothing more
13342.body:
Gyllenborg arrives in Kansas City as an undrafted rookie, rostered in essentially zero leagues and carrying a dynasty value that barely registers at TE78. The profile underneath explains the cold market: a 9% drop rate tied to concentration lapses, stiff hips that cap his short-area burst, and a contested-catch rate under 47% that flags real trouble against physical coverage. His 2025 route usage was narrow too, with roughly 60% of his routes funneled into outs, ins, flats, and crossers, and just 5.4 yards after the catch per reception to show for it.

The blocking is raw and needs significant development before he earns meaningful snaps. Add a checkered injury history -- a 2024 high ankle sprain and a 2025 hamstring -- and there's little here for redraft managers. A favorable schedule does nothing to move the needle when a player has no defined role. Treat him strictly as a deep-league taxi flier in tight end premium formats and move on.

13345.name: Jonah Coleman
13345.headline: Buried Behind Two Backs, But Worth A Late Dart
13345.body:
Coleman enters his rookie season stuck third on Denver's depth chart, behind J.K. Dobbins and R.J. Harvey in a crowded backfield. As a fourth-round pick at a compact 5-foot-8, 220 pounds, he profiles as a short-yardage and goal-line bruiser more than a feature option, and his draft cost reflects that with an ADP near pick 150. At Washington he piled up 1,811 yards and 25 scores on 349 carries while adding 54 grabs, flashing the contact balance that makes him intriguing if the picture clears.

The path to relevance runs through Dobbins, whose chronic foot trouble limited him to 10 games a year ago. Should that repeat, or Harvey settle into a passing-down role, Coleman's short-yardage value spikes into flex territory. He's a cheap stash with a clear injury-leverage profile, though a featured workload likely waits until 2027.

13346.name: Denzel Boston
13346.headline: Big-bodied rookie with a crowded path to targets
13346.body:
Boston arrives in Cleveland as a Day 2 pick (39th overall) after leading Washington in receiving yards, bringing a 6-foot-4, 215-pound frame, reliable hands and contested-catch chops. The trade-off is athletic: he skipped the 40 at the combine and won't separate with speed, so his game leans on size and ball skills rather than burst. His current draft cost reflects the wait-and-see profile, landing around WR63 and 158th overall.

The early-season picture is the real obstacle. He's stacked behind Jerry Jeudy and competing with KC Concepcion and Harold Fannin Jr. for a target share that won't be his by default, and Cleveland's shaky quarterback situation caps the ceiling for every pass-catcher.
