Treat him as a late-round dart in deeper formats and let the snap count earn your trust before he reaches your roster.

13347.name: Demond Claiborne
13347.headline: Rare 4.37 speed, but stuck third on Minnesota's depth chart
13347.body:
Claiborne arrives in Minnesota with genuine juice: a 4.37 forty translates to real game breakaway speed, second only to Jeremiyah Love among this rookie class, and he flashed cutback feel on zone runs at Wake Forest, where he piled up 2,599 career rushing yards and 26 scores, including a 907-yard, 10-touchdown All-ACC senior season. The athletic ceiling is the whole pitch here.

The path to touches, though, is choked. He sits third behind Jordan Mason and Aaron Jones, and projections reflect that reality at just 27.7 carries, 121.5 rushing yards and roughly 20 fantasy points for the year. Spotty vision, shaky hands (five fumbles and six drops as a senior) and limited pass protection cap any quick climb. Jones' shaky durability is the only real lever. Treat Claiborne as a dynasty stash and speculative handcuff, not a 2026 redraft asset.

13348.name: J'Mari Taylor
13348.headline: UDFA bowling-ball back chasing a roster spot in Jacksonville
13348.body:
Taylor joins Jacksonville as an undrafted free agent, and the market treats him accordingly: an expert consensus rank of RB101 and No. 334 overall leaves him rostered in essentially zero leagues. His college tape sells the appeal of a 5-foot-9, 199-pound runner who finished last season with 1,062 rushing yards and an ACC-best 14 rushing touchdowns. He pairs that with real three-down upside, posting 43 catches on 54 targets and holding up in protection (one sack, five hurries allowed).

The questions are athletic and situational. Evaluators flag pedestrian burst, a 10.4% drop rate, and thin receiving efficiency at 0.91 yards per route. With no current projection and a crowded depth chart ahead of him, he's a camp body and special-teams bet first. Stash him only in the deepest dynasty formats and wait for an injury or a preseason flash to force the issue.

13349.name: Eli Stowers
13349.headline: Mackey-winning rookie waits behind Goedert in Philly
13349.body:
Philadelphia spent the 54th overall pick on Stowers, a 6-foot-3, 239-pound Vanderbilt product who captured the 2025 John Mackey Award after a 62-catch, 769-yard, four-touchdown season. The athletic profile is loud: a 4.51 forty and a 45.5-inch vertical hint at a long-term mismatch weapon. The problem for redraft purposes is the depth chart. Dallas Goedert still owns the starting job, and Stowers projects as the No. 2, capping his year-one ceiling.

That reality shows up in his pricing. He carries a #194 overall ADP, TE29, with expert ranks landing around TE34, while dynasty managers value him far higher at TE10 in early second-round rookie territory. The modest projection of roughly 23 catches, 232 yards and 1.5 scores reflects a rotational rookie role. Questions about whether his frame holds up in-line could pinch snaps further. Stash him and bet on the breakout once Philadelphia's tight end depth chart opens up.

13353.name: Chris Brazzell
13353.headline: Late-Round Field Stretcher Buried On Carolina's Depth Chart
13353.body:
Carolina spent a third-round pick on the 6-foot-4 vertical threat, and the college tape backs the investment: Brazzell led the SEC with nine receiving touchdowns in his final Tennessee season while soaking up 26.7% of the offense's receiving yards. That blend of size, downfield speed and red-zone production is exactly the archetype that can stay fantasy-relevant on thin volume, even as a rookie still rounding out his route tree.

The problem for 2026 is opportunity. He opens behind Tetairoa McMillan and Jalen Coker in a passing game projected to rank among the league's worst, capping his early ceiling to splash plays rather than steady targets. Treat him as a late-round dart throw in redraft, monitoring camp for a path to the WR3 role. Dynasty managers should value him higher, banking on the profile to cash in once the offense and his usage catch up.

13355.name: DJ Rogers
13355.headline: Undrafted TCU tight end fighting for Dallas snaps
13355.body:
Rogers arrives in Dallas as an undrafted rookie out of TCU, where he hauled in 34 catches for 319 yards and a pair of scores in 2025. He profiles as a move tight end whose value lives near the line of scrimmage, with roughly 80 percent of his college targets coming within nine yards. The footwork is polished enough to win on short routes, but the speed, burst, and blocking strength all grade as ordinary, capping the ceiling.

The depth chart is the real obstacle. Jake Ferguson and Luke Schoonmaker both return ahead of him, leaving Rogers to scrap for reserve reps and special-teams value rather than a defined receiving role. That reality shows up in the ranks: he sits down near TE139 and is essentially unrostered. Treat him as a deep-stash dynasty name who only matters in standard formats if an injury opens the door.

13357.name: Lance Mason
13357.headline: UDFA buried behind Barner and Arroyo, deep stash
13357.body:
Mason signed with Seattle as an undrafted free agent, and the path to fantasy relevance is essentially blocked. AJ Barner and rookie Elijah Arroyo sit firmly atop the depth chart, leaving Mason fighting for a peripheral, blocking-and-emergency-target role. In 2025 he played a touch over half his snaps as an inline tight end, working short hitch and flat routes as a chain-mover rather than a downfield threat, and the production reflected it.

The athletic testing offers little to dream on. A 4.71 forty, jumps that stall below the 58th percentile, and stiff route-running cap his ceiling, and he forced just one missed tackle while averaging 5.6 yards after the catch. Ranked outside the top 170 tight ends with no meaningful projection, Mason is a name to file away, not draft. Leave him on waivers in every format and revisit only if injuries clear a sudden runway ahead of him.

13380.name: Brenen Thompson
13380.headline: Rookie burner with 4.26 wheels chasing a No. 3 role
13380.body:
A fourth-round pick out of Mississippi State, Thompson arrives in Los Angeles carrying genuine track speed, having clocked a 4.26 forty at the combine. That gear translated on tape, where he turned 57 grabs into 1,054 yards and six scores last fall. New coordinator Mike McDaniel is reportedly intrigued by the profile, and a low ECR around the 370 range means there is almost no draft-day cost to chasing the upside.

The path, though, is narrow. McConkey and Johnston own the top two spots, and Thompson must beat out Tre Harris just to claim the No. 3 job. At 5'9" and 170 pounds, he projects as a pure field-stretcher who struggles to break tackles and may cede red-zone work. Projections of roughly 10 catches for 121 yards reflect that low-volume reality. Stash him in deep dynasty formats and monitor the camp battle, but he is a bench flier in seasonal leagues.

13394.name: Josh Cameron
13394.headline: Crowded Jacksonville room buries late-round Baylor rookie
13394.body:
Jacksonville spent the 191st overall pick on Cameron, a 6'1", 224-pound rookie out of Baylor, and the depth chart immediately works against him. He lands behind back-to-back first-rounders in Brian Thomas Jr. and Travis Hunter, plus veteran Jakobi Meyers and Parker Washington, leaving almost nothing on the table for a Day 3 flier. Sixth-round classmate CJ Williams is essentially fighting him for the same final roster spot.

The college tape offers something to dream on: Cameron paced Baylor in every major receiving category over his final two seasons, hauled in 59.3% of contested targets in 2024 with a single drop, and plays like a tackle-breaking runner after the catch. But he projects to the slot despite lining up wide 88.7% of the time, and his route tree is thin. Projections sit at zero across the board. He's a camp watch and dynasty taxi stash only, needing injuries to matter.

13402.name: Skyler Bell
13402.headline: A twitchy UConn flier buried in Buffalo's committee
13402.body:
Bell arrives as a fourth-round rookie out of UConn, and the appeal is mostly about traits: a 192-pound frame, sudden acceleration, and standout work after the catch, where he forced 26 missed tackles across his final two college seasons and averaged better than eight yards after every reception. The downside is just as clear. His release work and route polish are unfinished, he struggled with drops and body catches in college, and roughly four of every five targets came on shorter routes, so the deep-field upside isn't there yet.

The bigger problem is opportunity. Buffalo runs a true receiver-by-committee, and Bell has to climb past Joshua Palmer, Keon Coleman, and Khalil Shakir just to sniff regular volume behind DJ Moore. That caps him as a speculative late-round dart in deep redraft formats and a mid-round rookie swing in dynasty, where the athletic ceiling tied to Josh Allen makes him worth a cheap bet.

13403.name: Jam Miller
13403.headline: Late-round Alabama back buried behind Henderson and Stevenson
13403.body:
A seventh-round pick out of Alabama, Miller (245th overall) lands in a crowded New England backfield that already runs through TreVeyon Henderson and Rhamondre Stevenson. That leaves him fighting for RB3 work with no clear path to touches unless an injury opens the rotation. The projections reflect it: roughly 34 carries for 156 yards and a score, plus a couple of catches, all adding up to about 24 fantasy points across the year.

The profile is more concerning than the depth chart alone. His efficiency dipped to 3.9 yards per carry with a visible loss of burst, and the supporting skills lag, including shaky pass protection (seven pressures allowed on 49 snaps) and five drops over two years. He fits the gap-scheme run game on paper, but with Stevenson signed for the foreseeable future, Miller is a stash at best and essentially undraftable in standard leagues right now.

13405.name: Kaytron Allen
13405.headline: Rookie wild card in a wide-open Washington backfield
13405.body:
Drafted in the sixth round (187th overall), Allen walks into a Commanders backfield with no settled pecking order. He'll battle Jacory Croskey-Merritt, Rachaad White, and Jerome Ford for touches, and none of them has locked down a defined role, which is exactly the kind of murky depth chart that lets a rookie play his way up. His profile is a grinder's: 217 pounds, a low, balanced base that breaks tackles, and the Penn State career rushing record (4,180 yards) built across two productive seasons. The knocks are speed and lateral agility, plus passing-down chops he still needs to prove.

Priced as an afterthought (RB59, near pick 195) and rostered in under 5% of leagues, he's a pure upside stash rather than a draft-day target. Modest projections of roughly 111 rushing yards and a touchdown reflect the committee fog. Draft late, watch the camp battle, and pounce if he separates early.

13407.name: Carson Towt
13407.headline: Towering Colts rookie tight end is a name to watch, not start
13407.body:
Towt arrives in Indianapolis as a developmental tight end carrying intriguing physical tools: a 6'8", 250-pound frame out of Notre Dame that gives him a massive catch radius and red-zone target potential. But the practical reality for 2026 is buried deep on the depth chart, with roughly zero percent roster share and no published projections to point to. He profiles as a true rookie project, not a Week 1 contributor.

The ranking landscape backs that up — he sits around the 21st tight end, firmly in waiver-wire territory rather than draftable range. There's no ADP, no established target role, and no proven production to bet on yet. Until he climbs the Colts' pecking order, Towt is a deep-league stash whose size flashes upside but whose 2026 fantasy value is negligible. Monitor camp reps before spending any roster spot.

13408.name: Tanner Koziol
13408.headline: Pass-catching rookie tight end behind Jacksonville's incumbents
13408.body:
Koziol arrives in Jacksonville as a rookie tight end with no NFL snaps to evaluate, which is why our advanced database is empty for him. A productive small-school and Group of Five receiving tight end, he profiles as a move-piece pass-catcher rather than an in-line blocker, the type of player who needs a defined route role to matter for fantasy. With zero professional usage on record, any 2026 projection rests entirely on training-camp reps and depth-chart movement.

The Jaguars already roster veteran tight-end options ahead of him, and rookie tight ends almost never produce startable fantasy lines, the position's notoriously steep learning curve buries even high-pedigree prospects. Without meaningful draft capital or an established target path, Koziol is a name to monitor in deep dynasty tight-end-premium formats only. For 2026 redraft, he is an undraftable waiver-wire flyer until he earns a role.

13411.name: Zavion Thomas
13411.headline: Burner Rookie Buried On A Crowded Depth Chart
13411.body:
The Bears spent the 89th pick on Thomas, betting on track-meet speed rather than college production, and the bet is a gamble. His LSU profile is thin: a target share that sat in the 19th percentile and just 1.52 yards per route run, the kind of efficiency that makes the early selection a head-scratcher. At 5-foot-10 and 22 years old, he lands behind a reshuffled but still busy pass-catching room, and barring injuries ahead of him he has a steep climb toward weekly relevance.

The one real path is scheme. Ben Johnson reportedly wants a gadget role that mixes in backfield snaps, the same blueprint Green Bay used with Savion Williams. That could manufacture touches his route metrics never would. Still, Year 1 looks limited, and he is rostered in roughly 1% of leagues for good reason. Leave him on the wire and revisit only if his snap count climbs midseason.

13412.name: Kendrick Law
13412.headline: Torn ACL erases rookie year before it starts
13412.body:
Detroit spent a fifth-round pick on the 5-foot-11, 203-pound Kentucky transfer, the kind of late flier who needs camp reps and special-teams snaps to claw onto the radar. That path closed the moment Law tore his ACL this offseason, an injury that wipes out the entire 2026 campaign before he ever lines up. A Day 3 receiver buried on a stacked depth chart had a narrow margin for relevance even at full health; with a knee reconstruction ahead, there is nothing to project.

The ranks reflect the reality, slotting him around WR195 in redraft and roughly WR166 in dynasty, and he sits rostered in essentially no leagues. There is no usable role, no target share, and no statistical case to make. Leave him untouched in every 2026 format and revisit only if he flashes in a healthy 2027 camp.

13413.name: Cyrus Allen
13413.headline: Deep dynasty flier buried behind Kansas City's crowded top three
13413.body:
Allen arrives in Kansas City as a fifth-round rookie out of Cincinnati, where he stacked 51 grabs for 674 yards and 13 scores across 13 games in 2025. That touchdown production hints at real upside, but the path to volume is steep: Rashee Rice and Xavier Worthy are entrenched, with Tyquan Thornton, Jalen Royals and Allen scrapping over the leftover snaps behind them. He profiles as a target-earner with genuine separation skills and positional flexibility, the kind of trait set that can force its way onto the field if camp breaks right.

The market treats him accordingly. He sits near 283rd in early ADP and inside WR144 in redraft rankings, strictly a late-round dart in deep formats. Dynasty is where the appeal lives, as a Round 3 rookie-draft cost for a player on a high-powered offense. Projections of roughly 6 catches and 80 yards on the season underscore the reality: monitor training camp, but do not draft him expecting standalone value.

13414.name: Kaelon Black
13414.headline: A speed-back lottery ticket buried behind McCaffrey in San Francisco
13414.body:
San Francisco spent a third-round pick (No. 90) on Black, but the immediate path is narrow. He lands behind Christian McCaffrey and joins Jordan James and Isaac Guerendo in a fight for secondary touches, so 2026 projections sit modestly around 98 carries for 388 yards, three scores, and roughly 67 total fantasy points. His college tape backs the role: 186 totes for 1,040 yards and 10 touchdowns at Indiana in 2025, with the profile of a runway runner who wins on speed, burst, and clean vision.

The upside is leverage. McCaffrey turns 30 after a 450-touch 2025, and one absence would vault Black into real volume. Temper the enthusiasm, though. He brings almost no receiving resume (12 career targets), lacks power and contact balance, and Shanahan's history of developing late backs is spotty. Draft him as a cheap handcuff near pick 226, with a longer dynasty case if 2027 opens up.

13417.name: De'Zhaun Stribling
13417.headline: Rookie depth flier in a crowded San Francisco room
13417.body:
San Francisco spent the 33rd overall pick on Stribling, a 6-2, 207-pound Ole Miss product who posted 55 catches, 811 yards and six scores across 15 games last fall. That draft capital signals real long-term interest, and tying his rookie year to Brock Purdy gives the upside a credible ceiling. But the path to early volume is narrow: Mike Evans, Christian Kirk and Ricky Pearsall sit ahead of him on a reshuffled depth chart, leaving him fourth in a competitive pecking order.

The market reflects that logjam, with an expert rank near 229 and an ADP around 165 (67th among receivers). Projections of roughly 32 grabs, 440 yards and two to three touchdowns for about 58 fantasy points fit a depth piece, not a weekly starter. A brutal schedule (30th-toughest, worst-case playoff slate) compounds the risk. Treat him as a cheap dynasty stash with a clearer 2027 payoff than an immediate redraft contributor.

13418.name: Robert Henry
13418.headline: Deep Washington backfield flier with no measurable role
13418.body:
Henry profiles as a fringe depth back in Washington carrying no NFL advanced stats, no recorded draft capital, and no presence in the top five of the Commanders' running back room. That room is stacked ahead of him: Jacory Croskey-Merritt leads it, with Rachaad White, Kaytron Allen, Jeremy McNichols, and Jerome Ford all slotted before him. With no career carries, targets, or snap data to evaluate, there is simply no statistical foundation for a projection, and his most likely 2026 outcome is a practice-squad or end-of-bench assignment.

In an offense built around Jayden Daniels' rushing and a committee of established backs, there is no realistic standalone path to touches barring a cascade of injuries. He holds no redraft value and minimal dynasty appeal beyond a speculative camp-body watch. The verdict is straightforward: waiver-wire material at best, and not worth a roster spot in any standard format until he earns a defined role.

13420.name: Bryce Lance
13420.headline: Fourth-round Saints rookie is a dynasty stash, not a 2026 starter
13420.body:
The Saints used a fourth-round pick on a 6-foot-3, 204-pound receiver who posted back-to-back 1,000-yard seasons at North Dakota State and tested like an athlete, running 4.34 with a 41-inch vertical. The college production and frame are real, but so is the traffic ahead of him: Chris Olave and Jordyn Tyson headline the room, with Juwan Johnson commanding targets from the slot and tight end spot. That logjam buries Lance on the depth chart, and the projection of roughly 16 catches for 204 yards and a score reflects a player fighting for snaps rather than touches.

For redraft purposes he's essentially undraftable, sitting around pick 298 ADP and rostered in about 1% of leagues. The dynasty case is sharper. A 61.5% contested-catch rate and a 3.8% drop rate point to reliable hands, and if Olave moves on after 2026, Lance has a clear runway to a WR2 role. Treat him as a late rookie-draft flier, not a contributor.

13421.name: Eli Raridon
13421.headline: Rookie TE2 stashed behind Henry with a clear future runway
13421.body:
New England spent the 95th overall pick on Raridon, a 6-foot-6, 245-pound Notre Dame product who slots in as TE2 behind Hunter Henry while battling Julian Hill and CJ Dippre for the early-down and pass-game scraps. His college tape was modest — 32 grabs for 482 yards and zero scores as a senior — and the redraft projection of roughly 19 catches, 196 yards and a touchdown reflects a near-zero role this fall. At an ADP near 256 (TE41) and rostered in about 1% of leagues, he's strictly a dynasty hold rather than a 2026 contributor.

The appeal is the runway. Henry hits free agency after this season, and at 22 with athletic testing that flashes (4.62 forty, strong jumps), Raridon profiles as the heir if New England lets the veteran walk. The caveat is health: two prior right-knee ACL tears (2021, 2022) and a still-raw route tree temper the ceiling even if he eventually wins the job.

13423.name: Eli Heidenreich
13423.headline: Navy convert is a versatile dart throw, not a 2026 contributor
13423.body:
Pittsburgh spent a seventh-round pick (230th overall) on Heidenreich, and that draft slot tells the story: he lands behind a crowded backfield with a projected ADP near 285 overall and a roster rate around 1 percent. His Navy tape is genuinely intriguing, splitting time as a runner (44 percent of snaps in the backfield), a slot weapon (15 percent) and a perimeter receiver (33 percent). In 2025 he stacked 1,157 rushing yards on minimal volume, posting a 6.5 per-carry clip, and added 79 catches for 941 yards and six scores at 18.5 yards a grab.

The problem is fit and opportunity. He profiles as a slot receiver more than a tailback, yet he is buried on a depth chart that gives him almost no path to early snaps. Treat him as a dynasty taxi-squad stash who needs a role to materialize before he matters in redraft. For 2026, expect minimal touches and effectively zero standard-league value.

13424.name: Seth McGowan
13424.headline: Deep Colts backup stuck behind Taylor and Giddens
13424.body:
McGowan enters 2026 without recorded draft capital and no NFL advanced-stat profile, slotting in as Indianapolis's RB3 behind workhorse Jonathan Taylor and 2025 draftee DJ Giddens. That is a difficult ladder to climb: Taylor is an entrenched bell-cow, and Giddens carries the higher draft pedigree and developmental priority for the backup role. McGowan's path to even spot duty would require multiple injuries ahead of him, and there is no defined change-of-pace or passing-down carve-out to bank on.

With no usage data to anchor a projection, his fantasy case is purely contingent and remote. The Colts' offense can support a productive runner, but that value is concentrated in Taylor, with Giddens next in line for any spillover. For redraft purposes McGowan is an undraftable name to leave on the wire, and even in deep dynasty he is no better than a speculative end-of-bench stash pending a training-camp surge or an injury cascade.

13430.name: RJ Maryland
13430.headline: Undrafted Packers tight end stuck behind a crowded room
13430.body:
Maryland enters 2026 as an undrafted depth tight end in Green Bay with no NFL advanced-stat footprint to lean on, which tells you everything about his current standing. The Packers' room is jammed at the top: Tucker Kraft is the entrenched starter, 2023 second-rounder Luke Musgrave sits second, and Josh Whyle adds a third option ahead of him, leaving Maryland fourth or fifth on the depth chart and off the fantasy radar entirely. With zero career routes, targets, or snaps logged, there is no production baseline to project.

The realistic path to relevance is a special-teams or blocking role first, with receiving usage only if multiple injuries hit ahead of him. That is not a profile worth a roster spot in any redraft format, and even in deep tight-end-premium dynasty leagues he is a name-to-watch rather than a stash. For 2026, leave him on the waiver wire and revisit only if camp reports flag a surprise climb up the depth chart.

13431.name: Miles Kitselman
13431.headline: Buried Lions tight end behind LaPorta's entrenched role
13431.body:
Kitselman is a developmental Detroit tight end with no NFL advanced stats and no draft capital recorded in our data, signaling an undrafted addition fighting for a roster spot. The Lions' tight-end room is among the league's most crowded, headlined by Sam LaPorta with Brock Wright, Tyler Conklin and Zach Horton layered ahead of any depth piece. That stack of established names leaves no realistic snap or target pathway for a fringe prospect in 2026.

Without any production history to evaluate, the analysis rests on role, and it points one direction. LaPorta commands the receiving work while Wright handles in-line blocking, so even injury attrition would likely promote a veteran before a project. Kitselman profiles as a practice-squad candidate rather than a fantasy contributor. There is no redraft case and barely a dynasty one; he is a deep-league name to file away, but for now he belongs on the waiver wire in every format.

13432.name: Khalil Dinkins
13432.headline: UDFA depth tight end with minimal fantasy footprint in 2026
13432.body:
Dinkins arrives in San Francisco as an undrafted rookie out of Penn State, where his pass-catching usage was marginal. At 6-4 and 226 pounds, he projects as a developmental reserve added strictly to fill out the position group, and his contract reflects that bet: just $275,000 guaranteed. The market treats him accordingly, slotting him around 481st overall and 100th among tight ends while leaving him rostered in essentially zero leagues.

With no meaningful college production to extrapolate and no 2026 projection on the board, there is nothing here to chase in fantasy formats. He would need an injury ahead of him on the depth chart and a real expansion of his receiving role before warranting a roster spot. Until that happens, he belongs on the waiver wire in all standard and deep leagues, a name worth filing away only if camp reports start pointing somewhere unexpected.

13434.name: Will Kacmarek
13434.headline: Third-string Dolphins tight end with no fantasy path
13434.body:
Kacmarek enters 2026 as a developmental tight end with no NFL production on record and no advanced-stats footprint to evaluate. Miami’s depth chart tells the story: he sits third behind Greg Dulcich and Ben Sims, with Seydou Traore and Cole Turner crowding the room behind him. A team rarely funnels meaningful target volume to a TE3, and there is nothing in his profile—no documented draft capital, no logged college breakout—that suggests a leap up the order is imminent.

For redraft purposes this is an easy call. With Dulcich and Sims ahead of him and a passing game that already spreads its tight end work thin, Kacmarek projects for the handful of snaps that come with blocking duty and emergency depth, not the routes that generate fantasy points. He is not rosterable in any standard format and is, at best, a deep-dynasty name to file away if a camp injury reshuffles the order. The verdict: waiver-wire fodder, leave him undrafted.

13435.name: Joe Royer
13435.headline: Royer is a developmental stash, not a redraft target
13435.body:
Cleveland grabbed Royer 170th overall in the fifth round, and his rookie role reflects that pedigree. He enters camp battling Jack Stoll and Blake Whiteheart for backup snaps behind starter Harold Fannin Jr., so the runway to volume is narrow. A hamstring issue sidelined him through combine and pro-day testing, costing him valuable early reps in a tight end room where the depth chart is already crowded above him.

The profile is intriguing for the patient. At Cincinnati he hauled in 79 catches working primarily from the slot, ranked fourth in yards after the catch per reception in 2025, and dropped just two passes across his final two seasons. Route diversity and pass protection still need polish. With projections near four points per game and essentially zero roster ownership, treat him as a dynasty taxi-squad flier and leave him alone in redraft.

13436.name: Kentrel Bullock
13436.headline: Camp longshot eyeing a Bengals backfield foothold
13436.body:
Bullock enters 2026 as a deep roster flier in Cincinnati, currently held in essentially none of redraft leagues. At 24 and out of South Alabama, the 5-foot-10, 204-pound back has yet to carve out a defined role, sitting low on a depth chart that gives him no immediate path to touches. With no established ADP and no draft-day buzz, he profiles strictly as a watch-list name until camp clarifies the pecking order behind the starters.

The situation does him no favors either: Cincinnati's backfield draws the league's 28th-toughest schedule for running backs in 2026, squeezing whatever early-down or change-of-pace work might trickle down. Treat Bullock as a stash only in the deepest formats. He needs an injury ahead of him or a standout preseason to register on the fantasy radar, and until then there is no reason to spend a pick on the profile.

13437.name: Noah Whittington
13437.headline: Undrafted Texans RB4 buried with no path
13437.body:
Whittington enters 2026 as a fringe roster piece with no advanced-stats footprint to speak of, which itself tells the story: he has not earned meaningful NFL touches. Houston's depth chart slots him fourth at running back, sitting behind feature back David Montgomery, rookie-class change-of-pace Woody Marks, and Jawhar Jordan. With essentially no draft capital and a backfield already three-deep ahead of him, there is no realistic standalone role to project for redraft purposes.

His only path to relevance is a multi-injury cascade ahead of him, and even then he would compete with Jordan for scraps rather than inherit a workload. There is no receiving carve-out, no goal-line equity, and no projectable volume to bank on. For 2026 he is a non-factor in standard and PPR leagues alike, a camp body and special-teams contributor at most. Leave him on the waiver wire and only monitor if Houston's backfield is hit by injuries late in the summer.

13477.name: Colbie Young
13477.headline: Stash The Bengals Rookie, But Don't Draft Him Yet
13477.body:
Cincinnati spent a fourth-round pick (140th overall) on Young, a 6-foot-5, 218-pound rookie out of Georgia who lands in one of the league's most top-heavy receiver rooms. Ja'Marr Chase and Tee Higgins lock down the targets, with Andrei Iosivas, entering a contract year, holding the next snaps. That leaves Young buried, and an ADP near 326 overall reflects it. The projection line is tiny: roughly 3.5 catches, 43 yards and a fraction of a touchdown.

The path to relevance is real but narrow. The Bengals are thin behind their two stars, so the No. 3 job is genuinely up for grabs. Young's size gives him a red-zone and contested-catch profile worth monitoring if he flashes early. For redraft purposes there's no reason to spend a pick here. In dynasty rookie formats, though, he's a cheap dart throw whose value spikes the moment an opportunity opens.

13491.name: Kaden Wetjen
13491.headline: Day-three return man with a long climb up Pittsburgh's depth chart
13491.body:
Pittsburgh spent a fourth-round pick on Wetjen, and at 5-9, 193 the 24-year-old Iowa product arrives as a depth flier rather than a rotation lock. He's buried in a crowded receiver room, carries no meaningful ADP, and sits around WR173 in dynasty and WR266 in redraft consensus. The projections tell the same story: roughly 1.6 catches for 19 yards plus a couple of carries, totaling about four fantasy points across a full year. There's nothing here for standard formats.

Where he earns a roster spot is on special teams. Wetjen handled both punt and kickoff duties at Iowa, and that return ability is his fastest path to touches and his real fantasy hook in leagues that award return yardage. The schedule offers a soft early stretch against New England, Cincinnati and Cleveland, but until he climbs ahead of established bodies, he stays a deep-dynasty stash and return-only special.

13533.name: Barion Brown
13533.headline: Track-speed returner buried on a crowded Saints depth chart
13533.body:
Brown arrives in New Orleans as a sixth-round flier whose calling card is raw, vertical speed and a special-teams resume that includes six collegiate kick-return touchdowns. The Saints already roster an established group of pass catchers, so the rookie projects as a depth option fighting for snaps rather than a target earner. The film flags are real: a 36.7% contested-catch rate, drop rates above 12% in two college seasons, and a usage chart where 54.2% of his 2025 looks came within nine yards of the line. That is a limited route tree masking limited current value.

Draft cost matches the profile, with an ADP near pick 347 and a WR201 expert ranking that keeps him off standard-league radars entirely. His realistic 2026 path to relevance runs through the return game, which makes him a return-yardage and deep-dynasty consideration only. Treat him as a speculative stash, not a contributor you start.

13554.name: CJ Williams
13554.headline: Sixth-round flyer buried in a crowded Jacksonville room
13554.body:
Jacksonville spent the 203rd pick on Williams, and the math is unforgiving: he lands in one of the league's deepest receiver groups with no established role, no published projections, and essentially zero roster share anywhere. After three quiet years split between USC and Wisconsin that netted just 35 grabs, the 6-1, 205-pound transfer finally broke out at Stanford in 2025, going for 59 catches, 749 yards and 6 scores. Encouragingly, he accounted for 42.8% of the Cardinal's receiving touchdowns, a sign the red-zone trust was real.

For 2026, treat him as a watch-list name rather than a draft target. He shouldn't be on redraft boards and is a long shot even in rookie pools; the realistic path is a late-summer dynasty stash if camp reps and a practice-squad opening break his way. The easy receiver schedule helps the offense, not his usage. File the talent away and revisit only if the depth chart thins.

13603.name: Gregory Desrosiers
13603.headline: Undrafted Charger camp body behind Hampton workhorse
13603.body:
Desrosiers is an undrafted free-agent back with no NFL advanced stats, listed as a depth piece on a Chargers roster that just invested heavily in the position. Omarion Hampton headlines the backfield as the bell-cow, with Keaton Mitchell providing explosive change-of-pace and Kimani Vidal and Jaret Patterson also ahead in the rotation. That leaves Desrosiers essentially fifth or sixth on the ground-game pecking order.

With zero career touches to model and no draft capital to suggest a coaching staff is committed to developing him, his 2026 outlook is roster-bubble at best. The realistic outcome is a practice-squad stash or a special-teams role, neither of which carries fantasy value. He would need an avalanche of injuries to even reach the active rotation. Desrosiers is a non-factor in redraft and only a name for the deepest dynasty taxi squads to track through training camp. Leave him on the waiver wire.

13621.name: Dillon Bell
13621.headline: Undrafted Georgia depth piece, purely a long-term flier
13621.body:
Bell arrived in Minnesota as an undrafted rookie, and his current standing reflects that: rostered in essentially 0% of leagues, carried at an ADP of 393 overall and 162 among receivers, with a lone expert ranking pegging him at WR265. There are no 2026 projections attached to his name and no NFL stat line yet, which tells you everything about where he sits on the roster. He is a developmental add brought in for depth, not for production.

The college resume offers something to dream on. Across 56 games at Georgia he piled up 119 catches for 1,269 yards and 11 touchdowns, profiling as a big, physical receiver who breaks tackles and can be schemed touches on gadget looks. The 2026 slate is favorable, grading out as the league's softest receiver schedule. None of that earns a draft pick this summer, but he is worth a watchlist note if injuries open a path.

13661.name: Ethan Conner
13661.headline: Undrafted Troy tight end fighting for a Jacksonville roster spot
13661.body:
Conner arrives in Jacksonville as an undrafted free agent out of Troy, where three seasons in a modest receiving role still earned him Second-Team All-Sun Belt recognition. There is no NFL track record to lean on here: no projections, no established depth-chart slot, and no defined connection to the offense. He is essentially a camp body competing for a backup tight end job, and right now he is rostered in close to zero percent of fantasy leagues for good reason.

The schedule notes are the only thing approaching a positive angle, with a soft early stretch against Denver, New England, and Cincinnati and a middling 16th-ranked tight end strength of schedule. But matchups mean nothing without snaps, and Conner has to earn those first. Leave him on the waiver wire and revisit only if he beats out the depth chart and carves out real playing time.

13712.name: Hayden Large
13712.headline: Bears TE is a watch-list dart, not a draft pick
13712.body:
Large enters 2026 as a developmental piece in Chicago's tight end room, a 24-year-old Iowa product with the prototypical 6-foot-4, 250-pound build teams covet at the position. The problem is opportunity: he sits near zero percent rostered, carries no established draft consensus, and hasn't earned an expert ranking, which tells you everything about where the staff sees him on the depth chart heading into the season.

The schedule does him no favors either, with the position drawing one of the league's tougher slates against opposing defenses. Treat Large as a pure speculative stash rather than a startable asset. If an injury opens a path to snaps, his size and frame make him worth a quick look, but until the targets materialize he belongs on the waiver wire in all standard formats. Monitor camp reports before spending anything.

13725.name: Dominic Richardson
13725.headline: Deep Cowboys flier with a runway worth tracking
13725.body:
Richardson lands at the far end of fantasy radars heading into 2026, carrying an expert consensus rank near RB202 and a roster rate hovering around zero percent. That single-evaluator footprint tells you everything about where the Tulsa product sits today: a developmental back on the Dallas roster fighting for snaps, not a draftable name in standard formats. At 6-foot, 204 pounds, he profiles as a between-the-tackles option who first has to earn a defined role before any usage projections make sense.

The one tailwind is context. Dallas draws one of the league's softer running-back schedules in 2026, which inflates the ceiling for whichever backs ultimately command touches. Richardson is purely a waiver-wire monitor, not a draft-day target, but injuries ahead of him on the depth chart could open a path. Stash him on your watchlist and let camp clarify whether the opportunity becomes real.

13750.name: TJ Harden
13750.headline: A deep Browns sleeper waiting on opportunity
13750.body:
Harden enters 2026 as a true under-the-radar name, rostered in essentially zero leagues and buried in expert consensus at RB171 (overall ECR around 543). At just 22 and built at 6-0, 218 out of SMU, the Cleveland back has the frame to handle real volume, but the projections and game logs simply aren't there yet, leaving him a pure depth-chart bet rather than a draftable asset on day one.

The schedule, at least, would reward a breakout if the touches ever arrive. Cleveland's slate grades out as the eighth-easiest the rest of the way, and the fantasy playoff stretch against New York, Baltimore, and Indianapolis ranks among the friendliest in the league. Treat Harden as a watch-list stash in deeper formats: monitor camp reps and injuries ahead of him, and pounce only once he carves out a defined role.

13778.name: EJ Smith
13778.headline: Deep-roster Chiefs back with no clear path to touches
13778.body:
Smith enters 2026 as a fringe name on Kansas City's running back board, carrying an expert consensus rank near RB #191 and a roster rate hovering around zero across leagues. At 5-foot-11 and 211 pounds heading into his age-24 season, the former Texas A&M back fits the build of a complementary runner, but there is no projected workload, game-log history, or established role pointing toward fantasy relevance. Only one ranker has bothered to slot him at all, which tells you how the market views his standing.

The lone wrinkle in his favor is scheduling: Kansas City draws a soft 2026 slate, opening against Denver as the easiest matchup on the board and closing the fantasy playoffs against New England, San Francisco, and the Chargers in a similarly forgiving stretch. That matters only if injuries thin the depth chart and force opportunity his way. Until then, he is a watch-list flier, not a draftable asset in standard formats.

13834.name: Cody Hardy
13834.headline: Developmental Saints tight end with no fantasy footprint yet
13834.body:
Hardy enters 2026 as a roster long-shot in New Orleans, rostered in essentially zero leagues and going undrafted across formats. At 6-foot-5 and 271 pounds out of NC State, he fits the classic blocking-first body type teams stash at the back of a depth chart, and there is no established production or projected workload to build a case around. Until he carves out snaps, he is a name to monitor rather than a player to target.

The path forward is purely about opportunity. With no measurable receiving role, no draft capital invested in him as a fantasy asset, and a logjam ahead of him on the tight end depth chart, any value would have to come from injuries or a training-camp surprise. For now he belongs on deep dynasty watch lists only, and redraft managers can comfortably leave him on the wire.

13844.name: Coleman Bennett
13844.headline: Deep Bears backfield flier with no clear path yet
13844.body:
Bennett enters 2026 as a fringe name buried on Chicago's depth chart, and the market reflects it: he carries an expert consensus rank near RB213, sits in roughly zero percent of rosters, and has drawn a grade from just a single ranker. The Kennesaw State product checks in at 5-foot-11, 185 pounds, profiling as a smaller back who has yet to carve out meaningful snaps behind the Bears' established options.

The schedule offers little reason to chase him. Chicago's backfield faces the 23rd-toughest slate of run defenses, with a tougher-than-average rest-of-season draw and a middling playoff stretch against Buffalo, Green Bay, and Detroit. Until Bennett climbs the rotation and earns real volume, he stays strictly a watch-list stash rather than a draftable asset. Monitor camp reps and any injury news ahead of him, but there's no actionable role to bank on right now.

13870.name: Quentin Moore
13870.headline: Deep Commanders tight end with no fantasy path
13870.body:
Moore is a roster-fringe tight end with no advanced-stat history and no established NFL role to project from. In Washington he is buried behind Chig Okonkwo as the primary pass-catcher, blocking specialist John Bates, and 2024 draft pick Ben Sinnott, a stack that leaves Moore essentially off the fantasy radar with negligible projected routes in any meaningful personnel grouping.

With no draft pedigree on record and zero measurable production, there is no redraft or dynasty case to build. His realistic 2026 ceiling is special-teams and emergency-tight-end duty, which carries no startable value in fantasy formats. Leave him on the waiver wire and revisit only if multiple injuries clear the room and camp reports surface an unexpected role bump; until then he is a clear waiver-wire name.

13873.name: Sam Scott
13873.headline: Undrafted Jets back buried with no NFL track record
13873.body:
Scott profiles as a camp-body addition for the Jets with no draft capital on record and no advanced-stat footprint to date. He sits at the very bottom of a crowded backfield headlined by Breece Hall, with Braelon Allen, Isaiah Davis, and Kene Nwangwu all ahead of him for early-down, third-down, and return work. There is simply no defined role here, and the depth ahead is younger and more established, leaving practice-squad survival as the most realistic 2026 outcome.

With zero NFL carries, targets, or snaps to ground a projection, any fantasy case is pure speculation tied to a string of injuries opening a door he would still have to win. Even then, the Jets would likely lean on the proven options before a roster long shot. For all redraft and most dynasty formats, Scott is a clear waiver-wire name to ignore until he shows something tangible in preseason. Do not roster him.

13906.name: Jaden Nixon
13906.headline: Deep-roster Packers back with no fantasy footprint yet
13906.body:
Nixon sits at the very bottom of the redraft radar, carrying an expert consensus rank of RB196 and showing up on roughly zero percent of rosters. With no 2025 production on file and no published projections, there is nothing concrete pointing to a defined role in Green Bay's backfield. The UCF product checks in at 5-foot-10 and 199 pounds, a build that fits a complementary change-of-pace profile rather than a between-the-tackles workhorse.

For now he is purely a watch-list name whose value hinges entirely on injuries or attrition ahead of him on the depth chart. The schedule does offer a sliver of intrigue: Green Bay opens against soft run defenses, and the fantasy playoff slate grades out manageable. None of that matters until Nixon earns touches, so treat him as a waiver-wire contingency, not a draftable asset, until a clear path to carries emerges.

13909.name: Elijah Tau-Tolliver
13909.headline: Deep Ravens backfield flier with size to monitor
13909.body:
Tau-Tolliver lands in Baltimore as a back-end name in a crowded backfield, currently rostered in essentially no leagues and slotted around RB214 in expert consensus. There's no draft capital priced in yet, no projection, and no clear path to early touches, so any 2026 redraft value is purely speculative until the depth chart sorts itself out behind the established options.

What keeps him on the radar is the frame. At 6'0" and 204 pounds out of Michigan State, he has the build of a between-the-tackles grinder who could carve out short-yardage or goal-line work if the Ravens thin out ahead of him. Treat him as a watch-list stash, not a draft-day target. If an injury opens a lane in this run-heavy offense, he becomes a waiver name worth a flier rather than a player you reach for now.

13913.name: Patrick Gurd
13913.headline: Undrafted Raiders tight end stuck behind Bowers wall
13913.body:
Gurd profiles as a deep depth piece with no fantasy footprint and no advanced-stat history to lean on. He sits behind one of the league's most lopsided tight-end rooms in Las Vegas, where Brock Bowers is a target-hog centerpiece, Michael Mayer is the established TE2, and Ian Thomas offers veteran insurance. That stacks Gurd at or near the bottom of the depth chart with effectively zero projected routes in three-receiver sets.

With no draft capital on record and no measurable NFL production, there is no realistic redraft or even near-term dynasty case here. His path to relevance requires multiple injuries ahead of him plus a role expansion that nothing in his resume yet supports. For all 2026 formats, this is a clear waiver-wire name to leave alone unless training-camp reports show a surprising special-teams-to-offense climb.

13949.name: Damon Bankston
13949.headline: Camp-body back buried at the bottom of New York's depth chart
13949.body:
Bankston arrives in New York as a deep-roster flier rather than a fantasy asset, an undrafted-caliber back out of New Mexico checking in at 5'10" and 186 pounds. Expert consensus pegs him near the very bottom of the position at RB208, he carries no statistical projection for the year, and he is rostered in essentially zero leagues. That combination tells the whole story: he is fighting for a spot on the back end of the Giants' backfield, not for touches that matter on Sundays.

The schedule offers little to dream on either, even if a path to snaps opened up. New York draws a soft early slate, but a Week 1 game against Dallas and an easy opening trio against the Rams, Titans and Cardinals only matter if Bankston survives cutdowns first. Until he forces his way into a defined role, treat him purely as a watch-list name in deep dynasty formats.

13957.name: Kenny Fletcher
13957.headline: Sixth tight end in a crowded Buccaneers room
13957.body:
Fletcher enters Tampa Bay with no NFL advanced stats and a developmental profile, buried at the bottom of a deep tight end room. Cade Otton owns the starting job, with Payne Durham, Ko Kieft, Bauer Sharp and Devin Culp all ahead on the depth chart, leaving Fletcher as essentially the sixth option at the position. In a Baker Mayfield offense that already spreads targets across Mike Evans, Emeka Egbuka, Chris Godwin and Otton, there is simply no target volume left to claim.

With zero measurable production to project from, Fletcher is a camp-body and practice-squad candidate whose path to relevance would require a string of injuries plus a special-teams contribution to stick on the roster. He carries no redraft value and only the faintest deep-dynasty speculation as a name to monitor. For 2026, he belongs on the waiver wire in every format, with no realistic route to weekly fantasy usefulness.

13964.name: Myles Montgomery
13964.headline: Deep New England back blocked by a crowded room
13964.body:
Montgomery arrives in New England without any NFL advanced stats and no draft pedigree in our records, marking him an undrafted long shot. The Patriots' backfield is already full, with Rhamondre Stevenson and second-year burner TreVeyon Henderson set to handle the early-down and passing-game splits, plus Lan Larison and Jam Miller chasing the leftover work. That depth gives a name like Montgomery essentially no projectable touches in 2026.

With no rushing or receiving data to anchor an outlook, the verdict comes down to the depth chart, and it is unforgiving. Montgomery is competing for a practice-squad designation more than a meaningful role, and even multiple injuries ahead of him would likely elevate established options first. There is no redraft path and only faint dynasty appeal contingent on a strong camp. For now he is a clear waiver-wire player in all formats, worth a look only if the preseason reveals real burst.

14029.name: Johnny Pascuzzi
14029.headline: Camp body in a crowded Chargers tight end room
14029.body:
Pascuzzi joins Los Angeles on a reserve track, set to fight for a back-end spot in the tight end group once OTAs open. There is no draft capital, no ADP, and no projection attached to his name, and he sits rostered in essentially zero leagues. That is the honest read on a player who signed to compete rather than to start, and nothing about his arrival signals a path to meaningful snaps in a room that already has its pecking order established.

The one mild tailwind is scheduling: his 2026 slate grades out as the eighth-easiest for the position, opening against Arizona, the sixth-softest matchup. But strength of schedule only matters if you are on the field, and Pascuzzi has to win a job first. Treat him as a deep-roster name to monitor through training camp, not a draftable asset. If injuries thin the depth chart ahead of him, revisit; otherwise leave him on waivers.

14041.name: Anderson Castle
14041.headline: Deep Colts backfield flier with size but no defined role
14041.body:
Castle enters 2026 as a developmental piece buried on the Indianapolis depth chart, carrying an expert consensus rank of RB #193 and sitting rostered in essentially zero leagues. There are no 2026 projections attached to his profile, and he logged no measurable production in 2025, so any fantasy case rests entirely on opportunity that has not yet materialized. At 6'0" and 221 pounds out of Duke, he brings the frame teams covet for short-yardage and goal-line work, but frame alone does not move the needle without touches.

The one genuinely encouraging note is scheduling: Indianapolis draws the fifth-easiest slate for running backs, and the fantasy-playoff stretch against Tennessee, Cincinnati, and Cleveland grades as the softest in the league. That matters only if Castle climbs the ladder. Until injuries or attrition hand him a role, treat him as a waiver-wire name to monitor rather than a draftable asset.

14044.name: Miles Davis
14044.headline: Undrafted Long Shot Fighting For Panthers Backfield Snaps
14044.body:
Davis arrives in Carolina as an undrafted free agent, and the early reality is sobering: he heads into OTAs scrapping for a depth role behind an established rotation rather than walking into carries. Expert consensus pegs him around the 150th running back and roughly 492nd overall, the kind of placement reserved for camp bodies who need an injury or a standout summer to crack the radar. He is currently on essentially zero percent of rosters.

The path forward is narrow but not closed. Carolina's backfield offers reps to whoever earns them in pads, and a strong preseason could vault him onto the practical-squad bubble. Just temper the timeline: the Panthers face the league's toughest projected schedule for backs in 2026, so even an opportunity would come against unforgiving fronts. For now, this is a watch-list dart, not a draftable asset.
